## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Valuation Mandate** — Examiners award *method marks* even if the final numerical answer is incorrect. Therefore, every step below is intentionally explicit. **Do not skip lines in your exam script.**

---

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Tip Deflection of a Cantilever Beam Under End Point Load

**Statement:** A cantilever beam of length $L$, flexural rigidity $EI$, is subjected to a concentrated transverse load $F$ at its free end. Derive the tip deflection $\delta$.

**Step 1 — Set up the coordinate system**
Place the origin at the clamped end (x = 0) and let x increase toward the free end (x = L). The deflection $w(x)$ is positive in the upward direction.

**Step 2 — Identify the bending moment at a section x**
Consider a section at distance $x$ from the clamped end. The portion to the right of the section is in equilibrium under the load $F$. The internal bending moment is:

$$M(x) = -F(L - x)$$

(The negative sign follows the standard convention that a downward load produces a negative bending moment.)

**Step 3 — Apply the Euler-Bernoulli moment-curvature relation**
The relationship between bending moment and beam curvature is:

$$EI \frac{d^{2}w}{dx^{2}} = M(x)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the moment expression**

$$EI \frac{d^{2}w}{dx^{2}} = -F(L - x)$$

**Step 5 — Integrate once to obtain the slope equation**

$$EI \frac{dw}{dx} = -F \left( Lx - \frac{x^{2}}{2} \right) + C_1$$

**Step 6 — Apply the boundary condition for slope at the clamped end**
At $x = 0$, the slope is zero (clamped wall): $\frac{dw}{dx}\big|_{x=0} = 0$.

Substituting $x = 0$: $0 = 0 + C_1$, therefore $C_1 = 0$.

**Step 7 — Integrate again to obtain the deflection equation**

$$EI \, w(x) = -F \left( \frac{Lx^{2}}{2} - \frac{x^{3}}{6} \right) + C_2$$

**Step 8 — Apply the boundary condition for deflection at the clamped end**
At $x = 0$, the deflection is zero: $w(0) = 0$.

Substituting: $0 = 0 + C_2$, therefore $C_2 = 0$.

**Step 9 — Write the complete deflection equation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w(x) &= -\frac{F}{EI} \left( \frac{Lx^{2}}{2} - \frac{x^{3}}{6} \right) \\
     &= -\frac{Fx^{2}}{6EI}(3L - x)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 10 — Evaluate at the free end (x = L) to find the tip deflection**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta = w(L) &= -\frac{F \cdot L^{2}}{6EI}(3L - L) \\
              &= -\frac{F \cdot L^{2}}{6EI} \cdot 2L \\
              &= -\frac{FL^{3}}{3EI}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **magnitude** of the tip deflection is:

$$\boxed{\delta_{cantilever} = \frac{FL^{3}}{3EI}}$$

The negative sign indicates downward deflection (opposite to the assumed positive direction).

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity Check**: A longer beam ($\uparrow L$), thinner beam ($\downarrow h$, hence $\downarrow I$), and softer material ($\downarrow E$) all **increase** deflection — consistent with physical intuition. ✓

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Spring Constant of a Cantilever Beam

**Statement:** Derive the equivalent spring constant of a cantilever beam of length $L$ loaded transversely at its free end.

**Step 1 — Recall from Derivation 3.1** that the tip deflection under a transverse point load $F$ at the free end is:

$$\delta = \frac{FL^{3}}{3EI}$$

**Step 2 — The definition of a linear spring** is:

$$F = k \cdot \delta \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \frac{F}{\delta}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\delta$ into the spring definition**

$$k = \frac{F}{FL^{3} / 3EI} = \frac{3EI}{L^{3}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $I$ for a rectangular cross-section** of width $b$ and thickness $h$:

$$I = \frac{bh^{3}}{12}$$

**Step 5 — Final expression for the spring constant of a rectangular cantilever**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_{cantilever} &= \frac{3E \cdot \frac{bh^{3}}{12}}{L^{3}} \\
               &= \frac{Ebh^{3}}{4L^{3}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{k_{cantilever} = \frac{Ebh^{3}}{4L^{3}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cube Law of MEMS Design** — Notice that the spring constant scales with the **CUBE of beam thickness $h^3$** but the **INVERSE CUBE of length $1/L^3$**. This is the foundational scaling law exploited in MEMS — a 2× thinner beam makes the device **8× more compliant**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bending Stress in a Beam Under Pure Bending

**Statement:** Derive the bending stress distribution across a beam of rectangular cross-section subjected to a pure bending moment $M$.

**Step 1 — Geometric assumption (Euler-Bernoulli)**
Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis after bending. A fibre at distance $y$ from the neutral axis has length:

$$L' = (\rho - y) \, d\theta = (\rho - y)\frac{dx}{\rho}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the strain in that fibre**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon &= \frac{L' - L}{L} = \frac{(\rho - y)dx/\rho - dx}{dx} \\
            &= \frac{-y}{\rho} = -\frac{y}{\rho}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Hooke's law to get the stress**

$$\sigma = E\varepsilon = -\frac{Ey}{\rho}$$

**Step 4 — Note that curvature** $\kappa = 1/\rho$, and recognize that $E/\rho$ is constant for a given beam and moment. Let us write:

$$\sigma = -\frac{M \, y}{I}$$

This last step is justified by requiring force equilibrium: the stress distribution must produce a net moment equal to the applied moment $M$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= -\int_A \sigma \cdot y \, dA \\
  &= -\int_A \left(-\frac{Ey}{\rho}\right) y \, dA \\
  &= \frac{E}{\rho} \int_A y^{2} \, dA = \frac{E}{\rho} I
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $1/\rho$:**

$$\frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{M}{EI}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back into the stress equation**

$$\boxed{\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}}$$

**Step 7 — Maximum bending stress** occurs at the surface (farthest fibre) where $y = h/2$:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M \cdot (h/2)}{I} = \frac{6M}{bh^{2}}$$

(for a rectangular cross-section with $I = bh^3/12$).

> [!TIP]
> **MEMS Failure Mode** — The maximum bending stress is the first quantity an engineer checks to ensure $\sigma_{max} < \sigma_{yield}/FoS$. This is the most common numerical asked in KTU exams.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Thermal Stress in a Constrained MEMS Beam

**Statement:** A MEMS beam of length $L$ is constrained between two rigid walls and subjected to a temperature change $\Delta T$. Derive the resulting axial thermal stress.

**Step 1 — Free thermal expansion** (if unconstrained) would be:

$$\Delta L_{free} = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$$

where $\alpha$ is the coefficient of thermal expansion (1/°C).

**Step 2 — The constraint prevents this expansion.** To restore the original length, the walls must apply a compressive force $F_T$. The corresponding mechanical compression is:

$$\Delta L_{mech} = \frac{F_T \cdot L}{AE}$$

**Step 3 — Equilibrium condition** (net length change = 0):

$$\Delta L_{free} - \Delta L_{mech} = 0$$

$$\alpha L \Delta T - \frac{F_T L}{AE} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the thermal force**

$$F_T = \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot A \cdot E$$

**Step 5 — Compute the thermal stress** by dividing by area $A$:

$$\boxed{\sigma_T = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Exam Trap** — Students often forget that this stress is **compressive** (when $\Delta T > 0$ and $\alpha > 0$). The sign matters when combining with residual deposition stress for total stress computation.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix — When to Use What

| Problem Type | Primary Formula | Key Parameter | Common Mistake |
|--------------|-----------------|---------------|----------------|
| Axial loading | $\sigma = F/A$ | Use actual cross-section $A$ | Forgetting the hole in a perforated beam |
| Cantilever tip force | $\delta = FL^3 / 3EI$ | $I = bh^3/12$ for rectangle | Confusing $b$ and $h$ (height is bending direction) |
| Distributed load | $w = qL^4 / 8EI$ | Distributed load $q$ in N/m | Using total load $F$ instead of $q \cdot L$ |
| Torsion of shaft | $\tau = Tr/J$ | $J = \pi d^4/32$ for solid shaft | Mixing up $J$ (polar) with $I$ (area) |
| Buckling (Euler) | $P_{cr} = \pi^2 EI / L_e^2$ | Effective length $L_e$ | Wrong boundary condition coefficient |
| Thermal stress | $\sigma_T = E\alpha \Delta T$ | Material-specific $\alpha$ | Sign error |
| Resonant frequency | $f_0 \propto \sqrt{k/m}$ | Effective mass $\approx 0.24 m$ | Using total mass instead of effective |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of MEMS Mechanical Design Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Design Requirements] --> B[Select Material and Process]
    B --> C[Choose Geometry Parameters]
    C --> D[Compute Stress and Strain]
    D --> E{Is Stress within Limit}
    E -->|No| F[Revise Geometry or Material]
    F --> C
    E -->|Yes| G[Compute Deflection and Stiffness]
    G --> H[Evaluate Resonant Frequency]
    H --> I{Is Frequency in Target Band}
    I -->|No| F
    I -->|Yes| J[Check Factor of Safety and Fatigue]
    J --> K{FoS within Acceptable Range}
    K -->|No| F
    K -->|Yes| L[Finalize Design and Fabricate]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the Flowchart** — The diagram captures the *iterative nature* of MEMS design. Because the design space is constrained by lithographic resolution and process compatibility, designers loop through geometry revision (steps C → F) several times before convergence. This is a recurring theme in **KTU 2024 design-oriented questions** (CO1, CO4 mapping).

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Classification of MEMS Mechanical Components

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root[Mechanical Micro Components] --> Beam1[Beams]
    Root --> Memb[Diaphragms and Membranes]
    Root --> Plate[Plates]
    Root --> Spring[Springs and Flexures]
    Root --> Gear[Micro Gears and Couplings]
    Root --> Tors[Torsional Structures]
    Beam1 --> Cant[Cantilever Beam]
    Beam1 --> CC[Clamped Clamped Beam]
    Beam1 --> SS[Simply Supported Beam]
    Spring --> Fold[Folded Beam Flexure]
    Spring --> Crab[Crab Leg Flexure]
    Tors --> TBar[Torsion Bar]
    Tors --> Mir[Micro Mirror]
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Stress Analysis Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Load[Applied Load] --> TypeA{Type of Loading}
    TypeA -->|Axial| NA[Normal Stress sigma eq F by A]
    TypeA -->|Transverse| BM[Bending Stress sigma eq M y by I]
    TypeA -->|Twisting| TR[Shear Stress tau eq T r by J]
    TypeA -->|Thermal| TS[Thermal Stress sigma T eq E alpha deltaT]
    NA --> Cmb[Combine Stresses]
    BM --> Cmb
    TR --> Cmb
    TS --> Cmb
    Cmb --> VM[Compute Von Mises Equivalent Stress]
    VM --> Check{sigma VM less than sigma yield by FoS}
    Check -->|Yes| Safe[Design is Safe]
    Check -->|No| Fail[Redesign Required]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read the decision tree** — Identify the loading on your MEMS component, branch to the appropriate stress equation, then combine the principal stresses via the **Von Mises criterion** before checking against the yield strength divided by the chosen factor of safety. This flow is the **backbone of KTU Part B (14-mark) design questions**.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assessment Pattern Reference (KTU 2024 Scheme OEMET836)**
> - **Part A**: 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks
> - **Part B (ESE Module-Level)**: 1 question × 14 marks (with internal choice, i.e., Q-A or Q-B, each with sub-parts 7 + 7)
> - All questions map to **Bloom Levels L2–L4** (Understand, Apply, Analyze)

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

> Define the following terms with respect to MEMS mechanical design:
> (a) Young's Modulus
> (b) Poisson's Ratio
> (c) Factor of Safety

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Young's Modulus (E):** It is the ratio of longitudinal stress to longitudinal strain within the elastic limit. It measures the intrinsic stiffness of a material. For Silicon, $E_{Si} \approx 190$ GPa. Units: **N/m² or Pa**.

**(b) Poisson's Ratio (ν):** It is the negative of the ratio of lateral strain to axial strain when a material is subjected to uniaxial loading. Mathematically, $\nu = -\varepsilon_{lateral} / \varepsilon_{axial}$. For Silicon, $\nu_{Si} \approx 0.28$. It is **dimensionless**.

**(c) Factor of Safety (FoS):** It is the ratio of the material's failure strength (yield or ultimate) to the maximum allowable stress in the component. $FoS = \sigma_{yield} / \sigma_{max}$. It is dimensionless and typically chosen between **1.5 and 4** for MEMS devices. **[3 marks]**

---

#### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> List and briefly explain any three important mechanical properties to be considered while selecting a material for MEMS structural design.

**Model Answer:**

The three most important mechanical properties are:

1. **Young's Modulus (E):** Determines the stiffness of the microstructure. Higher $E$ leads to stiffer beams but also higher resonant frequencies. For example, Silicon Carbide (SiC) has $E \approx 450$ GPa, much higher than Polysilicon.

2. **Yield Strength (σ_y):** Determines the maximum stress the material can withstand before permanent deformation. For brittle materials like Silicon, the *fracture strength* (~1–7 GPa depending on surface defects) replaces yield strength.

3. **Density (ρ):** Affects the inertial mass of moving parts and thus the dynamic response. Lower density structures (e.g., **Aluminum, ρ = 2700 kg/m³**) have higher resonant frequencies for the same stiffness.

*Other valid properties: Poisson's ratio, Fatigue strength, Fracture toughness (K_IC), Hardness, Thermal expansion coefficient (α).* **[3 marks]**

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Module Choice Questions

#### ✦ Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q.A.** A MEMS cantilever beam made of single-crystal Silicon is used as the sensing element of an accelerometer. The beam has length $L = 500 \,\mu m$, width $b = 50 \,\mu m$, and thickness $h = 5 \,\mu m$. A proof-mass of $m = 1 \times 10^{-9}$ kg is attached at the free end.

> **(a)** Derive the expression for the tip deflection of a cantilever beam under a transverse point load at the free end. Calculate the deflection when the beam experiences an inertial force of $F = 5 \,\mu N$ at the tip. Assume $E = 190$ GPa.
>
> **(b)** Derive the spring constant expression for a cantilever beam of rectangular cross-section. Compute the spring constant and the natural resonant frequency of the beam.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of tip deflection (7 marks):**

Refer to **Derivation 3.1** in Section 3 of these notes for the complete step-by-step derivation. The final result is:

$$\delta = \frac{FL^{3}}{3EI}$$

**Numerical evaluation:**

Step 1 — Compute the second moment of area for the rectangular cross-section:

$$I = \frac{bh^{3}}{12} = \frac{(50 \times 10^{-6})(5 \times 10^{-6})^{3}}{12}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \frac{(50 \times 10^{-6})(125 \times 10^{-18})}{12} \\
  &= \frac{6250 \times 10^{-24}}{12} \\
  &= 5.2083 \times 10^{-22} \, m^{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Substitute into the deflection formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta &= \frac{(5 \times 10^{-6})(500 \times 10^{-6})^{3}}{3 \times (190 \times 10^{9}) \times (5.2083 \times 10^{-22})} \\
       &= \frac{(5 \times 10^{-6})(1.25 \times 10^{-13})}{3 \times (190 \times 10^{9}) \times (5.2083 \times 10^{-22})} \\
       &= \frac{6.25 \times 10^{-19}}{2.969 \times 10^{-10}} \\
       &= 2.105 \times 10^{-9} \, m \\
       &= 2.105 \, nm
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\delta \approx 2.1$ nm. **[7 marks — 5 for derivation, 2 for calculation]**

---

**(b) Spring constant and resonant frequency (7 marks):**

**Derivation of spring constant** (Refer to Derivation 3.2):

$$k_{cantilever} = \frac{Ebh^{3}}{4L^{3}}$$

**Numerical evaluation of k:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k &= \frac{(190 \times 10^{9})(50 \times 10^{-6})(5 \times 10^{-6})^{3}}{4(500 \times 10^{-6})^{3}} \\
  &= \frac{(190 \times 10^{9})(50 \times 10^{-6})(125 \times 10^{-18})}{4(1.25 \times 10^{-13})} \\
  &= \frac{1.1875 \times 10^{-9}}{5.0 \times 10^{-13}} \\
  &= 2375 \, N/m
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $k \approx 2.375 \times 10^{3}$ N/m. **[3 marks]**

**Natural resonant frequency** using $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_0 &= \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{2375}{1 \times 10^{-9}}} \\
    &= \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{2.375 \times 10^{12}} \\
    &= \frac{1.541 \times 10^{6}}{2\pi} \\
    &= 2.453 \times 10^{5} \, Hz \\
    &= 245.3 \, kHz
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $f_0 \approx 245.3$ kHz. **[4 marks]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Q.A)**
> 1. **Confusing $I = bh^3/12$ vs $I = hb^3/12$** — The bending direction is governed by the dimension perpendicular to the load. Since the load is transverse and the beam is thin in the $h$ direction, $h$ must be cubed.
> 2. **Unit Errors** — Always convert micrometers to meters **before** substituting. $L = 500 \,\mu m = 500 \times 10^{-6}$ m, not $500$ m.
> 3. **Forgetting to use effective mass** — In a 14-mark question, if asked for an exact $f_0$, use $m_{eff} \approx 0.24 \rho L b h$ (distributed mass model). Using total mass $m$ at the tip is an *approximation* that overestimates $f_0$.

---

#### ✦ Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q.B.** A MEMS pressure sensor uses a square Silicon diaphragm of side $a = 2$ mm and thickness $h = 25 \,\mu m$ to sense pressure. The diaphragm is clamped at all four edges.

> **(a)** Derive the bending stress equation for a beam under pure bending, $\sigma = My/I$. Explain the meaning of each term in the equation.
>
> **(b)** For a uniformly applied pressure $P = 50$ kPa, calculate the maximum bending stress at the centre of the diaphragm using the small-deflection plate theory result: $\sigma_{max} = \frac{0.308 P a^{2}}{h^{2}}$ (for square plate, clamped edges, Poisson's ratio = 0.3). Use $E = 190$ GPa.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of bending stress (7 marks):**

Refer to **Derivation 3.3** in Section 3. The key steps are:

1. Assume *plane sections remain plane* (Euler-Bernoulli hypothesis).
2. A fibre at distance $y$ from the neutral axis experiences strain $\varepsilon = -y/\rho$ where $\rho$ is the radius of curvature.
3. Apply Hooke's law: $\sigma = E\varepsilon = -Ey/\rho$.
4. Use moment equilibrium to find $1/\rho = M/EI$, hence $\sigma = My/I$.

**Term explanations:**

- **σ (Bending stress):** The longitudinal stress induced by bending, in Pa.
- **M (Bending moment):** Internal moment at the cross-section, in N·m.
- **y (Distance from neutral axis):** Perpendicular distance from the centroidal neutral axis to the fibre in question, in m.
- **I (Second moment of area):** Geometric property of the cross-section that resists bending, in m⁴. For a rectangle: $I = bh^3/12$. **[7 marks]**

---

**(b) Maximum bending stress calculation (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Identify given values:
- $P = 50 \times 10^{3}$ Pa
- $a = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $h = 25 \times 10^{-6}$ m

Step 2 — Compute $a^2$ and $h^2$:

$$a^{2} = (2 \times 10^{-3})^{2} = 4 \times 10^{-6} \, m^{2}$$

$$h^{2} = (25 \times 10^{-6})^{2} = 625 \times 10^{-12} = 6.25 \times 10^{-10} \, m^{2}$$

Step 3 — Substitute into the given stress formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{max} &= \frac{0.308 \times (50 \times 10^{3}) \times (4 \times 10^{-6})}{6.25 \times 10^{-10}} \\
             &= \frac{0.308 \times 2 \times 10^{-1}}{6.25 \times 10^{-10}} \\
             &= \frac{6.16 \times 10^{-2}}{6.25 \times 10^{-10}} \\
             &= 9.856 \times 10^{7} \, Pa \\
             &= 98.56 \, MPa
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 4 — Verify the Factor of Safety:
Assume yield strength of Si $\sigma_y \approx 7$ GPa:

$$FoS = \frac{7 \times 10^{9}}{9.856 \times 10^{7}} \approx 71$$

**Result:** $\sigma_{max} \approx 98.56$ MPa, $FoS \approx 71$ — design is **very safe**. **[7 marks]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Q.B)**
> 1. **Wrong coefficient in plate formula** — The coefficient 0.308 is valid **only for a clamped square plate with $\nu = 0.3$**. Using 0.308 for a *circular* or *simply supported* plate is incorrect.
> 2. **Not computing FoS** — KTU examiners specifically allocate marks (usually 1–2) for a *design verification statement* (e.g., "Since $FoS > 1.5$, the design is safe"). Omitting it costs easy marks.
> 3. **Ignoring the small-deflection assumption** — The formula $\sigma_{max} = 0.308 P a^2 / h^2$ is valid **only when $w_{max} < 0.2h$**. For high pressures or thin diaphragms, large-deflection theory must be used.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this checklist and revise it 24 hours before your ESE.**

**🔑 Foundational Concepts**
- ☐ MEMS mechanical design is **scaling-down of classical mechanics** — same equations, smaller dimensions.
- ☐ The four pillars: **Stress, Strain, Deflection, Stiffness**.

**🔑 Material Constants (Memorize)**
- ☐ $E_{Si} = 190$ GPa, $\nu_{Si} = 0.28$, $\rho_{Si} = 2330$ kg/m³
- ☐ $E_{Steel} \approx 200$ GPa (comparison anchor)
- ☐ $\sigma_{y, PolySi} \approx 1$–3 GPa
- ☐ $K_{IC, Si} \approx 1$ MPa·m^(1/2)

**🔑 Key Equations (Memorize with Units)**
- ☐ $\sigma = F/A$ — axial stress
- ☐ $\sigma = My/I$ — bending stress
- ☐ $\delta = FL^3 / 3EI$ — cantilever tip deflection
- ☐ $k = 3EI / L^3$ — cantilever spring constant
- ☐ $k = 192EI / L^3$ — clamped-clamped center-load spring constant
- ☐ $\sigma_T = E\alpha\Delta T$ — thermal stress (sign matters!)
- ☐ $FoS = \sigma_{yield} / \sigma_{applied}$

**🔑 The Cube Law**
- ☐ $k \propto h^3$ — Beam thickness dominates stiffness.
- ☐ $k \propto 1/L^3$ — Halving the beam length increases stiffness **8×**.

**🔑 Common Exam Traps**
- ☐ Confusing $b$ and $h$ in the moment-of-inertia formula.
- ☐ Forgetting to convert $\mu$m to m before substitution.
- ☐ Using total mass instead of effective mass in $f_0$ calculation.
- ☐ Missing the sign in thermal stress.
- ☐ Using 0.308 plate coefficient for the wrong boundary condition.
- ☐ Failing to verify FoS after computing maximum stress.

**🔑 KTU 2024 Bloom-Level Mapping**
- ☐ L1 (Remember): Definitions of E, ν, FoS — Part A questions.
- ☐ L2 (Understand): Explain beam theory and boundary conditions — Part A or Part B sub-part.
- ☐ L3 (Apply): Compute deflection, spring constant — Part B (a).
- ☐ L4 (Analyze): Design verification with FoS, combine stress states — Part B (b).

**🔑 Last-Minute Memory Aid — "K-H-S-D"**
- **K** = Spring constant (k = 3EI/L³ for cantilever)
- **H** = Hooke's law (σ = Eε)
- **S** = Stress (σ = My/I for bending)
- **D** = Deflection (δ = FL³/3EI for cantilever)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip** — Always draw a **Free Body Diagram (FBD)** before writing any equation. Examiners award 1–2 marks for the FBD even if the subsequent calculation has minor errors. A clear FBD communicates your *method* — the most heavily weighted component in 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Working Principles of Microsystems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET836 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Working Principles of Microsystems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Working Principles of Microsystems** refers to the fundamental physical, chemical, biological, and engineering laws that govern the operation of miniaturized devices that integrate mechanical, electrical, optical, fluidic, and thermal elements on a single substrate (typically silicon) to perform sensing, actuation, signal processing, and communication functions.

In the formal KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, a **microsystem** is not merely a "small machine" — it is an integrated, multi-domain, transducer-based device in which at least one physical dimension is on the **micrometer scale** (typically **1 µm to 1 mm**), and in which **energy conversion (transduction)** between non-electrical and electrical domains is the central operating principle.

The working principle, therefore, is rooted in three foundational pillars:

1. **Transduction** — converting one form of energy into another (e.g., mechanical strain → voltage).
2. **Scaling Physics** — how fundamental forces (inertial, viscous, electrostatic, surface tension) change when device dimensions shrink.
3. **Energy Domain Coupling** — the simultaneous interaction of mechanical, electrical, thermal, magnetic, chemical, and optical effects at the microscale.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The working principle of *any* microsystem can always be reduced to identifying **the input energy domain → the transduction mechanism → the output energy domain → the signal conditioning path**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Think of a microsystem exactly like a **human sensory organ embedded in a phone**:

| Human Analogy | Microsystem Equivalent |
|---------------|------------------------|
| Skin (touches pressure) | Piezoresistive pressure sensor |
| Inner ear (senses vibration) | Capacitive accelerometer |
| Tongue (tastes chemicals) | ISFET / Chemiresistor |
| Eye (sees light) | Photodiode / Phototransistor |
| Hand muscles (grip) | Electrostatic / Thermal microactuator |

The key insight is this: just as your skin converts a *mechanical poke* into a *nerve impulse* (an electrical signal), a microsensor converts a *physical quantity* into an *electrical signal* via a **transducer**.

> [!TIP]
> A useful student mnemonic — **"I-O-M-E"**:
> - **I**nput energy domain (what is being measured?)
> - **O**utput energy domain (usually electrical)
> - **M**echanism of transduction (how does conversion happen?)
> - **E**lectronics (signal conditioning and readout)

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Microsystem Working Principles

The following physical constants and standard metrics appear repeatedly in KTU questions on this topic:

- **Density of silicon:** **ρ_Si ≈ 2.33 × 10³ kg/m³**
- **Young's modulus of silicon:** **E_Si ≈ 190 GPa (along ⟨110⟩ direction)**
- **Dielectric constant of vacuum:** **ε₀ ≈ 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**
- **Permittivity of free space for electrostatic force**
- **Surface tension of water:** **γ ≈ 72 mN/m at 25 °C**
- **Viscosity of air at STP:** **μ_air ≈ 1.8 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s**
- **Piezoelectric coefficient of quartz:** **d₁₁ ≈ 2.3 pC/N**

## 1.4 Why "Working Principles" Is a Separate Module Topic

In the KTU 2024 OEMET836 syllabus, "Working Principles" is treated as a *meta-topic* — it is the conceptual lens through which every sensor and actuator in subsequent modules is analyzed. Every device, whether a gyroscope, a micropump, or an RF MEMS switch, can be mapped onto a **standardized working-principle framework**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standardized Framework:** Every microsystem is described by:
> 1. The **input physical stimulus** (force, pressure, temperature, light, chemical concentration, magnetic field).
> 2. The **sensing/actuation mechanism** (piezoresistive, capacitive, piezoelectric, thermoelectric, electrochemical, optical, magnetic).
> 3. The **output electrical signal** (voltage, current, impedance change, frequency shift, phase shift).
> 4. The **packaging and interface** (electrical, mechanical, thermal, fluidic).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Primary Energy Domains in Microsystems

Every microsystem operates in or between these six energy domains. KTU 2024 questions frequently ask students to *identify the input-output domain pair*.

| Domain | Typical Stimulus/Response | Representative Variable |
|--------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| Mechanical | Force, displacement, pressure, strain | F [N], x [m], P [Pa], ε [–] |
| Electrical | Voltage, current, charge, impedance | V [V], I [A], Q [C], Z [Ω] |
| Thermal | Temperature, heat flux, entropy | T [K], Q [W], ΔS [J/K] |
| Magnetic | Magnetic field, flux density | H [A/m], B [T] |
| Chemical | Concentration, pH, gas partial pressure | C [mol/m³], pH [–] |
| Optical | Light intensity, wavelength, phase | I [W/m²], λ [m], φ [rad] |

> [!NOTE]
> A device is classified as a **sensor** when energy flows *from* a non-electrical domain *to* the electrical domain. A device is classified as an **actuator** when energy flows *from* the electrical domain *to* a non-electrical domain.

## 2.2 Major Transduction Mechanisms (Operating Principles)

The working principle of a microsystem is governed by its dominant transduction mechanism. Below is the canonical KTU table:

| Transduction Type | Physical Law Exploited | Typical Devices |
|------------------|------------------------|-----------------|
| **Piezoresistive** | Change in resistivity under strain | Pressure sensors, accelerometers, strain gauges |
| **Capacitive** | Change in capacitance with gap/area change | Accelerometers, gyroscopes, microphones |
| **Piezoelectric** | Charge generation under stress (and converse) | Energy harvesters, ultrasonic transducers, inkjet heads |
| **Electrostatic** | Force between charged plates | Comb drives, RF switches, micromirrors |
| **Thermal (Conductive)** | Heat transfer depends on flow rate | Mass flow sensors, Pirani gauges |
| **Thermoelectric** | Seebeck effect / Peltier effect | Thermopiles, micro-coolers |
| **Resonant** | Frequency shift under mass/stress loading | Resonant mass sensors, gyroscopes |
| **Magnetic (Lorentz)** | F = IL × B | MEMS loudspeakers, magnetometers |
| **Optical** | Refractive index change / interference | MOEMS, interferometric sensors |
| **Electrochemical** | Nernst / Butler-Volmer equation | ISFET, biosensors, pH sensors |

## 2.3 Scaling Laws — The Heart of Microsystem Working Principles

When a device's linear dimension **L** is scaled by a factor **α (0 < α < 1)**, all physical quantities scale according to specific power laws. These are **the most heavily tested formulas in KTU OEMET836 Module 1**.

### 2.3.1 Geometric Scaling Rules

| Quantity | Original | Scaled | Scaling Exponent |
|----------|----------|--------|------------------|
| Length | L | αL | α¹ |
| Area | L² | α²L² | α² |
| Volume | L³ | α³L³ | α³ |
| Velocity (typical) | v | v | α⁰ |
| Acceleration | a | a | α⁰ |
| Force | F ∝ ρL²v² | α²F | α² |
| Mass | m ∝ ρL³ | α³m | α³ |
| Pressure (inertial) | P ∝ F/A | P | α⁰ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pressure (force per unit area) is scale-invariant!** This single fact explains why microsystems experience enormous structural stresses relative to their macro counterparts.

### 2.3.2 Force-Ratio Scaling (Most Important Concept for KTU)

When a device shrinks, different forces scale at different rates. The dominant force at the microscale shifts dramatically:

| Force Type | Scaling with L | Dominant At |
|-----------|----------------|-------------|
| Inertial force (F = ma) | α⁴ | Macroscale |
| Gravitational force (F = mg) | α³ | Macroscale |
| Viscous force (F = 6πμrv) | α¹ | Microscale |
| Surface tension (F = γL) | α¹ | Microscale |
| Electrostatic force (F = εAV²/2d²) | α² (capacitor type) | Microscale |
| Magnetic force (F = B²A/2μ₀) | α² | Microscale |
| Elastic force (F = kx, k ∝ E·w·t/L) | α¹ | Both |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** At the microscale, **surface and viscous forces dominate**, while **gravitational and inertial forces become negligible**. This is the *single most important conceptual takeaway* of Module 1.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table contains **every formula a KTU OEMET836 student must memorize** for "Working Principles of Microsystems". **All pipes are rendered as `\vert` to preserve table syntax.**

| \# | Formula Name | Equation | SI Units | Used For |
|---|--------------|----------|----------|----------|
| 1 | Capacitive transducer | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | F (Farads) | Gap-change sensors, gap-change actuators |
| 2 | Capacitive gap change | $\dfrac{\Delta C}{C} = \dfrac{\Delta d}{d}$ (small signal) | dimensionless | Linear displacement sensing |
| 3 | Capacitive area change | $\dfrac{\Delta C}{C} = \dfrac{\Delta A}{A}$ | dimensionless | Lateral displacement sensing |
| 4 | Piezoresistive gauge factor | $G = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$ | dimensionless | Sensitivity of strain gauges |
| 5 | Piezoresistive change | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = \pi_l \sigma_l + \pi_t \sigma_t$ | dimensionless | Multi-axial stress in silicon |
| 6 | Electrostatic force (parallel plate) | $F_e = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 d^2}$ | N | Comb drives, parallel plate actuators |
| 7 | Electrostatic pressure | $P_e = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 V^2}{2 d^2}$ | Pa | Pull-in voltage derivation |
| 8 | Pull-in voltage | $V_{PI} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 k d_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 A}}$ | V | Stable operating range limit |
| 9 | Piezoelectric charge | $Q = d \cdot F$ | C | Direct piezoelectric effect |
| 10 | Piezoelectric voltage | $V = \dfrac{d \cdot F}{C}$ | V | Sensor signal estimation |
| 11 | Beam stiffness (cantilever) | $k = \dfrac{E w t^3}{4 L^3}$ | N/m | Spring constant of micro-beams |
| 12 | Resonant frequency (cantilever) | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m_{eff}}} = \dfrac{1.875^2}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{E I}{\rho A L^4}}$ | Hz | Resonant sensor design |
| 13 | Mass loading sensitivity | $\Delta f = - \dfrac{f_0}{2}\dfrac{\Delta m}{m_{eff}}$ | Hz | Mass sensor calibration |
| 14 | Reynolds number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v L_c}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Flow regime classification |
| 15 | Pressure drop (laminar) | $\Delta P = \dfrac{12 \mu L Q}{w h^3}$ | Pa | Microfluidic channel design |
| 16 | Thermal conductance | $G_{th} = k_{th}\dfrac{A}{L}$ | W/K | Micro heat transfer |
| 17 | Seebeck EMF | $V = S \cdot \Delta T$ | V | Thermopile output |
| 18 | Piezo-resistive coeff. (Si) | $\pi_{44} \approx 138 \times 10^{-11}$ | Pa⁻¹ | Silicon gauge factor calc. |
| 19 | Surface-to-volume ratio scaling | $\dfrac{A}{V} \propto \dfrac{1}{L}$ | m⁻¹ | Explains dominance of surface effects |
| 20 | Capacitance variation (comb drive) | $C = N \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | F | Comb-drive actuator/sensor |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Strategy Tip:** Memorize formulas **1, 2, 6, 7, 8, 11, and 12** first — these alone cover ~80% of the numerical problems in Module 1.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding working principles is **not academic** — it directly determines:

1. **Device selection** for a given application (e.g., why choose piezoresistive over capacitive for a high-pressure tire sensor — because of large force-handling capacity despite temperature cross-sensitivity).
2. **Process compatibility** (piezoresistive sensors require doping; capacitive sensors need precise gap definition).
3. **Failure analysis** (pull-in voltage violation, fatigue, stiction — all derive from working-principle equations).
4. **System integration** with ASICs, signal conditioning circuits, and packaging.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section presents exhaustive derivations of the **four most-tested working-principle equations** in KTU OEMET836 Module 1. Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Electrostatic Force Between Parallel Plates

**Statement:** Show that the attractive electrostatic force between two parallel conducting plates of area **A**, separated by gap **d**, carrying voltage **V**, is

$$F_e = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 d^2}$$

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Write the stored electrostatic energy.**

For a parallel plate capacitor, the capacitance is

$$C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}$$

The energy stored in the electric field is

$$U = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^2$$

Substituting $C$:

$$U = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d} \cdot V^2 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 d}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the principle of virtual work.**

The attractive force is the negative gradient of stored energy with respect to gap displacement, holding voltage constant:

$$F_e = - \dfrac{\partial U}{\partial d}\bigg\vert_{V=\text{const}}$$

Differentiating $U = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2} \cdot d^{-1}$ with respect to $d$:

$$\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial d} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2} \cdot (-1) \cdot d^{-2} = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 d^2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the negative sign.**

$$F_e = - \left( -\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 d^2}\right) = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 d^2}$$

This is the magnitude of the attractive electrostatic force pulling the plates together.

**Step 4 — Interpret the result.**

The force scales as $V^2$, $A$, and $1/d^2$. Doubling the voltage **quadruples** the force; halving the gap **quadruples** the force — explaining the strong non-linear behavior of electrostatic microsystems near pull-in.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Pull-In Voltage

**Statement:** Derive the pull-in voltage for a parallel-plate electrostatic actuator with spring constant **k**, initial gap **d₀**, and plate area **A**.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Establish force balance.**

A movable plate is suspended on a mechanical spring of stiffness **k**. Mechanical restoring force opposes electrostatic attraction:

$$F_{\text{mech}} = k \cdot x$$

where $x$ is displacement from initial position. The gap becomes $d = d_0 - x$.

**Step 2 — Write the electrostatic force at the displaced gap.**

$$F_e = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 (d_0 - x)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Equilibrium condition.**

At static equilibrium:

$$\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 (d_0 - x)^2} = k x$$

**Step 4 — Introduce normalized displacement.**

Let $y = x / d_0$, so $x = y d_0$ and $d_0 - x = d_0 (1 - y)$:

$$\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 d_0^2 (1 - y)^2} = k y d_0$$

**Step 5 — Recognize that pull-in occurs at the stability limit.**

Pull-in occurs at the point where $dF_e/dx = dF_{\text{mech}}/dx$, beyond which the electrostatic force always exceeds the mechanical restoring force.

Differentiating both sides with respect to $x$:

$$\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{(d_0 - x)^3} = k$$

**Step 6 — Solve the two simultaneous equations.**

From Step 5:

$$\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{(d_0 - x)^3} = k$$

From Step 3:

$$\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 (d_0 - x)^2} = k x$$

Dividing the Step 3 equation by the Step 5 equation:

$$\dfrac{(d_0 - x)}{2} = x$$

$$\therefore \quad d_0 - x = 2x \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = \dfrac{d_0}{3}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute back to find the pull-in voltage.**

From Step 5 with $x = d_0/3$, so $d_0 - x = 2d_0/3$:

$$\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V_{PI}^2}{(2 d_0/3)^3} = k$$

$$V_{PI}^2 = \dfrac{8 k d_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 A}$$

$$\boxed{V_{PI} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 k d_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 A}}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Beyond 1/3 of the initial gap, the actuator snaps in. Designers must operate below this voltage — typically at **0.4 to 0.7 of $V_{PI}$** for a stable working range.

## 3.3 Derivation of Resonant Frequency for a Cantilever Beam

**Statement:** Derive the fundamental resonant frequency of a cantilever beam of length **L**, width **w**, thickness **t**, density **ρ**, and Young's modulus **E**.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Euler-Bernoulli beam equation.**

The transverse deflection $u(x, t)$ of a uniform beam is governed by:

$$E I \dfrac{\partial^4 u}{\partial x^4} + \rho A \dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = 0$$

where $I = \dfrac{w t^3}{12}$ is the second moment of area and $A = w t$ is the cross-sectional area.

**Step 2 — Separation of variables.**

Assume $u(x, t) = X(x) \cdot T(t)$. Substituting and dividing by $X T$:

$$E I \dfrac{X^{\text{IV}}(x)}{X(x)} = -\rho A \dfrac{T''(t)}{T(t)} = \rho A \omega^2$$

where $\omega^2$ is the separation constant.

**Step 3 — Spatial ODE and boundary conditions.**

For a cantilever beam, the boundary conditions are:

- $u(0, t) = 0$ (clamped end, zero deflection)
- $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{x=0} = 0$ (clamped end, zero slope)
- $\dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}\bigg\vert_{x=L} = 0$ (free end, zero bending moment)
- $\dfrac{\partial^3 u}{\partial x^3}\bigg\vert_{x=L} = 0$ (free end, zero shear force)

**Step 4 — General solution.**

The spatial solution is:

$$X(x) = C_1 \cos(\beta x) + C_2 \sin(\beta x) + C_3 \cosh(\beta x) + C_4 \sinh(\beta x)$$

where $\beta = \sqrt[4]{\dfrac{\rho A \omega^2}{E I}}$.

**Step 5 — Apply clamped-end conditions (x = 0).**

$X(0) = 0 \Rightarrow C_1 + C_3 = 0$, so $C_3 = -C_1$.

$X'(0) = 0 \Rightarrow \beta(C_2 + C_4) = 0$, so $C_4 = -C_2$.

Therefore:

$$X(x) = C_1[\cos(\beta x) - \cosh(\beta x)] + C_2[\sin(\beta x) - \sinh(\beta x)]$$

**Step 6 — Apply free-end conditions (x = L).**

Setting $X''(L) = 0$ and $X'''(L) = 0$ gives the characteristic equation:

$$\cos(\beta L) \cdot \cosh(\beta L) = -1$$

**Step 7 — Solve the transcendental equation for the first root.**

The smallest non-trivial root is:

$$\beta_1 L \approx 1.875$$

Therefore:

$$\beta_1 = \dfrac{1.875}{L}$$

**Step 8 — Solve for the resonant frequency.**

From $\beta^4 = \dfrac{\rho A \omega^2}{E I}$:

$$\omega_1 = \beta_1^2 \sqrt{\dfrac{E I}{\rho A}}$$

Substituting $I = \dfrac{w t^3}{12}$ and $A = w t$:

$$\omega_1 = \left(\dfrac{1.875}{L}\right)^2 \sqrt{\dfrac{E \cdot w t^3 / 12}{\rho \cdot w t}} = \dfrac{1.875^2}{L^2}\sqrt{\dfrac{E t^2}{12 \rho}}$$

$$\boxed{f_1 = \dfrac{1.875^2}{2 \pi L^2}\sqrt{\dfrac{E}{12 \rho}} \cdot t}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Resonant frequency scales with $t / L^2$. To make a resonator "tune" to a desired frequency, **L** is the dominant design variable; thickness **t** provides secondary tuning.

## 3.4 Derivation of Capacitance Change for a Differential Capacitive Sensor

**Statement:** A capacitive accelerometer consists of a movable central plate of mass **m** suspended by a spring **k**, between two fixed outer plates at gap $d_0$. Show that for small displacement **x**, the differential capacitance is linear in **x**.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Geometry at rest.**

At rest, $x = 0$. The two gaps are equal:

$$d_1 = d_2 = d_0$$

The two capacitances are equal:

$$C_1 = C_2 = C_0 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0}$$

**Step 2 — Geometry under acceleration.**

When the central mass displaces by **x**, one gap shrinks and the other grows:

$$d_1 = d_0 - x, \quad d_2 = d_0 + x$$

**Step 3 — Compute new capacitances.**

$$C_1 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0 - x}, \quad C_2 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0 + x}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the differential output.**

$$\Delta C = C_1 - C_2 = \varepsilon_0 A \left(\dfrac{1}{d_0 - x} - \dfrac{1}{d_0 + x}\right)$$

Combining over a common denominator:

$$\Delta C = \varepsilon_0 A \cdot \dfrac{(d_0 + x) - (d_0 - x)}{(d_0 - x)(d_0 + x)} = \varepsilon_0 A \cdot \dfrac{2x}{d_0^2 - x^2}$$

**Step 5 — Small-signal linearization.**

For $x \ll d_0$, $d_0^2 - x^2 \approx d_0^2$:

$$\boxed{\Delta C \approx \dfrac{2 \varepsilon_0 A}{d_0^2}\, x = \dfrac{2 C_0}{d_0}\, x}$$

This is a linear function of displacement, which is why **differential capacitive readout is preferred** over single-ended readout for accelerometer signal extraction.

## 3.5 Scaling-Law Worked Example (Numerical)

**Problem:** A macroscopic sensor of characteristic length **L = 10 cm** is scaled down by a factor of **α = 1/1000** (i.e., new L = 100 µm). If the original device experienced an inertial force of **F_macro = 1 N**, find (a) the new inertial force, (b) the new mass, (c) the new surface tension force, assuming original surface tension force = 10 N.

**Solution:**

**(a) New inertial force:** Inertial force scales as $F \propto L^2$ when velocity is preserved, and as $F \propto L^4$ when acceleration is preserved. The KTU 2024 convention is "velocity preserved" (typical for fluid/thermal problems), so:

$$F_{\text{micro}} = F_{\text{macro}} \cdot \alpha^2 = 1 \cdot (10^{-3})^2 = 1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ N}$$

**(b) New mass:**

$$m_{\text{micro}} = m_{\text{macro}} \cdot \alpha^3 = m_{\text{macro}} \cdot 10^{-9}$$

If $m_{\text{macro}} = 1 \text{ kg}$, then $m_{\text{micro}} = 10^{-9} \text{ kg} = 1 \text{ µg}$.

**(c) New surface tension force:** Scales linearly with L:

$$F_{st,\text{micro}} = F_{st,\text{macro}} \cdot \alpha = 10 \cdot 10^{-3} = 0.01 \text{ N}$$

**Comparison of ratios:**

$$\dfrac{F_{\text{inertial}}}{F_{\text{surface tension}}} \bigg\vert_{\text{macro}} = \dfrac{1}{10} = 0.1$$

$$\dfrac{F_{\text{inertial}}}{F_{\text{surface tension}}} \bigg\vert_{\text{micro}} = \dfrac{10^{-6}}{10^{-2}} = 10^{-4}$$

**Conclusion:** At the macroscale, inertial forces dominate surface forces by a factor of 10. At the microscale, surface forces dominate inertial forces by a factor of **10,000**. This is the qualitative essence of microsystem working principles.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Universal Microsystem Working-Principle Block Architecture

The block diagram below captures the **universal functional flow** of every microsystem — sensor, actuator, or complete microsystem-on-chip (SoC). It is the single most important schematic a KTU OEMET836 student should be able to draw from memory.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Physical Domain] --> B[Transduction Element]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning]
    C --> D[Output Electrical Signal]
    D --> E[Microcontroller or ASIC]
    E --> F[Communication and Display]
    A -- feedback loop --> G[Packaging and Environment]
    G -- mechanical thermal fluidic --> B

    classDef domain fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#9b2226,color:#000
    classDef transd fill:#bee1e6,stroke:#0a6e74,color:#000
    classDef elec fill:#fdf6b2,stroke:#9d8002,color:#000
    classDef ext fill:#cddafd,stroke:#1a3a8a,color:#000

    class A,G domain
    class B transd
    class C,D elec
    class E,F ext
```

### Block-by-Block Explanation

| Block | Function | Examples |
|-------|----------|----------|
| **Input Physical Domain** | The energy domain of the stimulus | Pressure, acceleration, temperature, light, chemical species |
| **Transduction Element** | The micro-scale sensing/actuating core | Piezoresistor, capacitor, piezoelectric crystal, comb drive |
| **Signal Conditioning** | Amplification, filtering, linearization | Op-amp, charge amplifier, demodulator |
| **Output Electrical Signal** | The conditioned electrical quantity | Voltage, current, frequency, digital word |
| **Microcontroller/ASIC** | Decision-making and control | ARM Cortex, custom CMOS ASIC |
| **Communication/Display** | External interface | SPI, I²C, UART, LCD, RF transmitter |
| **Packaging/Environment** | The mechanical, thermal, and chemical interface to the outside world | Hermetic seal, feedthroughs, parylene coating |

## 4.2 Working Principle Decision Tree — Mapping Stimulus to Transduction

The following decision-flow schematic is used by MEMS designers to *choose* the right transduction mechanism for a given stimulus. KTU frequently asks students to reproduce or interpret this tree.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[What is the input stimulus]
    start -- pressure force --> piezres{Piezoresistive available}
    piezres -- yes --> PZR[Use piezoresistive silicon strain gauge]
    piezres -- no --> CAP1[Use capacitive gap change]
    start -- acceleration --> CAP2[Use capacitive differential comb drive]
    start -- temperature --> THER[Use thermistor or thermopile Seebeck]
    start -- light intensity --> PHOTO[Use photodiode or phototransistor]
    start -- chemical concentration --> CHEM[Use ISFET or chemiresistor]
    start -- magnetic field --> MAG[Use Hall sensor or Lorentz force device]
    start -- mass loading on resonator --> RES[Use resonant frequency shift]
    start -- actuation required --> ACT[Electrostatic thermal or piezoelectric actuator]

    classDef ok fill:#d8f3dc,stroke:#1b4332,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#7a5c00,color:#000
    classDef endnode fill:#e0e1dd,stroke:#222,color:#000

    class PZR,CAP1,CAP2,THER,PHOTO,CHEM,MAG,RES,ACT ok
    class start,piezres decision
```

## 4.3 Force-Dominance Diagram at Different Length Scales

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Macro[L greater than 10 cm]
        A1[Inertial force dominates]:::macro
        A2[Gravity dominates]:::macro
        A3[Surface tension negligible]:::macro
    end
    subgraph Meso[1 mm to 10 cm]
        B1[Inertial still significant]:::meso
        B2[Elastic and viscous comparable]:::meso
    end
    subgraph Micro[1 micrometer to 1 mm]
        C1[Surface tension dominates]:::micro
        C2[Electrostatic dominates for actuation]:::micro
        C3[Viscous drag dominates for fluids]:::micro
        C4[Gravity is negligible]:::micro
    end
    subgraph Nano[L less than 1 micrometer]
        D1[Van der Waals and Casimir forces dominate]:::nano
        D2[Quantum effects appear]:::nano
    end

    classDef macro fill:#fae0e4,stroke:#7a1f3d,color:#000
    classDef meso fill:#fcd5ce,stroke:#a44a3f,color:#000
    classDef micro fill:#d8e2dc,stroke:#344e41,color:#000
    classDef nano fill:#eaddff,stroke:#3c096c,color:#000
```

This diagram is the **visual signature** of microsystem working principles — the inversion of dominant force regimes is what makes "working at the microscale" qualitatively, not just quantitatively, different.

## 4.4 Closed-Loop Control Architecture of an Actuated Microsystem

For active microsystems (e.g., micromirrors, microvalves, AFM probes), a closed-loop architecture is mandatory. Below is the standard block diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Ref[Reference Input] --> Sum[(Summing Junction)]
    Sum -- error signal --> Ctrl[Controller]
    Ctrl --> Driver[High Voltage Driver]
    Driver --> MEMS[MEMS Actuator]
    MEMS --> Plant[Mechanical Output Stage]
    Plant -- output --> PlantOut[Physical Output]
    Plant -- position feedback --> FB[Sensor or Capacitive Position Readout]
    FB --> Sum

    classDef ref fill:#e2f0cb,stroke:#386641,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#bde0fe,stroke:#1d3557,color:#000
    classDef act fill:#ffcdb2,stroke:#9d4e15,color:#000
    classDef fb fill:#ffc8dd,stroke:#9d2c7d,color:#000

    class Ref ref
    class Sum,Ctrl ctrl
    class Driver,MEMS,Plant,PlantOut act
    class FB fb
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Context:** While the exam focus of Module 1 is on *open-loop* working principles, examiners often include one sub-question on **closed-loop positioning** of micro-actuators to test understanding of feedback control at the microscale.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following questions are modeled on actual KTU OEMET836 End-Semester Examination (ESE) patterns: **Part A (3-mark short answers)** and **Part B (14-mark descriptive with internal choice)**.

---

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**List and briefly explain the six major energy domains in microsystems. Give one example of a microsystem that converts energy from each non-electrical domain to the electrical domain.**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

The six major energy domains are:

1. **Mechanical** — pressure, force, displacement, strain. Example: piezoresistive pressure sensor.
2. **Thermal** — temperature, heat flux. Example: thermopile using Seebeck effect.
3. **Magnetic** — magnetic field strength. Example: Hall sensor.
4. **Chemical** — concentration, pH. Example: ISFET pH sensor.
5. **Optical** — light intensity, wavelength. Example: photodiode.
6. **Electrical** — voltage, current, charge (the output domain in most cases).

*(1 mark for listing, 1 mark for any 3 examples, 1 mark for brief correctness.)*

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Define transduction. Differentiate between a sensor and an actuator using the concept of energy flow.**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

**Transduction** (1 mark) is the conversion of energy from one physical domain to another — typically from a non-electrical form to an electrical form (or vice versa).

**Difference between sensor and actuator** (2 marks):

- A **sensor** is a transducer in which energy flows **from a non-electrical domain INTO the electrical domain**. Example: accelerometer converting mechanical vibration into voltage.
- An **actuator** is a transducer in which energy flows **FROM the electrical domain INTO a non-electrical domain**. Example: electrostatic comb drive converting voltage into mechanical motion.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Modified)]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the working principle of **(i)** a piezoresistive pressure sensor, and **(ii)** a capacitive accelerometer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the pull-in voltage expression for a parallel-plate electrostatic actuator. Discuss the design implications for stable operation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a) — 7 marks

**(i) Piezoresistive pressure sensor (3.5 marks):**

A piezoresistive pressure sensor consists of four piezoresistors diffused onto a silicon diaphragm, typically arranged in a **Wheatstone bridge** configuration. When external pressure is applied, the diaphragm deflects. This deflection creates in-plane mechanical strain $\varepsilon$ in the diaphragm.

The piezoresistive effect (discovered by Smith, 1954) states that the resistivity of a semiconductor changes under mechanical stress:

$$\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = \pi_l \sigma_l + \pi_t \sigma_t$$

where $\pi_l$ and $\pi_t$ are the longitudinal and transverse piezoresistive coefficients, and $\sigma_l, \sigma_t$ are the longitudinal and transverse stresses.

Two resistors experience **tensile stress** (placed at the diaphragm edge along the $\langle 110 \rangle$ direction), and two experience **compressive stress** (placed perpendicular). This differential arrangement converts the pressure-induced strain into a differential voltage output of the Wheatstone bridge.

**Working principle summary:** Pressure → diaphragm deflection → strain → change in resistance → bridge output voltage.

**(ii) Capacitive accelerometer (3.5 marks):**

A capacitive accelerometer consists of a **proof mass** suspended by compliant springs. Underneath and above the proof mass are fixed "anchor" plates, forming a **differential capacitor**.

When the device accelerates, the proof mass experiences an inertial force $F = ma$ and displaces by $x = ma/k$, where **k** is the spring stiffness of the suspension.

This displacement changes the two gaps:

$$d_1 = d_0 - x, \quad d_2 = d_0 + x$$

The capacitances become:

$$C_1 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0 - x}, \quad C_2 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0 + x}$$

The differential capacitance $\Delta C = C_1 - C_2$ is linearly proportional to displacement **x** for small $x \ll d_0$:

$$\Delta C \approx \dfrac{2 \varepsilon_0 A}{d_0^2}\, x = \dfrac{2 \varepsilon_0 A}{d_0^2} \cdot \dfrac{m a}{k}$$

This differential capacitance is read out by a charge amplifier or switched-capacitor circuit to produce an output voltage proportional to acceleration.

**Working principle summary:** Acceleration → inertial force → proof-mass displacement → differential capacitance change → voltage proportional to acceleration.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b) — 7 marks

**Derivation of pull-in voltage (5 marks):**

Consider a parallel-plate capacitor with movable upper plate suspended on a spring of stiffness **k**, with initial gap $d_0$ and plate area **A**. When voltage **V** is applied, the electrostatic force attracts the plates, while the spring provides restoring force.

The electrostatic attractive force is:

$$F_e = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 (d_0 - x)^2}$$

At static equilibrium:

$$\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 (d_0 - x)^2} = k x \quad \cdots (1)$$

Pull-in occurs at the **instability point** where the derivative of the electrostatic force with respect to gap displacement equals the spring constant:

$$\dfrac{d F_e}{dx}\bigg\vert_{\text{at pull-in}} = k$$

Differentiating $F_e$ with respect to $x$:

$$\dfrac{d F_e}{dx} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{(d_0 - x)^3}$$

Setting this equal to $k$:

$$\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{(d_0 - x)^3} = k \quad \cdots (2)$$

Dividing (1) by (2):

$$\dfrac{(d_0 - x)}{2} = x \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = \dfrac{d_0}{3}$$

Substituting $x = d_0 / 3$ into (2):

$$V_{PI} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 k d_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 A}}$$

**Design implications (2 marks):**

1. The actuator must operate below **V_{PI}** to avoid snap-down. A safe operating voltage is **V ≤ 0.7 V_{PI}**.
2. Beyond pull-in, the plates collide and may weld together (**stiction failure**).
3. Increasing the gap $d_0$ increases $V_{PI}$ linearly with $d_0^{3/2}$, but reduces the available displacement — a fundamental trade-off.
4. For high-reliability applications, **capacitive position feedback** is used to operate the actuator in closed-loop mode, well below pull-in.

---

### Question B — Option 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Modified)]**

**(a)** Explain the scaling laws in microsystems. With a suitable example, show how surface forces dominate over volume forces as the device shrinks. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the fundamental resonant frequency of a cantilever beam used as a resonant mass sensor. Show how a mass loading on the cantilever changes its resonant frequency. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a) — 7 marks

**Explanation of scaling laws (3 marks):**

When the characteristic length of a device is scaled by a factor $\alpha$ (with $0 < \alpha < 1$ for miniaturization), all physical quantities change according to power-law rules. The most important are:

| Quantity | Scaling |
|----------|---------|
| Length | $\alpha$ |
| Area | $\alpha^2$ |
| Volume | $\alpha^3$ |
| Mass | $\alpha^3$ |
| Force (inertial) | $\alpha^2$ to $\alpha^4$ |
| Force (surface tension) | $\alpha$ |
| Force (viscous) | $\alpha$ |
| Force (gravitational) | $\alpha^3$ |

**Numerical demonstration of surface-force dominance (4 marks):**

Consider a water droplet of radius **r**.

- **Gravitational force** (volume force): $F_g = m g = \rho \cdot \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \cdot g \propto r^3$
- **Surface tension force** at the contact line: $F_{st} = 2 \pi r \gamma \propto r$

Their ratio:

$$\dfrac{F_g}{F_{st}} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3 g}{2 \pi r \gamma} = \dfrac{2 \rho g r^2}{3 \gamma}$$

For a droplet of $r = 1 \text{ mm} = 10^{-3} \text{ m}$:

$$\dfrac{F_g}{F_{st}} = \dfrac{2 \cdot 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot (10^{-3})^2}{3 \cdot 0.072} \approx \dfrac{0.0196}{0.216} \approx 0.09$$

So surface tension dominates by a factor of ~10.

For $r = 100 \text{ µm} = 10^{-4} \text{ m}$:

$$\dfrac{F_g}{F_{st}} = \dfrac{2 \cdot 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot (10^{-4})^2}{3 \cdot 0.072} \approx 0.0009$$

So surface tension now dominates by a factor of ~1100.

**Conclusion:** As $\alpha$ decreases, the ratio $F_g/F_{st}$ decreases as $\alpha^2$, confirming that surface (and other $\alpha$-linear forces) progressively dominate volume forces in microsystems.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b) — 7 marks

**Derivation of fundamental resonant frequency (4 marks):**

For a uniform cantilever beam of length **L**, width **w**, thickness **t**, density **ρ**, and Young's modulus **E**, the Euler-Bernoulli equation is:

$$E I \dfrac{\partial^4 u}{\partial x^4} + \rho A \dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = 0$$

with $I = \dfrac{w t^3}{12}$ and $A = w t$.

Applying clamped-free boundary conditions and solving the characteristic equation $\cos(\beta L)\cosh(\beta L) = -1$ gives the first non-trivial root:

$$\beta_1 L \approx 1.875$$

The fundamental resonant frequency is therefore:

$$f_1 = \dfrac{1.875^2}{2 \pi L^2}\sqrt{\dfrac{E t^2}{12 \rho}}$$

or equivalently:

$$f_1 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m_{\text{eff}}}}$$

with $k = \dfrac{E w t^3}{4 L^3}$ and $m_{\text{eff}} = 0.24 \rho w t L$ for a cantilever.

**Mass-loading effect (3 marks):**

When an added mass $\Delta m$ attaches to the cantilever tip, the effective mass increases:

$$m_{\text{eff}}' = m_{\text{eff}} + \Delta m$$

The new resonant frequency is:

$$f_1' = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m_{\text{eff}} + \Delta m}}$$

For small $\Delta m \ll m_{\text{eff}}$, the fractional frequency shift is:

$$\dfrac{\Delta f}{f_1} = \dfrac{f_1' - f_1}{f_1} \approx -\dfrac{\Delta m}{2 m_{\text{eff}}}$$

$$\Delta f \approx -\dfrac{f_1}{2}\dfrac{\Delta m}{m_{\text{eff}}}$$

The negative sign indicates a **decrease** in resonant frequency. By measuring $\Delta f$ precisely using an oscillator circuit, the attached mass $\Delta m$ can be determined — this is the working principle of **resonant mass sensors** used in chemical and biological detection.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 places students lose marks in Module 1 questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers — KTU examiners deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** per unannotated unit.
> 2. **Confusing input/output energy domains** in a sensor description — always state explicitly *"energy flows FROM ... TO electrical output"*.
> 3. **Omitting the small-signal approximation** in capacitance derivations — students write $\Delta C = \dfrac{2 \varepsilon_0 A x}{d_0^2 - x^2}$ and lose the linearization step.
> 4. **Drawing piezoresistive sensors without showing the Wheatstone bridge** — examiners expect all four resistors arranged in a bridge.
> 5. **Miscalculating pull-in position as $d_0/2$ instead of $d_0/3$** — this is a very common error in derivations.
> 6. **Confusing force scaling $\alpha^2$ with $\alpha^3$** — remember: inertial force scales as $\alpha^2$ when velocity is preserved, $\alpha^4$ when acceleration is preserved.
> 7. **Skipping boundary conditions** in beam derivations — always list the four BCs for a cantilever explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Working Principles of Microsystems"**

### 🔑 Conceptual Foundations
- Microsystems operate by **transduction** between six energy domains.
- **Scaling** is governed by power laws: length $\alpha$, area $\alpha^2$, volume $\alpha^3$.
- **Pressure is scale-invariant** — explains high stress in microstructures.
- **Surface-to-volume ratio $\propto 1/L$** — explains dominance of surface forces.

### 🔑 The Six Domains
- Mechanical, Electrical, Thermal, Magnetic, Chemical, Optical.

### 🔑 The Major Transduction Mechanisms
- Piezoresistive, Capacitive, Piezoelectric, Electrostatic, Thermoelectric, Resonant, Magnetic, Electrochemical, Optical.

### 🔑 The Five Must-Know Equations
- Capacitance: $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}$
- Electrostatic force: $F_e = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 d^2}$
- Pull-in voltage: $V_{PI} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 k d_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 A}}$
- Cantilever stiffness: $k = \dfrac{E w t^3}{4 L^3}$
- Resonant frequency: $f_1 = \dfrac{1.875^2}{2\pi L^2}\sqrt{\dfrac{E t^2}{12 \rho}}$

### 🔑 The Pull-In Position
- $x_{PI} = d_0 / 3$ (NOT $d_0/2$ — this is the most common student error).

### 🔑 The Force-Regime Hierarchy at Microscale
- Dominant: **Surface tension, Viscous, Electrostatic**
- Negligible: **Gravitational, Inertial** (compared to macro devices)

### 🔑 The Universal Microsystem Block Diagram
- **Input Domain → Transducer → Signal Conditioning → Electrical Output → MCU/ASIC → Communication**

### 🔑 Real-World Mapping Examples (for 2-line answers)
- Pressure sensor → Piezoresistive
- Accelerometer → Capacitive differential
- Gyroscope → Coriolis force on capacitive comb
- Microphone → Piezoelectric or capacitive
- Flow sensor → Thermal convective (hot wire)
- pH sensor → ISFET (electrochemical)

### 🔑 Exam Strategy
- Always annotate units in numericals.
- Always list boundary conditions in derivations.
- Always state the small-signal approximation explicitly.
- Always show the energy-domain pair in sensor/actuator descriptions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Engineering Science for Microsystems Design and Fabrication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET836 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Engineering Science for Microsystems Design and Fabrication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Engineering Science for Microsystems"?

**Engineering Science for Microsystems Design and Fabrication** is the interdisciplinary foundation block that unifies atomic-level physics, materials science, solid-state chemistry, and continuum mechanics to enable the deliberate engineering of micro-scale devices (typically **1 µm to 1 mm** in feature size).

In the KTU 2024 OEMET836 syllabus (Module 2), this topic provides the scientific bedrock — the **"why"** behind choosing silicon over steel, the **"how"** of dopant diffusion, and the **"what"** of crystal planes — that any MEMS designer must internalize before touching a single mask layout.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box**
> *Microsystems Engineering Science* = the systematic study of **atomic bonding**, **crystal geometry**, **defect mechanics**, and **transport phenomena** (diffusion) in solids, applied to the predictive design and reliable fabrication of MEMS/NEMS structures.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "LEGO Brick" Viewpoint

Imagine building a micro-system as if you were constructing a tiny city out of **LEGO bricks**:

| LEGO World | MEMS Engineering Science |
|------------|--------------------------|
| Brick types (2×4, 2×2, slopes) | Atoms: Si, Ge, O, C |
| Snap-fit geometry | Interatomic bonds (covalent, ionic) |
| Brick arrangement patterns | Crystal lattices (FCC, BCC, Diamond Cubic) |
| Broken or missing bricks | Defects (vacancies, dislocations) |
| Air seeping between bricks | Diffusion (Fick's laws) |
| Instruction sheet for builds | Miller Indices & slip planes |

At the micro-scale, you cannot just "pour" material and expect bulk behavior. Surface atoms behave differently from interior atoms. This **surface-to-volume ratio** explodes at small scales — a cube of **1 µm³** has ~**50%** of its atoms on the surface, completely dominating its mechanical, thermal, and electrical personality.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** MEMS design is fundamentally a fight against **surface forces** (van der Waals, surface tension, Casimir) that are negligible in the macroscopic world but dominant at the micro-scale.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants appear repeatedly in this module and MUST be memorized:

- **Avogadro's Number:** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$
- **Boltzmann Constant:** $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$
- **Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
- **Elementary Charge:** $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$
- **Vacuum Permittivity:** $\epsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$
- **Silicon Lattice Constant:** $a_{Si} = 0.543 \text{ nm}$
- **Silicon Atomic Density:** $5.0 \times 10^{22} \text{ atoms/cm}^3$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Atomic Foundation — Bonding in Microsystems Materials

All matter in microsystems is held together by **primary (strong)** and **secondary (weak)** bonds. KTU examiners love to test the energy-scale hierarchy.

### 2.1.1 Primary Bonds (Energy: 1–10 eV)

1. **Ionic Bonding**
   - Electron transfer from metal → non-metal
   - Example: **NaCl**, **SiO₂** (partially ionic)
   - High melting point, brittle, electrically insulating
   - Coulombic attraction: $F = \dfrac{k \cdot q^+ \cdot q^-}{r^2}$

2. **Covalent Bonding**
   - Shared electron pairs between atoms
   - Example: **Si—Si**, **Si—Ge**, **C—C** in diamond
   - **Directional** bonds → controls crystal geometry
   - Silicon forms **4 covalent bonds** in a tetrahedral (109.5°) arrangement

3. **Metallic Bonding**
   - Delocalized "sea" of electrons
   - Example: **Al, Cu, Au** (used as MEMS conductors)
   - Non-directional, ductile, high electrical conductivity

### 2.1.2 Secondary Bonds (Energy: 0.01–0.1 eV)

- **Van der Waals** (London dispersion forces): critical in stiction during MEMS release
- **Hydrogen bonding**: relevant in polymer MEMS (SU-8, PMMA)
- These cause **stiction** — the #1 failure mode of released MEMS structures

## 2.2 Crystal Geometry — The Heart of Single-Crystal Silicon MEMS

Silicon crystallizes in the **Diamond Cubic (DC)** structure, which can be viewed as **two interpenetrating Face-Centered Cubic (FCC) lattices** offset by (a/4, a/4, a/4).

### 2.2.1 The Three Key Cubic Structures

| Property | Simple Cubic (SC) | BCC | FCC | Diamond Cubic |
|----------|-------------------|-----|-----|---------------|
| Atoms per unit cell | 1 | 2 | 4 | 8 |
| Coordination number | 6 | 8 | 12 | 4 |
| Atomic packing factor (APF) | 0.52 | 0.68 | **0.74** | 0.34 |
| Example material | Polonium | α-Fe, W | Cu, Al, Au | **Si, Ge, Diamond** |

> [!NOTE]
> **APF Insight:** Despite having the highest packing in pure metals, silicon's diamond structure is **only 34% packed** — meaning 66% is "empty space." This emptiness is precisely what enables **dopant diffusion** during fabrication.

### 2.2.2 Miller Indices — Speaking the Language of Planes

Miller Indices (hkl) are **reciprocals of intercepts** made by a plane on the crystal axes, reduced to smallest integers.

**Step-by-step recipe:**
1. Find plane intercepts on x, y, z axes
2. Take reciprocals
3. Clear fractions
4. Enclose in parentheses (hkl)

For negative intercepts, use an **overbar**: e.g., $(\bar{1}10)$.

**KTU Critical Families for Silicon MEMS:**

| Family | Notation | Atomic Density | MEMS Significance |
|--------|----------|----------------|-------------------|
| {100} | (100), (010), etc. | $2/a^2$ | Standard wafer, anisotropic KOH etching |
| {110} | (110), (101), etc. | $2.83/a^2$ | Cleavage plane, fastest etch stop |
| {111} | (111), etc. | $4/a^2\sqrt{3}$ | Slowest etch plane, used for V-grooves |

## 2.3 Imperfections in Solids — Where Real MEMS Live

**Perfect crystals do not exist.** Defects are not "flaws" — they are **engineered features** in MEMS.

### 2.3.1 The 4D Defect Taxonomy

- **0-D Point Defects**
  - **Vacancy** (missing atom) — dominant at high T
  - **Interstitial** (extra atom in gap)
  - **Substitutional impurity** (dopant on lattice site) — basis of all semiconductor devices
  - **Frenkel pair** (vacancy + interstitial)
  - **Schottky defect** (cation + anion vacancy in ionic crystals)

- **1-D Line Defects — Dislocations**
  - **Edge dislocation**: extra half-plane of atoms
  - **Screw dislocation**: helical ramp of atoms
  - Burgers vector **b** characterizes them
  - Cause **yielding** and **work hardening** in MEMS thin films

- **2-D Planar Defects**
  - Grain boundaries (polycrystalline metals like Al)
  - Twin boundaries
  - Stacking faults
  - Surfaces (themselves are defects!)

- **3-D Volume Defects**
  - Precipitates, voids, inclusions
  - Critical for fatigue failure in MEMS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Topic:** Dislocation density $\rho$ directly affects yield strength via the **Hall-Petch equation**, tested frequently.

## 2.4 Diffusion — The Transport Engine of Fabrication

**Fick's First Law** (steady-state diffusion):

$$J = -D \dfrac{dC}{dx}$$

where:
- $J$ = flux (atoms/cm²·s)
- $D$ = diffusion coefficient (cm²/s)
- $\dfrac{dC}{dx}$ = concentration gradient

**Fick's Second Law** (non-steady-state):

$$\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

**Arrhenius Temperature Dependence of D:**

$$D = D_0 \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)$$

where $E_a$ is the **activation energy** and $D_0$ is the pre-exponential factor.

**Common Solutions to Fick's Second Law:**

1. **Constant Surface Concentration:** $C(x,t) = C_s \operatorname{erfc}\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$
2. **Constant Total Dose (Gaussian):** $C(x,t) = \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{\pi D t}} \exp\left(-\dfrac{x^2}{4Dt}\right)$

> [!NOTE]
> **Diffusion Length:** $L_D = \sqrt{Dt}$ — the characteristic distance a dopant travels in time $t$.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|----------|---------------|
| 1 | Coulombic Bond Force | $F = k q^+ q^- / r^2$ | N |
| 2 | APF (FCC) | $0.74$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | APF (BCC) | $0.68$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Atomic Density (FCC) | $4/a^3$ | atoms/cm³ |
| 5 | Atomic Density (BCC) | $2/a^3$ | atoms/cm³ |
| 6 | d-spacing (cubic) | $d_{hkl} = a/\sqrt{h^2+k^2+l^2}$ | nm |
| 7 | Fick's First Law | $J = -D(dC/dx)$ | atoms/cm²·s |
| 8 | Fick's Second Law | $\partial C/\partial t = D(\partial^2 C/\partial x^2)$ | — |
| 9 | Diffusion Coefficient | $D = D_0 \exp(-E_a/k_B T)$ | cm²/s |
| 10 | Diffusion Length | $L_D = \sqrt{Dt}$ | µm |
| 11 | Yield Strength (Hall-Petch) | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y/\sqrt{d}$ | MPa |
| 12 | Young's Modulus (Anisotropic Si) | varies: 130–188 GPa | GPa |
| 13 | Thermal Expansion (Si) | $2.6 \times 10^{-6} /K$ | /K |
| 14 | Debye Length | $L_D = \sqrt{\epsilon k_B T / (q^2 N)}$ | m |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Crystal Anisotropy of Silicon** → enables precise V-groove etching for pressure sensor diaphragms and microfluidic channels
- **Defect Engineering** → dislocation-free Float Zone (FZ) silicon wafers are used for high-reliability MEMS inertial sensors
- **Diffusion Engineering** → boron diffusion through SiO₂ masks creates the piezoresistive gauges in pressure sensors
- **Surface Science** → explains why released comb-drives sometimes stick permanently (stiction)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Atomic Packing Factor (APF) for FCC

The APF is the **fraction of volume in a unit cell actually occupied by atoms**. We derive it for FCC because it is the densest sphere packing and a frequent KTU question.

### Step 1 — Count Effective Atoms per FCC Unit Cell

In an FCC cell:
- 8 corner atoms, each shared by 8 cells → contribution $= 8 \times \dfrac{1}{8} = 1$
- 6 face-centered atoms, each shared by 2 cells → contribution $= 6 \times \dfrac{1}{2} = 3$
- Total effective atoms $N = 1 + 3 = 4$ atoms per unit cell

### Step 2 — Volume of Atoms

Each atom is approximated as a hard sphere of radius $R$. The volume of one atom:

$$V_{atom} = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi R^3$$

Total atomic volume in cell:

$$V_{atoms} = 4 \times \dfrac{4}{3}\pi R^3 = \dfrac{16}{3}\pi R^3$$

### Step 3 — Relate R to Lattice Constant a (for FCC)

In FCC, atoms touch along the **face diagonal**:

$$\text{Face diagonal} = a\sqrt{2} = 4R$$

Therefore:

$$R = \dfrac{a\sqrt{2}}{4}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute and Simplify

$$V_{atoms} = \dfrac{16}{3}\pi \left(\dfrac{a\sqrt{2}}{4}\right)^3$$

$$V_{atoms} = \dfrac{16}{3}\pi \cdot \dfrac{a^3 \cdot 2\sqrt{2}}{64}$$

$$V_{atoms} = \dfrac{16 \pi a^3 \cdot 2\sqrt{2}}{192} = \dfrac{\pi a^3 \sqrt{2}}{6}$$

### Step 5 — Compute APF

$$\text{APF} = \dfrac{V_{atoms}}{V_{cell}} = \dfrac{\pi a^3 \sqrt{2}/6}{a^3} = \dfrac{\pi \sqrt{2}}{6}$$

Numerically:

$$\text{APF} = \dfrac{3.1416 \times 1.4142}{6} = \dfrac{4.4429}{6} \approx 0.7405$$

This confirms the famous result that FCC achieves ~**74.05% packing efficiency**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Logic Recap:** We converted atom count → atomic volume → substituted geometric relation between R and a → cancelled with cell volume. This is the gold-standard KTU derivation pattern.

## 3.2 Derivation: Diffused Dopant Profile from Fick's Second Law

Given an **infinite source** at the surface ($C_s$ constant), with the wafer initially undoped ($C(x,0) = 0$ for $x > 0$), derive the concentration profile.

### Step 1 — Set Up Boundary Conditions

$$\begin{aligned}
C(x, 0) &= 0 \quad \text{for } x > 0 \\
C(0, t) &= C_s \quad \text{for } t > 0 \\
C(\infty, t) &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Error Function Solution

The solution to Fick's Second Law under these boundary conditions is:

$$C(x,t) = C_s \left[1 - \operatorname{erf}\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)\right] = C_s \operatorname{erfc}\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Junction Depth Derivation

For a p-n junction formed by diffusing boron into n-type silicon of background concentration $C_B$, the junction occurs where:

$$C(x_j, t) = C_B$$

Therefore:

$$C_B = C_s \operatorname{erfc}\left(\dfrac{x_j}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Solve for Junction Depth

$$\operatorname{erfc}\left(\dfrac{x_j}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right) = \dfrac{C_B}{C_s}$$

Taking the inverse complementary error function:

$$\dfrac{x_j}{2\sqrt{Dt}} = \operatorname{erfc}^{-1}\left(\dfrac{C_B}{C_s}\right)$$

Thus the **junction depth** is:

$$x_j = 2\sqrt{Dt} \cdot \operatorname{erfc}^{-1}\left(\dfrac{C_B}{C_s}\right)$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Example (Typical KTU Problem)

**Given:** Boron diffusion into Si, $C_s = 10^{20} \text{ cm}^{-3}$, $C_B = 10^{15} \text{ cm}^{-3}$, $D = 10^{-14} \text{ cm}^2/\text{s}$, $t = 3600 \text{ s}$.

**Step 5a:** Compute the ratio:

$$\dfrac{C_B}{C_s} = \dfrac{10^{15}}{10^{20}} = 10^{-5}$$

**Step 5b:** Lookup inverse erfc value (standard tables):

$$\operatorname{erfc}^{-1}(10^{-5}) \approx 3.123$$

**Step 5c:** Compute diffusion length:

$$\sqrt{Dt} = \sqrt{10^{-14} \times 3600} = \sqrt{3.6 \times 10^{-11}} \approx 6.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ cm}$$

**Step 5d:** Compute junction depth:

$$x_j = 2 \times 6.0 \times 10^{-6} \times 3.123 \approx 3.75 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm} = 0.375 \text{ µm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Doubling the diffusion time increases $x_j$ by only $\sqrt{2}$ (~41%), not 2×. Diffusion is intrinsically **sub-linear in time** — a frequent board exam "trick question."

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Property Comparison for MEMS Materials

| Property | Single-Crystal Si | Poly-Si | SiO₂ | Si₃N₄ | Al | SU-8 | Ti |
|----------|------------------|---------|------|--------|----|------|------|
| Young's Modulus (GPa) | 130–188 | 160 | 70 | 290 | 70 | 4 | 110 |
| Density (g/cm³) | 2.33 | 2.33 | 2.27 | 3.17 | 2.70 | 1.20 | 4.51 |
| CTE (×10⁻⁶/K) | 2.6 | 2.6 | 0.5 | 3.3 | 23 | 52 | 8.6 |
| Resistivity (Ω·cm) | 10⁻⁴ to 10³ | 10⁻³ to 10³ | >10¹⁴ | >10¹⁴ | 2.7 µΩ·cm | >10¹⁶ | 42 µΩ·cm |
| Etch in HF | No | No | **Yes** | No | No | No | No |
| KOH Etch Rate | Anisotropic | Similar | Very High | Very Low | No | No | No |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Al** (used as conductor in CMOS-MEMS) with **Ti** (used as adhesion layer). Al etches in many alkaline solutions; Ti is highly resistant.
