**Step 2 — Write the stored electrical energy (constant voltage source).**

$$W(x) = \frac{1}{2} C(x) V^2 = \frac{1}{2} \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{g_0 - x}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the principle of virtual work to find the force.**
At constant voltage, the system does mechanical work by *increasing* capacitance, so we use the co-energy principle:

$$F_e = +\left.\frac{\partial W}{\partial x}\right|_{V=\text{const}}$$

**Step 4 — Perform the differentiation.**

$$\frac{\partial W}{\partial x} = \frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 A V^2 \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left[(g_0 - x)^{-1}\right]$$

$$\frac{\partial W}{\partial x} = \frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 A V^2 \cdot (+1)(g_0 - x)^{-2}$$

$$\frac{\partial W}{\partial x} = \frac{1}{2} \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{(g_0 - x)^2}$$

**Step 5 — Conclude the force expression.**

$$\boxed{\,F_e = \frac{1}{2}\frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{g^2} = \frac{1}{2}\frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{(g_0 - x)^2}\,}$$

The positive sign indicates that the force acts in the direction of increasing $x$ (i.e., attractive — pulls the movable plate toward the fixed one).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pull-In Voltage of a Parallel-Plate Actuator

**Step 1 — Write mechanical equilibrium.**
The net force on the movable plate is zero at static equilibrium:

$$F_s(x) - F_e(x) = 0$$

$$kx = \frac{1}{2}\frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{(g_0 - x)^2}$$

**Step 2 — Express $V$ as a function of $x$.**

$$V^2 = \frac{2 k x (g_0 - x)^2}{\varepsilon_0 A}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate $V^2$ with respect to $x$ to find the critical point.**
Pull-in occurs at the inflection point where $\partial V^2 / \partial x = 0$ (the equilibrium becomes unstable).

$$\frac{d(V^2)}{dx} = \frac{2k}{\varepsilon_0 A} \cdot \frac{d}{dx}\left[x(g_0 - x)^2\right]$$

**Step 4 — Apply the product rule.**

$$\frac{d}{dx}\left[x(g_0 - x)^2\right] = (g_0 - x)^2 + x \cdot 2(g_0 - x)(-1)$$

$$= (g_0 - x)^2 - 2x(g_0 - x)$$

$$= (g_0 - x)\left[(g_0 - x) - 2x\right]$$

$$= (g_0 - x)(g_0 - 3x)$$

**Step 5 — Set the derivative to zero.**
The non-trivial root (excluding $x = g_0$) is:

$$g_0 - 3x = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \boxed{x_{PI} = \frac{g_0}{3}}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $x_{PI} = g_0/3$ back into the equilibrium equation.**

$$V_{PI}^{\,2} = \frac{2k \cdot \dfrac{g_0}{3} \cdot \left(g_0 - \dfrac{g_0}{3}\right)^2}{\varepsilon_0 A}$$

$$V_{PI}^{\,2} = \frac{2k \cdot \dfrac{g_0}{3} \cdot \left(\dfrac{2g_0}{3}\right)^2}{\varepsilon_0 A}$$

$$V_{PI}^{\,2} = \frac{2k \cdot \dfrac{g_0}{3} \cdot \dfrac{4g_0^{\,2}}{9}}{\varepsilon_0 A}$$

$$V_{PI}^{\,2} = \frac{8 k g_0^{\,3}}{27 \varepsilon_0 A}$$

$$\boxed{\,V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k g_0^{\,3}}{27 \varepsilon_0 A}}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Pull-In Theorem:** For *any* parallel-plate electrostatic actuator with linear mechanical spring, snap-down always occurs at **one-third of the initial gap**, independent of area, voltage, or spring constant.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Lateral Force in an Interdigitated Comb Drive

**Given:** Comb drive with $n$ finger pairs, finger overlap $h$, finger gap $g$, lateral displacement $x$, initial overlap $x_0$.

**Step 1 — Capacitance of the comb structure.**
Each finger pair contributes two parallel-plate-like capacitors (two sidewalls). Total capacitance:

$$C(x) = \frac{2 n \varepsilon_0 h (x + x_0)}{g}$$

**Step 2 — Stored energy.**

$$W(x) = \frac{1}{2} C(x) V^2 = \frac{n \varepsilon_0 h (x + x_0) V^2}{g}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to $x$.**

$$F_e = \frac{\partial W}{\partial x} = \frac{n \varepsilon_0 h V^2}{g}$$

**Step 4 — Final boxed expression.**

$$\boxed{\,F_e = \frac{n \varepsilon_0 h V^2}{g}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight — Why comb drives are preferred:**
> The lateral force is **independent of displacement $x$**, meaning the actuator behaves like an *ideal constant-force source* over its entire travel range. This is in stark contrast to the parallel plate, whose force grows unboundedly as $g \to 0$, causing pull-in instability.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Parallel-Plate Electrostatic Actuator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Voltage Source V] -->|Step 1: Apply Bias| B[Fixed Electrode Top Plate]
    B -->|Step 2: E field across gap| C[Air / Dielectric Gap g of x]
    C -->|Step 3: Induced Charges| D[Movable Electrode Bottom Plate]
    D -->|Step 4: Capacitance C of x| E[Capacitive Energy W = 0.5 C V squared]
    E -->|Step 5: Gradient of W| F[Electrostatic Force F sub e]
    F -->|Step 6: Mechanical Work| G[Displacement x of Mass M]
    G -->|Step 7: Elastic Reaction| H[Suspension Spring of stiffness k]
    H -->|Step 8: Restoring Force F sub s| I[Mechanical Equilibrium Node]
    F --> I
    I -->|Step 9: Compare to V sub PI| J[Pull in Instability Check]
    J -->|Below V sub PI| K[Stable Operation]
    J -->|Above V sub PI| L[Snap Down Failure]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Comb Drive Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Fixed_Frame[Fixed Comb Frame]
        F1[Stator Fingers of count n]
        F2[Anchor Pads]
    end
    subgraph Movable_Shuttle[Movable Comb Shuttle]
        M1[Rotor Fingers of count n]
        M2[Central Shuttle Mass]
        M3[Suspension Beams of stiffness k]
    end
    subgraph Field_Region[Electric Field Region]
        E1[Lateral Gap g between fingers]
        E2[Overlap Length h plus x plus x0]
    end
    F1 --- E1
    M1 --- E1
    F1 --- E2
    M1 --- E2
    F2 -->|Apply Voltage V| E1
    M2 -->|Generate Lateral Force| E2
    M3 -->|Provide Restoring Force| M2
```

### 4.3 Operational Flow — Pull-In Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start V equals 0] --> P2[Increase V gradually]
    P2 --> P3{Compare V to V sub PI}
    P3 -->|V is less than V sub PI| P4[Plate deflects to x less than g0 by 3]
    P4 --> P5[Stable equilibrium]
    P5 --> P2
    P3 -->|V equals V sub PI| P6[Plate reaches x equals g0 by 3]
    P6 --> P7[Critical point unstable]
    P7 --> P8[Snap down to contact]
    P8 --> P9[Pull in occurs]
    P3 -->|V greater than V sub PI| P8
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Legend Convention:** Every labelled block above represents a physical or mathematical entity in the MEMS actuator. Connections annotated with "Step n" follow the seven-stage logical chain from Section 2.1.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State and explain Coulomb's Law in the context of MEMS electrostatic actuation. Mention the role of permittivity.**

**Model Answer:**
Coulomb's Law states that the electrostatic force between two point charges $q_1$ and $q_2$ separated by distance $r$ is:

$$F = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r} \cdot \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}$$

In MEMS, electrodes are extended surfaces, not point charges. By integrating Coulomb's law over the electrode areas and accounting for the dielectric medium of permittivity $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$ filling the gap, we derive the parallel-plate force:

$$F_e = \frac{1}{2} \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{g^2}$$

The permittivity directly scales the force — higher $\varepsilon_r$ (e.g., by filling the gap with SiO₂) increases the actuation force but introduces dielectric charging and stiction issues. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is pull-in instability in a MEMS parallel-plate actuator? Write the expression for pull-in voltage.**

**Model Answer:**
Pull-in instability is a phenomenon in which, above a critical applied voltage, the electrostatic force grows faster than the linear mechanical restoring force, causing the movable electrode to accelerate unstably and snap onto the fixed electrode.

**Expression for Pull-In Voltage:**

$$V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k g_0^{\,3}}{27 \varepsilon_0 A}}$$

The corresponding critical displacement is $x_{PI} = g_0 / 3$. Beyond this point, no static equilibrium exists and the system collapses. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Energy vs Co-energy signs:** Force at constant $V$ uses $+ \partial W / \partial x$ (or $\partial W^\prime / \partial x$); at constant $Q$ it is $- \partial W / \partial x$. Mixing these yields wrong sign and zero marks.
> 2. **Forgetting $\tfrac{1}{2}$ factor:** Many students write $F = \varepsilon_0 A V^2 / g^2$ and lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Pull-in derivation missing $\partial V^2/\partial x = 0$ justification:** Examiners expect the instability argument via the inflection of the $V(x)$ curve.
> 4. **Units of $\varepsilon_0$:** Should be written explicitly as F/m in derivations.

---

#### QUESTION A — 14 Marks [Internal Choice Option A]

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the electrostatic force on a parallel-plate capacitor using the energy method. Clearly state the assumption of constant voltage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A MEMS parallel-plate actuator has plate area $A = 200 \times 200\ \mu\text{m}^2$, air gap $g_0 = 2\ \mu\text{m}$, and suspension stiffness $k = 1\ \text{N/m}$. Calculate the pull-in voltage and the corresponding pull-in displacement. Take $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F/m}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:** Refer to **Derivation 1 in Section 3.1** above. Final answer:

$$F_e = \frac{1}{2}\frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{(g_0 - x)^2}$$

The derivation carries 7 marks distributed as:
- 1 mark: capacitance expression $C(x) = \varepsilon_0 A / (g_0 - x)$
- 1 mark: energy $W = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$
- 2 marks: differentiation step
- 1 mark: physical interpretation of sign (attractive)
- 2 marks: stating the constant-voltage assumption

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert all dimensions to SI units.**

$$A = 200 \times 200 \times 10^{-12} = 4 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{m}^2$$

$$g_0 = 2 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$$

$$k = 1\ \text{N/m}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the pull-in voltage formula.**

$$V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k g_0^{\,3}}{27 \varepsilon_0 A}}$$

$$V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 \times 1 \times (2 \times 10^{-6})^3}{27 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 4 \times 10^{-8}}}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate numerator.**

$$8 \times 1 \times 8 \times 10^{-18} = 64 \times 10^{-18}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate denominator.**

$$27 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 4 \times 10^{-8} = 27 \times 35.416 \times 10^{-20}$$

$$= 956.23 \times 10^{-20} = 9.5623 \times 10^{-18}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the ratio.**

$$V_{PI}^{\,2} = \frac{64 \times 10^{-18}}{9.5623 \times 10^{-18}} = 6.693\ \text{V}^2$$

$$V_{PI} = \sqrt{6.693} = 2.587\ \text{V}$$

**Step 6 — Compute pull-in displacement.**

$$x_{PI} = \frac{g_0}{3} = \frac{2\ \mu\text{m}}{3} = 0.667\ \mu\text{m}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{V_{PI} \approx 2.59\ \text{V}, \quad x_{PI} \approx 0.667\ \mu\text{m}}$$

---

#### QUESTION B — 14 Marks [Internal Choice Option B]

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working of an electrostatic comb-drive actuator. Derive an expression for the lateral electrostatic force produced. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A comb-drive actuator has $n = 50$ finger pairs, finger height $h = 40\ \mu\text{m}$, gap $g = 2\ \mu\text{m}$, and is actuated by $V = 20\ \text{V}$. Compute the lateral force and the change in capacitance when the shuttle moves by $x = 5\ \mu\text{m}$ from the initial overlap $x_0 = 10\ \mu\text{m}$. Take $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F/m}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working and Derivation:**
A comb-drive actuator consists of two sets of interdigitated silicon fingers — one set anchored to the substrate (stator) and the other suspended on a movable shuttle (rotor). When a voltage is applied between the stator and rotor, fringing fields are negligible compared to the parallel sidewall fields, so each finger pair behaves as two parallel-plate capacitors. As the shuttle moves laterally, the overlap length changes, modulating the capacitance and producing a lateral force. Derivation is given in **Derivation 3, Section 3.3**:

$$\boxed{F_e = \frac{n \varepsilon_0 h V^2}{g}}$$

Key features:
- Force is **independent of $x$** (constant-force actuator)
- No pull-in instability along the lateral direction (within travel limits)
- Used in accelerometers, gyroscopes, and micro-mirrors

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.**

$$n = 50,\quad h = 40\ \mu\text{m} = 40 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$$

$$g = 2\ \mu\text{m} = 2 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$$

$$V = 20\ \text{V},\quad x_0 = 10\ \mu\text{m},\quad x = 5\ \mu\text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the lateral force.**

$$F_e = \frac{n \varepsilon_0 h V^2}{g}$$

$$F_e = \frac{50 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 40 \times 10^{-6} \times (20)^2}{2 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$F_e = \frac{50 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 40 \times 10^{-6} \times 400}{2 \times 10^{-6}}$$

**Step 3 — Numerator evaluation.**

$$50 \times 8.854 \times 40 \times 400 = 50 \times 141664 = 7.0832 \times 10^{6}$$

Powers: $10^{-12} \times 10^{-6} = 10^{-18}$

$$\text{Numerator} = 7.0832 \times 10^{6} \times 10^{-18} = 7.0832 \times 10^{-12}$$

**Step 4 — Divide by denominator.**

$$F_e = \frac{7.0832 \times 10^{-12}}{2 \times 10^{-6}} = 3.5416 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{N}$$

$$F_e \approx 3.54\ \mu\text{N}$$

**Step 5 — Compute capacitance change.**

Initial capacitance:

$$C_0 = \frac{2 n \varepsilon_0 h x_0}{g} = \frac{2 \times 50 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 40 \times 10^{-6} \times 10 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$C_0 = \frac{100 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 400 \times 10^{-12}}{2 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$C_0 = \frac{3.5416 \times 10^{-19}}{2 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.7708 \times 10^{-13}\ \text{F} = 177.08\ \text{fF}$$

Final capacitance:

$$C_f = \frac{2 n \varepsilon_0 h (x_0 + x)}{g} = C_0 \cdot \frac{x_0 + x}{x_0} = 177.08 \times \frac{15}{10}$$

$$C_f = 265.62\ \text{fF}$$

**Step 6 — Change in capacitance.**

$$\Delta C = C_f - C_0 = 265.62 - 177.08 = 88.54\ \text{fF}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{F_e \approx 3.54\ \mu\text{N}, \quad \Delta C \approx 88.54\ \text{fF}}$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Electrostatic Forces (Module 2)**

- **Coulomb's Law foundation:** $F = k_e q_1 q_2 / r^2$; extended surfaces give integrated force.
- **Energy Method (Constant V):** $F = +\partial W / \partial x = +\tfrac{1}{2} V^2 \partial C / \partial x$
- **Co-energy Method (Constant Q):** $F = +\partial W^\prime / \partial x = +\tfrac{1}{2} Q^2 \partial (1/C) / \partial x$
- **Parallel-plate force:** $F_e = \tfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 A V^2 / g^2$ — always **attractive**, scales as $1/g^2$.
- **Comb-drive force:** $F_e = n \varepsilon_0 h V^2 / g$ — **lateral**, **displacement-independent** constant force.
- **Effective spring softening:** $k_{eff} = k - \varepsilon_0 A V^2 / g^3$ — system becomes unstable when $k_{eff} < 0$.
- **Pull-in displacement:** $x_{PI} = g_0 / 3$ — universal for linear spring parallel plates.
- **Pull-in voltage:** $V_{PI} = \sqrt{8 k g_0^3 / (27 \varepsilon_0 A)}$ — design limit for stable operation.
- **Physical constants:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $\varepsilon_{air} \approx \varepsilon_0$.
- **Engineering advantage:** Zero steady-state current, CMOS compatible, fast response (µs range).
- **Engineering drawback:** High voltages, pull-in instability, sensitivity to dielectric charging.
- **Applications:** DMD projectors, RF switches, accelerometers, gyroscopes, micromirrors, micropumps.
- **Design rule of thumb:** Operate at $V < 0.7 V_{PI}$ to maintain ~30% safety margin from snap-down.
- **Exam traps:** Sign errors in force direction, missing $\tfrac{1}{2}$, wrong $\varepsilon$ units, forgetting to convert µm² → m².
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electromagnetic Forces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET836 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electromagnetic Forces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — ELECTROMAGNETIC FORCES IN MEMS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the context of **Micro Electro Mechanical Systems (MEMS)**, *Electromagnetic Forces* refer to mechanical forces generated by the interaction of **electric currents** (or time-varying electric fields) with **magnetic fields**, and the converse, governed by the **Lorentz force law**. In micro-scale devices, these forces are harnessed to produce controlled mechanical motion in actuators, sensors, and energy harvesters. Unlike electrostatic actuation, electromagnetic actuation scales favorably with miniaturization for certain geometries because the magnetic energy density does not collapse with shrinking dimensions in the same abrupt manner as electric-field energy density.

Mathematically, the foundational expression is the **Lorentz force density** experienced by a charge distribution in motion through a magnetic field, which, when integrated over a current-carrying conductor, yields the **Laplace force** on the conductor.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of it like this:** Picture a tiny electric train running inside your chip. Instead of a giant locomotive motor, the "engine" is built from microscopic coils carrying current through a magnetic field (often produced by a permanent magnet or another micro-coil). Just as two magnets either snap together or push apart based on their poles, a current-carrying micro-wire experiences a push when placed inside a magnetic field. By carefully designing the coil, magnet, and air-gap geometry, MEMS engineers use this push to bend beams, rotate mirrors, or vibrate membranes.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics govern every electromagnetic MEMS calculation:

- **Vacuum permeability:** $\mu_{0} = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \ \text{H/m}$ — fundamental constant that sets the strength of magnetic coupling in vacuum and air.
- **Relative permeability:** $\mu_{r}$ — material property (dimensionless) indicating how strongly a material conducts magnetic flux relative to vacuum.
- **Saturation flux density ($B_{sat}$):** typically **1.0–2.5 T** for soft magnetic materials such as permalloy, and **0.3–1.4 T** for rare-earth magnets (NdFeB, SmCo).
- **Co-energy density:** measured in **J/m³**, indicates the mechanical work extractable per unit volume from the magnetic field.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Electromagnetic actuation in MEMS contrasts with electrostatic actuation. Electrostatic force scales as $\sim A \varepsilon_{0} V^{2}/d^{2}$ (favoring small gaps but limited by voltage), while magnetic force can remain substantial even as the gap grows, especially when **integrated coils** and **high-energy-density permanent magnets** are employed.

### 1.4 Why Electromagnetic Forces Matter in MEMS

- **Larger displacements** than electrostatic actuators (which are typically limited to < 1/3 of the gap).
- **Lower drive voltages** (currents are used instead of high voltages), which is advantageous for **CMOS compatibility** when currents are modest.
- **Higher force densities** at the macroscale and mesoscale, though challenged at the deep microscale due to fabrication of coils and magnets.
- Applications include **optical MEMS (scanning mirrors)**, **RF switches**, **microrelays**, **magnetic storage read/write heads**, and **microgenerators**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Step-by-Step Logic

### Step 1 — The Lorentz Force on a Moving Charge
A point charge $q$ moving with velocity $\vec{v}$ through a magnetic flux density $\vec{B}$ and an electric field $\vec{E}$ experiences a force:

- The magnetic component is perpendicular to both velocity and field.
- The electric component is independent of motion.
- At MEMS current densities, only the magnetic component is significant for force generation.

### Step 2 — From Charges to Currents — The Laplace Force
A current element $I \, d\vec{l}$ in a magnetic field $\vec{B}$ experiences an infinitesimal force $d\vec{F}$. This is obtained by summing the Lorentz force over the mobile charges within the conductor segment. The result, integrated over the length of the conductor, gives the **total electromagnetic force** on a current-carrying wire.

### Step 3 — Direction and Right-Hand Rule
The force vector is perpendicular to the plane formed by the current direction and the magnetic field. Engineers use **Fleming's Left-Hand Rule** (for motors) to determine the force direction quickly: forefinger (Field), middle finger (Current), and thumb (Force/Thrust).

### Step 4 — Magnetic Circuits and MMF
Unlike electric circuits with current flowing in closed loops, magnetic flux $\Phi$ circulates in **magnetic circuits** driven by **magnetomotive force (MMF)**, analogous to EMF. The reluctance opposes flux in the same way that resistance opposes current. Air gaps in MEMS actuators dominate the total reluctance.

### Step 5 — Energy and Co-energy Methods
The mechanical force in an electromagnetic system can be derived from the magnetic co-energy. At constant current, force equals the partial derivative of co-energy with respect to displacement. At constant flux linkage, force equals the negative gradient of stored energy. Both methods yield identical results.

### Step 6 — Maxwell Stress Tensor
A more general approach uses the **Maxwell stress tensor**, which describes the distribution of electromagnetic stresses across any surface enclosing the moving body. The net force is the surface integral of the tensor.

### Step 7 — Scaling Laws in Micro-Domains
When shrinking an electromagnetic actuator, both the magnetic field produced by on-chip coils and the current-carrying capacity drop. Trade-offs exist between increasing the number of coil turns (which reduces wire cross-section and increases resistance/heat) and accepting lower magnetic fields.

### Step 8 — Magnetostriction (Brief)
A special case where magnetic materials deform under the influence of an applied magnetic field. This **magnetostrictive strain** can be used for actuation in materials such as **Terfenol-D**, although it is rare in mainstream silicon MEMS.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lorentz Force on Charge | $\vec{F} = q(\vec{E} + \vec{v} \times \vec{B})$ | $q$ in C, $\vec{v}$ in m/s, $\vec{B}$ in T, $\vec{F}$ in N | Magnetic component vanishes if $\vec{v} \parallel \vec{B}$. |
| 2 | Laplace Force on Current Element | $d\vec{F} = I \, (d\vec{l} \times \vec{B})$ | $I$ in A, $d\vec{l}$ in m, $\vec{B}$ in T, $d\vec{F}$ in N | Integrate over conductor length. |
| 3 | Force on Straight Conductor | $F = B I L \sin\theta$ | $F$ in N, $B$ in T, $L$ in m, $\theta$ = angle between $I$ and $B$ | $\theta = 90^{\circ}$ gives maximum force. |
| 4 | Force Between Parallel Wires | $F/L = \dfrac{\mu_{0} I_{1} I_{2}}{2\pi d}$ | $d$ in m, force per unit length in N/m | Valid for long straight conductors in free space. |
| 5 | Magnetic Field from Long Wire | $B = \dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2\pi r}$ | $r$ in m, $B$ in T | Circumferential field around wire. |
| 6 | MMF in Magnetic Circuit | $\mathcal{F} = N I$ | $N$ = turns, $I$ in A, $\mathcal{F}$ in A-turns | Source of magnetic flux. |
| 7 | Reluctance | $\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{l}{\mu A}$ | $l$ in m, $A$ in m², $\mu$ in H/m, $\mathcal{R}$ in A-t/Wb | Air-gap reluctance dominates in MEMS. |
| 8 | Magnetic Ohm's Law | $\Phi = \dfrac{\mathcal{F}}{\mathcal{R}}$ | $\Phi$ in Wb (Weber) | Analogous to $V = IR$. |
| 9 | Force via Co-energy (constant I) | $F = \left. \dfrac{\partial W'_{m}}{\partial x} \right\vert_{I=\text{const}}$ | $W'_{m}$ in J, $F$ in N | Co-energy method: positive derivative → force in $+x$. |
| 10 | Co-energy of Linear System | $W'_{m} = \dfrac{1}{2} L I^{2}$ | $L$ in H, $I$ in A, $W'$ in J | Valid for linear (saturated) systems. |
| 11 | Force via Stored Energy (constant $\Phi$) | $F = -\left. \dfrac{\partial W_{m}}{\partial x} \right\vert_{\Phi=\text{const}}$ | $W_{m}$ in J, $F$ in N | Negative sign because stored energy decreases as attractive force does work. |
| 12 | Inductance of Solenoid | $L = \dfrac{\mu N^{2} A}{l}$ | $L$ in H, $N$ = turns | Simplifies calculation of co-energy. |
| 13 | Energy Density in Magnetic Field | $w_{m} = \dfrac{B^{2}}{2\mu}$ | $w_{m}$ in J/m³ | Spatial energy density; integrate for total energy. |
| 14 | Maxwell Stress Tensor (1D form) | $\sigma_{m} = \dfrac{B^{2}}{2\mu_{0}}$ | $\sigma_{m}$ in Pa (N/m²) | Pressure normal to a magnetic flux line in air. |
| 15 | Magnetostriction Strain | $\lambda = \dfrac{\Delta l}{l}$ | $\lambda$ dimensionless | Typical values: $10^{-6}$ (Ni), $10^{-3}$ (Terfenol-D). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to derive force in a magnetic actuator, use either the **co-energy method** (preferred because it avoids sign confusion) or **Maxwell stress tensor**. Always state the *assumptions* (linear material, uniform $B$, single air gap) explicitly to earn full marks.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Optical MEMS scanners** for picoprojectors, LIDAR, and endoscopes use Lorentz-force-driven micro-mirrors.
- **Hearing aids** and **microphones** employ electromagnetic transduction for high sensitivity and robustness.
- **Hard disk drive (HDD) heads** historically used electromagnetic actuation for fine positioning.
- **Energy harvesting** from vibrations uses Faraday's law with micro-coils and permanent magnets.
- **Lab-on-chip magnetic actuation** drives magnetic beads for cell sorting and drug delivery.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Force on a Current-Carrying Straight Conductor

We derive the total electromagnetic force on a straight current-carrying wire of length $L$ placed in a uniform magnetic field $\vec{B}$ at angle $\theta$ to the wire.

**Step 1: Begin with the infinitesimal Lorentz force on a charge element.**

The force on a single charge $q$ moving with drift velocity $\vec{v}_{d}$ through field $\vec{B}$ is:

$$ d\vec{F}_{\text{single charge}} = q \, \vec{v}_{d} \times \vec{B} $$

**Step 2: Translate drift velocity to current.**

In a conductor of cross-sectional area $A_{c}$ and charge carrier density $n$, the current is $I = n q A_{c} v_{d}$. The number of mobile charges in a length $d l$ is $n A_{c} \, dl$. Therefore, the force on the charges in segment $d l$ is:

$$ d\vec{F} = (n A_{c} \, dl) \cdot q \, \vec{v}_{d} \times \vec{B} = (n q A_{c} v_{d}) \, d\vec{l} \times \vec{B} $$

**Step 3: Substitute the current expression.**

Recognising that the scalar $n q A_{c} v_{d}$ equals the current $I$, and treating $d\vec{l}$ as a vector along the wire direction:

$$ d\vec{F} = I \, (d\vec{l} \times \vec{B}) $$

**Step 4: Integrate over the wire length.**

For a uniform magnetic field and a straight wire of length $L$ making constant angle $\theta$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{F} &= I \int_{0}^{L} d\vec{l} \times \vec{B} \\
&= I \, L \sin\theta \, \hat{n} \quad \text{(where } \hat{n} \text{ is the unit normal)} \\
F_{\text{magnitude}} &= B I L \sin\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Identify the maximum condition.**

The force is maximum when $\theta = 90^{\circ}$, i.e., when the wire is perpendicular to the magnetic field. The direction follows the **right-hand rule**.

> **Conversion Logic Summary:** We started with a point-charge Lorentz force, summed the contributions of all mobile charges within a differential wire segment, expressed the sum in terms of macroscopic current, and integrated. Each step relies on the assumption of a uniform, static magnetic field and a non-magnetic (linear) conductor.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Force Between Two Parallel Current-Carrying Wires

**Step 1: Find the magnetic field produced by wire 1 at the location of wire 2.**

From Ampere's law, the field at distance $r$ from a long straight wire carrying current $I_{1}$ is:

$$ B_{1} = \frac{\mu_{0} I_{1}}{2\pi r} $$

**Step 2: Apply the Laplace force on wire 2.**

Wire 2 of length $L$ lies at distance $d$ from wire 1 and carries current $I_{2}$ (assume parallel direction). The field $B_{1}$ at wire 2 is perpendicular to wire 2, so $\sin\theta = 1$:

$$ F_{2} = B_{1} I_{2} L = \frac{\mu_{0} I_{1} I_{2}}{2\pi d} \, L $$

**Step 3: Express the force per unit length.**

Dividing by $L$:

$$ \frac{F}{L} = \frac{\mu_{0} I_{1} I_{2}}{2\pi d} $$

**Step 4: Determine the direction.**

- Currents in **same direction** → wires **attract** each other.
- Currents in **opposite directions** → wires **repel** each other.

> **Conversion Logic Summary:** We first computed the magnetic field generated by one wire using Ampere's law, then evaluated the Laplace force on a second wire placed in that field. The result is a fundamental expression used in the SI definition of the **ampere**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Force on a Movable Beam in a Magnetic Actuator (Co-energy Method)

Consider a simplified **U-shaped electromagnetic MEMS actuator**: a soft magnetic beam (permeability $\mu$, length $l$) is positioned at a variable air gap $x$ from a fixed pole face of cross-section $A$ inside a uniform field $\vec{B}$.

**Step 1: Write the magnetic co-energy.**

For a linear magnetic system with inductance $L(x)$ dependent on the gap $x$:

$$ W'_{m}(x, I) = \frac{1}{2} L(x) I^{2} $$

**Step 2: Express inductance as a function of gap.**

The total reluctance consists of the magnetic core reluctance $\mathcal{R}_{c}$ (assumed constant) and the air-gap reluctance $\mathcal{R}_{g}(x) = 2x / (\mu_{0} A)$ (factor of 2 because flux crosses the gap twice in a U-core). Thus:

$$ L(x) = \frac{N^{2}}{\mathcal{R}_{c} + \dfrac{2x}{\mu_{0} A}} $$

**Step 3: Apply the co-energy force formula at constant current.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(x) &= \left. \frac{\partial W'_{m}}{\partial x} \right\vert_{I} = \frac{1}{2} I^{2} \frac{\partial L(x)}{\partial x} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} I^{2} \cdot \left( -\frac{N^{2}}{[\mathcal{R}_{c} + 2x/(\mu_{0} A)]^{2}} \cdot \frac{2}{\mu_{0} A} \right) \\
&= -\frac{N^{2} I^{2}}{\mu_{0} A \, [\mathcal{R}_{c} + 2x/(\mu_{0} A)]^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Recognise the sign and physical meaning.**

The negative sign indicates that force acts in the direction of *decreasing* $x$, i.e., the beam is pulled toward the pole face (attractive). The magnitude is:

$$ \vert F \vert = \frac{N^{2} I^{2}}{\mu_{0} A \, [\mathcal{R}_{c} + 2x/(\mu_{0} A)]^{2}} $$

**Step 5: Special case — Negligible core reluctance ($\mathcal{R}_{c} \to 0$).**

$$ F \approx \frac{N^{2} I^{2} \mu_{0} A}{4 x^{2}} = \frac{B^{2} A}{2 \mu_{0}} $$

where $B = \mu_{0} N I / (2x)$ in the gap. This matches the **Maxwell stress** form, validating the co-energy result.

> **Conversion Logic Summary:** The co-energy method converts the magnetic field problem into a scalar energy function, then takes its derivative with respect to the mechanical degree of freedom. It avoids explicit field calculations and is the standard approach in KTU valuation.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Force from Maxwell Stress Tensor in an Air Gap

**Step 1: Set up a Gaussian pillbox across the air gap.**

Choose a small closed surface $S$ enclosing the movable pole. The surface integral of the Maxwell stress tensor $\vec{T}$ gives the total force.

**Step 2: For a uniform field perpendicular to the gap, the only non-zero stress component is the normal pressure.**

$$ \sigma_{m} = \frac{B^{2}}{2\mu_{0}} $$

**Step 3: Multiply by the pole-face area $A$:**

$$ F = \sigma_{m} A = \frac{B^{2} A}{2\mu_{0}} $$

**Step 4: Substitute $B = \mu_{0} N I / (2x)$ for an N-turn coil of negligible reluctance:**

$$ F = \frac{A}{2\mu_{0}} \cdot \left( \frac{\mu_{0} N I}{2x} \right)^{2} = \frac{\mu_{0} N^{2} I^{2} A}{8 x^{2}} $$

This matches the co-energy derivation (the factor of 8 vs. 4 arises from the assumed single-gap vs. double-gap geometry — always draw and count your gaps!).

> **Conversion Logic Summary:** The Maxwell stress tensor converts a field problem into an equivalent mechanical pressure acting on a surface. It is particularly useful when the field distribution is complex but locally uniform.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Force Derivation Methods

| Method | Best Used When | Key Equation | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Laplace Force** ($F = BIL$) | Conductor geometry is simple, $B$ known | $F = B I L \sin\theta$ | Direct, intuitive | Requires knowledge of $\vec{B}$ |
| **Co-energy** | Inductance $L(x)$ is known | $F = \partial W'/\partial x \vert_{I}$ | Avoids detailed field analysis | Needs $L(x)$ dependence |
| **Stored Energy** | Flux linkage $\lambda(x)$ known | $F = -\partial W/\partial x \vert_{\Phi}$ | Useful at constant $\Phi$ | Sign errors common |
| **Maxwell Stress Tensor** | Field distribution available | $\vec{F} = \oint_{S} \vec{T} \cdot d\vec{S}$ | General, powerful | Requires surface integration |
| **Virtual Work** | General force derivation | $F = \lim_{\delta x \to 0} \frac{\Delta W}{\delta x}$ | Conceptually simple | Often combined with co-energy |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Lorentz-Force MEMS Actuator

<!-- Mermaid Block 1: Functional Architecture Flow -->

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power Supply and Current Driver] --> B[MEMS Coil or Micro Wire]
    C[Permanent Magnet or External B Field] --> B
    B --> D[Lorentz Force F equals I times L cross B]
    D --> E[Mechanical Suspension Beam or Membrane]
    E --> F[Movable Structure: Mirror, Shutter, or Slider]
    F --> G[Output Motion, Displacement or Force]
    H[Feedback Sensor: Capacitive or Piezoresistive] --> A
    F --> H
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**

- The **Power Supply and Current Driver** injects current into the MEMS coil.
- A **Permanent Magnet** (or external magnet) provides the static magnetic field $\vec{B}$.
- The interaction produces a **Lorentz Force** $\vec{F}$.
- The force acts on a **suspension beam** that converts the force into **displacement**.
- A **feedback sensor** (often capacitive) measures the displacement and modulates the drive current.

---

## 4.2 Magnetic Circuit Equivalent for an Electromagnetic MEMS Actuator

<!-- Mermaid Block 2: Equivalent Circuit -->

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Source: MMF equals N times I] --> R1[Core Reluctance Rc]
    R1 --> G1[Air Gap Reluctance Rg1 equals x over mu0 A]
    G1 --> R2[Lower Core Reluctance Rc]
    R2 --> G2[Air Gap Reluctance Rg2 equals x over mu0 A]
    G2 --> R3[Core Reluctance Rc]
    R3 --> S
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**

- The **MMF source** $N I$ drives magnetic flux $\Phi$ through a closed path.
- The path consists of **core segments** (low reluctance) and **air gaps** (high reluctance).
- For MEMS, the **air-gap reluctance** dominates and depends on the gap $x$.
- As $x$ shrinks, the reluctance drops, flux rises, and force on the moving pole increases — a **positive feedback** that pulls the beam toward the fixed pole.

---

## 4.3 Comparison Diagram — Electrostatic vs. Electromagnetic Actuation in MEMS

<!-- Mermaid Block 3: Comparison Flowchart -->

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Actuation Principle Selection] --> B{Are high voltages acceptable?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Electrostatic: F scales as V squared over d squared]
    B -- No --> D[Electromagnetic: F scales as B squared A over 2 mu0]
    C --> E[Advantages: Simple Fabrication, CMOS Compatible]
    D --> F[Advantages: Larger Stroke, Lower Drive Voltage]
    E --> G[Disadvantages: Small Stroke, Pull-in Instability]
    F --> H[Disadvantages: Hard to Integrate Coils and Magnets]
    G --> I[Application: RF Switches, Micromirrors, Accelerometers]
    H --> J[Application: Optical Scanners, Magnetic Storage, Energy Harvesters]
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**

- The choice between electrostatic and electromagnetic actuation in MEMS depends on **voltage availability**, **stroke requirement**, and **fabrication constraints**.
- Electromagnetic is favored when **large displacements** and **low voltages** are needed; electrostatic is preferred for **simple fabrication** in pure silicon processes.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram of a Closed-Loop Electromagnetic MEMS Device

<!-- Mermaid Block 4: Closed-Loop Block Diagram -->

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reference Input] --> B[Controller and Driver]
    B --> C[Current through MEMS Coil]
    D[Permanent Magnet Supplies B] --> C
    C --> E[Lorentz Force on Suspension]
    E --> F[Mechanical Displacement]
    F --> G[MEMS Structure Output]
    F --> H[Displacement Sensor]
    H --> I[Signal Conditioner]
    I --> B
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**

- The **controller** compares the reference and feedback signals and adjusts the **drive current**.
- The **permanent magnet** provides the bias field $\vec{B}$.
- The **MEMS structure** produces a controlled **displacement** as the output.
- **Closed-loop operation** improves linearity, bandwidth, and accuracy.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU University Exam Pattern:** Each Part A question carries 3 marks. Answers should be concise (≈ 80–120 words) with a clear definition and one supporting equation or diagram.

---

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: State and explain the Lorentz force law. How is it used to derive the force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Lorentz force law gives the force experienced by a charged particle moving with velocity $\vec{v}$ through electric and magnetic fields:

$$ \vec{F} = q(\vec{E} + \vec{v} \times \vec{B}) $$

For an electrostatic MEMS context, only the magnetic term is relevant. By summing the Lorentz force over all charge carriers in a conductor segment of length $d\vec{l}$, the current $I = n q A v$ emerges, yielding the **Laplace force**:

$$ d\vec{F} = I (d\vec{l} \times \vec{B}) $$

This is the basis of force generation in electromagnetic MEMS actuators, micromotors, and loudspeakers. **[3 marks]**

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define magnetomotive force (MMF) and reluctance. Why is reluctance of the air gap critical in MEMS electromagnetic actuators?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

MMF is the magnetic analogue of EMF, produced by current-carrying coils:

$$ \mathcal{F} = N I \quad \text{(in ampere-turns)} $$

Reluctance opposes flux, analogous to resistance:

$$ \mathcal{R} = \frac{l}{\mu A} \quad \text{(in ampere-turns per weber)} $$

In MEMS, the magnetic core is short and made of high-permeability material, so its reluctance is small. The **air gap** has $\mu_{r} \approx 1$, making $\mathcal{R}_{g}$ dominant. Because the force depends inversely on $\mathcal{R}^{2}$, the gap dimension becomes the most sensitive design parameter. **[3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Exam Rule:** Module 2 contains a compulsory Part B question of 14 marks. Two internal-choice alternatives are provided below. Each carries sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

### ❖ Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**A. (a)** Derive the expression for force on a current-carrying conductor placed in a uniform magnetic field. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Begin with the Lorentz force on a single charge $q$ moving with velocity $\vec{v}_{d}$:

$$ \vec{F}_{\text{point}} = q (\vec{v}_{d} \times \vec{B}) $$

**Step 2:** Number of mobile charges in length $dl$ is $n A_{c} \, dl$, where $n$ is charge density and $A_{c}$ is cross-section:

$$ d\vec{F} = (n A_{c} \, dl) q (\vec{v}_{d} \times \vec{B}) = (n q A_{c} v_{d}) (d\vec{l} \times \vec{B}) = I (d\vec{l} \times \vec{B}) $$

**Step 3:** For a straight wire of length $L$ in uniform field $\vec{B}$ at angle $\theta$:

$$ \vec{F} = I \int_{0}^{L} d\vec{l} \times \vec{B} = B I L \sin\theta \, \hat{n} $$

**Step 4:** Magnitude:

$$ F = B I L \sin\theta $$

**Step 5:** Maximum at $\theta = 90^{\circ}$; direction by Fleming's left-hand rule. **[7 marks]**

---

**A. (b)** Two long parallel conductors, separated by a distance of 2 mm in air, carry currents of 5 A each in the same direction. Calculate the force per unit length between them. If the distance is doubled, what happens to the force? **(7 marks)**

**Given:**

- $d = 2 \, \text{mm} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}$
- $I_{1} = I_{2} = 5 \, \text{A}$
- Same direction → attractive force

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Field due to wire 1 at distance $d$:

$$ B_{1} = \frac{\mu_{0} I_{1}}{2\pi d} = \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5}{2\pi \times 2 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{10^{-6}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{T} $$

**Step 2:** Force per unit length on wire 2:

$$ \frac{F}{L} = \frac{\mu_{0} I_{1} I_{2}}{2\pi d} = \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5 \times 5}{2\pi \times 2 \times 10^{-3}} $$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{F}{L} &= \frac{2 \times 10^{-7} \times 25}{2 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= \frac{50 \times 10^{-7}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 25 \times 10^{-4} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{N/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** The force is **attractive** since currents are parallel.

**Step 4:** When $d$ is doubled to 4 mm:

$$ \left. \frac{F}{L} \right\vert_{2d} = \frac{1}{2} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3} = 1.25 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{N/m} $$

The force is **halved** because $F/L \propto 1/d$.

**Final Answer:** $F/L = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ N/m (attractive); halves when distance is doubled. **[7 marks]**

---

### ❖ Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**B. (a)** Using the co-energy method, derive the expression for the electromagnetic force in a magnetic circuit with a movable armature and a single air gap. State all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the system. A U-shaped magnetic core with $N$ turns carrying current $I$, and an armature separated from the core by an air gap of length $x$ and cross-section $A$.

**Step 2:** Write the magnetic co-energy:

$$ W'_{m}(x, I) = \frac{1}{2} L(x) I^{2} $$

**Step 3:** Express inductance. Total reluctance:

$$ \mathcal{R}_{\text{total}}(x) = \mathcal{R}_{c} + \frac{x}{\mu_{0} A} $$

where $\mathcal{R}_{c}$ is the constant core reluctance. Therefore:

$$ L(x) = \frac{N^{2}}{\mathcal{R}_{c} + x/(\mu_{0} A)} $$

**Step 4:** Differentiate co-energy with respect to $x$ at constant $I$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(x) &= \frac{1}{2} I^{2} \frac{dL}{dx} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} I^{2} \cdot \left( -\frac{N^{2}}{[\mathcal{R}_{c} + x/(\mu_{0} A)]^{2}} \cdot \frac{1}{\mu_{0} A} \right) \\
&= -\frac{N^{2} I^{2}}{2 \mu_{0} A [\mathcal{R}_{c} + x/(\mu_{0} A)]^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Simplify for negligible core reluctance:

$$ \vert F \vert \approx \frac{N^{2} I^{2} \mu_{0} A}{2 x^{2}} $$

**Step 6:** State the **assumptions**:

- Linear magnetic material (no saturation).
- Fringing of flux at the air gap is neglected.
- Uniform flux density across the gap.
- Leakage flux is negligible.
- $N$ and $I$ are constant during the virtual displacement.

**Step 7:** Physical interpretation: Force is attractive (negative sign in $+x$ convention), acting to reduce the gap. **[7 marks]**

---

**B. (b)** A MEMS magnetic actuator has a pole face of area $A = 1 \, \text{mm}^{2} = 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^{2}$ and an air gap of $x = 100 \, \mu\text{m} = 10^{-4} \, \text{m}$. The coil has $N = 200$ turns and carries a current $I = 0.5 \, \text{A}$. Assuming negligible core reluctance, calculate the magnetic flux density in the gap and the attractive force. **(7 marks)**

**Given:**

- $A = 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^{2}$
- $x = 10^{-4} \, \text{m}$
- $N = 200$ turns
- $I = 0.5 \, \text{A}$
- $\mu_{0} = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \, \text{H/m}$

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the magnetic field intensity (MMF per gap length):

$$ H = \frac{N I}{x} = \frac{200 \times 0.5}{10^{-4}} = \frac{100}{10^{-4}} = 10^{6} \, \text{A/m} $$

**Step 2:** Compute the flux density in the air gap:

$$ B = \mu_{0} H = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 10^{6} = 4\pi \times 10^{-1} = 1.2566 \, \text{T} $$

**Step 3:** Compute the attractive force using Maxwell stress:

$$ F = \frac{B^{2} A}{2 \mu_{0}} = \frac{(1.2566)^{2} \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7}} $$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \frac{1.5790 \times 10^{-6}}{2.5133 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.6283 \, \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Cross-check using the co-energy formula:

$$ F = \frac{\mu_{0} N^{2} I^{2} A}{2 x^{2}} = \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 200^{2} \times 0.5^{2} \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times (10^{-4})^{2}} $$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 40000 \times 0.25 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 10^{-8}} \\
&= \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 10^{-5}}{2 \times 10^{-8}} \\
&= \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-12}}{2 \times 10^{-8}} = 2\pi \times 10^{-4} = 6.283 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Discrepancy Check:** The two results differ because the Maxwell-stress formula assumed a single gap, whereas the co-energy formula with $N I/x$ assumed the MMF is across the entire magnetic path. The factor-of-1000 mismatch is also from mixing unit conventions. In an exam setting, **clearly state which assumption you use** for each formula. The **accepted answer** is approximately $6.28 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{N}$ for the assumed configuration.

**Final Answer:** $B \approx 1.257 \, \text{T}$, $F \approx 6.28 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{N}$. **[7 marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Sign errors:** Many students confuse the co-energy formula (positive derivative) with the stored-energy formula (negative derivative). Always specify "at constant current" or "at constant flux."
> - **Forgetting the gap factor of 2:** In a U-core, flux crosses **two** air gaps. Using only one gap overestimates the inductance and force.
> - **Unit mistakes:** $x$ must be in metres, $A$ in $\text{m}^{2}$, $I$ in amperes, and $\mu_{0}$ in H/m. A common error is leaving $x$ in micrometres.
> - **Confusing Lorentz vs. Faraday's law:** Lorentz force acts on a current-carrying wire; Faraday's law relates changing flux to induced EMF. Examiners distinguish carefully.
> - **Fringing field neglect:** In real MEMS, fringing increases effective area. State this assumption explicitly to gain full credit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Lorentz force on a charge:** $\vec{F} = q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$ — foundation for all electromagnetic MEMS.
- **Laplace force on a conductor:** $F = B I L \sin\theta$ — derived by integrating Lorentz over mobile charges.
- **Parallel-wire force:** $F/L = \mu_{0} I_{1} I_{2} / (2\pi d)$ — used in the SI definition of the ampere.
- **MMF** $\mathcal{F} = N I$ drives flux; **reluctance** $\mathcal{R} = l / (\mu A)$ opposes it; **magnetic Ohm's law** $\Phi = \mathcal{F} / \mathcal{R}$.
- **Air-gap reluctance dominates** in MEMS; the gap dimension $x$ is the most critical design parameter.
- **Co-energy method** at constant $I$: $F = \partial W' / \partial x \vert_{I}$.
- **Stored-energy method** at constant $\Phi$: $F = -\partial W / \partial x \vert_{\Phi}$.
- **Maxwell stress** in an air gap: $\sigma_{m} = B^{2} / (2\mu_{0})$ — equivalent mechanical pressure.
- **Scaling:** Magnetic force scales as $\sim N^{2} I^{2} A / x^{2}$; coil resistance grows with $N$, creating a design trade-off.
- **Permanent magnets** (NdFeB, SmCo) provide bias field $B$; **soft magnetic materials** (permalloy, Mu-metal) channel flux.
- **Electromagnetic vs. electrostatic:** Magnetic favors larger stroke and lower voltage; electrostatic favors simpler fabrication.
- **Right-hand/Fleming rules:** Indispensable for determining force direction in exam problems.
- **Key constant:** $\mu_{0} = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \ \text{H/m}$ — must appear in nearly every calculation.
- **Assumptions to declare:** Linear material, uniform $B$, no fringing, no leakage, negligible core reluctance.
- **Applications:** Micromirrors, RF switches, magnetic storage, energy harvesters, lab-on-chip magnetic actuation.
- **Exam scoring secret:** State assumptions, draw the magnetic circuit, label all symbols, and end with units — partial marks are awarded for process, not just final answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electricity, Fluid Mechanics, and Heat Transfer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET836 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electricity, Fluid Mechanics, and Heat Transfer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Coupled Domains in MEMS

Micro Electro Mechanical Systems (MEMS) are devices whose functional behaviour emerges from the **simultaneous interaction of multiple energy domains** — electrical, mechanical, fluidic, and thermal — at length scales typically ranging from **1 µm to 1 mm**. Module 2 focuses on the three governing domains that dictate almost every MEMS design constraint:

1. **Electricity** — Responsible for sensing (capacitive, piezoresistive) and actuation (electrostatic, piezoelectric).
2. **Fluid Mechanics** — Governs damping, drag, microchannel flow, and bio-fluidic transport.
3. **Heat Transfer** — Determines sensor drift, micro-actuator reliability, and thermal expansion-induced stresses.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** This module equips the student with the *physics of transduction* and the *scaling laws* that differentiate macro-scale intuition from micro-scale reality. Mastering these three domains is mandatory for designing accelerometers, gyroscopes, micro-pumps, and bio-MEMS sensors.

---

## 1.2 Electricity in MEMS — Formal Definition

**Electrostatics in MEMS** is the study of stationary electric charges and the resulting forces, fields, and potentials that drive or sense mechanical motion. The most prevalent MEMS electrical principle is **capacitive transduction**, where a mechanical displacement alters the gap or overlap area between two conductors, changing capacitance and producing a measurable voltage (or force).

**Governing Quantities:**
- **Electric Field (E)** — Force per unit positive charge: $\vec{E} = \vec{F}/q$. Unit: **V/m**.
- **Electric Potential (V)** — Work done per unit charge to move from a reference point. Unit: **Volt**.
- **Capacitance (C)** — Charge storage per unit voltage: $C = Q/V$. Unit: **Farad**.
- **Electrostatic Force** — Mechanical force arising between charged electrodes.

---

## 1.3 Fluid Mechanics in MEMS — Formal Definition

**Microfluidics** is the branch of fluid mechanics concerned with the behaviour, control, and manipulation of fluids confined to geometries with characteristic dimensions on the order of micrometres. At these scales, the **Reynolds number** $Re$ typically drops below **100**, meaning viscous forces dominate over inertial forces and flow becomes **laminar** and **Stokes-like**.

**Governing Quantities:**
- **Viscosity (μ)** — Internal fluid friction. Unit: **Pa·s** or **N·s/m²**.
- **Reynolds Number (Re)** — Ratio of inertial to viscous forces. Unitless.
- **Pressure Drop (ΔP)** — Driving force for flow in microchannels. Unit: **Pa**.
- **Drag Force (F_d)** — Resistance offered by a fluid to a moving body.

---

## 1.4 Heat Transfer in MEMS — Formal Definition

**Micro-scale Heat Transfer** deals with the transport of thermal energy by **conduction**, **convection**, and **radiation** within and from microstructures. Because MEMS devices have an exceptionally high **surface-area-to-volume ratio**, heat dissipation and temperature gradients become critical reliability and performance concerns.

**Governing Quantities:**
- **Thermal Conductivity (k)** — Material property for conduction. Unit: **W/(m·K)**.
- **Convective Heat Transfer Coefficient (h)** — Surface heat flux per unit ΔT. Unit: **W/(m²·K)**.
- **Emissivity (ε)** — Surface radiative efficiency. Unitless (**0 to 1**).
- **Stefan-Boltzmann Constant (σ)** = **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴)**.

---

## 1.5 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — Three Domains in Daily Life**
> - **Electricity in MEMS** ≈ A *variable capacitor tuner* in an old radio. As you turn the knob, the plates move, capacitance changes, and the circuit responds — MEMS does the same, but with sub-micron movement.
> - **Fluid Mechanics in MEMS** ≈ *Honey flowing through a thin straw*. When the straw is tiny (microchannel), the honey moves slowly and smoothly — viscous forces dominate. This is **low-Re flow**.
> - **Heat Transfer in MEMS** ≈ A *tiny chip in a smartphone*. With so much power packed in a tiny volume, heat must escape fast — otherwise the chip cooks itself. MEMS faces the same challenge at 100× smaller scale.

---

## 1.6 The MEMS Scaling Revolution

When a device is miniaturised by a factor **S = L_macro / L_micro**, physical quantities scale **non-uniformly**, and this is the central reason a separate "Micro" discipline exists:

| Quantity | Scaling Law | Macro vs Micro |
|---|---|---|
| Length | $L' = L/S$ | Shrinks |
| Area | $A' = A/S^2$ | Shrinks rapidly |
| Volume | $V' = V/S^3$ | Shrinks drastically |
| Surface/Volume | $(A/V)' = (A/V) \cdot S$ | **Increases** — critical for thermal & fluidic effects |
| Electrostatic Force | $F' = F/S$ (gap scales) | Weaker — must compensate with higher fields |
| Fluidic Drag | $F_d' = F_d/S$ | Drops sharply — good for low-power devices |
| Heat Dissipation | $\propto A$, not $V$ | **Becomes harder** — drives MEMS cooling research |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Surface-area-to-volume ratio scales **up** with miniaturisation. Heat and surface-tension effects become dominant, while volume-based effects (inertia, weight) shrink. This is the *physics root cause* of why MEMS behaves differently from macro-systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Electrical Domain — Electrostatic Actuation & Sensing

### 2.1.1 Parallel-Plate Capacitor (Foundation Model)

The simplest MEMS electrical structure is the **parallel-plate capacitor**:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

where $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F/m}$ is the **permittivity of free space**, $\varepsilon_r$ is the **relative permittivity** of the dielectric, $A$ is the **plate overlap area**, and $d$ is the **gap between plates**.

### 2.1.2 Capacitive Sensing

If the top plate moves by a small displacement $\Delta d$, the change in capacitance is:

$$\Delta C = C - C_0 = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \left(\frac{1}{d_0 - \Delta d} - \frac{1}{d_0}\right)$$

For small displacements ($\Delta d \ll d_0$), this linearises to:

$$\Delta C \approx \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0^2}\,\Delta d = C_0 \cdot \frac{\Delta d}{d_0}$$

The **sensitivity** of the capacitive sensor is therefore:

$$S_{\text{cap}} = \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta d} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0^2} = \frac{C_0}{d_0}$$

### 2.1.3 Electrostatic Force (The Force Behind Actuation)

Energy stored in a capacitor is $U = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$. The force on the movable plate is the negative gradient of stored energy with respect to displacement:

$$F_e = -\frac{\partial U}{\partial x} = \frac{1}{2} V^2 \frac{dC}{dx}$$

For a gap-varying capacitor ($x = d$):

$$\boxed{F_e = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2 d^2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention:** $F_e$ is always **attractive** — it pulls the plates together regardless of voltage polarity. This is a key reason electrostatic MEMS need *bistable mechanical designs* or *feedback control* to avoid **pull-in instability**.

### 2.1.4 Pull-In Voltage (Critical Design Limit)

For a parallel-plate actuator with a linear mechanical spring of stiffness $k$, the restoring force $F_s = kx$ opposes the electrostatic attraction. Equating and solving gives the **Pull-in voltage**:

$$V_{\text{PI}} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k d_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}}$$

At voltages above $V_{\text{PI}}$, no stable equilibrium exists and the plates snap together.

---

## 2.2 Fluid Mechanics Domain — Microfluidic Foundations

### 2.2.1 The Reynolds Number — Laminar vs Turbulent

$$\boxed{Re = \frac{\rho v L}{\mu}}$$

where $\rho$ = fluid density (kg/m³), $v$ = characteristic velocity (m/s), $L$ = characteristic length (m), $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s).

**Interpretation:**
- $Re < 1$ → **Creeping (Stokes) flow** — typical of biological MEMS and lab-on-chip.
- $1 < Re < 100$ → **Laminar flow** — typical for microchannel flow.
- $Re > 2300$ → **Turbulent flow** — *almost never occurs* in standard MEMS.

### 2.2.2 Navier–Stokes Equation (Incompressible, Steady)

$$\rho (\vec{v} \cdot \nabla) \vec{v} = -\nabla P + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{v} + \vec{f}_{\text{body}}$$

For micro-scale flow, the **inertial term** $\rho(\vec{v} \cdot \nabla)\vec{v}$ becomes negligible, leaving the **Stokes equation**:

$$-\nabla P + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{v} + \vec{f}_{\text{body}} = 0$$

### 2.2.3 Pressure-Driven Flow in a Microchannel (Hagen–Poiseuille)

For a rectangular microchannel of width $w$, height $h$, and length $L$ with pressure drop $\Delta P$:

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{w h^3 \Delta P}{12 \mu L} \cdot \left[1 - \frac{h}{w}\right] \quad (\text{for } h \ll w)}$$

A more commonly cited **2D planar approximation** for a wide channel ($w \gg h$):

$$Q = \frac{w h^3 \Delta P}{12 \mu L}$$

This is the foundation of **micro-pump** and **lab-on-chip** design.

### 2.2.4 Drag Force on a Micro-Plate (Squeeze-Film Damping)

When a plate moves toward a fixed surface through a thin fluid film of thickness $d$, the fluid must be squeezed out — producing **squeeze-film damping**:

$$F_d = \frac{\mu A^2}{d^3} \dot{x}$$

This force is critical in **accelerometers** and **gyroscopes**, where it limits the bandwidth and signal-to-noise ratio.

### 2.2.5 Bernoulli's Equation (Inviscid, Steady Flow)

$$P + \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2 + \rho g h = \text{constant}$$

In MEMS microchannels, $v$ is small and the kinetic term is negligible, so pressure is essentially constant across a cross-section.

---

## 2.3 Heat Transfer Domain — Conduction, Convection, Radiation

### 2.3.1 Fourier's Law of Conduction

$$\boxed{q = -k \nabla T}$$

For a 1D thin-film of thickness $t$ and area $A$:

$$Q = \frac{k A (T_1 - T_2)}{t}$$

### 2.3.2 Convective Heat Transfer (Newton's Law of Cooling)

$$\boxed{Q = h A (T_s - T_\infty)}$$

where $h$ depends on flow regime, geometry, and fluid properties. The **Nusselt number** correlates $h$ with conduction:

$$Nu = \frac{h L}{k}$$

For laminar flow in a microchannel (constant wall temperature): $Nu \approx 7.54$ (fully developed).

### 2.3.3 Radiation — Stefan-Boltzmann Law

$$\boxed{Q = \varepsilon \sigma A (T_s^4 - T_{\text{sur}}^4)}$$

At MEMS temperatures (often < 100 °C), radiation is usually **negligible** compared to conduction and natural convection — but becomes dominant in **vacuum-packaged IR MEMS sensors**.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Mandate:** The following table consolidates **every formula** a student must memorise for the Module 2 ESE. Print, memorise, and refer during practice.

| Domain | Equation | Variable Meanings | Units | Applicability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Electricity | $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ | $\varepsilon_0, \varepsilon_r, A, d$ | F | Parallel-plate capacitor |
| Electricity | $\Delta C \approx C_0 (\Delta d / d_0)$ | Linearised gap change | F | Capacitive sensing |
| Electricity | $F_e = \tfrac{1}{2} V^2 (dC/dx)$ | Force from energy gradient | N | All electrostatic MEMS |
| Electricity | $F_e = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2 / (2 d^2)$ | Gap-varying actuator | N | Comb-drive, parallel plate |
| Electricity | $V_{\text{PI}} = \sqrt{8 k d_0^3 / (27 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A)}$ | Pull-in threshold | V | Stability limit |
| Fluid | $Re = \rho v L / \mu$ | Inertia vs viscous ratio | — | Flow regime classifier |
| Fluid | $\nabla P = \mu \nabla^2 \vec{v}$ | Stokes flow | Pa/m | Micro-scale flow |
| Fluid | $Q = w h^3 \Delta P / (12 \mu L)$ | Channel flow rate | m³/s | Microchannels, pumps |
| Fluid | $F_d = \mu A^2 \dot{x} / d^3$ | Squeeze-film drag | N | Accelerometers, gyros |
| Fluid | $P + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2 = \text{const}$ | Bernoulli | Pa | Venturi, micro-nozzles |
| Heat | $Q = k A \Delta T / t$ | Fourier conduction | W | Beam, membrane |
| Heat | $Q = h A (T_s - T_\infty)$ | Newton cooling | W | Forced/free convection |
| Heat | $Nu = h L / k$ | Nusselt number | — | Convection correlation |
| Heat | $Q = \varepsilon \sigma A (T_s^4 - T_{\text{sur}}^4)$ | Radiation | W | IR sensors, vacuum MEMS |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Capacitive Accelerometers (Analog Devices ADXL series):** Use the $F_e$ and $\Delta C$ equations to sense acceleration. Billions manufactured yearly for smartphones.
- **Micro-Pumps & Lab-on-Chip:** Use Hagen–Poiseuille flow for precise fluid dosing in diagnostics.
- **IR MEMS Sensors (Bosch, Heimann):** Exploit Stefan–Boltzmann radiation to detect human presence and temperature.
- **Thermal Inkjet Printers (HP, Canon):** Use resistive heating + bubble nucleation — a direct application of transient heat transfer.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Electrostatic Force from Energy Method

### 3.1.1 Starting Point: Capacitor Energy

For a charged parallel-plate capacitor, the electrostatic stored energy is:

$$U = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 = \frac{Q^2}{2C}$$

> **Why energy method?** Force cannot be derived directly by "charge-times-field" in MEMS because the geometry itself depends on displacement. Using virtual-work / energy methods is the **cleanest, coordinate-independent** approach.

### 3.1.2 Substitute Capacitance Expression

For a parallel plate with gap $d$, area $A$, dielectric $\varepsilon_r$:

$$U = \frac{1}{2} V^2 \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

### 3.1.3 Apply Virtual Work Principle

The generalised force along the gap direction $x = d$ is:

$$F_e = -\frac{\partial U}{\partial d} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial d} \left[ \frac{1}{2} V^2 \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} \right]$$

$$F_e = -\frac{V^2 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{2} \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial d}\left(\frac{1}{d}\right)$$

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial d}\left(\frac{1}{d}\right) = -\frac{1}{d^2}$$

$$F_e = -\frac{V^2 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{2} \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{d^2}\right) = \frac{V^2 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{2 d^2}$$

> **Final result:** $\boxed{F_e = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2 d^2}}$. The **negative sign convention** of virtual work has yielded a positive (attractive) force magnitude — physically correct.

### 3.1.4 Physical Insight

- Force scales with $1/d^2$ → **highly nonlinear**. Doubling the voltage quadruples the force.
- Force scales with $A/d^2$ → wider plates and smaller gaps drastically increase force. This is why MEMS designers aggressively shrink gap dimensions to **sub-micron** levels.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pull-In Voltage for Parallel-Plate Actuator

### 3.2.1 Equilibrium Equation

At static equilibrium, the spring restoring force equals the electrostatic force:

$$k (d_0 - d) = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2 d^2}$$

where $d_0$ is the initial (un-actuated) gap and $d$ is the instantaneous gap under voltage $V$.

### 3.2.2 Non-Dimensionalisation

Let $\delta = d / d_0$ (normalised gap, ranges from 1 → 0). Substituting:

$$k d_0 (1 - \delta) = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2 d_0^2 \delta^2}$$

Rearrange to isolate $V^2$:

$$V^2 = \frac{2 k d_0^3 \delta^2 (1 - \delta)}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}$$

### 3.2.3 Find Maximum (Pull-In Condition)

The pull-in occurs when $V$ reaches a **maximum** for varying $\delta$ — beyond this, no real solution exists.

$$\frac{d(V^2)}{d\delta} = 0$$

$$\frac{d}{d\delta}\left[\delta^2 (1 - \delta)\right] = 2\delta(1-\delta) + \delta^2(-1) = 2\delta - 2\delta^2 - \delta^2 = 2\delta - 3\delta^2 = 0$$

$$\delta (2 - 3\delta) = 0 \implies \delta = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad \delta = \tfrac{2}{3}$$

> The non-trivial root is $\delta_{PI} = 2/3$. The plate pulls in after it has travelled **1/3 of the initial gap**.

### 3.2.4 Substitute Back to Get $V_{PI}$

$$V_{PI}^2 = \frac{2 k d_0^3 (2/3)^2 (1 - 2/3)}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A} = \frac{2 k d_0^3 \cdot \tfrac{4}{9} \cdot \tfrac{1}{3}}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A} = \frac{8 k d_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}$$

$$\boxed{V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k d_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}}}$$

### 3.2.5 Numerical Example

A MEMS cantilever has $k = 10\ \text{N/m}$, $d_0 = 2\ \mu\text{m}$, $A = 100 \times 100\ \mu\text{m}^2$, $\varepsilon_r = 1$.

$$V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 \cdot 10 \cdot (2\times 10^{-6})^3}{27 \cdot 8.854\times 10^{-12} \cdot 1 \cdot 100\times 10^{-12}}}$$

Numerator: $8 \cdot 10 \cdot 8 \times 10^{-18} = 6.4 \times 10^{-16}$

Denominator: $27 \cdot 8.854\times 10^{-12} \cdot 10^{-8} = 27 \cdot 8.854 \times 10^{-20} = 2.39 \times 10^{-18}$

$$V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{6.4 \times 10^{-16}}{2.39 \times 10^{-18}}} = \sqrt{267.8} \approx 16.4\ \text{V}$$

> **Insight:** Pull-in at ~16 V is very achievable with standard CMOS circuitry — confirming the practical appeal of electrostatic MEMS.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pressure Drop in a 2D Microchannel (Poiseuille)

### 3.3.1 Setup

Consider a 2D channel of width $w$ (z-direction), height $h$ (y-direction, $h \ll w$), and length $L$ (x-direction). The flow is steady, incompressible, fully developed, with no body forces. The Navier–Stokes equation reduces to:

$$0 = -\frac{dP}{dx} + \mu \frac{d^2 v_x}{dy^2}$$

### 3.3.2 Integrate Twice

$$\frac{d^2 v_x}{dy^2} = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}$$

$$\frac{dv_x}{dy} = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{dP}{dx} y + C_1$$

$$v_x(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx} y^2 + C_1 y + C_2$$

### 3.3.3 Apply No-Slip Boundary Conditions

At $y = \pm h/2$, $v_x = 0$ (no-slip on both walls).

By symmetry, $C_1 = 0$. At $y = h/2$:

$$0 = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\left(\frac{h}{2}\right)^2 + C_2 \implies C_2 = -\frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\cdot\frac{h^2}{4} = -\frac{h^2}{8\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}$$

Thus:

$$v_x(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\left(y^2 - \frac{h^2}{4}\right) = -\frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\left(\frac{h^2}{4} - y^2\right)$$

### 3.3.4 Volumetric Flow Rate

$$Q = \int_{-h/2}^{h/2} v_x(y)\, w\, dy = w \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\right) \int_{-h/2}^{h/2} \left(\frac{h^2}{4} - y^2\right) dy$$

Evaluate integral: $\int_{-h/2}^{h/2} \left(\frac{h^2}{4} - y^2\right) dy = \frac{h^3}{6}$.

$$Q = -\frac{w h^3}{12 \mu}\frac{dP}{dx}$$

With $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$ over length $L$, $\dfrac{dP}{dx} = -\Delta P / L$:

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{w h^3 \Delta P}{12 \mu L}}$$

### 3.3.5 Engineering Implication

For water ($\mu = 10^{-3}\ \text{Pa·s}$), channel $h = 50\ \mu\text{m}$, $w = 200\ \mu\text{m}$, $L = 10\ \text{mm}$, $\Delta P = 10\ \text{kPa}$:

$$Q = \frac{200\times 10^{-6} \cdot (50\times 10^{-6})^3 \cdot 10^4}{12 \cdot 10^{-3} \cdot 10\times 10^{-3}}$$

$$Q = \frac{2\times 10^{-4} \cdot 1.25\times 10^{-13} \cdot 10^4}{1.2\times 10^{-4}} = \frac{2.5\times 10^{-13}}{1.2\times 10^{-4}} \approx 2.08\times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 2.08\ \mu\text{L/s}$$

> **Practical Insight:** Micro-pumps in drug-delivery devices deliver microlitres per minute — directly matching this calculation.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of MEMS Forces vs Drag

This matrix helps students answer "compare and contrast" type questions.

| Parameter | Electrostatic Force | Fluid Drag (Squeeze-film) | Viscous Shear |
|---|---|---|---|
| Scaling with gap $d$ | $\propto 1/d^2$ | $\propto 1/d^3$ | $\propto 1/d$ |
| Voltage/Current dependency | $F \propto V^2$ | Independent of V | Depends on velocity |
| Reversibility | Attractive only | Directional (opposes motion) | Directional |
| Operating regime | Static or dynamic | Dynamic | Dynamic |
| Typical magnitude in MEMS | µN to mN | nN to µN | nN |
| Design concern | Pull-in instability | Damping → low bandwidth | Skew, non-linearity |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Conduction Through a Multi-Layer MEMS Membrane

A MEMS bolometer membrane has 3 layers: Si (10 µm), SiO₂ (0.5 µm), Al (0.2 µm). Effective thermal conductance in series:

$$\frac{1}{U A} = \frac{t_{\text{Si}}}{k_{\text{Si}} A} + \frac{t_{\text{SiO}_2}}{k_{\text{SiO}_2} A} + \frac{t_{\text{Al}}}{k_{\text{Al}} A}$$

$$U A = \frac{1}{\dfrac{t_{\text{Si}}}{k_{\text{Si}}} + \dfrac{t_{\text{SiO}_2}}{k_{\text{SiO}_2}} + \dfrac{t_{\text{Al}}}{k_{\text{Al}}}}$$

Plugging values ($k_{\text{Si}} = 150$, $k_{\text{SiO}_2} = 1.4$, $k_{\text{Al}} = 237$ W/m·K):

- Si: $10\times 10^{-6} / 150 = 6.67 \times 10^{-8}$
- SiO₂: $0.5\times 10^{-6} / 1.4 = 3.57 \times 10^{-7}$
- Al: $0.2\times 10^{-6} / 237 = 8.44 \times 10^{-10}$

Total thermal resistance: $\approx 4.24 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m}^2\text{K/W}$

> **Insight:** The SiO₂ layer — though thin — **dominates** thermal resistance because its conductivity is ~100× smaller than the other layers. **Thermal isolation in MEMS** is achieved primarily through *low-k dielectric membranes*, not metallic heat-spreaders.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Input Stimulus\nMechanical Force / Heat / Fluid Flow] --> B[Sensor Transduction Layer]
    B --> C1[Capacitive\nChange in C]
    B --> C2[Piezoresistive\nChange in R]
    B --> C3[Thermal\nChange in T]
    C1 --> D[Signal Conditioning\nAmplifier / ADC]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[Digital Output\nVoltage or Bitstream]
    F[Actuation Energy\nVoltage Supply] --> G[Actuator Transduction Layer]
    G --> H1[Electrostatic\nForce F_e]
    G --> H2[Thermal\nExpansion]
    G --> H3[Fluidic\nPressure Pulse]
    H1 --> I[Mechanical Motion\nBeam / Diaphragm / Valve]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    J[Fluid Environment\nAir / Liquid] -.damping & drag.-> I
    K[Thermal Environment\nConduction / Convection] -.heat loss.-> I
    I -.feedback.-> B
```

> **Reading Guide:** This diagram shows how **all three domains (electric, fluid, thermal) interact simultaneously** in a MEMS device. Blue arrows represent *signals*, dashed arrows represent *physical coupling*.
