# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define electrostatic force in a parallel-plate MEMS actuator and state its dependency on gap distance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The electrostatic force in a parallel-plate MEMS actuator is the attractive force generated between two charged electrodes separated by a small gap. It is derived from the energy stored in the capacitor and is given by:
>
> $$F_e = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2 d^2}$$
>
> **Gap dependency:** The force varies inversely with the **square of the gap distance** ($F_e \propto 1/d^2$). Therefore, halving the gap increases the force by a factor of 4. This strong non-linearity is exploited in MEMS to generate large forces with low voltages by aggressively scaling down the gap to sub-micron dimensions. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define Reynolds number and explain its significance in microfluidic MEMS devices.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The Reynolds number is the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in a fluid:
>
> $$Re = \frac{\rho v L}{\mu}$$
>
> **Significance in MEMS:**
> - In microchannels, the characteristic length $L$ is very small (µm), so $Re$ is typically **< 100**, often **< 1**.
> - Low $Re$ implies **viscous forces dominate** over inertial forces.
> - Flow is **laminar, smooth, and predictable** — there is no turbulence.
> - The **Stokes equation** (linear) replaces the full Navier–Stokes equation, simplifying design.
> - Mixing in such flows is purely by **molecular diffusion**, requiring serpentine or chaotic-advection micro-mixers. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** mix up units. Capacitance is in Farads, not Coulombs.
> - **Do NOT** forget the factor **½** in $F_e = \frac{1}{2}V^2(dC/dx)$ — losing it costs 2 marks.
> - **Do NOT** apply Bernoulli's equation in microchannels blindly — it is invalid when viscous effects dominate.
> - **Do NOT** state pull-in voltage as $V_{PI} = V_{\text{threshold}}$ — derive it with the **factor 8/27** explicitly.
> - **Do NOT** confuse Reynolds number thresholds: $Re < 2300$ is laminar (pipe flow); MEMS is typically $Re < 100$.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Derive the expression for the electrostatic force between the plates of a parallel-plate capacitor as used in MEMS actuators. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A MEMS cantilever accelerometer has a proof mass of $m = 10^{-9}\ \text{kg}$, spring constant $k = 0.1\ \text{N/m}$, plate area $A = 50 \times 50\ \mu\text{m}^2$, initial gap $d_0 = 2\ \mu\text{m}$, and $\varepsilon_r = 1$. Calculate the **pull-in voltage**. If the gap is reduced to $1\ \mu\text{m}$ by process optimisation, what is the new pull-in voltage, and what design lesson does this reveal? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Consider a parallel-plate capacitor with vacuum (or dielectric $\varepsilon_r$), area $A$, and gap $d$.

**Step 1:** Capacitance is $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$

**Step 2:** Stored electrical energy is $U = \dfrac{1}{2}CV^2 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2 d}$

**Step 3:** By the **principle of virtual work**, the force in the gap direction is the negative gradient of energy:

$$F_e = -\frac{\partial U}{\partial d} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial d}\left[\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2 d}\right] = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2 d^2}$$

**Assumptions:**
- Plates are parallel, infinite (edge effects neglected).
- Dielectric is linear, homogeneous, isotropic.
- Quasi-static: electric field equilibrates instantaneously.
- No fringing fields.
- Voltage source holds $V$ constant (charge may vary).

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Apply the pull-in voltage formula:

$$V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k d_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}}$$

**Step 2:** Plug in initial values — $k = 0.1$ N/m, $d_0 = 2\ \mu\text{m} = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $A = (50 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-9}$ m², $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $\varepsilon_r = 1$.

Numerator: $8 \times 0.1 \times (2\times 10^{-6})^3 = 0.8 \times 8 \times 10^{-18} = 6.4 \times 10^{-18}$

Denominator: $27 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-9} = 27 \times 22.135 \times 10^{-21} = 5.976 \times 10^{-19}$

$$V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{6.4 \times 10^{-18}}{5.976 \times 10^{-19}}} = \sqrt{10.71} \approx 3.27\ \text{V}$$

**Step 3:** For $d_0 = 1\ \mu\text{m}$, $d_0^3$ is reduced by $8\times$:

Numerator: $0.8 \times (1\times 10^{-6})^3 = 0.8 \times 10^{-18} = 8 \times 10^{-19}$

$$V_{PI,\text{new}} = \sqrt{\frac{8 \times 10^{-19}}{5.976 \times 10^{-19}}} = \sqrt{1.34} \approx 1.16\ \text{V}$$

**Step 4:** Design lesson:

> Halving the gap **reduces the pull-in voltage by a factor of $\sqrt{8} \approx 2.83$**. This reveals that **shrinking the gap is the most effective design lever** to reduce actuation voltage. However, this comes at the cost of **reduced stroke** (only 1/3 of gap before pull-in), **higher sensitivity to fabrication tolerances**, and **increased squeeze-film damping**. Designers must balance these trade-offs.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** What is the Reynolds number? With a neat sketch, explain how it classifies flow regimes. A microfluidic channel of rectangular cross-section ($w = 200\ \mu\text{m}$, $h = 50\ \mu\text{m}$) carries water at an average velocity of $5\ \text{mm/s}$. Density $\rho = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$, dynamic viscosity $\mu = 10^{-3}\ \text{Pa·s}$. Calculate the Reynolds number and comment on the flow regime. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the Hagen–Poiseuille equation for fully-developed laminar flow between two parallel plates separated by distance $h$, and compute the volumetric flow rate $Q$ if the pressure drop is $\Delta P = 5\ \text{kPa}$, channel width $w = 200\ \mu\text{m}$, height $h = 50\ \mu\text{m}$, length $L = 20\ \text{mm}$, and $\mu = 10^{-3}\ \text{Pa·s}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Reynolds Number & Flow Regime (7 marks):**

**Definition:** Reynolds number is the dimensionless ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in a fluid:

$$Re = \frac{\rho v L}{\mu}$$

**Flow Classification (with sketch description):**

```
Re < 1            → Creeping/Stokes flow (microfluidics, bio-MEMS)
1 < Re < ~100     → Laminar flow (typical microchannels)
~100 < Re < 2300  → Transition
Re > 2300         → Turbulent (rare in MEMS)
```

**Calculation:**
Hydraulic diameter for rectangular channel: $D_h = \dfrac{2wh}{w+h} = \dfrac{2 \cdot 200 \cdot 50}{250} = 80\ \mu\text{m} = 8 \times 10^{-5}$ m.

$$Re = \frac{1000 \times 5\times 10^{-3} \times 8 \times 10^{-5}}{10^{-3}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-3}} = 0.4$$

**Comment:** $Re = 0.4 \ll 1$ confirms **creeping flow**. Viscous forces overwhelmingly dominate. Flow is **linear, reversible, and Stokes-regime** — no turbulence, no mixing by advection. This is the ideal regime for **laminar, predictable, dose-controlled drug delivery**.

**(b) Hagen–Poiseuille Derivation & Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Start from the simplified steady-state Navier–Stokes between two parallel plates (no body force, fully developed):

$$\frac{dP}{dx} = \mu \frac{d^2 u}{dy^2}$$

**Step 2:** Integrate twice:

$$u(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx} y^2 + C_1 y + C_2$$

**Step 3:** Apply no-slip at $y = \pm h/2$: by symmetry $C_1 = 0$, and at $y = h/2$, $u = 0$:

$$0 = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\frac{h^2}{4} + C_2 \implies C_2 = -\frac{h^2}{8\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}$$

So: $u(y) = -\dfrac{1}{2\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dx}\left(\dfrac{h^2}{4} - y^2\right)$

**Step 4:** Volumetric flow rate per unit width:

$$\frac{Q}{w} = \int_{-h/2}^{h/2} u(y) dy = -\frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx} \cdot \frac{h^3}{6} = -\frac{h^3}{12\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}$$

**Step 5:** With $\dfrac{dP}{dx} = -\Delta P / L$:

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{w h^3 \Delta P}{12 \mu L}}$$

**Step 6:** Plug in numbers: $w = 200\times 10^{-6}$ m, $h = 50\times 10^{-6}$ m, $\Delta P = 5 \times 10^3$ Pa, $L = 20 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $\mu = 10^{-3}$ Pa·s.

$$Q = \frac{(200\times 10^{-6}) \cdot (50\times 10^{-6})^3 \cdot 5 \times 10^3}{12 \cdot 10^{-3} \cdot 20 \times 10^{-3}}$$

Numerator: $200\times 10^{-6} \cdot 1.25 \times 10^{-13} \cdot 5 \times 10^3 = 1.25 \times 10^{-13}$

Denominator: $12 \times 10^{-3} \times 20 \times 10^{-3} = 2.4 \times 10^{-4}$

$$Q = \frac{1.25 \times 10^{-13}}{2.4 \times 10^{-4}} = 5.21 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s} \approx 0.52\ \mu\text{L/s}$$

**Insight:** A 20-mm long, 50-µm tall microchannel delivers sub-microlitre flow rates — perfect for **drug delivery patches** and **microfluidic diagnostics**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2 (Electricity, Fluid Mechanics, Heat Transfer)**

### Electricity
- ☐ Capacitance of parallel plate: $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$
- ☐ Electrostatic force: $F_e = \tfrac{1}{2} V^2 (dC/dx)$; for gap actuator, $F_e = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2 / (2 d^2)$
- ☐ Pull-in voltage: $V_{PI} = \sqrt{8 k d_0^3 / (27 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A)}$; occurs at **1/3 of initial gap**
- ☐ Capacitive sensitivity: $\Delta C / C_0 = \Delta d / d_0$ (linearised)
- ☐ $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m — memorise
- ☐ Electrostatic force is **always attractive**, never repulsive

### Fluid Mechanics
- ☐ Reynolds number: $Re = \rho v L / \mu$; **MEMS is typically $Re < 100$**
- ☐ Hagen–Poiseuille (planar): $Q = w h^3 \Delta P / (12 \mu L)$
- ☐ Squeeze-film drag: $F_d = \mu A^2 \dot{x} / d^3$
- ☐ At low Re, **inertia is negligible** — Stokes equation applies
- ☐ Mixing in microfluidics requires special designs (T-mixers, chaotic mixers)
- ☐ Hydraulic diameter for rectangular duct: $D_h = 4A/P$

### Heat Transfer
- ☐ Fourier conduction: $Q = k A \Delta T / t$
- ☐ Newton cooling: $Q = h A (T_s - T_\infty)$
- ☐ Nusselt: $Nu = h L / k$
- ☐ Stefan-Boltzmann: $Q = \varepsilon \sigma A (T_s^4 - T_{\text{sur}}^4)$, $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴)
- ☐ Surface-area-to-volume ratio **increases** at small scales → heat dissipation is critical
- ☐ Low-$k$ dielectric layers (SiO₂, air gaps) provide **thermal isolation**

### Cross-Cutting Concepts
- ☐ MEMS scaling laws: area $\sim 1/S^2$, volume $\sim 1/S^3$, A/V $\sim S$
- ☐ Always identify which domain *dominates* — electric, fluid, or thermal
- ☐ Coupling is bidirectional: electrical actuation → mechanical motion → fluid drag → thermal dissipation
- ☐ For exam problems, **always state assumptions** explicitly (no-slip, incompressibility, isothermal, quasi-static)
- ☐ Conversion: 1 µm = $10^{-6}$ m; remember to convert **before** substituting into formulas
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Materials for MEMS and Microsystems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET836 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Materials for MEMS and Microsystems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are "Materials for MEMS"?

In the context of Micro Electro Mechanical Systems (MEMS), **materials** refer to the carefully selected **substrates, structural layers, functional layers, sacrificial layers, and packaging media** used to fabricate miniaturized mechanical and electrical components. Unlike conventional mechanical engineering — where bulk metals, alloys, and woods dominate — MEMS operates at the **micron to millimeter scale**, where material behavior is governed by surface forces, crystallography, thin-film physics, and quantum-scale effects rather than bulk macroscopic properties.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (OEMET836, Module 3):**
> *Materials for MEMS and Microsystems* covers the **substrates (Silicon, Quartz, Glass, Polymers)**, **additive/deposition materials (SiO₂, Si₃N₄, SiC, polysilicon)**, **functional materials (piezoceramics, shape memory alloys, magnetostrictive materials)**, and **metallization layers (Au, Al, Pt, Ti, Cr)** required for designing microsensors, microactuators, and integrated microsystems.

The choice of material in MEMS is **not arbitrary** — it dictates:
- Fabrication compatibility (e.g., CMOS-compatible processes)
- Mechanical performance (stiffness, fatigue life, residual stress)
- Thermal behavior (CTE mismatch, operating temperature)
- Electrical functionality (piezoelectric, piezoresistive, conductive)
- Biocompatibility (for biomedical MEMS)
- Cost and scalability

---

## 1.2 The Intuitive "House-Building" Analogy 🏠

Imagine you are an engineer tasked with building a **tiny house** that is only **1 mm wide** and must contain:
- **Walls** (structural rigidity)
- **Windows** (optical transparency)
- **Wires** (electrical conduction)
- **Springs** (mechanical flexibility)
- **Sensors** (pressure, temperature, chemical detection)

You cannot use **brick, steel beams, or copper wires** the same way you would in a full-scale house. Instead, you need:
- **Silicon** as the walls (semiconductor + mechanical strength)
- **Glass/Quartz** as the windows (optical clarity + thermal stability)
- **Gold/Aluminum thin films** as wires (high conductivity, easy deposition)
- **Polysilicon** as the springs (tunable Young's modulus)
- **PZT or BaTiO₃** as the sensors (piezoelectric response)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** MEMS materials are chosen based on a **multi-objective optimization** between mechanical, electrical, chemical, thermal, and biological properties — *not* on a single dominant criterion. A material that is mechanically excellent but not CMOS-compatible is essentially **useless** in standard MEMS fabrication.

---

## 1.3 The Four Pillars of MEMS Material Selection

A KTU 2024 examiner expects students to recognize that MEMS materials are categorized into **four functional pillars**:

| Pillar | Primary Role | Examples |
|--------|-------------|----------|
| **Substrate Materials** | Mechanical base / wafer | Silicon, Quartz, Glass, Polymers |
| **Structural Materials** | Active mechanical elements | Polysilicon, Single-crystal Si, SiC |
| **Functional Materials** | Sensing / actuation | PZT, BaTiO₃, Shape Memory Alloys (NiTi) |
| **Interconnect & Packaging** | Electrical routing / sealing | Au, Al, Cu, Ti, SU-8, Glass caps |

---

## 1.4 Why Silicon Dominates MEMS (The "Why" Question)

The single most important material in MEMS is **single-crystal Silicon (Si)**. The reasons are non-obvious to first-time learners:

1. **Near-perfect mechanical properties:** Young's modulus (~190 GPa) comparable to steel (~200 GPa), yet **3× lighter**.
2. **No mechanical hysteresis / No plasticity:** Silicon fractures elastically rather than deforming plastically — ideal for **fatigue-free** microsystems.
3. **Exceptional piezoresistivity:** The piezoresistive gauge factor of Si is **~100× higher** than metal strain gauges.
4. **CMOS compatibility:** Si integrates seamlessly with IC fabrication, enabling **on-chip electronics + mechanics**.
5. **Abundant & low cost:** Si is the second most abundant element in Earth's crust (after oxygen), extracted from **silica (SiO₂)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics to Remember:**
> - Young's modulus of Si: **E ≈ 190 GPa** (along ⟨111⟩ direction).
> - Density of Si: **ρ = 2.33 g/cm³**.
> - Fracture toughness of Si: **K_IC ≈ 0.7–1.0 MPa·m^(1/2)** (brittle, but predictable).
> - Piezoresistive gauge factor of Si: **π ≈ 50–175 × 10⁻¹¹ Pa⁻¹**.

---

## 1.5 A Brief Historical Note (Context for Examiners)

The dominance of Silicon in MEMS traces back to the **surface micromachining** revolution pioneered by **Kensall Wise (University of Michigan, 1980s)** and **bulk micromachining** innovations by **Kurt Petersen (1970s–1980s)**. The KTU syllabus implicitly assumes students understand that **MEMS evolved as a child of the IC industry** — hence the silicon-centric material palette.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of MEMS Materials

For KTU board-level clarity, materials are best classified into a **three-tier hierarchy**:

```
Tier 1: Substrate Materials
        ├── Single-crystal Silicon (Si)
        ├── Polycrystalline Silicon (Polysilicon)
        ├── Quartz (SiO₂ crystal)
        ├── Glass (Pyrex 7740, Borofloat 33)
        └── Polymers (SU-8, PDMS, PMMA, PI)

Tier 2: Functional / Active Materials
        ├── Piezoelectric Ceramics (PZT, BaTiO₃, ZnO, AlN)
        ├── Shape Memory Alloys (Nitinol — NiTi)
        ├── Magnetostrictive Materials (Terfenol-D)
        ├── Electroactive Polymers (EAPs)
        └── Thermoresponsive materials (bimorphs)

Tier 3: Interconnect / Packaging Materials
        ├── Metals (Au, Al, Cu, Pt, Ti, Cr, W)
        ├── Ceramics (Al₂O₃, SiC, Si₃N₄)
        ├── Encapsulants (SU-8, BCB, parylene-C)
        └── Bonding materials (glass frit, Au-Si eutectic, anodic)
```

---

## 2.2 Detailed Material Analysis — Tier 1: Substrates

### 2.2.1 Single-Crystal Silicon (Si)

Silicon possesses a **diamond cubic crystal structure** with lattice constant **a = 5.431 Å**. Each Si atom is covalently bonded to 4 neighbors in tetrahedral coordination.

**Critical Material Constants:**
- Atomic number Z = 14, atomic mass M = 28.086 g/mol
- Band gap E_g = **1.12 eV** (at 300 K)
- Intrinsic carrier concentration n_i ≈ **1.5 × 10¹⁰ cm⁻³** (at 300 K)
- Thermal conductivity k = **150 W/m·K**
- Coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) α ≈ **2.6 × 10⁻⁶ /°C**

> [!NOTE]
> **Crystal Orientation Matters in MEMS:**
> Silicon wafers are classified by their **surface orientation**:
> - (100) wafers → used for **bulk micromachining** with KOH/TMAH etchants.
> - (111) wafers → preferred for **piezoresistive sensors** due to high longitudinal gauge factor.
> - (110) wafers → enable **vertical sidewall micromachining** in advanced applications.

### 2.2.2 Quartz (SiO₂ Crystal)

Quartz is the **single-crystal polymorph of silicon dioxide** exhibiting **piezoelectricity**. AT-cut quartz is the industry standard for MEMS oscillators due to its near-zero temperature coefficient at room temperature.

### 2.2.3 Glass (Pyrex 7740)

Borosilicate glass (Corning Pyrex 7740) is widely used as a **substrate and anodic bonding partner** with silicon because its CTE (~3.3 × 10⁻⁶ /°C) closely matches that of silicon.

### 2.2.4 Polymers

| Polymer | Key Property | Typical Application |
|---------|-------------|---------------------|
| **SU-8** | Negative photoresist, very tall aspect ratios | Microfluidic channels, structural layers |
| **PDMS** | Elastomer, biocompatible | Soft lithography, biomedical MEMS |
| **PMMA** | Thermoplastic, optically transparent | X-ray lithography, optical MEMS |
| **Polyimide** | High thermal stability | Flexible electronics, dielectric layers |

---

## 2.3 Detailed Material Analysis — Tier 2: Functional Materials

### 2.3.1 Piezoelectric Materials

A piezoelectric material converts **mechanical stress ↔ electrical charge**. The fundamental constitutive equations (in Voigt notation) are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_i &= d_{ijk} \sigma_{jk} + \epsilon_{ij}^T E_j \\
\epsilon_{ij} &= s_{ij}^{E} \sigma_{jk} + d_{ijk} E_k
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $D_i$ = electric displacement (C/m²)
- $d_{ijk}$ = piezoelectric charge coefficient (C/N or m/V)
- $\sigma_{jk}$ = mechanical stress (Pa)
- $\epsilon_{ij}^T$ = permittivity under constant stress (F/m)
- $E_j$ = electric field (V/m)
- $s_{ij}^{E}$ = elastic compliance under constant electric field (1/Pa)

**Common Piezoelectric Materials in MEMS:**

| Material | d₃₃ (pC/N) | Curie Temp T_c (°C) | Use Case |
|----------|-----------|---------------------|----------|
| **PZT (lead zirconate titanate)** | 300–600 | 200–350 | High-force actuators, inkjet printheads |
| **BaTiO₃** | 190 | 120 | Capacitive sensors |
| **ZnO** | 12 | ~400 | SAW filters, thin-film sensors |
| **AlN (aluminum nitride)** | 5 | >1000 | CMOS-compatible RF MEMS |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why PZT Dominates Despite Toxicity:** PZT offers a **piezoelectric coefficient (d₃₃)** that is **~50× greater** than ZnO and ~100× greater than AlN. However, PZT contains **lead (Pb)** — RoHS-restricted in EU. Hence **AlN** is preferred for **CMOS-integrated** MEMS where toxicity and process compatibility matter.

### 2.3.2 Shape Memory Alloys (SMA) — Nitinol (NiTi)

Nitinol exhibits the **shape memory effect** and **superelasticity** via a **martensite ↔ austenite** phase transformation. It can recover strains up to **~8%** upon heating above its **transformation temperature** (typically 30–100 °C, tunable by Ni/Ti ratio).

### 2.3.3 Magnetostrictive Materials — Terfenol-D

Terfenol-D (Tb₀.₃Dy₀.₇Fe₂) produces strain under a magnetic field (magnetostriction λ ≈ **1000–2000 ppm**), used in **MEMS sonar transducers** and high-force microactuators.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Below is the complete formula arsenal needed to solve any MEMS-materials problem in KTU 2024 ESE.**

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---------|------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\sigma = E \cdot \epsilon$ | Hooke's Law (uniaxial) | Pa = GPa · (unitless) |
| 2 | $\sigma = F / A$ | Engineering stress | Pa |
| 3 | $\epsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ | Engineering strain | unitless |
| 4 | $G = E / 2(1 + \nu)$ | Shear modulus from E and Poisson ratio | Pa |
| 5 | $\nu = -\epsilon_{trans} / \epsilon_{axial}$ | Poisson's ratio | unitless |
| 6 | $\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_F \cdot \epsilon$ | Piezoresistive gauge equation | unitless |
| 7 | $G_F = 1 + 2\nu + \pi_l E$ | Longitudinal gauge factor for Si | unitless |
| 8 | $f_{res} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$ | Resonant frequency of cantilever | Hz |
| 9 | $k = \frac{E w t^3}{4 L^3}$ | Spring constant of cantilever | N/m |
| 10 | $\Delta f = f_0 \sqrt{1 + \frac{\Delta m}{m}}$ | Mass-loading frequency shift in resonator | Hz |
| 11 | $R_a = \frac{\rho L}{A}$ | Resistivity-based resistance (Ω) | Ω |
| 12 | $\sigma_{res} = \sigma_{thermal} = \frac{E}{1-\nu} \Delta T \cdot \Delta\alpha$ | Residual stress from CTE mismatch | Pa |
| 13 | $d = \frac{n \lambda}{2}$ | Bragg diffraction condition | m |
| 14 | $C = \frac{\epsilon_0 \epsilon_r A}{d}$ | Capacitance of parallel-plate | F |
| 15 | $F_{electrostatic} = \frac{1}{2} \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} V^2$ | Electrostatic actuation force | N |

> **CRITICAL:** In the table above, vertical bars (e.g., for **|stress|** or **|strain|**) are rendered as absolute magnitude notation, written in your exam answer as $\vert \sigma \vert$ or $\vert \epsilon \vert$, **not** as `|` which would break markdown.

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Where Each Material Class Shines

| Industry / Domain | Material Choice | Why |
|-------------------|-----------------|-----|
| **Automotive Airbag Accelerometers** | Single-crystal Si + polysilicon | Piezoresistive readout, bulk-micromachined proof mass |
| **Smartphone Gyroscopes** | Si + Au electrodes | Coriolis-force capacitive detection |
| **Inkjet Printheads** | PZT thin films on Si | High d₃₃ enables droplet ejection at kHz |
| **Biomedical Implants** | Ti, Parylene-C, PDMS | Biocompatibility + hermetic sealing |
| **RF Filters (BAW/SAW)** | AlN, ZnO on Si | CMOS-compatible piezoelectric thin films |
| **Optical MEMS (DLP)** | Si mirrors + Al reflectors | Low mass + high reflectivity |
| **Microfluidics** | PDMS, SU-8, Glass | Optical transparency + biocompatibility |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Piezoresistive Gauge Factor for Silicon

The piezoresistive effect is the **change in electrical resistivity of a semiconductor under mechanical strain**. This is **the** foundational relationship for MEMS pressure sensors and accelerometers.

### Step 1: Start from the Definition of Resistance

$$
R = \frac{\rho L}{A}
$$

Take the logarithmic differential (this is the standard perturbation approach):

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \frac{\Delta L}{L} - \frac{\Delta A}{A}
$$

### Step 2: Express Geometric Changes in Terms of Strain

For uniaxial strain $\epsilon$ along the current flow direction:

$$
\frac{\Delta L}{L} = \epsilon
$$

For a uniform cross-section under axial strain, lateral strain is $-\nu \epsilon$ (Poisson contraction). Therefore:

$$
\frac{\Delta A}{A} \approx 2 \cdot (-\nu \epsilon) = -2\nu \epsilon
$$

### Step 3: Substitute Geometric Contributions

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \epsilon + 2\nu \epsilon
$$

### Step 4: Introduce Piezoresistive Coefficient π

For semiconductors, the fractional resistivity change is dominated by the **piezoresistive coefficient** $\pi_l$ (longitudinal, in Pa⁻¹):

$$
\frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} = \pi_l \cdot \sigma = \pi_l \cdot E \cdot \epsilon
$$

### Step 5: Combine All Terms

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \pi_l E \epsilon + \epsilon + 2\nu \epsilon
$$

Factor out $\epsilon$:

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \left(1 + 2\nu + \pi_l E\right) \cdot \epsilon
$$

### Step 6: Define the Gauge Factor G_F

$$
G_F = 1 + 2\nu + \pi_l E
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_F \cdot \epsilon
$$

> **Numerical Example (p-type Si, ⟨111⟩ direction):**
> - $E \approx 190 \text{ GPa} = 1.9 \times 10^{11} \text{ Pa}$
> - $\pi_l \approx 100 \times 10^{-11} \text{ Pa}^{-1}$
> - $\nu \approx 0.28$
> - $\pi_l E \approx 1.9 \times 10^{11} \times 100 \times 10^{-11} = 190$
> - $1 + 2\nu = 1 + 0.56 = 1.56$
> - $G_F \approx 190 + 1.56 \approx 191.6$

This is **why Si gauge factors are 100× larger than metal strain gauges** (whose $G_F \approx 2$).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Resonant Frequency of a MEMS Cantilever Beam

A cantilever beam is the simplest MEMS mechanical structure. Deriving its resonance is essential for **resonant sensors** (mass sensors, gas sensors, biosensors).

### Step 1: Equation of Motion (Euler-Bernoulli Beam Theory)

$$
EI \frac{\partial^4 w(x,t)}{\partial x^4} + \rho A \frac{\partial^2 w(x,t)}{\partial t^2} = 0
$$

where $w(x,t)$ is the transverse deflection, $E$ is Young's modulus, $I$ is the second moment of area, $\rho$ is mass density, and $A$ is the cross-sectional area.

### Step 2: Apply Harmonic Assumption

Assume $w(x,t) = W(x) \cdot e^{j\omega t}$. Substitute into the equation:

$$
EI \frac{d^4 W(x)}{dx^4} - \rho A \omega^2 W(x) = 0
$$

Define $\beta^4 = \dfrac{\rho A \omega^2}{EI}$:

$$
\frac{d^4 W}{dx^4} - \beta^4 W = 0
$$

### Step 3: General Solution

$$
W(x) = C_1 \cos(\beta x) + C_2 \sin(\beta x) + C_3 \cosh(\beta x) + C_4 \sinh(\beta x)
$$

### Step 4: Apply Cantilever Boundary Conditions

For a cantilever of length $L$ fixed at $x=0$ and free at $x=L$:

- $W(0) = 0$ → $C_1 + C_3 = 0$
- $\dfrac{dW}{dx}\Big\vert_{x=0} = 0$ → $C_2 + C_4 = 0$
- $\dfrac{d^2 W}{dx^2}\Big\vert_{x=L} = 0$ (zero bending moment at free end)
- $\dfrac{d^3 W}{dx^3}\Big\vert_{x=L} = 0$ (zero shear force at free end)

### Step 5: Solve for Non-Trivial Constants

The transcendental equation obtained is:

$$
\cos(\beta L) \cosh(\beta L) = -1
$$

The smallest positive root gives $\beta_1 L \approx 1.875$.

### Step 6: Extract the Fundamental Frequency

$$
\omega_1 = \beta_1^2 \sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A}} = (1.875)^2 \sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A}}
$$

For a rectangular cantilever of width $w$, thickness $t$, length $L$:

$$
I = \frac{wt^3}{12}, \quad A = wt
$$

Therefore:

$$
f_1 = \frac{1.875^2}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{E t^2}{12 \rho L^4}} \approx 0.162 \frac{t}{L^2} \sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}}
$$

### Step 7: Express Spring Constant Equivalently

Since $\omega_1 = \sqrt{k/m}$:

$$
k = \frac{E w t^3}{4 L^3}, \quad m_{eff} = 0.24 \rho L w t
$$

> **Final Compact Form:**
>
> $$
> f_{res} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{E w t^3}{4 L^3 \cdot m_{eff}}}
> $$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Residual Stress from CTE Mismatch in Bimorph

When two materials with different coefficients of thermal expansion (CTE) are bonded and cooled, **residual stress** develops. This is critical for MEMS reliability.

### Step 1: Define Bimorph Geometry

A thin film of material B (CTE $\alpha_B$, thickness $t_B$, Young's modulus $E_B$) deposited on substrate A (CTE $\alpha_A$, much thicker).

### Step 2: Strain Mismatch

$$
\Delta \epsilon = (\alpha_A - \alpha_B) \cdot \Delta T
$$

### Step 3: Biaxial Stress in the Film (Plane Stress Approximation)

For a thin film constrained biaxially, the in-plane stress is:

$$
\sigma_{film} = \frac{E_B}{1 - \nu_B} \cdot \Delta \epsilon
$$

### Step 4: Substitute Strain Mismatch

$$
\sigma_{film} = \frac{E_B}{1 - \nu_B} \cdot (\alpha_A - \alpha_B) \cdot \Delta T
$$

> **Sign Convention:**
> - If $\alpha_B > \alpha_A$ and $\Delta T < 0$ (cooling from deposition), $\sigma_{film} > 0$ → **tensile stress** (film wants to contract but is held by substrate).
> - Conversely, if $\alpha_B < \alpha_A$, the film develops **compressive stress** → potential for **buckling** if stress exceeds critical value.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Material Selection Decision Framework

The **Ashby material selection methodology** (originally by Michael Ashby, Cambridge) is the gold standard for engineering material selection. Applied to MEMS, the relevant performance metrics are:

| Performance Metric (M) | Definition | Maximize / Minimize |
|------------------------|------------|---------------------|
| $\dfrac{E}{\rho}$ | Specific stiffness | **Maximize** |
| $\dfrac{\sigma_y}{\rho}$ | Specific strength | **Maximize** |
| $\dfrac{k}{\alpha}$ | Thermal stability index | **Maximize** |
| $\dfrac{d_{ij}}{\epsilon}$ | Piezoelectric figure of merit | **Maximize** |
| $\rho \cdot \alpha$ | Thermal distortion coefficient | **Minimize** |
| $\tan \delta$ | Mechanical loss (Q⁻¹) | **Minimize** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matrix Matters in KTU:** Questions on "justify material X for application Y" require students to **explicitly cite** at least two performance metrics from this matrix and compare numerical values for competing materials.

---

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Functional Architecture Flow of MEMS Material Classification

The following Mermaid block-diagram maps the **decision hierarchy** a MEMS designer follows when selecting materials, organized as a top-down functional flow rather than a physical layout.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeM1[Application Requirement] --> nodeM2{Function Type}
    nodeM2 -->|Sensing| nodeM3[Functional Material Layer]
    nodeM2 -->|Actuation| nodeM4[Active Material Layer]
    nodeM2 -->|Structural Support| nodeM5[Substrate Selection]

    nodeM3 --> nodeM6{Piezoresistive?}
    nodeM3 -->|Yes| nodeM7[Use Doped Single Crystal Silicon]
    nodeM3 -->|No| nodeM8{Piezoelectric?}
    nodeM8 -->|Yes| nodeM9[Use PZT or AlN Thin Film]
    nodeM8 -->|No| nodeM10[Use Capacitive Electrode Metal]

    nodeM4 --> nodeM11{Displacement Range?}
    nodeM4 -->|Small nm| nodeM12[Electrostatic: Si plus Au]
    nodeM4 -->|Medium um| nodeM13[Piezoelectric: PZT]
    nodeM4 -->|Large mm| nodeM14[Thermal: Shape Memory Alloy NiTi]

    nodeM5 --> nodeM15{Temperature Stability?}
    nodeM5 -->|Critical| nodeM16[Use Quartz or SOI]
    nodeM5 -->|Standard| nodeM17[Use Silicon Wafer]
    nodeM5 -->|Flexible| nodeM18[Use Polymer Substrate PDMS or PI]

    nodeM7 --> nodeM19[Selected: Doped Si]
    nodeM9 --> nodeM20[Selected: PZT Film]
    nodeM12 --> nodeM21[Selected: Si plus Au Electrodes]
    nodeM14 --> nodeM22[Selected: Nitinol SMA]
    nodeM16 --> nodeM23[Selected: Quartz Wafer]
    nodeM17 --> nodeM24[Selected: Si Wafer]
    nodeM18 --> nodeM25[Selected: PDMS Film]
```

> **Reading the Flow:**
> - The diagram captures the **decision tree** a MEMS process engineer traverses.
> - Notice the **branching at "Piezoelectric?"** — this is where most KTU examiners probe student understanding (e.g., "Why AlN over PZT for a CMOS-integrated sensor?").

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Functional Architecture of a MEMS Pressure Sensor Cross-Section

The following Mermaid block-diagram illustrates the **material layering** in a typical piezoresistive MEMS pressure sensor die, viewed as a functional cross-section rather than a literal scale drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeL1[Top Bonded Glass Cap Layer] --> nodeL2[Sealing Cavity at Vacuum Reference]
    nodeL2 --> nodeL3[Silicon Diaphragm Active Layer]
    nodeL3 --> nodeL4[Boron Doped Piezoresistor Layer]
    nodeL4 --> nodeL5[Silicon Dioxide Insulation Layer]
    nodeL5 --> nodeL6[Silicon Handle Wafer Base Layer]
    nodeL6 --> nodeL7[Bottom Contact Metal Layer]
    nodeL7 --> nodeL8[External Wire Bond Pad Layer]

    nodeL1 -.Anodic Bonding.-> nodeL2
    nodeL3 -.Anisotropic KOH Etching.-> nodeL4
    nodeL4 -.LPCVD Deposition.-> nodeL5
    nodeL5 -.Thermal Oxidation.-> nodeL6
    nodeL6 -.Sputtering of Aluminum.-> nodeL7
```

> **Material-to-Layer Mapping:**
> - **Top Glass Cap**: Pyrex 7740 (anodic bonding partner with Si).
> - **Diaphragm**: Single-crystal Si (thickness 10–50 µm after KOH etching).
> - **Piezoresistor**: Boron-diffused p-type Si (gauge factor ~100).
> - **Insulation**: Thermally grown SiO₂ (1 µm, 1000 °C wet oxidation).
> - **Handle Wafer**: Bulk Si (400–500 µm, supports the thin diaphragm).
> - **Bond Pad**: Al (1 µm, sputtered) or Au (for wire bonding).

# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: List any three key reasons why single-crystal silicon is the dominant substrate material in MEMS fabrication.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Superior mechanical properties:** Single-crystal Si exhibits a Young's modulus of **~190 GPa**, comparable to steel, yet with **3× lower density** (2.33 g/cm³). It fails in **brittle (elastic) mode** without plastic deformation, ensuring **fatigue-free** long-term operation.

2. **Exceptional piezoresistivity:** The piezoresistive gauge factor of doped Si is **~100**, compared to ~2 for metals, enabling highly sensitive strain/pressure sensors with simple readout electronics.

3. **CMOS process compatibility:** Si integrates seamlessly with existing IC fabrication infrastructure (oxidation, lithography, doping, etching), allowing **monolithic integration** of MEMS + signal conditioning circuitry on a single chip.

**(Optional 4th mark-earning point):** Abundance and low cost — Si is the second most abundant element in Earth's crust, extracted from silica (SiO₂) at industrial scale.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between piezoelectric and piezoresistive effects. Give one example MEMS material for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Piezoelectric Effect | Piezoresistive Effect |
|--------|----------------------|------------------------|
| **Definition** | Generation of electric charge (or voltage) under mechanical stress | Change in electrical resistivity under mechanical stress |
| **Nature of Output** | Voltage / charge (no current required) | Resistance change (current-driven readout) |
| **Mechanism** | Non-centrosymmetric crystal lattice (dipole reorientation) | Band-structure modification under strain |
| **Material Example** | **PZT, AlN, ZnO, Quartz** | **Doped single-crystal Silicon** |
| **Typical Application** | Microactuators, SAW filters, inkjet printheads | Pressure sensors, accelerometers, strain gauges |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

#### (a) Classify the materials used in MEMS and Microsystems. Discuss in detail the properties and applications of **silicon** and **piezoelectric materials** as MEMS materials. **[7 marks]**

#### (b) Explain the **Czochralski process** for silicon wafer preparation. Discuss the role of crystal orientation (100), (110), (111) in MEMS fabrication. **[7 marks]**

---

##### ✅ Model Solution for (a):

**Classification of MEMS Materials (3 marks):**

Materials for MEMS are classified into **four broad categories**:

1. **Substrate Materials:** Single-crystal Si, polysilicon, SOI, quartz, glass (Pyrex 7740), polymers (SU-8, PDMS, PMMA, polyimide).
2. **Structural / Functional Materials:** Polysilicon, SiC, Si₃N₄, single-crystal Si.
3. **Active / Smart Materials:** Piezoelectric (PZT, AlN, ZnO, BaTiO₃), shape memory alloys (NiTi / Nitinol), magnetostrictive (Terfenol-D), electroactive polymers.
4. **Interconnect and Packaging Materials:** Au, Al, Cu, Pt, Ti, Cr, parylene-C, glass frit, eutectic Au-Si.

**Silicon — Properties and Applications (2 marks):**

- Diamond cubic crystal, lattice constant a = 5.431 Å, band gap 1.12 eV.
- Young's modulus ≈ **190 GPa**, density 2.33 g/cm³, CTE 2.6 × 10⁻⁶ /°C, thermal conductivity 150 W/m·K.
- **Applications:** Piezoresistive pressure sensors, capacitive accelerometers, gyroscopes, inkjet printheads (with PZT), microfluidic channels (with glass), micromirror arrays (DLP).

**Piezoelectric Materials — Properties and Applications (2 marks):**

- Exhibit direct (mechanical → electrical) and converse (electrical → mechanical) piezoelectric effects.
- PZT (d₃₃ ≈ 300–600 pC/N, T_c ≈ 200–350 °C) — high-force actuators, inkjet printheads, ultrasonic transducers.
- AlN (d₃₃ ≈ 5 pC/N, T_c > 1000 °C) — CMOS-compatible RF MEMS, BAW/SAW filters.
- ZnO — thin-film piezoelectric sensors, SAW devices.
- Quartz — oscillators, gyroscopes (rate sensors).

---

##### ✅ Model Solution for (b):

**Czochralski Process (4 marks):**

The Czochralski (CZ) process is the dominant industrial method for producing **single-crystal silicon ingots** used in MEMS and IC fabrication.

**Step-by-Step Process:**

1. **Crucible Loading:** High-purity polysilicon chunks are loaded into a **quartz crucible** inside a CZ puller chamber.
2. **Melting:** The polysilicon is heated to **~1420 °C** (above Si melting point of 1414 °C) using RF induction heating.
3. **Doping:** Trace amounts of dopants (B, P, As, Sb) are added to control n-type or p-type conductivity and resistivity.
4. **Seed Introduction:** A small, precisely oriented **single-crystal silicon seed** (typically (100) or (111)) is dipped into the melt.
5. **Crystal Pulling:** The seed is slowly withdrawn upward while simultaneously rotating. The melt **solidifies** at the seed–melt interface, replicating the seed's crystal orientation.
6. **Neck and Crown Formation:** A thin "neck" is first grown to eliminate dislocations, followed by a "crown" that expands to the desired diameter.
7. **Body Growth:** The main cylindrical body of the ingot is grown at a controlled pull rate (~1–2 mm/min) to maintain **single-crystallinity**.
8. **Tail and Cool-Down:** After reaching target length, the ingot tapers to a tail, then is slowly cooled to avoid thermal shock.

The resulting ingot is then **sliced into wafers** (typically 200 mm, 300 mm, or 450 mm diameter) using a **diamond wire saw**, followed by **lapping, etching, and polishing** to produce prime-grade wafers.

**Role of Crystal Orientation in MEMS (3 marks):**

| Orientation | Etching Behavior in KOH/TMAH | Typical MEMS Application |
|-------------|-------------------------------|---------------------------|
| **(100)** | Fast etching of (100) planes; exposes (111) sidewalls at 54.74° | **Bulk micromachined diaphragms, V-grooves, inkjet nozzles** |
| **(110)** | Reveals vertical (111) sidewalls perpendicular to surface | **Vertical comb-drive actuators, microchannels** |
| **(111)** | Very slow etch rate (highest atom density) | **Stop-etch layers, piezoresistors** (high longitudinal gauge factor along ⟨111⟩ direction) |

> **Examiner Tip:** When answering crystal-orientation questions, **always draw a small sketch** showing the etch-pit profile for (100) vs. (111) — this earns you the "presentation mark" that KTU examiners reserve for visualization.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### (a) Explain the piezoresistive effect in silicon. Derive the expression for the gauge factor of a piezoresistor. **[7 marks]**

#### (b) Discuss the properties, advantages, and applications of **polymers (SU-8, PDMS, PMMA)** and **metals (Au, Al, Pt, Ti)** as MEMS materials. **[7 marks]**

---

##### ✅ Model Solution for (a):

**Piezoresistive Effect in Silicon (3 marks):**

The piezoresistive effect is the change in electrical resistivity of a semiconductor when subjected to mechanical strain. In silicon, this effect arises because applied stress modifies the **energy band structure**, altering the **effective mass** and **mobility** of charge carriers.

Unlike metals (where the piezoresistive effect is small, gauge factor ~2), silicon exhibits **gauge factors 50–100× higher** due to its anisotropic band structure.

**Derivation of Gauge Factor (4 marks):**

*Step 1:* Start with the resistance equation $R = \rho L / A$.

*Step 2:* Logarithmic differentiation:
$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \frac{\Delta L}{L} - \frac{\Delta A}{A}$$

*Step 3:* For uniaxial strain $\epsilon$, geometric terms yield:
$$\frac{\Delta L}{L} = \epsilon, \quad \frac{\Delta A}{A} = -2\nu \epsilon$$

*Step 4:* The piezoresistive contribution is:
$$\frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} = \pi_l \cdot \sigma = \pi_l E \epsilon$$

*Step 5:* Combining:
$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \left(1 + 2\nu + \pi_l E\right) \epsilon$$

*Step 6:* Defining gauge factor:
$$G_F = 1 + 2\nu + \pi_l E$$

For p-type Si along ⟨111⟩, with $\pi_l \approx 100 \times 10^{-11}$ Pa⁻¹, $E \approx 190$ GPa, $\nu \approx 0.28$:

$$G_F \approx 1.56 + 190 \approx 191.56$$

> **Examination Note:** Show **every algebraic step explicitly**. Examiners at KTU deduct marks for skipping the substitution step.

---

##### ✅ Model Solution for (b):

**Polymers in MEMS (3.5 marks):**

| Polymer | Key Property | Application |
|---------|-------------|-------------|
| **SU-8** | Negative photoresist; aspect ratio > 20:1; optically transparent; chemically resistant | Microfluidic channel walls, structural layers, bio-MEMS |
| **PDMS** | Elastomer (E ≈ 1 MPa); biocompatible; gas-permeable; transparent (UV-VIS) | Soft lithography, organ-on-chip, microvalves |
| **PMMA** | Thermoplastic; transparent; low cost; X-ray sensitive | X-ray lithography, optofluidic devices, lab-on-chip |

**Advantages of Polymers:**
- Low cost, rapid prototyping.
- Compatible with biological samples (biocompatibility).
- Easy bonding (plasma treatment for PDMS-PDMS bonding).

**Disadvantages:**
- Low Young's modulus limits mechanical stiffness.
- Poor thermal stability (most decompose below 250 °C).
- High CTE leads to large thermal drift.

**Metals in MEMS (3.5 marks):**

| Metal | Key Property | Application |
|-------|-------------|-------------|
| **Au (Gold)** | Excellent conductivity (4.1 × 10⁷ S/m), inert, biocompatible | Bonding pads, electrochemical biosensors, RF MEMS electrodes |
| **Al (Aluminum)** | Low resistivity (3.5 × 10⁷ S/m), low cost, CMOS-compatible | IC interconnects, reflective coatings in micromirrors |
| **Pt (Platinum)** | High melting point (1768 °C), chemically inert | High-temperature electrodes, catalytic gas sensors |
| **Ti (Titanium)** | Excellent adhesion to Si/SiO₂, biocompatible | Adhesion layer between Au and Si, biomedical implants |

**Advantages of Metals:**
- High electrical and thermal conductivity.
- Ductility allows wire bonding.
- Provide reflective surfaces for optical MEMS.

**Disadvantages:**
- CTE mismatch with Si can cause film delamination.
- Electromigration in Al at high current densities.
- High cost (Au, Pt).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks on Materials-for-MEMS Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Piezoresistive and Piezoelectric effects.** Memorize the distinction: *piezoresistive = resistance change*, *piezoelectric = charge/voltage generation*.
> 2. **Forgetting units in derivations.** Always write units (Pa, N/m, Hz) at every step of a derivation.
> 3. **Stating "silicon has good mechanical properties" without numbers.** KTU examiners expect **numerical values**: $E \approx 190$ GPa, $\rho = 2.33$ g/cm³, $G_F \approx 100$–$200$.
> 4. **Omitting the "why" in crystal orientation questions.** Do not just say "(100) wafers are used in bulk micromachining." Instead: *"(100) wafers are preferred because the (100) plane etches ~400× faster than (111) in KOH, enabling controlled diaphragm formation."*
> 5. **Skipping the gauge factor derivation step where $\pi_l E$ dominates.** Many students write $G_F = 1 + 2\nu + \pi_l E$ but fail to numerically evaluate the dominant term ($\pi_l E \approx 190$) vs. the negligible geometric term ($1 + 2\nu \approx 1.56$). **Always quantify.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅

### 🚀 Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **Single-crystal Si** is the workhorse of MEMS due to its **high Young's modulus (~190 GPa)**, **exceptional piezoresistivity ($G_F \approx 100$–200)**, and **CMOS compatibility**.
- [x] **Czochralski process** produces single-crystal Si ingots via seed-pulling from a 1420 °C melt; the resulting wafers are sliced along **(100)**, **(110)**, or **(111)** orientations.
- [x] **Crystal orientation matters:** (100) → bulk micromachining; (110) → vertical sidewalls; (111) → high piezoresistive gauge factor.
- [x] **Piezoresistive gauge factor** $G_F = 1 + 2\nu + \pi_l E$, where the $\pi_l E$ term **dominates** for semiconductors.
- [x] **Piezoelectric materials** (PZT, AlN, ZnO, BaTiO₃, Quartz) convert mechanical stress ↔ electrical charge; governed by constitutive equations with coefficients $d_{ijk}$.
- [x] **AlN is preferred for CMOS-integrated RF MEMS** due to its compatibility and lead-free nature; **PZT** offers the highest $d_{33}$ but contains toxic lead.
- [x] **Quartz (AT-cut)** is preferred for **precision oscillators** due to near-zero temperature coefficient at room temperature.
- [x] **Glass (Pyrex 7740)** is the standard anodic bonding partner for silicon due to closely matched CTE.
- [x] **Polymers** (SU-8, PDMS, PMMA) offer low cost, biocompatibility, and rapid prototyping but have **low stiffness** and **poor thermal stability**.
- [x] **Metals** (Au, Al, Pt, Ti, Cu) provide electrical interconnects; Ti is the **adhesion layer** of choice, Au is the **biocompatible conductor**, Al is **CMOS-compatible**, Pt is **high-temperature stable**.
- [x] **Shape memory alloys** (Nitinol / NiTi) can recover strains up to **~8%** and are used in **microgrippers** and **microvalves**.
- [x] **Magnetostrictive materials** (Terfenol-D) produce strain under a magnetic field with $\lambda \approx 1000$–$2000$ ppm — used in **sonar and ultrasound MEMS**.
- [x] **Residual stress** in thin films arises from **CTE mismatch** during cooling: $\sigma_{film} = \frac{E_B}{1 - \nu_B} (\alpha_A - \alpha_B) \Delta T$.
- [x] **Cantilever resonant frequency** $f_1 \approx 0.162 \frac{t}{L^2} \sqrt{E/\rho}$.
- [x] **Material selection** follows the **Ashby methodology** based on performance metrics like $E/\rho$, $\sigma_y/\rho$, and $d_{ij}/\epsilon$.
- [x] **Smart materials** (PZT, NiTi, Terfenol-D, hydrogel) form the active layer in **adaptive MEMS** and **microactuators**.

### 🎯 Final Memory Hook

> *"In MEMS, **materials are not chosen — they are constrained** by CMOS compatibility, thermal budget, and fabrication route. A material that cannot survive 400 °C and anisotropic wet etching is, in practice, **invisible** to the MEMS engineer."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fabrication Technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET836 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fabrication Technologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are MEMS Fabrication Technologies?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> MEMS Fabrication Technologies refer to the systematic set of micro-scale manufacturing processes used to build mechanical and electro-mechanical components (typically ranging from **1 µm to 1 mm**) by sequentially adding, removing, and shaping materials on a substrate (usually a silicon wafer). These processes are borrowed from, and extensions of, the semiconductor integrated circuit (IC) manufacturing industry, forming the physical backbone of any Micro-Electro-Mechanical System.

In the simplest terms, MEMS fabrication is the art of **building tiny machines using the same tools we use to build computer chips** — photolithography, etching, deposition, doping, and bonding.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Sandwich & Cookie Cutter" Method:**
> Imagine you are baking a 3-layer sandwich cookie at microscopic scale:
> - The **silicon wafer** is your kitchen counter (the substrate).
> - **Photolithography** is like a stencil/cookie-cutter — you shine light through a patterned mask to draw shapes.
> - **Etching** is like cutting dough — it physically removes material where the stencil said "cut here."
> - **Deposition** is like spreading peanut butter — you add a thin new layer on top.
> - **Bonding** is like pressing two cookies together to make a sandwich.
> - **Doping** is like injecting flavor (impurity atoms) into specific regions to change electrical behavior.
>
> Just as a baker repeats steps — spread, cut, bake, layer — MEMS fabrication repeats cycles of **deposit → pattern → etch → release** to build complex 3D microstructures like cantilevers, membranes, gears, and channels.

## 1.3 Why is Fabrication Important in MEMS?

Without fabrication technologies, MEMS devices remain theoretical. The choice of fabrication process directly determines:
- **Geometry** (shape, size, depth of features)
- **Material properties** (residual stress, hardness, conductivity)
- **Device performance** (resonance frequency, sensitivity, Q-factor)
- **Cost and scalability** (wafer-level batch production = cheaper per device)

## 1.4 Major Categories of MEMS Fabrication

| Category | Core Idea | Typical Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Bulk Micromachining** | Etch *into* the substrate itself | Membranes, cantilevers, channels |
| **Surface Micromachining** | Build *on top of* the substrate | Beams, plates, suspended structures |
| **LIGA Process** | Deep X-ray lithography + electroplating | Tall metal microstructures |
| **Bonding Techniques** | Join two wafers permanently | Sealed cavities, 3D stacks |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 3 Focus:**
> The OEMET836 Module 3 specifically emphasizes: (a) Photolithography, (b) Wet & Dry Etching, (c) CVD/PVD deposition, (d) Bulk and Surface Micromachining, (e) LIGA, and (f) Wafer Bonding. Each of these is treated in the sections that follow.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Photolithography — The Pattern Transfer Process

Photolithography is the foundational process that defines geometric patterns on a substrate using light.

### Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Wafer Cleaning** — Removes organic/inorganic contaminants using RCA-1 (NH₄OH + H₂O₂) and RCA-2 (HCl + H₂O₂) solutions.
2. **Dehydration Bake** — Removes moisture at ~150°C to improve photoresist adhesion.
3. **Priming (HMDS coating)** — Hexamethyldisilazane is applied to make the wafer hydrophobic, improving resist wetting.
4. **Photoresist Spin Coating** — A liquid polymer (resist) is dropped and spun at high RPM to form a uniform thin film.
5. **Soft Bake** — Evaporates solvent from the resist (~90°C for 60 sec).
6. **Exposure** — UV light is projected through a **photomask** onto the resist.
   - **Positive resist**: Exposed regions become soluble (removed in developer).
   - **Negative resist**: Exposed regions become cross-linked (insoluble).
7. **Post-Exposure Bake (PEB)** — Reduces standing-wave effects.
8. **Development** — Wafer is dipped in developer (TMAH or NaOH-based) to dissolve exposed/unexposed regions.
9. **Hard Bake** — Hardens the remaining resist for etch resistance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Why" Matters:**
> Resolution of photolithography is fundamentally limited by the wavelength of light used. Shorter wavelengths can pattern finer features.

## 2.2 Resolution Formula (Rayleigh Criterion)

The minimum feature size resolvable by an optical lithography system is given by:

$$R = k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA}$$

Where:
- $R$ = minimum resolvable feature size (µm)
- $k_1$ = process constant (typically 0.4–0.8)
- $\lambda$ = wavelength of exposure light (nm)
- $NA$ = numerical aperture of the lens

The **Depth of Focus (DOF)** is given by:

$$DOF = k_2 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> Reducing $\lambda$ (e.g., moving from 365 nm i-line to 193 nm ArF excimer laser) allows smaller transistor gates and finer MEMS features — driving Moore's Law.

## 2.3 Etching Technologies

Etching selectively removes material not protected by the photoresist mask.

### 2.3.1 Wet Etching
- **Isotropic wet etching**: Etches equally in all directions (e.g., HNA — Hydrofluoric + Nitric + Acetic acid for Si).
- **Anisotropic wet etching**: Etches at different rates depending on crystallographic orientation.

For silicon in KOH (Potassium Hydroxide):

$$Etch\ Rate\ Ratio:\ \frac{(100)}{(111)} \approx 100:1$$

This means (111) planes etch ~100 times slower than (100) planes, enabling precise pyramidal cavities, V-grooves, and pits.

$$\text{V-Groove depth:}\ d = \frac{w}{\sqrt{2}}$$

Where $w$ = mask opening width.

### 2.3.2 Dry Etching
- **RIE (Reactive Ion Etching)**: Combines chemical (reactive radicals) and physical (ion bombardment) mechanisms.
- **DRIE (Deep Reactive Ion Etching)**: Alternates etch (SF₆) and passivation (C₄F₈) steps to achieve very high aspect ratios (up to **50:1**), known as the **BOSCH process**.

$$\text{Bosch Process Cycle:}\ T_{cycle} = T_{etch} + T_{passivation}$$

$$\text{Aspect Ratio} = \frac{Depth}{Width}$$

## 2.4 Deposition Technologies

| Technique | Mechanism | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Thermal Oxidation** | Si + O₂ → SiO₂ | Gate oxide, insulating layer |
| **CVD (Chemical Vapor Deposition)** | Gas-phase chemical reaction deposits solid film | Polysilicon, SiO₂, Si₃N₄ |
| **LPCVD (Low-Pressure CVD)** | Lower pressure → better uniformity | Polysilicon beams |
| **PECVD (Plasma-Enhanced CVD)** | Plasma activates reaction at lower temperature | Insulating layers over metals |
| **PVD (Physical Vapor Deposition)** | Physical ejection of atoms from target | Aluminum, gold metallization |
| **Sputtering** | Argon ion bombardment ejects target atoms | Metal films |
| **Evaporation** | Thermal heating vaporizes source material | Aluminum, gold (e-beam) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:**
> CVD gives conformal step coverage (uniform coating over steps), while PVD gives directional coating — useful for "lift-off" patterning.

## 2.5 Bulk Micromachining

Bulk micromachining selectively removes significant portions of the silicon substrate to form structures such as membranes, cantilevers, and channels.

$$\text{Membrane thickness:}\ t_m = t_{wafer} - d_{etch}$$

Where $d_{etch}$ is the depth etched from the back side.

**Etch Stop Techniques:**
- **Boron etch stop**: Heavy boron doping ($>5\times10^{19}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$) virtually stops KOH etching.
- **Electrochemical etch stop**: Reverse-biased p-n junction stops etching at the junction.

## 2.6 Surface Micromachining

Surface micromachining builds mechanical structures by depositing and selectively removing **sacrificial layers**.

$$\text{Sacrificial layer thickness} = \text{Gap height under released structure}$$

Typical process flow:
1. Deposit **sacrificial layer** (e.g., SiO₂).
2. Deposit **structural layer** (e.g., polysilicon).
3. Pattern structural layer.
4. **Release** — etch away sacrificial layer using HF (hydrofluoric acid), leaving the structure free-standing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Stiction Alert:**
> After sacrificial etching, capillary forces during drying can pull microstructures down and stick them to the substrate permanently. Critical point drying or sublimation drying is used to mitigate this.

## 2.7 LIGA Process

**LIGA** is a German acronym for **L**ithographie, **G**alvanoformung (electroforming), **A**bformung (molding).

Steps:
1. **Deep X-ray Lithography** — Synchrotron radiation exposes a thick PMMA resist.
2. **Development** — Removes exposed PMMA, leaving deep mold cavities.
3. **Electroforming** — Fills the mold with metal (typically nickel) via electroplating.
4. **Molding** — Replica is used as a mold insert for plastic injection molding of micro parts.

$$\text{LIGA aspect ratio} > 100$$

Achievable structural heights: **hundreds of microns to millimeters** — far deeper than optical lithography allows.

## 2.8 Wafer Bonding

| Type | Mechanism | Typical Temperature |
|---|---|---|
| **Anodic Bonding** | Glass-Si, high voltage (~1000 V) and heat | ~400°C |
| **Fusion Bonding** | Direct Si-Si bonding, surface-activated | ~1000°C |
| **Eutectic Bonding** | Au-Si eutectic (~363°C) | ~400°C |
| **Adhesive Bonding** | Intermediate polymer layer (e.g., SU-8, BCB) | <250°C |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bonding Utility:** Used to fabricate sealed cavities (pressure sensors), 3D stacks, and packaging for MEMS devices.

## 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $R = k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA}$ | Rayleigh resolution limit | µm |
| $DOF = k_2 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA^2}$ | Depth of focus | µm |
| $\text{Etch\ Selectivity} = \frac{E_{film}}{E_{substrate}}$ | Ratio of etch rates | dimensionless |
| $\text{Aspect\ Ratio} = \frac{D}{W}$ | Depth over width | dimensionless |
| $d_{V-groove} = \frac{w}{\sqrt{2}}$ | Anisotropic KOH etch depth | µm |
| $\text{Deposition\ Rate} = \frac{m}{\rho \cdot A \cdot t}$ | Mass per unit area per time | nm/s |
| $\text{Boron\ stop\ at}\ N_B > 5\times10^{19}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$ | Etch stop concentration | cm⁻³ |
| $\text{Stiction\ Force}\ F = \frac{12\pi \eta \gamma L^2}{g^2}$ | Capillary adhesion force | N |
| $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$ | Cantilever resonance | Hz |
| $\sigma = \frac{F}{A}$ | Stress in thin film | Pa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the Rayleigh formula and the V-groove depth formula** — they are extremely high-yield for KTU board exams.

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pressure Sensors**: Bulk micromachined silicon diaphragm + piezoresistive elements.
- **Accelerometers (ADXL series)**: Surface micromachined proof mass + capacitive sensing.
- **Inkjet Printers**: Nozzle arrays made by deep RIE / anisotropic wet etching.
- **Gyroscopes**: Comb-drive structures built via surface micromachining.
- **Bio-MEMS**: LIGA-fabricated microchannels for lab-on-a-chip.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: V-Groove Depth in (100) Silicon Anisotropic Etching

**Problem Setup:**
A square window of width $w$ is opened on a (100)-oriented silicon wafer and etched in KOH. Because the (111) planes etch ~100× slower than (100) planes, the etch self-terminates along four (111) planes forming a pyramidal V-groove.

**Step 1 — Geometry:**
The four (111) planes intersect the (100) surface along the edges of the square window. The angle between the (100) plane and a (111) plane is $\theta = 54.74°$.

**Step 2 — Trigonometric Relationship:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Tan}(\theta) &= \frac{d}{\frac{w}{2}} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Here $d$ = etch depth, $w/2$ = half-width of mask opening.

**Step 3 — Substitute Known Angle Value:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan(54.74°) &\approx 1.0 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \frac{w}{2} \cdot \tan(54.74°) \\
d &= \frac{w}{2} \cdot 1 \\
d &= \frac{w}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — let me be precise. The correct geometry gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \frac{w}{2\sqrt{2}} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Actually, the **standard KTU board result** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \frac{w}{2} \cdot \tan(54.74°) \\
&= \frac{w}{2\sqrt{2}} \cdot \text{(correction factor)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Let me derive cleanly using the angle between (100) and (111):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan(\theta) &= \frac{w/2}{d} \\
\Rightarrow d &= \frac{w/2}{\tan(\theta)} \\
\theta &= 54.74°, \quad \tan(54.74°) = \sqrt{2} \\
\Rightarrow d &= \frac{w}{2\sqrt{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

So the **final, frequently-quoted, simplified form** is:

$$
d \approx \frac{w}{2\sqrt{2}} \quad \text{or equivalently}\quad d = \frac{w}{2}\cot(54.74°)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** For a mask opening wider than $w_{max} = \sqrt{2}\cdot t_{wafer}$, the V-groove punches through the wafer entirely — this sets the maximum etchable opening for a given wafer thickness.

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Achievable Aspect Ratio in DRIE (Bosch Process)

**Step 1 — Define the Bosch Cycle:**

$$
T_{total} = N_{cycles} \cdot T_{cycle}
$$

Where $N_{cycles}$ = number of etch-passivation cycles.

**Step 2 — Depth per Cycle:**
Each cycle removes approximately a fixed depth $\Delta d$ (typically 0.5–2 µm).

$$
D = N_{cycles} \cdot \Delta d
$$

**Step 3 — Scallop Formation:**
Due to cyclic switching, sidewalls exhibit a characteristic **scallop pattern** with amplitude $S$:

$$
S = \frac{\Delta d}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Aspect Ratio:**

$$
AR = \frac{D}{W} = \frac{N_{cycles} \cdot \Delta d}{W}
$$

For very narrow trenches ($W \to 0$), $AR \to \infty$, but practical limits (~50:1) arise from:
- Aspect Ratio Dependent Etching (ARDE)
- Ion shadowing at high $AR$
- RIE lag

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Etching Process Comparison

| Parameter | Wet Isotropic | Wet Anisotropic (KOH) | RIE | DRIE (Bosch) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Selectivity to SiO₂** | Low | Very high | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Directionality** | Isotropic | Crystallographic | Anisotropic | Highly anisotropic |
| **Aspect Ratio** | ~1:1 | ~1:1 | ~5:1 | **~50:1** |
| **Mask material** | Si₃N₄, Au | SiO₂, Si₃N₄ | Resist, SiO₂ | Resist, SiO₂ |
| **Sidewall profile** | Rounded | Angled (54.74°) | Vertical | Vertical + scallops |
| **Throughput** | High (batch) | High (batch) | Low (single) | Low (single) |
| **CMOS compatible** | Limited | Poor (K⁺ contamination) | Excellent | Excellent |

## 3.4 Stress & Deflection in Surface Micromachined Cantilever

A polysilicon cantilever of length $L$, width $b$, thickness $t$, with residual stress $\sigma$:

**Stiffness:**

$$
k = \frac{E \cdot b \cdot t^3}{4 L^3}
$$

Where $E$ = Young's modulus (polysilicon ≈ **160 GPa**).

**Residual stress-induced deflection:**

$$
\delta_{stress} = \frac{\sigma \cdot L^2}{2 E \cdot t}
$$

**Resonance frequency:**

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m_{eff}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{E \cdot t^2}{12 \rho L^4}}
$$

Where $\rho$ = density of structural material (polysilicon ≈ **2330 kg/m³**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Residual stress in surface micromachined films (typically 100 MPa – 1 GPa) is a major design concern — it can bow cantilevers and buckle plates.

## 3.5 Stiction Force Derivation (Capillary Model)

The capillary force pulling a microstructure toward the substrate after wet release:

$$
F_{stiction} = \frac{12 \pi \eta \gamma L \cos\theta_c}{g^2}
$$

Where:
- $\eta$ = perimeter correction factor
- $\gamma$ = surface tension of liquid (~72 mN/m for water)
- $L$ = length of contact
- $\theta_c$ = contact angle
- $g$ = gap between structure and substrate

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Stiction is the **#1 yield-killer** in surface micromachining. Reducing $L$ and increasing $g$ are key mitigations.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — MEMS Fabrication Process Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[MEMS FABRICATION TECHNOLOGIES] --> nodeB[PATTERNING]
    nodeA --> nodeC[MATERIAL MODIFICATION]
    nodeA --> nodeD[MATERIAL REMOVAL]
    nodeA --> nodeE[MATERIAL ADDITION]
    nodeA --> nodeF[ASSEMBLY]

    nodeB --> nodeB1[Photolithography]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[Electron Beam Lithography]
    nodeB --> nodeB3[X Ray Lithography]

    nodeC --> nodeC1[Ion Implantation]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Thermal Oxidation]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[Diffusion Doping]

    nodeD --> nodeD1[Wet Etching]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[Dry Etching]
    nodeD1 --> nodeD1A[Isotropic HNA]
    nodeD1 --> nodeD1B[Anisotropic KOH TMAH EDP]
    nodeD2 --> nodeD2A[RIE]
    nodeD2 --> nodeD2B[DRIE BOSCH]

    nodeE --> nodeE1[Chemical Vapor Deposition]
    nodeE --> nodeE2[Physical Vapor Deposition]
    nodeE1 --> nodeE1A[LPCVD]
    nodeE1 --> nodeE1B[PECVD]
    nodeE2 --> nodeE2A[Sputtering]
    nodeE2 --> nodeE2B[Evaporation]

    nodeF --> nodeF1[Bulk Micromachining]
    nodeF --> nodeF2[Surface Micromachining]
    nodeF --> nodeF3[LIGA Process]
    nodeF --> nodeF4[Wafer Bonding]
    nodeF4 --> nodeF4A[Anodic]
    nodeF4 --> nodeF4B[Fusion]
    nodeF4 --> nodeF4C[Eutectic]
    nodeF4 --> nodeF4D[Adhesive]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Surface Micromachining Process Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    step1[Substrate Silicon Wafer] --> step2[Deposit Sacrificial Layer SiO2]
    step2 --> step3[Deposit Structural Layer Polysilicon]
    step3 --> step4[Photolithography Pattern Structural Layer]
    step4 --> step5[Reactive Ion Etch Structural Layer]
    step5 --> step6[Release in HF Etches Sacrificial Layer]
    step6 --> step7[Free Standing MEMS Structure Cantilever Beam Plate]
    step7 --> step8[Critical Point Drying Avoid Stiction]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Bulk Micromachining (Backside Etch)

```mermaid
graph TD
    wafer[Silicon Wafer 100] --> ox[Thermal Oxidation Both Sides]
    ox --> mask[Pattern Backside Oxide Mask Opening]
    mask --> etch[KOH Anisotropic Etch]
    etch --> mem[Thin Silicon Membrane Forms]
    mem --> sense[Piezoresistor Pattern on Frontside]
    sense --> final[Pressure Sensor Diaphragm Ready]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — LIGA Process Sequence

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Metallic Substrate] --> B[Spin Thick PMMA Resist]
    B --> C[Deep X Ray Exposure Through Mask]
    C --> D[Developer Removes Exposed PMMA]
    D --> E[Electroforming Nickel Fills Mold]
    E --> F[Strip Remaining PMMA]
    F --> G[Free Standing Nickel Microstructure]
    G --> H[Use as Mold Insert for Plastic Replication]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Block — Wafer Bonding Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    start{Wafer Bonding Need} --> temp{Temperature Constraint}
    temp -->|Below 400 C| anodic[Anodic Glass Si 1000 V]
    temp -->|Below 400 C| eutectic[Eutectic Au Si 363 C]
    temp -->|Below 250 C| adh[Adhesive SU 8 BCB]
    temp -->|Above 800 C| fusion[Fusion Si Si 1000 C]

    start --> herm{Hermetic Seal Required}
    herm -->|Yes| anodic
    herm -->|Yes| fusion
    herm -->|No| adh
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Rationale:**
> Mermaid is used to render the **process architecture** and **decision logic** rather than physical cross-sections, ensuring clean and reliable rendering per protocol.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Bulk Micromachining. List two etch-stop techniques used in bulk micromachining of silicon.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Bulk Micromachining is a MEMS fabrication technique in which significant portions of the silicon substrate are selectively removed using chemical (wet) or plasma (dry) etching to create three-dimensional microstructures such as membranes, cantilevers, and channels. (2 marks)

Two etch-stop techniques: (1 mark)
1. **Boron etch stop** — Heavy boron doping ($>5\times10^{19}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$) drastically reduces KOH etch rate.
2. **Electrochemical etch stop** — A reverse-biased p-n junction terminates etching at the junction boundary.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is LIGA? Mention two of its key advantages.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

LIGA is a German acronym for **L**ithographie (X-ray lithography), **G**alvanoformung (electroforming), and **A**bformung (molding) — a MEMS fabrication process for producing tall, high-aspect-ratio metallic microstructures. (2 marks)

Two advantages: (1 mark)
1. Very high aspect ratios (>100:1) — structural heights up to millimeters.
2. Excellent sidewall verticality and surface finish.
3. Compatible with subsequent plastic injection molding for mass production.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Option 1) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **photolithography process** used in MEMS fabrication. Discuss the difference between **positive and negative photoresists**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Photolithography Steps:**
1. Wafer cleaning (RCA-1 and RCA-2).
2. Dehydration bake (~150°C).
3. HMDS priming for adhesion.
4. Spin-coating photoresist (e.g., 4000 RPM → ~1 µm film).
5. Soft bake (~90°C for 60 sec).
6. UV exposure through photomask.
7. Post-exposure bake.
8. Development in TMAH or NaOH developer.
9. Hard bake (~120°C).
10. Inspection under microscope.

**Positive vs Negative Resist:**

| Property | Positive Resist | Negative Resist |
|---|---|---|
| Exposed regions | Become soluble (removed) | Become insoluble (remain) |
| Resolution | Higher | Lower (swelling) |
| Common chemicals | AZ-series, S1800 | SU-8 (thick), NR7 |
| Typical use | Fine-line patterning | Thick, structural layers |

(7 marks — full process + diagram + comparison)

---

**(b)** Derive the **minimum resolvable feature size** in optical lithography using Rayleigh's criterion. State two techniques used to push lithography resolution below the diffraction limit. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The diffraction-limited resolution $R$ of an optical projection system is given by Rayleigh's criterion:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $\lambda$ = exposure wavelength
- $NA$ = numerical aperture of the projection lens
- $k_1$ = process-dependent constant (≈ 0.4 for production)

**Derivation logic:**
- Two point sources are resolvable when the central maximum of one diffraction pattern coincides with the first minimum of the other.
- This produces a minimum intensity dip of ~26% (Rayleigh criterion).
- Mathematically: $\sin\theta = 1.22 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{D}$, and $NA = n \sin\theta$.
- Substituting and rearranging yields $R = 0.61 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA}$, generalized to $k_1 \frac{\lambda}{NA}$.

**Techniques to beat the diffraction limit:**
1. **Phase-shifting masks** — reduce $k_1$ below 0.4.
2. **Immersion lithography** — high-index fluid between lens and wafer increases $NA$.
3. **Shorter wavelength sources** — 193 nm ArF, EUV (13.5 nm).

(7 marks — derivation + techniques)

---

### Question B (Option 2) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the **anisotropic wet etching of silicon** using KOH. Show that a square mask opening on a (100) wafer produces a pyramidal cavity of depth $d = \frac{w}{2\sqrt{2}}$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Mechanism of KOH Anisotropic Etching:**
- KOH + H₂O attacks silicon via oxidation + dissolution reactions:
$$\text{Si} + 2\text{OH}^- + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{SiO}_2(\text{OH})_2^{2-} + 2\text{H}_2$$
- The etch rate depends strongly on crystallographic orientation due to differences in atomic density and back-bond structure.
- (100) planes have 2 back-bonds per atom → faster etching.
- (111) planes have 3 back-bonds → slowest etching.
- Etch rate ratio: $R_{(100)} / R_{(111)} \approx 100:1$.

**Derivation of cavity depth:**

The angle between (100) surface and (111) planes is $\theta = 54.74°$, with:

$$
\tan(54.74°) = \sqrt{2}
$$

For a square mask of side $w$, half-width is $w/2$. Geometry:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan(\theta) &= \frac{w/2}{d} \\
\Rightarrow d &= \frac{w/2}{\tan(\theta)} = \frac{w/2}{\sqrt{2}} \\
d &= \frac{w}{2\sqrt{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

(7 marks)

---

**(b)** Describe the **LIGA process** in detail with its key steps. State its advantages over conventional lithography. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**LIGA Process Steps:**
1. **Substrate preparation** — Conductive substrate (e.g., metallized silicon or copper plate).
2. **PMMA resist coating** — Thick (100 µm – several mm) polymethyl methacrylate resist is bonded or cast.
3. **Deep X-ray lithography** — Synchrotron radiation (λ ≈ 0.2–1 nm) exposes PMMA through a gold absorber mask.
4. **Development** — Exposed PMMA dissolved in GG developer (diethyl glycol mono-butyl ether + morpholine + ethanolamine + water).
5. **Electroforming** — Mold cavities filled with nickel (or copper, gold) by electroplating.
6. **PMMA stripping** — Removed with acetone or plasma, leaving free-standing metal microstructure.
7. **Replication** — Metal mold insert used for hot embossing or injection molding of polymer replicas.

**Advantages over conventional lithography:**
- Aspect ratios > 100 (vs ~10 in optical).
- Structural heights up to **several mm**.
- Excellent sidewall smoothness (< 50 nm roughness).
- Compatible with metallic, ceramic, and polymer materials.
- Enables high-volume replication.

(7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Bulk vs Surface micromachining** — Bulk = substrate removal; Surface = layer-additive with sacrificial etch.
> 2. **Wrong angle in V-groove derivation** — Many students use $\theta = 45°$ instead of the correct **54.74°** for the (100)–(111) angle.
> 3. **Forgetting etch-stop mechanisms** — Just listing "wet etching" without etch-stop techniques loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping stiction** — In any surface micromachining question, omitting stiction causes 2-mark deduction.
> 5. **Missing the acronym expansion of LIGA** — Always expand the German terms in full.
> 6. **No units in numerical answers** — KTU examiners strictly deduct ½ mark per missing unit.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**
>
> - Photolithography is the **pattern definition** process; resolution given by **Rayleigh: $R = k_1 \lambda / NA$**.
> - **Positive resist**: exposed areas removed. **Negative resist**: exposed areas remain.
> - **Wet etching**: chemical, batch-friendly. **Dry etching**: plasma-based, anisotropic.
> - **KOH etch rate ratio** (100):(111) ≈ **100:1**; angle = **54.74°**.
> - **V-groove depth**: $d = w / (2\sqrt{2})$ — memorize this formula.
> - **RIE** = Reactive Ion Etching; **DRIE / Bosch** = deep RIE with cyclic etch-passivation for AR up to 50:1.
> - **Bulk micromachining** = etches *into* the wafer; **Surface micromachining** = builds *on top* using sacrificial layers.
> - **Stiction** is the dominant failure mode in surface micromachining — prevented by critical-point drying.
> - **LIGA** = Lithographie + Galvanoformung + Abformung; AR > 100; uses synchrotron X-rays.
> - **Deposition methods**: CVD (chemical, conformal), PVD (physical, directional).
> - **Wafer bonding types**: Anodic (~400°C), Fusion (~1000°C), Eutectic (~363°C Au-Si), Adhesive (<250°C).
> - **Boron etch stop** activates at $N_B > 5\times10^{19}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$.
> - Cantilever resonance: $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$.
> - Polysilicon Young's modulus ≈ **160 GPa**, density ≈ **2330 kg/m³** — frequently quoted numerical values.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Photolithography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET836 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Photolithography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Photolithography** is a planar microfabrication process that uses light to transfer a geometric pattern from a *photomask* (or *reticle*) onto a photosensitive polymer film (the *photoresist*) deposited on a substrate. The process is the foundational patterning step in MEMS and IC manufacturing, enabling feature sizes down to the sub-micron regime.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU OEMET836 Module 3):**
> Photolithography is studied under *Micromachining Processes*. Students must understand optical exposure systems, photoresist chemistry, and the resolution-limiting parameters of the lithographic process.

**Standard Process Vocabulary Used in KTU Answer Scripts:**

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Photoresist** | Light-sensitive polymer spin-coated on the substrate |
| **Photomask** | Glass plate with a chromium pattern defining the geometry |
| **Exposure** | Illumination of resist through the mask with UV light |
| **Development** | Selective dissolution of exposed (or unexposed) resist |
| **Resolution (R)** | Smallest feature that can be reliably printed |
| **Numerical Aperture (NA)** | Light-gathering ability of the projection lens |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **The Stencil & Spray-Paint Analogy:**
> Imagine you want to paint a complex design on a wall. The cheapest, fastest way is to:
> 1. Cut a *stencil* (this is the **photomask**),
> 2. Tape it to the wall (this is **aligning the mask to the substrate**),
> 3. Spray paint over it (this is **UV exposure** through the clear regions of the mask),
> 4. Peel off the stencil — the paint only sticks where the stencil had holes (**selective exposure**),
> 5. Wash the wall to remove the un-painted glue (this is **chemical development**).
>
> Photolithography is the silicon-wafer equivalent of this stencil process, but performed at micrometer precision using ultraviolet light instead of spray paint.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Wavelength (λ)** of typical exposure sources: **g-line = 436 nm, h-line = 405 nm, i-line = 365 nm, DUV = 248 nm / 193 nm**.
- **Numerical Aperture (NA)** of modern stepper lenses: **0.5 – 0.93**.
- **Resolution constant k₁** (process-dependent): **0.4 – 0.8** (theoretical lower bound = 0.25).
- **Spin speed** for photoresist coating: **1000 – 6000 rpm**.
- **Soft-bake temperature**: **90 °C – 100 °C** for 60 s on a hot plate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Rayleigh Criterion governs everything in photolithography.** Memorize the two master equations:
> $$\text{Resolution: } R = \frac{k_1 \lambda}{NA}$$
> $$\text{Depth of Focus: } \text{DOF} = \frac{k_2 \lambda}{NA^2}$$
> Every improvement (smaller λ, larger NA) that reduces R also reduces DOF, creating a fundamental trade-off.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sequential Process Flow (Eight Canonical Steps)

Photolithography is *not* a single event — it is a tightly choreographed sequence. Each step has measurable tolerances. The full canonical flow:

1. **Substrate Preparation (Dehydration Bake)**
   - The wafer (typically Si ⟨100⟩) is heated to **~200 °C for 30 min** to drive off adsorbed moisture.
   - *Why?* A water film causes *resist popping* and poor adhesion. Hexamethyldisilazane (HMDS) priming is often applied to make the surface hydrophobic.

2. **Photoresist Application (Spin Coating)**
   - Liquid photoresist is dispensed, and the wafer is spun at high speed.
   - Centrifugal force spreads the resist; solvent evaporates leaving a uniform film.
