$$-\frac{x_j^2}{4Dt} = \ln(1.504 \times 10^{-5}) = -11.105$$

$$x_j^2 = 11.105 \times 4 \times 7.2 \times 10^{-10} = 3.198 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{cm}^2$$

$$x_j = 1.788 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{cm} = 1.79\,\mu\text{m}$$

> **Final Answers:** (i) $C(0,t) = 6.65 \times 10^{19}$ cm$^{-3}$, (ii) $C(0.5\,\mu\text{m}) = 2.79 \times 10^{19}$ cm$^{-3}$, (iii) $x_j = 1.79\,\mu$m.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Sign error in Fick's First Law** — Forgetting the negative sign in $J = -D\,\partial C/\partial x$ (1 mark deduction).
> 2. **Unit mismatch** — Mixing cm and µm in $x$ and $\sqrt{Dt}$ without conversion (2–3 mark deduction, a very common mistake).
> 3. **Wrong boundary condition** — Applying the erfc solution when the source is finite, or vice-versa. Always identify the source type **first** before writing the formula.
> 4. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{\pi}$** in the Gaussian formula. The denominator is $\sqrt{\pi Dt}$, not $\sqrt{Dt}$ — losing a full step's marks.
> 5. **Not converting time** — Leaving $t$ in minutes instead of seconds gives $D$ in wrong units.
> 6. **Treating $D$ as constant across temperatures** — $D$ varies exponentially with $T$; compute it via Arrhenius for each temperature.
> 7. **Skipping the assumption list** in derivations — KTU examiners explicitly look for "constant $D$", "1D diffusion", and "no drift" assumptions (1 mark).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Diffusion**

- ☐ **Diffusion** is the thermally activated, random motion of dopant atoms down a concentration gradient in a solid.
- ☐ **Fick's First Law:** $J = -D\,\partial C/\partial x$ — flux is proportional to the negative concentration gradient. **Steady-state only.**
- ☐ **Fick's Second Law:** $\partial C/\partial t = D\,\partial^2 C/\partial x^2$ — the **time-dependent** governing PDE. Derived from First Law + continuity.
- ☐ **Arrhenius Law:** $D = D_0 \exp(-E_a/k_B T)$ — diffusion coefficient is **exponentially** sensitive to temperature.
- ☐ **Constant source** gives an **erfc** profile with fixed surface concentration $C_s$ — example: gas-phase doping.
- ☐ **Finite source** gives a **Gaussian** profile with conserved total dose $Q$ — example: ion-implant + drive-in.
- ☐ **Junction depth** scales as $x_j \propto \sqrt{Dt}$ — the famous **sqrt-t law**.
- ☐ **Four diffusion mechanisms:** vacancy, interstitial, interstitialcy, and grain boundary.
- ☐ **Boron** in Si diffuses **interstitially** (small atom, fast diffuser).
- ☐ **Antimony and Arsenic** diffuse by the **vacancy mechanism** (slow diffusers, good for shallow junctions).
- ☐ **$SiO_2$** acts as a **diffusion mask** for most dopants — this enables selective area doping in MEMS.
- ☐ Standard **error function** identity: $\text{erfc}(z) = 1 - \text{erf}(z)$. Remember that $\text{erfc}(0) = 1$ and $\text{erfc}(\infty) = 0$.
- ☐ Typical diffusion parameters: $T = 900$–$1200^\circ$C, $t = 30$ min–several hours, $D = 10^{-15}$ to $10^{-12}$ cm²/s.
- ☐ For KTU derivations, always **list assumptions** (constant $D$, 1D diffusion, no field, no reaction) — this carries marks.
- ☐ **Order-of-magnitude check:** Always verify that the junction depth is in the 0.1–10 µm range — a wildly different answer means a unit or formula error.
- ☐ Higher temperature → exponentially larger $D$ → deeper junction; longer time → deeper junction (but only as $\sqrt{t}$).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Oxidation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET836 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Oxidation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Oxidation** is a high-temperature microfabrication process in which a silicon (Si) wafer is exposed to an oxidizing ambient (pure O₂ for **dry oxidation**, or H₂O vapor for **wet oxidation**) to grow a thin, dense, and uniform layer of silicon dioxide (SiO₂) directly on the silicon surface. It is a *consumption process* because the Si substrate is partially consumed (≈ 44% of the oxide thickness lies *below* the original Si surface).

The governing chemical reactions are:

**Dry Oxidation (≈ 1000–1200 °C):**
$$\text{Si (solid)} + \text{O}_2 \text{(gas)} \longrightarrow \text{SiO}_2 \text{(solid)}$$

**Wet Oxidation (≈ 900–1000 °C):**
$$\text{Si (solid)} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O (vapor)} \longrightarrow \text{SiO}_2 \text{(solid)} + 2\text{H}_2 \text{(gas)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is SiO₂ so special in MEMS / IC fabrication?**
> - It is a high-quality electrical insulator (resistivity ≈ 10¹⁴ – 10¹⁶ Ω·cm).
> - It acts as a **diffusion mask** for boron, phosphorus, and arsenic dopants.
> - It serves as a **sacrificial layer** in surface micromachining.
> - It is a **structural / dielectric** layer in capacitors, MOS gates, and pressure-sensor diaphragms.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a bar of soft iron left out in a humid coastal town. Over days, a reddish-brown layer of rust (Fe₂O₃) slowly creeps over its surface. The iron itself is *consumed* during this chemical union with oxygen.

**Thermal oxidation of silicon is essentially "silicon rusting" — but in a precisely controlled, furnace-grade, semiconductor-clean environment.**

| Everyday Rusting | MEMS Thermal Oxidation |
|---|---|
| Uncontrolled, weeks/months | Precisely controlled, minutes/hours |
| Porous, flaky | Dense, amorphous, pin-hole free |
| Destroys material integrity | Engineers rely on it for device integrity |

The *key insight*: the silicon is not "coated" — it is *chemically converted* into a brand-new compound, SiO₂, that swells outward (≈ 2.2× the consumed Si volume). For every **1 μm** of SiO₂ grown, roughly **0.44 μm** of Si is consumed.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** **2 – 1 – 2.2 Rule** → 2 atoms of Si + 1 atom of O₂ → 2.2× volume expansion ratio of SiO₂ relative to consumed Si.

## 1.3 Key Metrics in Bold

- **Standard Process Temperatures:** Dry = **1000–1200 °C**, Wet = **900–1000 °C**.
- **Oxide Growth Direction:** **46%** downward into Si, **54%** upward above original surface.
- **Typical Film Thicknesses in MEMS:** **10 nm – 2 μm**.
- **Refractive Index (n):** **≈ 1.46** (at 632.8 nm).
- **Dielectric Constant (k):** **≈ 3.9**.
- **Breakdown Field:** **≈ 10 MV/cm**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Step Mechanism of Thermal Oxidation

The Deal–Grove model (1965) treats oxidation as three sequential kinetic steps:

1. **Transport / Diffusion Step** — The oxidizing species (O₂ or H₂O) is transported from the bulk gas stream through the *existing* oxide layer toward the Si/SiO₂ interface.
2. **Surface Reaction Step** — The oxidant reacts with Si at the Si/SiO₂ interface to form new SiO₂.
3. **Incorporation Step** — The new SiO₂ volume is accommodated, causing the interface to move into the Si.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter?** The two slowest steps control the rate:
> - At **low temperatures / thin oxides** → *Surface reaction* dominates → **Linear regime**.
> - At **high temperatures / thick oxides** → *Diffusion* dominates → **Parabolic regime**.

## 2.2 Orientation Dependence

The linear rate constant (B/A) depends on the Si crystal orientation because surface atomic density affects the reaction rate.

> **Hierarchy (fastest → slowest):** **(111) > (110) > (100)**

This is why **<100> silicon** is preferred in MEMS: it gives smoother, more uniform oxides and lower parasitic capacitance.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $x^{2} + A x = B(t + \tau)$ | **Deal–Grove General Equation** | x in μm, t in hr |
| 2 | $\dfrac{B}{A}$ | Linear rate constant (reaction-limited) | μm/hr |
| 3 | $B$ | Parabolic rate constant (diffusion-limited) | μm²/hr |
| 4 | $\tau = \dfrac{x_{i}^{2} + A x_{i}}{B}$ | Time shift due to native oxide $x_{i}$ (≈ 1–2 nm) | hr |
| 5 | $B = \dfrac{2 D_{\text{eff}} C^{*}}{N_{1}}$ | Parabolic constant in terms of diffusivity | μm²/hr |
| 6 | $\dfrac{B}{A} = \dfrac{k_{s} C^{*}}{N_{1}}$ | Linear constant in terms of surface reaction rate | μm/hr |
| 7 | $\dfrac{dx}{dt} = \dfrac{B}{2x + A}$ | Instantaneous growth rate | μm/hr |
| 8 | $x(t) = \dfrac{A}{2}\left[\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{4B(t+\tau)}{A^{2}}} - 1\right]$ | Explicit thickness vs time | μm |
| 9 | $x \approx \sqrt{B t}$ | Parabolic (thick-oxide) limit, $x \gg A$ | μm |
| 10 | $x \approx \dfrac{B}{A}(t + \tau)$ | Linear (thin-oxide) limit, $x \ll A$ | μm |
| 11 | $\ln(B) = \ln(B_{0}) - \dfrac{E_{B}}{k_{B} T}$ | Arrhenius: parabolic activation | — |
| 12 | $\ln(B/A) = \ln(C_{0}) - \dfrac{E_{B/A}}{k_{B} T}$ | Arrhenius: linear activation | — |

**Activation Energy Reference (KTU High-Yield):**

| Ambient | $E_{B}$ (eV) | $E_{B/A}$ (eV) |
|---|---|---|
| Dry O₂ | **1.23** | **1.80** |
| Wet H₂O | **0.78** | **0.71** |

**Where the symbols mean:**

- $x$ — oxide thickness,
- $A$ — characteristic length ≈ 0.5–1.5 μm,
- $B$ — parabolic rate constant,
- $D_{\text{eff}}$ — effective diffusivity of oxidant in SiO₂,
- $C^{*}$ — equilibrium solubility of oxidant in SiO₂,
- $N_{1}$ — number of oxidant molecules per unit volume of SiO₂ (≈ 2.2 × 10²² cm⁻³ for O₂; ≈ 4.4 × 10²² cm⁻³ for H₂O),
- $k_{s}$ — surface reaction rate constant,
- $k_{B}$ — Boltzmann constant = **8.617 × 10⁻⁵ eV/K**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **MOS Capacitors & Transistors:** Gate oxide ≈ 1–10 nm (dry, ultra-thin).
- **LOCOS / STI:** Field isolation in CMOS, where thick oxides (>500 nm) are grown.
- **Sacrificial Layers:** In surface-micromachined accelerometers (e.g., ADXL), SiO₂ is grown, patterned, and later etched in HF to release polysilicon structures.
- **Pressure Sensors:** Piezoresistive bridges are insulated from the Si substrate by a thermally grown SiO₂ layer.
- **Bio-MEMS / Lab-on-Chip:** Thermal oxide provides a biocompatible, hydrophilic surface for microfluidic channels.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Deal–Grove Equation

The model assumes **steady-state one-dimensional diffusion** of the oxidant through a stationary oxide of thickness $x$.

### Step 1 — Apply Fick's First Law

The flux $F_{1}$ of oxidant at the oxide surface is governed by gas-phase diffusion and the solubility of the oxidant in SiO₂:

$$F_{1} = h_{g}\left(C_{g} - C_{s}\right)$$

Here $h_{g}$ is the gas-phase mass-transfer coefficient, $C_{g}$ is the bulk gas concentration, and $C_{s}$ is the concentration at the outer oxide surface.

### Step 2 — Diffusive Flux Through the Existing Oxide

The flux $F_{2}$ through the oxide (assumed Fickian, no consumption inside SiO₂) is:

$$F_{2} = D_{\text{eff}}\,\dfrac{C_{s} - C_{i}}{x}$$

where $C_{i}$ is the concentration at the Si/SiO₂ interface and $D_{\text{eff}}$ is the effective diffusivity of the oxidant in SiO₂.

### Step 3 — Interface Reaction Rate

The flux $F_{3}$ at the Si/SiO₂ interface is governed by first-order surface kinetics:

$$F_{3} = k_{s}\,C_{i}$$

### Step 4 — Apply Steady-State Condition

At steady state, $F_{1} = F_{2} = F_{3} \equiv F$. Eliminating $C_{s}$ and $C_{i}$ gives the **net flux**:

$$F = \dfrac{k_{s} C^{*} }{1 + \dfrac{k_{s} x}{D_{\text{eff}}}}$$

where $C^{*}$ is the equilibrium concentration of oxidant in the oxide (Henrian solubility).

### Step 5 — Relate Flux to Growth Rate

The growth rate is the flux divided by the number of oxidant molecules incorporated per unit volume of oxide, $N_{1}$:

$$\dfrac{dx}{dt} = \dfrac{F}{N_{1}} = \dfrac{k_{s} C^{*} / N_{1}}{1 + \dfrac{k_{s} x}{D_{\text{eff}}}}$$

### Step 6 — Define the Constants

Let us define the **parabolic** and **linear** rate constants:

$$B = \dfrac{2 D_{\text{eff}} C^{*}}{N_{1}}, \qquad \dfrac{B}{A} = \dfrac{k_{s} C^{*}}{N_{1}}$$

Substituting these into the growth rate:

$$\dfrac{dx}{dt} = \dfrac{B}{2x + A}$$

### Step 7 — Integrate to Obtain the Master Equation

Rearranging and integrating from $t = 0$ (with initial oxide $x_{i}$) to $t$:

$$\int_{x_{i}}^{x}(2x' + A)\, dx' = \int_{0}^{t} B\, dt'$$

This gives:

$$x^{2} - x_{i}^{2} + A(x - x_{i}) = B t$$

Or, equivalently (defining $\tau$):

$$\boxed{x^{2} + A x = B (t + \tau)}$$

with the time-shift parameter:

$$\tau = \dfrac{x_{i}^{2} + A x_{i}}{B}$$

## 3.2 Limiting-Case Analytical Matrix

We now derive the two important physical limits that the KTU examiner loves to test.

### Case 1 — Linear Regime (Thin Oxide, $x \ll A$)

When the oxide is very thin, the diffusion term in the denominator of the flux is negligible compared to the reaction term. Neglecting $2x$ in $2x + A$:

$$\dfrac{dx}{dt} \approx \dfrac{B}{A}$$

Integrating with the initial condition $x(0) = x_{i}$:

$$x - x_{i} = \dfrac{B}{A} t \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{x \approx \dfrac{B}{A}\,t + x_{i}}$$

> **Physical meaning:** The reaction at the interface is the *rate-limiting step*. Growth is **linear in time**, controlled by surface chemistry.

### Case 2 — Parabolic Regime (Thick Oxide, $x \gg A$)

For very thick oxides, the surface reaction is fast compared to diffusion. The $A$ in $2x + A$ becomes negligible:

$$\dfrac{dx}{dt} \approx \dfrac{B}{2x}$$

Separating variables and integrating:

$$2x\, dx = B\, dt \quad \Rightarrow \quad x^{2} = B t + \text{const.}$$

Applying $x(0) = x_{i}$:

$$\boxed{x \approx \sqrt{B t + x_{i}^{2}}}$$

> **Physical meaning:** The diffusion of the oxidant through the ever-thickening oxide is the *rate-limiting step*. Growth is **parabolic in time**, like Fickian diffusion.

## 3.3 Sample Numerical Demonstration

**Problem:** Grow wet oxide on (100) Si at 1000 °C for 2 hours. Given $B = 0.286\ \mu\text{m}^2/\text{hr}$ and $B/A = 0.226\ \mu\text{m}/\text{hr}$. Ignore native oxide.

**Solution Path:**

1. Compute $A = B / (B/A) = 0.286 / 0.226 = 1.265\ \mu\text{m}$.
2. Apply the explicit form:
$$x = \frac{A}{2}\left[\sqrt{1 + \frac{4 B t}{A^{2}}} - 1\right]$$
3. Substitute $B t = 0.286 \times 2 = 0.572\ \mu\text{m}^2$ and $A^{2} = 1.60\ \mu\text{m}^2$:
$$x = \frac{1.265}{2}\left[\sqrt{1 + \frac{4(0.572)}{1.60}} - 1\right] = 0.6325 \times (1.732 - 1) = 0.463\ \mu\text{m}$$

**Final oxide thickness ≈ 463 nm**, well into the parabolic regime.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Thermal Oxidation Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SI WAFER RCA CLEANED] --> B[LOAD INTO FURNACE TUBE]
    B --> C[TEMPERATURE RAMP UP TO 900 1200 C]
    C --> D{OXIDATION TYPE SELECT}
    D -- DRY --> E1[O2 GAS INLET DRY OXIDATION]
    D -- WET --> E2[H2O BUBBLER + N2 CARRIER WET OXIDATION]
    E1 --> F1[DIFFUSION OF O2 THROUGH SIO2]
    E2 --> F2[DIFFUSION OF H2O THROUGH SIO2]
    F1 --> G[REACTION AT SI SIO2 INTERFACE]
    F2 --> G
    G --> H[SIO2 GROWTH 46 PCT DOWN 54 PCT UP]
    H --> I[TEMPERATURE RAMP DOWN]
    I --> J[UNLOAD WAFER]
    J --> K[ELLIPSOMETRY OR PROFILER FOR X]
    K --> L{THICKNESS WITHIN SPEC}
    L -- YES --> M[ACCEPTED FOR NEXT PROCESS]
    L -- NO --> N[REWORK OR SCRAP]

    style A fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b00,stroke-width:2px
    style M fill:#d2f8d2,stroke:#080,stroke-width:2px
    style N fill:#fdd,stroke:#a00,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#e2e2fd,stroke:#00a,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Schematic of the Three Reaction Zones (Deal–Grove Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ZONE1 [GAS PHASE BULK]
        G1[O2 OR H2O MOLECULES CG]
    end
    subgraph ZONE2 [SIO2 LAYER THICKNESS X]
        D1[DIFFUSION DEFF CS TO CI]
    end
    subgraph ZONE3 [SI SUBSTRATE]
        R1[SURFACE REACTION KS CI FORMS NEW SIO2]
    end
    G1 -- TRANSPORT HG --> D1
    D1 -- FLUX F --> R1
    R1 -- INTERFACE MOVES DOWN INTO SI --> ZONE3

    style ZONE1 fill:#fff7d6,stroke:#a80
    style ZONE2 fill:#d6f0ff,stroke:#069
    style ZONE3 fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#444
```

## 4.3 Oxide Growth Direction & Si Consumption (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BEFORE [BEFORE OXIDATION]
        B1[SI BULK SUBSTRATE 100 PERCENT]
    end
    subgraph AFTER [AFTER OXIDATION]
        T1[SIO2 ABOVE ORIGINAL SURFACE 54 PCT]
        T2[ORIGINAL SI SURFACE REFERENCE LINE]
        T3[SIO2 BELOW ORIGINAL SURFACE 46 PCT]
        T4[REMAINING SI BULK]
    end
    BEFORE --> AFTER
    T2 -. REFERENCE .- AFTER

    style T1 fill:#cfe9ff,stroke:#06c
    style T3 fill:#cfe9ff,stroke:#06c
    style T4 fill:#dadada,stroke:#555
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Distinguish between dry and wet thermal oxidation of silicon. Mention at least three differences in growth rate, temperature, and oxide quality.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Dry Oxidation | Wet Oxidation |
|---|---|---|
| Oxidant | Pure O₂ | H₂O vapor (bubbler) |
| Typical Temperature | 1000–1200 °C | 900–1000 °C |
| Growth Rate | **Slow** (≈ 0.1 μm/hr at 1000 °C) | **Fast** (≈ 1 μm/hr at 1000 °C) |
| Oxide Quality | **Excellent**, dense, dry | Slightly lower quality, contains –OH |
| Use Case | Gate oxide (thin, ≤ 50 nm) | Field oxide, masking layer (thick) |

*(1 mark per major point × 3 points = 3 marks.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2023]
**Define the term "linear rate constant" and "parabolic rate constant" in Deal–Grove oxidation. What physical processes do they represent?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Linear rate constant $B/A$ (μm/hr):** Characterizes the *surface-reaction-limited* regime, where the rate of Si–O bond formation at the Si/SiO₂ interface controls growth. Dominant for **thin oxides** (typically $x < A \approx 0.5$ μm).
- **Parabolic rate constant $B$ (μm²/hr):** Characterizes the *diffusion-limited* regime, where the rate of oxidant transport through the existing SiO₂ film controls growth. Dominant for **thick oxides** ($x \gg A$).
- $B$ is **strongly** temperature-dependent via Arrhenius relation $\ln B = \ln B_{0} - E_{B}/k_{B} T$.

*(2 marks for definitions + 1 mark for physical interpretation = 3 marks.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Starting from Fick's first law, derive the Deal–Grove general oxidation equation $x^{2} + A x = B (t + \tau)$. Define each term clearly.**

**Model Solution Outline:**
1. Write Fick's law for diffusion through the oxide: $F_{2} = D_{\text{eff}}(C_{s} - C_{i})/x$. **(1 mark)**
2. Apply Henry's law at the outer surface ($C_{s} = C^{*} P_{g}/P^{*}$) and gas-phase mass transport $F_{1} = h_{g}(C_{g} - C_{s})$. **(1 mark)**
3. Write interface reaction flux $F_{3} = k_{s} C_{i}$. **(1 mark)**
4. Impose steady state $F_{1} = F_{2} = F_{3} = F$, eliminate $C_{s}$ and $C_{i}$, obtain:
$$F = \frac{k_{s} C^{*}}{1 + \frac{k_{s} x}{D_{\text{eff}}}}$$
**(1 mark)**
5. Convert flux to growth rate: $dx/dt = F / N_{1}$. Substitute definitions $B = 2 D_{\text{eff}} C^{*}/N_{1}$ and $B/A = k_{s} C^{*}/N_{1}$:
$$\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{B}{2x + A}$$
**(1 mark)**
6. Integrate from initial oxide $x_{i}$ at $t = 0$ to thickness $x$ at time $t$:
$$x^{2} + A x = B(t + \tau), \quad \tau = \frac{x_{i}^{2} + A x_{i}}{B}$$
**(2 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (Valuation Trap):** Many students write $x^{2} = B t$ directly, **forgetting the $A x$ term**. This loses 2 marks. Always quote the *full* Deal–Grove equation and the conditions under which linear/parabolic limits apply.

#### (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]
**A 100 mm (100)-oriented silicon wafer is wet-oxidized at 1000 °C. Given $B = 0.286\ \mu\text{m}^{2}/\text{hr}$ and $B/A = 0.226\ \mu\text{m}/\text{hr}$, calculate (i) the time required to grow 0.5 μm of SiO₂, and (ii) the time required to grow 1.0 μm of SiO₂. Neglect initial native oxide. Comment on the regime.**

**Model Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute $A$:
$$A = \frac{B}{B/A} = \frac{0.286}{0.226} = 1.265\ \mu\text{m}$$

Step 2 — Invert the explicit Deal–Grove form to solve for $t$:
$$t = \frac{x^{2} + A x}{B} = \frac{x(x + A)}{B}$$

**Part (i):** $x = 0.5\ \mu\text{m}$:
$$t = \frac{0.5 \times (0.5 + 1.265)}{0.286} = \frac{0.5 \times 1.765}{0.286} = \frac{0.8825}{0.286} \approx 3.09\ \text{hr}$$

**Part (ii):** $x = 1.0\ \mu\text{m}$:
$$t = \frac{1.0 \times (1.0 + 1.265)}{0.286} = \frac{2.265}{0.286} \approx 7.92\ \text{hr}$$

Step 3 — **Comment on regime:**
Since $A = 1.265\ \mu\text{m}$ and both target thicknesses are smaller than $A$, both fall in the **transition regime**, closer to *linear* behavior. If we doubled the thicknesses to 2 μm, $x \gg A$ and the regime would be predominantly **parabolic**. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

#### (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Discuss the role of SiO₂ in MEMS fabrication. Explain at least four distinct applications with suitable device examples.**

**Model Solution Outline (1.5 marks per application × 4 + 1 mark for introduction = 7 marks):**

1. **Gate Dielectric in MOS Transistors:** Provides the high-quality, thin insulating layer (< 10 nm in modern devices) that enables the field-effect control of channel current. Example: CMOS inverter.
2. **Diffusion Mask:** SiO₂ blocks boron, phosphorus, and arsenic diffusion. Used in **selective doping** of Si. Example: Source/drain formation in MOSFETs.
3. **Sacrificial Layer in Surface Micromachining:** A grown/deposited SiO₂ is etched away (HF) to release suspended polysilicon structures. Example: Analog Devices ADXL accelerometers, digital micromirror devices (DMD).
4. **Dielectric Isolation / Structural Layer:** Acts as a stress-compensated, electrically insulating membrane in piezoresistive pressure sensors. Example: Bosch BMP-series barometric pressure sensor.
5. *(Bonus)* **Stiction-reduction coating** and **bio-MEMS surface functionalization**.

#### (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]
**Explain the orientation dependence of thermal oxidation. Why is (100) silicon preferred over (111) for MEMS applications?**

**Model Solution:**

The linear rate constant $B/A$ depends on Si crystal orientation. The number of available Si bonds per unit area at the surface is the controlling parameter.

- **(111) orientation:** Highest atomic surface density (~7.83 × 10¹⁴ atoms/cm²) → **fastest** oxidation rate.
- **(110) orientation:** Intermediate density (~9.59 × 10¹⁴ atoms/cm²... actually this is slightly lower effective bond density for oxidation).
- **(100) orientation:** Lowest atomic surface density (~6.78 × 10¹⁴ atoms/cm²) → **slowest** but most *uniform* oxidation.

**Why (100) is preferred in MEMS:**
1. **Slower growth** gives better thickness control for thin gate oxides.
2. **Lower surface state density** $D_{it}$ → better electronic device performance.
3. **Anisotropic etching compatibility:** KOH etches (100) planes up to **400× faster** than (111) planes, enabling precise V-grooves, pyramidal pits, and suspended structures.
4. **Lower parasitic capacitance** in MOS structures.
5. **Better native oxide uniformity** → fewer pinholes in thin films.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often confuse *atomic surface density* with *available bond density*. The key is that **(111)** has 1 dangling bond per surface atom while **(100)** has 2, but the (111) surface has higher *atomic packing density*, so fewer total surface atoms are available per cm² for reaction — and yet the rate is *higher* on (111) due to the **higher density of available surface atoms per unit area**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Two oxidations:** Dry (O₂, high quality, slow) vs. Wet (H₂O, fast, slightly lower quality).
- [x] **Master equation:** $x^{2} + A x = B(t + \tau)$ — must be quoted *exactly* in KTU exams.
- [x] **Linear regime:** $x \ll A$ → growth $\propto t$, surface-reaction-limited, **thin oxides**.
- [x] **Parabolic regime:** $x \gg A$ → growth $\propto \sqrt{t}$, diffusion-limited, **thick oxides**.
- [x] **Activation energies (KTU favorite):** $E_{B} = 1.23$ eV (dry), $0.78$ eV (wet); $E_{B/A} = 1.80$ eV (dry), $0.71$ eV (wet).
- [x] **Consumption rule:** 0.44 μm of Si consumed per 1 μm of SiO₂ grown; 0.56 μm projects above the original surface.
- [x] **Orientation:** (111) > (110) > (100) in growth rate; **(100) preferred in MEMS** for etch compatibility.
- [x] **Role in MEMS:** Gate dielectric, diffusion mask, **sacrificial layer**, isolation, structural membrane.
- [x] **Native oxide:** $x_{i} \approx 1$–2 nm forms at room temperature in air — accounts for $\tau$ shift.
- [x] **Why thermal > deposited:** Thermally grown SiO₂ is denser, has lower interface trap density, and better Si/SiO₂ interface quality.
- [x] **Examiner's tip:** Always state the **assumptions** of the Deal–Grove model (1-D, steady-state, Fickian diffusion) before writing the equation in your answer script.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical Vapour Deposition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMET836 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Physical Vapour Deposition ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Vapour Deposition (PVD)** is a vacuum-deposition technique in which a source material in solid (or occasionally liquid) form is physically converted into its vapour phase (atoms, molecules, or ions) by means of thermal energy, kinetic energy of ion bombardment, or photon energy, and is subsequently transported under low-pressure (typically **10⁻² to 10⁻⁶ Torr**) conditions to a substrate where it condenses to form a thin film of the same composition as the source. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for MEMS fabrication (OEMET836 Module 3), PVD is grouped with Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD) under **Thin-Film Deposition Processes**, and the unit outcome expects the learner to compare the two families in terms of: - Film purity - Step coverage - Deposition rate - Substrate temperature - Equipment cost and process complexity. ### 1.2 The PVD Family Tree (KTU Classification) | Branch | Energy Source | Typical Use in MEMS | |---|---|---| | **Thermal Evaporation** (Resistive, E-beam) | Thermal (Joule / e⁻ beam) | Metal electrodes (Au, Al, Cr) | | **Sputtering** (DC, RF, Magnetron) | Kinetic (plasma ions) | Hard coatings, piezo films (AlN, ZnO) | | **Pulsed Laser Deposition (PLD)** | Photonic (Excimer laser) | Oxide thin films, complex stoichiometries | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Mist Fountain of Thin Films" > [!NOTE] > **Intuition:** Imagine boiling a kettle of water (the *source material*) inside a closed, partially evacuated glass bell jar. Molecules that gain enough thermal energy to escape the liquid surface fly out in straight lines until they hit the cooler walls of the jar. The *substrate* is placed inside the jar like a cold plate. Wherever a flying vapour atom strikes the cold plate, it "sticks" and freezes into a solid layer — atom by atom. > > In **thermal evaporation**, the kettle is heated electrically. In **sputtering**, instead of boiling, you throw tiny atomic "bullets" (plasma ions) at a solid target, knocking atoms loose. In **PLD**, a powerful laser pulse acts like a microscopic explosion, blasting atoms off the target surface. ### 1.4 Defining Physical Quantities (Bold Standard Metrics) - **Working Pressure:** **10⁻² – 10⁻⁶ Torr** (≈ 1.33 × 10⁻⁴ – 1.33 × 10⁻⁸ Pa) - **Deposition Rate:** **1 – 100 nm/min** (typical for evaporation), up to **µm/min** for magnetron sputtering. - **Mean Free Path (λ):** **10 cm – 1 m** at 10⁻⁴ Torr — much larger than chamber dimensions, ensuring line-of-sight transport. - **Substrate Temperature:** **25 °C – 500 °C** (room temperature for many metals). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Sequential Physical Stages of Every PVD Process > [!NOTE] > Every PVD technique — whether evaporation or sputtering — obeys the same universal three-stage physics sequence. Understanding this "pipeline" is the single most important KTU takeaway. **Stage 1 — Vapour Generation:** A solid target is converted into energetic atoms/ions/molecules. - *Evaporation*: $E_{thermal} \geq \Delta H_{vap}$ (latent heat of vaporisation). - *Sputtering*: Momentum transfer from incident ion (typically Ar⁺ at **300 – 1000 eV**) to target atom. The transfer is governed by the **kinematic factor**: $$K = \frac{4 M_1 M_2}{(M_1 + M_2)^2}$$ where $M_1$ is the ion mass, $M_2$ is the target atom mass. Maximum $K = 1$ when $M_1 = M_2$. **Stage 2 — Vapour Transport:** Atoms travel through vacuum with no collisions (mean free path $\lambda \gg L_{chamber}$). The relevant collisional parameter is: $$\lambda = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \, \pi \, d^2 \, P}$$ where $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K, $d$ is the atomic diameter (~ 0.3 nm), $P$ is the pressure. **Stage 3 — Film Growth (Condensation):** Atoms strike the substrate, lose kinetic energy (thermal accommodation), diffuse, and nucleate islands that coalesce into a continuous film. A key parameter is the **sticking coefficient** $S_c$: $$S_c = \frac{\text{Number of atoms that stick}}{\text{Number of atoms that strike}}$$ For most metals on clean substrates, **$S_c \approx 0.5$ – 1.0** at room temperature. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table consolidates every equation you must memorise for the OEMET836 Module 3 PVD examination. Keep it on a single revision sheet. | # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | KTU Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi\,d^2\,P}$ | Mean free path | Calculate vacuum level required | | 2 | $K = \dfrac{4 M_1 M_2}{(M_1 + M_2)^2}$ | Momentum transfer | Sputtering yield estimates | | 3 | $Y_s = \dfrac{\text{atoms ejected}}{\text{incident ion}}$ | Sputtering yield | DC/RF sputtering rate | | 4 | $\phi_m = \phi_0 \cos\theta \cos\phi / \pi r^2$ | Knudsen (cosine) law | Evaporation flux distribution | | 5 | $\dot{m} = p_s A \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{2 \pi k_B T}}$ | Hertz–Knudsen evaporation | Thermal evaporation rate | | 6 | $R = \dfrac{N \, M}{\rho \, N_A}$ | Deposition rate (thickness/time) | Process engineering | | 7 | $E_{th} = \dfrac{4\,(M_1 M_2)}{(M_1 + M_2)^2}\,E_{ion}$ | Energy transferred in elastic collision | Sputtering physics | | 8 | $V_s = V_p - V_b + V_{dc}$ | Plasma sheath relation | RF self-bias | > **CRITICAL TYPESET NOTE:** Whenever the absolute-value or magnitude notation appears *inside* a table row, it has been replaced with $\vert$ or `mid` to prevent markdown-table syntax breakage. Always use `\vert x \vert` instead of `|x|` inside tables. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **MEMS Metallisation:** PVD-deposited Al, Au, Cr/Au form electrical interconnects and bond pads. - **Optical MEMS (MOEMS):** Dielectric mirrors for DLP, optical filters use PVD-deposited $\text{SiO}_2$ / $\text{TiO}_2$ stacks. - **Piezoelectric MEMS:** Sputtered AlN or ZnO films power FBAR filters and inkjet heads. - **Tribological Coatings:** Hard PVD TiN coatings on micromachined tool bits and gears. - **Packaging & Hermetic Sealing:** PVD under-layer metallisation enables wafer-level bonding (anodic, eutectic). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Knudsen (Cosine) Law of Evaporation — $\phi_m(\theta)$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters:** In every KTU exam question on thermal evaporation, the examiner expects you to derive the angular distribution of mass flux from a point source. This is the single most-tested derivation in Module 3. **Premise:** A small evaporating surface element $dA$ at temperature $T$ emits atoms isotropically. At any point a distance $r$ away, the mass arriving per unit area per unit time isotropically emitted must satisfy conservation of mass. **Step 1.** Consider the source surface $dA$ emitting atoms into the half-space above it. Let $\dot{N}$ be the total rate of atoms leaving $dA$ (atoms per second). Because emission is isotropic into the solid angle of a hemisphere ($\Omega = 2\pi$ sr), the rate per unit solid angle is: $$\frac{d\dot{N}}{d\Omega} = \frac{\dot{N}}{2\pi}$$ **Step 2.** A receiver surface at distance $r$ subtends a solid angle $d\Omega = dA_r / r^2$. Therefore, the number of atoms striking $dA_r$ per second is: $$d\dot{N}_{received} = \frac{\dot{N}}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{dA_r}{r^2}$$ **Step 3.** The flux (atoms per unit area per second) on the receiver is obtained by dividing by $dA_r$: $$\phi = \frac{\dot{N}}{2\pi \, r^2}$$ **Step 4.** If the source surface is tilted, only the *projected* area $dA \cos\theta$ actually "sees" the receiver direction, where $\theta$ is the angle between the surface normal and the line joining source to receiver. Including the projected-area factor for both source and receiver (angle $\phi$ with the receiver normal): $$\boxed{\,\phi_m = \frac{\phi_0 \cos\theta \cos\phi}{\pi r^{2}}\,}$$ This is the **Knudsen cosine law** — the deposition rate is maximum when $\theta = \phi = 0$, i.e., on-axis, and falls off as the cosine of both tilt angles. This is why **planetary rotation of substrates** is used in industrial evaporators to improve uniformity. ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Hertz–Knudsen Equation for Evaporation Rate **Step 1.** A surface at temperature $T$ has a vapour pressure $p_s(T)$ described by the Clausius–Clapeyron relation: $$\ln p_s = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R T} + C$$ **Step 2.** In equilibrium, the rate of atoms leaving equals the rate returning (zero net flux). Using the kinetic theory of ideal gases, the flux of atoms hitting a surface from a gas at pressure $p$ is: $$\phi_{hit} = \frac{p}{\sqrt{2 \pi m k_B T}}$$ **Step 3.** For a free surface (no return flux), the net evaporative mass flux is: $$\dot{m} = (p_s - p) \sqrt{\frac{M}{2 \pi k_B T}}$$ **Step 4.** Under high vacuum ($p \ll p_s$), the equation reduces to: $$\boxed{\,\dot{m}_{max} \approx p_s \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{2 \pi k_B T}}\,}$$ This explains why **high-melting-point metals (W, Mo, Ta)** are hard to evaporate thermally — their $p_s$ at sensible temperatures is too low, hence the development of **electron-beam evaporation**. ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Mean Free Path Calculation — Typical Exam Number **Problem (KTU-Style):** A vacuum chamber operates at $P = 10^{-4}$ Torr. The atomic diameter of the residual gas (mostly nitrogen) is $d = 0.32 \text{ nm}$. The chamber is at $T = 300 \text{ K}$. Find the mean free path. **Step 1.** Convert pressure to SI: $P = 10^{-4} \times 133.322 = 1.333 \times 10^{-2}$ Pa. **Step 2.** Insert into $\lambda = k_B T / (\sqrt{2}\,\pi\,d^2 P)$: $$\lambda = \frac{(1.38 \times 10^{-23})(300)}{\sqrt{2} \, \pi \, (0.32 \times 10^{-9})^2 \, (1.333 \times 10^{-2})}$$ **Step 3.** Evaluate numerator: $$1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300 = 4.14 \times 10^{-21} \text{ J}$$ **Step 4.** Evaluate denominator — first $d^2$: $$(0.32 \times 10^{-9})^2 = 1.024 \times 10^{-19} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 5.** Combine: $$\sqrt{2}\,\pi\,d^2 P = 1.414 \times 3.1416 \times 1.024 \times 10^{-19} \times 1.333 \times 10^{-2} = 6.07 \times 10^{-21}$$ **Step 6.** Final result: $$\boxed{\,\lambda = \frac{4.14 \times 10^{-21}}{6.07 \times 10^{-21}} \approx 0.682 \text{ m}\,}$$ **Interpretation:** At $10^{-4}$ Torr, atoms can travel nearly a metre without collision — far exceeding any practical chamber size, validating the *line-of-sight* assumption of PVD. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing the Three PVD Variants | Parameter | Thermal Evap. | E-beam Evap. | DC/RF Sputtering | PLD | |---|---|---|---|---| | Energy source | Resistive heater | Focused e⁻ beam | Plasma ions | Excimer laser | | Achievable rate | Low–medium | Medium | Low–high | Very low (pulsed) | | Film purity | Excellent (no plasma) | Excellent | Risk of Ar inclusion | Excellent | | Step coverage | **Poor** (line-of-sight) | Poor | **Good** (energetic atoms) | Fair | | Stoichiometry | Elemental only | Elemental | Compounds OK | **Best for oxides** | | Substrate heating | Low | Medium | Medium–high | High (local) | | Capital cost | Low | Medium–high | Medium | High | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a PVD System Because PVD equipment diagrams involve vacuum hardware that is difficult to render with Mermaid syntax, the following diagram maps the *functional signal/material flow* of a generic PVD chamber, which is the level of abstraction expected in KTU Module 3 viva and theory questions. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Vacuum Pump Stack
Rotary + Turbo] --> B[Process Chamber
10^-6 Torr Base] B --> C[Energy Source Subsystem] C --> C1[Thermal: Tungsten Coil] C --> C2[E-beam: e-Gun + Sweep Coil] C --> C3[Plasma: RF/DC Generator + Match] C --> C4[Laser: Excimer 248 nm] C1 --> D[Target / Source Material] C2 --> D C3 --> D C4 --> D D --> E[Vapour Flux Stream
Mean Free Path L] B --> F[Substrate Holder
Heated + Planetary Rotated] E --> F F --> G[Thin Film Nucleation
Sticking Coefficient S_c] G --> H[In-situ Monitoring
QCM + Optical Sensor] H --> I[Process End-Point
Film Thickness Closed Loop] style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0277bd style B fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 style C fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a style D fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b style F fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32 style I fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 ``` **Reading the diagram:** The four energy-source branches (C1–C4) feed into the same target (D), which is the KTU-recognised unifying principle — **only the vapour-generation mechanism changes; transport and condensation physics remain identical.** ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: DC/RF Magnetron Sputtering Subsystem ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Ar Gas Inlet
MFC 10-50 sccm] --> B[Process Chamber
10^-3 Torr] B --> C[Cathode Target] C --> D[DC or RF Power Supply
300-1000 W] D --> E[Plasma Sheath
Negative Self-Bias] E --> F[Ar+ Ion Bombardment] F --> G[Sputtered Atoms] G --> H[Substrate on Anode] H --> I[Growing Thin Film] J[Magnet Array Behind Target] --> E style A fill:#e1f5fe style D fill:#fff3e0 style J fill:#f3e5f5 style I fill:#e8f5e9 ``` > [!NOTE] > The **magnet array (J)** traps secondary electrons in cycloidal paths near the cathode, dramatically increasing ionisation efficiency. This is why *magnetron* sputtering can operate at lower pressures (10⁻³ Torr) and achieve **10× higher deposition rates** than diode sputtering. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define Physical Vapour Deposition. List any two PVD techniques.* **Model Answer:** Physical Vapour Deposition (PVD) is a vacuum-based thin-film deposition process in which a solid source material is converted into its vapour phase by physical means (thermal energy, kinetic energy of ion bombardment, or photon energy), transported through a low-pressure environment, and condensed onto a substrate to form a thin film. **Two techniques:** (i) Thermal evaporation, (ii) DC/RF sputtering. *(Acceptable: e-beam evaporation, pulsed laser deposition.)* **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]** *Write the expression for mean free path of a vapour atom in a PVD chamber and state its significance.* **Model Answer:** The mean free path is $\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi\,d^{2}\,P}$. **Significance:** It represents the average distance a vapour atom travels before colliding with a residual gas molecule. For PVD to operate in the line-of-sight regime, $\lambda$ must be much larger than the source-to-substrate distance, which is achieved by lowering $P$ to **10⁻⁴ – 10⁻⁶ Torr**. --- ### 5.2 Part B Question Choice (14 Marks) #### **Question A — Full-Length 14-Mark Question** *(verified from KTU 2024 expected pattern)* > [KTU University Exam – May 2024] > **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **DC magnetron sputtering system** for thin-film deposition. Discuss the role of the magnetic field. *(7 marks)* > **(b)** Derive the **Knudsen cosine law** for evaporation from a point source. Show that the maximum flux occurs on the substrate normal to the source. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution — (a):** 1. **Chamber & Vacuum:** A stainless-steel chamber is evacuated to **10⁻⁶ Torr** base pressure by a turbomolecular pump backed by a rotary pump. Working pressure of **10⁻³ Torr** is then established by introducing Ar through a mass-flow controller. 2. **Target Cathode:** The sputter target (e.g., Al) is mounted on a water-cooled cathode. Behind the target sits a permanent-magnet array (typically inner-N, outer-S) producing a magnetic field parallel to the target surface. 3. **Plasma Ignition:** A DC voltage of **300–1000 V** is applied. The electric field $E$ (perpendicular to target) and magnetic field $B$ (parallel) form crossed fields. Secondary electrons emitted from the target are confined in **cycloidal $E \times B$ drift** paths near the target, raising the local ionisation efficiency. 4. **Sputtering Process:** Ar⁺ ions accelerated across the cathode dark space bombard the target with energies **300–1000 eV**. Momentum transfer (governed by $K = 4M_1M_2/(M_1+M_2)^2$) ejects target atoms (sputtering yield $Y_s \approx 0.5$–$2$ atoms/ion for metals). Ejected atoms travel ballistically to the substrate, where they condense. 5. **Role of Magnetic Field:** (i) Confines electrons → higher plasma density → higher ion current → higher deposition rate at lower pressure. (ii) Reduces electron bombardment of substrate → lower substrate heating. (iii) Enables **low-pressure (10⁻³ Torr)** operation with acceptable rates. **Model Solution — (b):** Refer to **Derivation 1 in Section 3.1**. The Knudsen law gives $\phi_m = \phi_0 \cos\theta \cos\phi / \pi r^2$. The maximum occurs when both $\theta = 0$ and $\phi = 0$, i.e., when the substrate surface is **perpendicular to the line joining it to the source**, and the source surface faces the substrate directly. Differentiating $\phi_m$ with respect to $\theta$ and setting to zero confirms $\theta = 0$ is the maximum (since $\cos\theta$ has maximum derivative zero at $\theta = 0$). #### **Question B — Internal Choice Alternative (14 Marks)** > [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] > **(a)** Explain the **thermal evaporation technique** with a schematic diagram. Why are refractory metals better deposited using **electron-beam evaporation**? *(7 marks)* > **(b)** A PVD chamber operates at a pressure of $5 \times 10^{-4}$ Torr and temperature 300 K. The mean atomic diameter of the residual gas molecule is 0.3 nm. Calculate the mean free path of the evaporated atom. State whether the chamber is suitable for line-of-sight PVD if the source-to-substrate distance is 25 cm. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution — (a):** 1. **Thermal Evaporation Process:** A source material (e.g., Au) is placed in a tungsten or molybdenum boat (or filament), which is resistively heated inside a vacuum chamber at **10⁻⁵ – 10⁻⁶ Torr**. When the boat temperature exceeds the source's melting point (Au: 1064 °C), vapour atoms are ejected thermally with rate given by the Hertz–Knudsen equation. 2. **Quartz Crystal Monitor (QCM):** A separate sensor crystal oscillates at 6 MHz; deposited mass lowers its frequency, providing real-time thickness feedback. 3. **Limitation with Refractory Metals:** W (Tm = 3422 °C), Mo (2623 °C), Ta (3017 °C) — the temperatures required exceed the capability of resistive boats (which themselves melt or react). Contamination from the crucible material is also severe. 4. **Electron-Beam Solution:** A focused high-power e-beam (5–30 kV, kW-level) is magnetically deflected onto the target inside a water-cooled copper crucible. Only the *top* of the source melts, so the molten pool is contained by its own surface tension with minimal crucible contact. Temperatures exceeding **3000 °C** are reached locally, evaporating even W. **Model Solution — (b):** Given: $P = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ Torr = $5 \times 10^{-4} \times 133.322 = 6.666 \times 10^{-2}$ Pa; $T = 300$ K; $d = 0.3 \times 10^{-9}$ m; $L = 0.25$ m. $$\lambda = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi\,d^{2}\,P} = \frac{(1.38 \times 10^{-23})(300)}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi\,(0.3 \times 10^{-9})^2\,(6.666 \times 10^{-2})}$$ Numerator: $4.14 \times 10^{-21}$. Denominator: $1.414 \times 3.1416 \times 9 \times 10^{-20} \times 6.666 \times 10^{-2} \approx 2.66 \times 10^{-20}$. $$\lambda \approx 0.156 \text{ m} = 15.6 \text{ cm}$$ Since $\lambda = 15.6$ cm $\lt L = 25$ cm, **the chamber is NOT strictly suitable** for line-of-sight PVD — atoms will suffer ~1 collision en route, leading to scattering losses and degraded film uniformity. **Recommendation:** Reduce pressure by ~2× (to $2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ Torr) so that $\lambda \geq 0.25$ m. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls** > 1. **Forgetting the $1/\pi$ factor** in the Knudsen cosine law. Students often write $\phi_m \propto \cos\theta \cos\phi / r^2$ and lose 2 marks for missing the normalisation constant that comes from integrating over a hemisphere. > 2. **Unit conversion in mean-free-path problems.** Always convert Torr → Pa ($1$ Torr $= 133.322$ Pa) *first* before substituting into SI formulae. Marks are specifically awarded for correct unit handling. > 3. **Confusing thermal evaporation with CVD.** A frequent error is attributing chemical reactions to PVD. PVD is purely *physical*; CVD involves *chemical* decomposition of precursors. > 4. **Stating "step coverage is good for evaporation."** Wrong — evaporation has **poor** step coverage (line-of-sight shadowing). Only sputtering and CVD have good step coverage. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 3 / Physical Vapour Deposition** - ✅ PVD = physical conversion of solid → vapour → thin film under vacuum (10⁻²–10⁻⁶ Torr). - ✅ Three PVD families: **Evaporation**, **Sputtering**, **PLD**. - ✅ All PVD obeys three universal stages: **vapour generation → vapour transport → film growth**. - ✅ Mean free path formula: $\lambda = k_B T / (\sqrt{2}\,\pi\,d^2 P)$. At 10⁻⁴ Torr, $\lambda \approx 0.7$ m. - ✅ **Knudsen cosine law:** $\phi_m = \phi_0 \cos\theta \cos\phi / \pi r^2$ — flux is maximum on-axis. - ✅ **Hertz–Knudsen rate:** $\dot{m} = p_s \sqrt{M/(2\pi k_B T)}$. - ✅ **Sputtering momentum transfer factor:** $K = 4M_1M_2/(M_1+M_2)^2$ (max = 1 when ion mass = target mass). - ✅ **Sticking coefficient** $S_c$ is typically **0.5–1.0** for metals on clean substrates. - ✅ **Thermal evaporation** = excellent purity, poor step coverage, low cost, limited to low-melting-point metals. - ✅ **E-beam evaporation** = needed for refractory metals (W, Mo, Ta) — local temperatures >3000 °C. - ✅ **DC Sputtering** = conductive targets; **RF Sputtering** = insulating targets (because of self-bias DC offset $V_{dc}$). - ✅ **Magnetron** = crossed $E \times B$ fields trap electrons → 10× higher rate at 10× lower pressure. - ✅ **PLD** = best for stoichiometric oxide films (e.g., PZT, BST, high-Tc superconductors). - ✅ Always use **$\vert$** or **\mid** instead of the literal pipe character **\vert** inside markdown tables to avoid syntax breakage. - ✅ Always convert Torr to Pascals **before** plugging into SI equations in numerical problems.

topics.title: Etching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET836 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Etching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Definition — Etching**
> **Etching** is a subtractive microfabrication process in which material is selectively removed from a substrate (typically silicon, silicon dioxide, metals, or polymers) using chemical, physical, or a combination of chemical–physical reactions, governed by a patterned mask (photoresist, oxide, nitride, or metal) to define the geometric features of a MEMS or IC device.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, etching is classified into two primary families:

| Family | Energy Source | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Wet Etching** | Chemical (liquid phase) | Pure chemical reaction between etchant and substrate |
| **Dry Etching** | Plasma / Ion Beam / Reactive Ion | Physical (sputtering) and/or chemical (radical reaction) |

> [!NOTE] **Syllabus Highlight**
> KTU Module 3 of *OEMET836 – Micro Electro Mechanical Systems* mandates coverage of **isotropic vs anisotropic etching**, **wet chemical etching** (KOH, TMAH, HF), **dry etching** (plasma, RIE, DRIE / Bosch process), **etch selectivity, etch rate, and anisotropy factor**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **stone sculptor carving a granite statue**:

- You cover the unwanted portions with a hard, wax-like material (**photoresist / mask**).
- You then either pour acid over the stone (**wet etching**) or fire high-speed sand at it (**dry etching / ion bombardment**).
- The exposed stone dissolves / chips away, while the protected stone remains intact.

In **MEMS**, the "stone" is a silicon wafer, the "wax mask" is photoresist or SiO₂, and the sculptor is the etchant (HF, KOH, SF₆ plasma, etc.). The **shape of the cavity, sidewall profile, and undercut** depend entirely on how the etchant interacts with the crystal planes of silicon.

## 1.3 The Three Sacred Metrics of Etching

Every etching process — whether for a pressure sensor diaphragm, an accelerometer proof mass, or a microfluidic channel — is evaluated by **three fundamental parameters** that examiners love to test:

> [!IMPORTANT] **The Trinity of Etching Parameters**
> 1. **Etch Rate (ER)** — *How fast?* Measured in **nm/min** or **μm/min**.
> 2. **Selectivity (S)** — *How cleanly does it stop at the mask/underlying layer?* A dimensionless ratio.
> 3. **Anisotropy (A)** — *How vertical are the sidewalls?* A dimensionless factor, **0 (fully isotropic) to 1 (fully anisotropic)**.

A perfect MEMS bulk micromachining process demands: **High Etch Rate, High Selectivity, and High Anisotropy (≈ 1)**.

## 1.4 Why Etching Matters in MEMS

Unlike CMOS ICs that are planar, MEMS devices require **3D sculpted structures**: cantilevers, membranes, channels, beams, and comb drives. Etching — combined with lithography — is the *only* practical route to realize these 3D microstructures in silicon. Without precise etching, there is **no MEMS**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Etching Processes

### A. Wet Chemical Etching

Wet etching uses **liquid-phase chemicals** to dissolve the exposed material. It is further classified by how the etch front progresses relative to the crystal lattice:

#### (i) Isotropic Wet Etching
- Etches in **all directions at equal rate**.
- No crystallographic preference.
- Produces **rounded sidewalls** with significant **undercut** beneath the mask.
- Classic example: **HF etching of SiO₂**, **HNA (HF + HNO₃ + CH₃COOH) etching of silicon**.

#### (ii) Anisotropic Wet Etching
- Etches **different crystallographic directions at different rates**.
- Exploits the differing atomic densities of silicon planes: **{100}, {110}, {111}**.
- Produces **well-defined geometric cavities** (V-grooves, pyramids, membranes).
- Classic examples: **KOH (Potassium Hydroxide)**, **TMAH (Tetramethyl Ammonium Hydroxide)**, **EDP (Ethylene Diamine Pyrocatechol)**.

#### (iii) The Magic of KOH on Si(100)
- Etches **Si(100) plane ~100× faster** than Si(111).
- This ratio is called the **Anisotropy Ratio**.
- Self-limiting **V-grooves** form when the etch reaches the {111} planes — these planes act as natural etch stops.
- **KOH etch rate of Si(100) at 80 °C, 30 wt% ≈ 1.4 μm/min**.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Anisotropic Etching Geometry (KTU Favourite)**
> For a rectangular mask opening aligned along ⟨110⟩ on a Si(100) wafer, the etched pit is a **pyramidal cavity** bounded by four {111} planes. The sidewall angle with respect to the wafer surface is **54.74°**.
> The depth-to-width relationship: $h = \frac{w}{\sqrt{2}}$ where $w$ is the mask opening width.

### B. Dry Etching (Plasma-Based)

Dry etching uses **gaseous reactive species and/or ions** generated in a plasma. It is the dominant technique in modern MEMS/IC fabs due to its superior control and compatibility with lithography.

| Dry Etching Type | Mechanism | Anisotropy | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Plasma Etching (PE)** | Chemical (free radicals) | Low–Medium | Barrel reactor, parallel plate |
| **Reactive Ion Etching (RIE)** | Chemical + Ion Bombardment | Medium–High | CF₄, SF₆ plasmas |
| **Sputter Etching** | Pure Physical (Ar⁺ ions) | High (but poor selectivity) | Ion milling |
| **DRIE — Bosch Process** | Alternating Etch/Passivation | Very High (≈ 1) | Deep Si etching |
| **ICP (Inductively Coupled Plasma)** | High-density plasma + bias | High | Modern DRIE tool |

#### The Bosch Process (Deep Reactive Ion Etching — DRIE)
This is the **workhorse** for high-aspect-ratio MEMS structures (through-wafer vias, gyroscopes, inkjet nozzles). Invented by **Robert Bosch GmbH (~1994)**, it alternates two steps cyclically:

1. **Etch step (SF₆)** — Sulfur hexafluoride plasma generates **F radicals** that isotropically attack silicon. Duration: ~5–10 s.
2. **Passivation step (C₄F₈)** — Octafluorocyclobutane deposits a **fluoropolymer (Teflon-like) film** on all surfaces. Duration: ~2–5 s.
3. The **ion bombardment** in the next etch step preferentially removes the polymer from horizontal surfaces (because ions travel vertically), exposing the silicon there for further etching. The vertical sidewalls remain protected.

> [!NOTE] **Side Effect — Scalloping**
> The cyclic nature of the Bosch process produces a characteristic **scalloped sidewall** (typical amplitude 50–500 nm). For ultra-smooth sidewalls, engineers use the **Cryogenic DRIE** variant, which uses continuous SF₆/O₂ plasma at low temperatures (−100 °C).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **Master these equations — they appear repeatedly in KTU ESE.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Etch Rate** | $ER = \dfrac{\Delta h}{\Delta t}$ | μm/min or nm/min |
| 2 | **Selectivity** | $S = \dfrac{ER_{\text{substrate}}}{ER_{\text{mask}}}$ | Dimensionless; ideally ≫ 1 |
| 3 | **Anisotropy Factor** | $A = 1 - \dfrac{ER_{\text{lateral}}}{ER_{\text{vertical}}}$ | 0 (isotropic) to 1 (fully anisotropic) |
| 4 | **Anisotropy Ratio** | $AR = \dfrac{ER_{\langle 100\rangle}}{ER_{\langle 111\rangle}}$ | For KOH, typically 30–100 |
| 5 | **Arrhenius Etch Rate** | $ER = ER_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)$ | $E_a$ in eV, $k_B = 8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/K |
| 6 | **V-groove Depth** | $h = \dfrac{w}{\sqrt{2}}$ | For Si(100), ⟨110⟩ aligned mask |
| 7 | **Mass Transport Limit** | $ER_{max} = \dfrac{D \cdot C_s}{\delta}$ | $D$ = diffusivity, $\delta$ = boundary layer |
| 8 | **DRIE Aspect Ratio** | $AR_{\text{hole}} = \dfrac{\text{depth}}{\text{width}}$ | Up to 50:1 achievable |
| 9 | **Undercut Width** | $u = ER_{\text{lateral}} \cdot t_{\text{etch}}$ | Critical for mask dimensioning |
| 10 | **Activation Energy (KOH-Si)** | $E_a \approx 0.595$ eV | For Si(100) in 30% KOH |

> [!WARNING] **Pitfall — Selectivity vs Anisotropy**
> These two are **frequently confused** in KTU exams. **Selectivity** compares etch rates between *two different materials* (substrate vs mask). **Anisotropy** compares etch rates in *different directions* of the *same material*.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

| Application | Preferred Etching |
|---|---|
| Pressure sensor diaphragms | **Anisotropic KOH / TMAH** wet etch (precise thickness control) |
| Comb-drive accelerometers | **DRIE / RIE** (vertical sidewalls for capacitor gap) |
| Through-Silicon Vias (TSV) | **DRIE Bosch process** (high aspect ratio) |
| Microfluidic channels | **Isotropic wet** (rounded channels) or **DRIE** (rectangular) |
| RF MEMS switches | **RIE** (smooth sidewalls for low loss) |
| CMOS gate definition | **RIE** (nanometre precision) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Anisotropy Factor from Etch Rate Measurements

The **anisotropy factor (A)** is the most-tested etching concept in KTU exams. Let us derive it from first principles.

**Step 1 — Define the two etch rates:**
Let $V_v$ be the vertical etch rate (depth direction, into the substrate) and $V_l$ be the lateral etch rate (sideways, undercut beneath the mask).

**Step 2 — Define the extreme cases:**
- If $V_l = 0$ (no lateral etching) → all etch proceeds vertically → **fully anisotropic** → $A = 1$.
- If $V_l = V_v$ (equal in all directions) → **fully isotropic** → $A = 0$.

**Step 3 — Construct a linear interpolation that satisfies both extremes:**

$$
A = 1 - \frac{V_l}{V_v}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify boundary conditions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Case A (fully anisotropic)}: & \quad V_l = 0 \\
& A = 1 - \frac{0}{V_v} = 1 \quad \checkmark \\[6pt]
\text{Case B (fully isotropic)}: & \quad V_l = V_v \\
& A = 1 - \frac{V_v}{V_v} = 0 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the undercut width over an etch time $t$:**

$$
u = V_l \cdot t = V_v (1 - A) \cdot t
$$

> **Interpretation:** A mask dimension must be oversized by $2u$ on each side to compensate for undercut — a critical design rule in KTU 2024 design projects.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — KOH V-Groove Geometry on Si(100)

When KOH etches a rectangular mask opening aligned along ⟨110⟩ on a Si(100) wafer, the etch stops on {111} planes.

**Step 1 — Geometry of the {111} planes:**
The angle between the {100} surface plane and the {111} sidewall plane is determined by their Miller indices:

$$
\cos\theta = \frac{h_1 h_2 + k_1 k_2 + l_1 l_2}{\sqrt{h_1^2 + k_1^2 + l_1^2}\,\sqrt{h_2^2 + k_2^2 + l_2^2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute {100} (h₁,k₁,l₁) = (1,0,0) and {111} (h₂,k₂,l₂) = (1,1,1):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos\theta &= \frac{(1)(1) + (0)(1) + (0)(1)}{\sqrt{1^2+0^2+0^2}\,\sqrt{1^2+1^2+1^2}} \\
&= \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \\
\theta &= \arccos\!\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \\
\theta &= 54.7356^\circ
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Relate depth $h$ to mask opening width $w$:**
From the right-triangle cross-section, $\tan\theta = \dfrac{w/2}{h}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= \frac{w/2}{\tan\theta} = \frac{w}{2\tan 54.7356^\circ} \\
\tan 54.7356^\circ &= \sqrt{2} \\
\therefore \quad h &= \frac{w}{2\sqrt{2}} \cdot 2 = \frac{w}{\sqrt{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final Result (KTU High-Yield):**

$$
\boxed{\,h = \frac{w}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707\, w\,}
$$

> **Interpretation:** For a mask opening of 100 μm, the V-groove depth will be ~70.7 μm. After this depth, the etch **self-terminates** because the {111} planes are essentially un-attacked by KOH. This is the foundation of **bulk micromachining** for diaphragms and V-grooves.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Arrhenius Temperature Dependence of Etch Rate

**Step 1 — Start with the Arrhenius equation:**

$$
ER = ER_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Take natural logarithm:**

$$
\ln(ER) = \ln(ER_0) - \frac{E_a}{k_B}\cdot \frac{1}{T}
$$

**Step 3 — This is linear in $1/T$.** A plot of $\ln(ER)$ vs $1/T$ yields slope $-E_a/k_B$ and intercept $\ln(ER_0)$.

**Step 4 — To find $E_a$ from two temperature data points** $(T_1, ER_1)$ and $(T_2, ER_2)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln(ER_1) - \ln(ER_2) &= -\frac{E_a}{k_B}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right) \\
\ln\!\left(\frac{ER_1}{ER_2}\right) &= \frac{E_a}{k_B}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right) \\
E_a &= \frac{k_B \ln(ER_1/ER_2)}{\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example (KTU-style):**
Given: $ER_1 = 0.5$ μm/min at $T_1 = 333$ K (60 °C); $ER_2 = 1.4$ μm/min at $T_2 = 353$ K (80 °C); $k_B = 8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/K.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_a &= \frac{(8.617\times 10^{-5})\,\ln(0.5/1.4)}{\frac{1}{353} - \frac{1}{333}} \\
&= \frac{(8.617\times 10^{-5})(-1.0296)}{(2.832\times 10^{-3}) - (3.003\times 10^{-3})} \\
&= \frac{-8.873\times 10^{-5}}{-1.71\times 10^{-4}} \\
E_a &\approx 0.519 \text{ eV}
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the experimental activation energy of Si(100) in KOH (literature: **~0.5–0.6 eV**), confirming the Arrhenius model.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — DRIE Aspect Ratio Limitation (RIE Lag)

In deep RIE, narrow trenches etch slower than wide trenches — known as **RIE Lag**. A simplified model:

**Step 1 — Assume the etch rate is limited by transport of reactive species to the bottom of the trench.**

**Step 2 — Fick's first law gives the flux $J$ at depth $h$:**

$$
J(h) = D \frac{C_0 - C_s}{h}
$$

where $D$ = diffusivity, $C_0$ = bulk concentration, $C_s$ = surface concentration.

**Step 3 — The etch rate is proportional to flux:**

$$
ER(h) \propto \frac{1}{h}
$$

**Step 4 — Narrower trenches have a higher effective $h/w$ ratio (aspect ratio), hence lower $ER$.** This is why **aspect-ratio-dependent etching (ARDE)** limits DRIE to about **50:1** in production.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Process | Etch Rate | Selectivity vs SiO₂ | Anisotropy | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **KOH (30%, 80 °C)** | 1.4 μm/min (Si{100}) | ~200 (vs SiO₂) | ~0.98 | Bulk micromachining |
| **TMAH (25%, 90 °C)** | 1.0 μm/min | ~1000 (vs SiO₂) | ~0.97 | CMOS-compatible |
| **HNA (isotropic Si)** | 1–5 μm/min | ~10 | ~0 | Smoothing, rounding |
| **RIE (SF₆)** | 0.5–2 μm/min | ~5 (vs resist) | 0.7–0.9 | Shallow features |
| **DRIE Bosch** | 2–10 μm/min | ~50 (vs resist) | >0.99 | Deep HAR features |
| **Cryo DRIE** | 1–3 μm/min | ~30 | >0.99 | Smooth sidewalls |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Etching Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["ETCHING PROCESSES"]:::root --> B["WET CHEMICAL ETCHING"]:::wet
    A --> C["DRY PLASMA ETCHING"]:::dry
    
    B --> B1["ISOTROPIC"]:::subwet
    B --> B2["ANISOTROPIC"]:::subwet
    
    B1 --> B1a["HF for SiO2"]
    B1 --> B1b["HNA for Silicon"]
    
    B2 --> B2a["KOH"]
    B2 --> B2b["TMAH"]
    B2 --> B2c["EDP"]
    
    C --> C1["PHYSICAL"]:::subdry
    C --> C2["CHEMICAL"]:::subdry
    C --> C3["PHYSIOCHEMICAL"]:::subdry
    
    C1 --> C1a["Sputter Etching - Ar ion"]
    C2 --> C2a["Barrel Plasma - CF4"]
    C3 --> C3a["RIE"]
    C3 --> C3b["ICP"]
    C3 --> C3c["DRIE Bosch Process"]
    C3 --> C3d["Cryogenic DRIE"]
    
    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:3px
    classDef wet fill:#0e7490,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef dry fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef subwet fill:#a7f3d0,stroke:#065f46,color:#000
    classDef sudry fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#000
```

## 4.2 KOH V-Groove Formation on Si(100) — Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["STEP 1: Si(100) Wafer with Thermal SiO2 Mask"]:::stage --> S2["STEP 2: Photolithography and Oxide Patterning"]:::stage
    S2 --> S3["STEP 3: BHF Etch to Open Mask Window"]:::stage
    S3 --> S4["STEP 4: KOH Etch 30 percent 80 C"]:::stage
    S4 --> S5["STEP 5: Etch Front Stops at Four 111 Planes"]:::stage
    S5 --> S6["STEP 6: Pyramid V Groove Final Structure"]:::stage
    S6 --> S7["STEP 7: Remove Mask BOE or HF Strip"]:::stage
    
    F1["h = w divided by root 2"]:::formula -.-> S5
    F2["Sidewall Angle = 54.74 degrees"]:::formula -.-> S6
    
    classDef stage fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef formula fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,color:#000,stroke-width:1.5px
```

## 4.3 Bosch DRIE Process — Cyclical Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["PHASE A: SF6 ETCH STEP Duration 5 to 10 s"]:::phaseA --> Q1["F radicals attack exposed Si isotropically"]:::detail
    Q1 --> P2["PHASE B: C4F8 PASSIVATION STEP Duration 2 to 5 s"]:::phaseB
    P2 --> Q2["Fluoropolymer Teflon like film coats ALL surfaces"]:::detail
    Q2 --> P3["PHASE C: ION BOMBARDMENT Vertical Ar or SFx ions"]:::phaseC
    P3 --> Q3["Polymer removed from horizontal surfaces only"]:::detail
    Q3 --> P4{"Sidewalls protected by polymer"}:::check
    P4 --> P5["Bottom Si exposed for next etch"]:::detail
    P5 --> P1
    
    R["RESULT: High Aspect Ratio Vertical Sidewalls with Scallops"]:::result
    P5 --> R
    
    classDef phaseA fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef phaseB fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef phaseC fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef detail fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#713f12,color:#000
    classDef check fill:#f3e8ff,stroke:#6b21a8,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Anisotropic vs Isotropic — Sidewall Profile Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ISO ["ISOTROPIC ETCHING - HF or HNA"]
        ISO_M["MASK"]:::mask
        ISO_E["Rounded cavity with UNDERCUT equal to depth"]:::cavity
        ISO_M --> ISO_E
    end
    
    subgraph ANISO ["ANISOTROPIC ETCHING - KOH or DRIE"]
        AN_M["MASK"]:::mask
        AN_E["Vertical or 54.74 degree cavity MINIMAL undercut"]:::cavityA
        AN_M --> AN_E
    end
    
    classDef mask fill:#374151,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef cavity fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000
    classDef cavityA fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#000
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define etching. List the three key parameters used to characterize any etching process and state the ideal value of each for MEMS bulk micromachining.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

**Definition (1 mark):**
Etching is the selective removal of material from a substrate using chemical, physical, or combined chemical–physical reactions through a patterned mask to define MEMS/IC microstructures.

**Three Key Parameters (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Definition | Ideal Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Etch Rate** | Material thickness removed per unit time | **High** (≥ 1 μm/min) |
| **Selectivity** | Ratio of substrate etch rate to mask etch rate | **High** (≫ 1) |
| **Anisotropy** | $A = 1 - V_l/V_v$ | **≈ 1** (fully anisotropic) |

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between isotropic and anisotropic etching. Give one example of each.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

| Feature | Isotropic Etching | Anisotropic Etching |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Etches equally in **all directions** | Etches **direction-dependent** |
| Undercut | **Large**, equal to etch depth | **Minimal** |
| Sidewall Profile | **Rounded** | **Vertical or angled planes** |
| Mask dependence | Mask + undercut | Mask + crystallographic planes |
| Example | **HF etching of SiO₂** | **KOH etching of Si(100)** |

**(1 mark)** for definition of each; **(1 mark)** for example.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> *As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer **one** of two internal-choice questions per module.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Module 3 Choice]`

**(a)** With a neat cross-sectional diagram, explain the formation of a **V-groove** when a Si(100) wafer is etched in **30% KOH at 80 °C** through a rectangular mask opening aligned along the ⟨110⟩ direction. Derive the relation between groove depth and mask opening width. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **selectivity** and **anisotropy** of KOH wet etching of silicon. How do **TMAH** and **EDP** compare with KOH as anisotropic etchants? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — V-Groove Formation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Process flow:**
1. A thermally grown SiO₂ layer (~1 μm) is patterned using photolithography and BHF.
2. The patterned wafer is immersed in **30 wt% KOH at 80 °C**.
3. KOH rapidly attacks Si(100) plane (~1.4 μm/min) and barely attacks Si(111) (~0.014 μm/min).
4. Four {111} planes emerge from the mask edges and form a **self-limiting pyramidal cavity**.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Geometric derivation:**

The angle between {100} surface and {111} sidewall:

$$
\cos\theta = \frac{(1)(1)+(0)(1)+(0)(1)}{(1)(\sqrt{3})} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \Rightarrow \theta = 54.74^\circ
$$

From the right-triangle geometry of the cross-section:

$$
\tan(54.74^\circ) = \frac{w/2}{h} = \sqrt{2}
$$

Solving for depth:

$$
\boxed{h = \frac{w}{2\sqrt{2}} \cdot 2 = \frac{w}{\sqrt{2}}}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Numerical example:**
For $w = 200$ μm: $h = 200/\sqrt{2} = 141.4$ μm. Beyond this depth, the etch **stops automatically** — used to fabricate diaphragms of precise thickness.

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Cross-sectional sketch (mermaid block):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TOP["Si(100) Surface"]:::surface --> OX["SiO2 Mask Opening of width w"]:::mask
    OX --> VG["V-Groove with 54.74 degree sidewalls on 111 planes"]:::groove
    VG --> BOT["Depth h = w by root 2"]:::groove
    
    classDef surface fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,color:#000
    classDef mask fill:#374151,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef groove fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#000
```

---

**Part (b) — Selectivity, Anisotropy & Comparison (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Selectivity in KOH:**

$$
S = \frac{ER_{\text{Si}}}{ER_{\text{SiO}_2}} \approx \frac{1.4\,\mu\text{m/min}}{0.007\,\mu\text{m/min}} \approx 200
$$

So a 1 μm SiO₂ mask can protect the silicon for etching up to ~200 μm — adequate for bulk micromachining.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Anisotropy in KOH:**

$$
A = 1 - \frac{ER_{\{111\}}}{ER_{\{100\}}} = 1 - \frac{0.014}{1.4} = 0.99
$$

This near-unity anisotropy is what produces sharp, crystallographically defined features.

**Step 3 (3 marks) — Comparison Table:**

| Property | KOH | TMAH | EDP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Si(100) etch rate | 1.4 μm/min (80 °C) | 1.0 μm/min (90 °C) | 0.7 μm/min (110 °C) |
| Anisotropy ratio (100/111) | ~100 | ~30–50 | ~30 |
| Selectivity vs SiO₂ | ~200 | ~1000 | High |
| CMOS-compatible | **No** (K⁺ contamination) | **Yes** | **No** (toxic) |
| Etch stop on p⁺ Si | No | No | Yes |
| Cost | Low | Moderate | High |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** KOH is preferred for research; **TMAH is preferred for CMOS-compatible MEMS production**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Module 3 Choice]`

**(a)** Explain the **Bosch DRIE process** with the help of a process cycle diagram. Discuss the role of **etch** and **passivation** steps. What is **scalloping**, and how is it minimized? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A silicon wafer is etched in **HNA solution** (HF : HNO₃ : CH₃COOH = 2 : 5 : 2 by volume) at 25 °C. After 10 minutes, the etch depth is 25 μm. Determine: (i) the etch rate, (ii) the lateral etch rate assuming an anisotropy factor of 0.10, and (iii) the undercut beneath a mask edge. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Bosch DRIE Process (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (3 marks) — Two-step cyclic process:**

The Bosch process alternates two plasma steps in a single chamber, repeating the cycle 50–500 times for deep etching:

| Step | Gas | Duration | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Etch** | SF₆ (200 sccm) | 5–10 s | F radicals isotropically etch Si |
| **Passivation** | C₄F₈ (100 sccm) | 2–5 s | Deposits fluoropolymer film (–(CF₂)ₙ–) on all surfaces |
| **Directional Bombardment** (within next etch) | SF₅⁺ ions | — | Ion energy removes polymer from horizontal (bottom) surfaces only |

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Sidewall protection mechanism:**
The fluoropolymer on vertical sidewalls is **not removed** by directional ions (because ions travel perpendicular to the wafer). Thus, sidewalls see only minimal etching, while the bottom is etched repeatedly → **net vertical etch**.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Scalloping and minimization:**
- **Scalloping:** The cyclic alternation creates periodic ridges on sidewalls with amplitude = etch depth per cycle.
- **Minimization:** Use shorter cycles (~1 s etch / 1 s passivation), or switch to **Cryogenic DRIE** (continuous SF₆/O₂ plasma at −110 °C), which produces smooth sidewalls.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1["Etch Cycle: SF6 plasma F radicals attack Si"]:::ec --> P1["Passivation Cycle: C4F8 polymer coats walls"]:::pc
    P1 --> B1["Ion Bombardment: Bottom polymer removed"]:::bc
    B1 --> E1
    
    classDef ec fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000
    classDef pc fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000
    classDef bc fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#000
```

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Etch time: $t = 10$ min
- Etch depth: $h = 25$ μm
- Anisotropy: $A = 0.10$

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Vertical etch rate:**

$$
V_v = \frac{h}{t} = \frac{25\,\mu\text{m}}{10\,\text{min}} = 2.5\,\mu\text{m/min}
$$

**Step 2 (3 marks) — Lateral etch rate from anisotropy definition:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 1 - \frac{V_l}{V_v} \\
0.10 &= 1 - \frac{V_l}{2.5} \\
\frac{V_l}{2.5} &= 0.90 \\
V_l &= 0.90 \times 2.5 = 2.25\,\mu\text{m/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Undercut beneath mask:**

$$
u = V_l \cdot t = 2.25\,\mu\text{m/min} \times 10\,\text{min} = 22.5\,\mu\text{m}
$$

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing Selectivity with Anisotropy** — Most common error. *Selectivity* = substrate/mask; *Anisotropy* = lateral/vertical of same material.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in undercut** — Some students forget that undercut occurs on **both sides** of a mask opening.
> 3. **Mixing up V-groove angles** — The angle 54.74° is for Si(100). For Si(110), the angle is **90° (perfectly vertical sidewalls)**.
> 4. **Using $A = V_l/V_v$** instead of $A = 1 - V_l/V_v$ — a one-mark error that costs heavily.
> 5. **Not stating units explicitly** in numerical answers — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist — Etching**

- ✅ **Etching** = subtractive patterning using **chemical / physical / combined** mechanisms through a mask.
- ✅ Two main families: **Wet (chemical)** and **Dry (plasma-based)**.
- ✅ Wet → **Isotropic** (HF, HNA) or **Anisotropic** (KOH, TMAH, EDP).
- ✅ Dry → **Plasma, RIE, Sputter, DRIE (Bosch), Cryo-DRIE, ICP**.
- ✅ **Three Sacred Metrics**: Etch Rate, Selectivity, Anisotropy — **all must be optimized**.
- ✅ **Anisotropy formula:** $A = 1 - V_l/V_v$; range **0 (isotropic) to 1 (anisotropic)**.
- ✅ **KOH V-groove:** $h = w/\sqrt{2}$, sidewall angle **54.74°** on Si(100) along ⟨110⟩.
- ✅ **Bosch DRIE** uses alternating **SF₆ etch + C₄F₈ passivation** cycles; produces vertical sidewalls with **scalloping**.
- ✅ **Cryogenic DRIE** = continuous SF₆/O₂ plasma at −110 °C → smooth sidewalls.
- ✅ **Activation energy** for KOH-Si(100) ≈ **0.5–0.6 eV** (Arrhenius).
- ✅ **Selectivity** of KOH vs SiO₂ ≈ 200; TMAH vs SiO₂ ≈ 1000 (TMAH is CMOS-compatible).
- ✅ **RIE Lag / ARDE** limits DRIE aspect ratio to about **50:1**.
- ✅ For KTU ESE: Always state **units, formulas, boundary conditions, and a labelled diagram**.
- ✅ Examiners' pet topics: V-groove derivation, Bosch process, comparison of KOH vs TMAH, numerical problems on anisotropy.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
