topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET836 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Micro Manufacturing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: MICRO MANUFACTURING — Core Foundations

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** *Micro Manufacturing* refers to the set of fabrication processes used to create structures, devices, and systems with dimensions in the **micrometer (µm)** range (typically **0.1 µm to 1000 µm**), forming the physical backbone of MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems). It is a direct technological descendant of semiconductor IC fabrication, adapted to handle not only silicon but also polymers, metals, ceramics, and glass.

## 1.1 The Two Pillars of MEMS Fabrication
Micro manufacturing for MEMS is broadly classified into:

1. **Silicon-Based Micromachining** — Uses silicon (Si) wafers as the primary substrate. Inherited from the microelectronics industry.
2. **Non-Silicon / Polymer-Based Micromachining** — Uses materials like glass, polymers (PDMS, PMMA, SU-8), metals, and ceramics. Often used for bio-MEMS, microfluidics, and rapid prototyping.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Classification:** Remember the single most tested classification:
> - **Bulk Micromachining** → Removes material *from the bulk* of the substrate.
> - **Surface Micromachining** → Builds structures *on top of* the substrate using deposited thin films.
> - **LIGA Process** → Combines X-ray lithography + electroplating for *high-aspect-ratio* structures.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for Freshers

Imagine you are a sculptor asked to carve a tiny elephant from a block of granite (say 1 cm × 1 cm) such that its trunk is only 50 µm wide. 

- **Bulk micromachining** is like carving *into* the block of granite using acid or a focused ion beam — you selectively eat away the stone to reveal your elephant.
- **Surface micromachining** is like building the elephant from layers of clay *on top of* a flat base — you deposit and pattern layer after layer.
- **LIGA** is like making a very deep, narrow trench in the granite and filling it with molten metal to get a tall, thin tail.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 of OEMET836 emphasizes **bulk micromachining (etching)**, **surface micromachining (sacrificial layers)**, and the **LIGA process** as the three cornerstones.

## 1.3 Why Micro Manufacturing Matters
- Enables **batch fabrication** — thousands of identical devices on a single 6-inch wafer, drastically reducing per-unit cost.
- Provides **monolithic integration** with on-chip electronics (CMOS + MEMS on the same die).
- Allows access to **high surface-area-to-volume ratios**, critical for sensors and actuators.

---

## 1.4 Photolithography — The Universal Patterning Step

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** *Photolithography* is the process of transferring geometric patterns from a photomask onto a photosensitive material (photoresist) coated on a substrate, using UV light exposure.

### Process Flow (Memorize This Sequence)
1. **Wafer Cleaning** — RCA cleaning to remove organic/inorganic contaminants.
2. **Dehydration Bake** — Removes moisture at ~150–200 °C.
3. **Priming (HMDS)** — Improves photoresist adhesion.
4. **Photoresist Coating (Spin Coating)** — Typically **1–2 µm thick** for positive resist (e.g., AZ5214, S1813).
5. **Soft Bake (Pre-Bake)** — Drives off solvent.
6. **Alignment & Exposure** — UV light through mask (contact, proximity, or projection mode).
7. **Post-Exposure Bake (PEB)** — For chemically amplified resists.
8. **Development** — Removes exposed (positive) or unexposed (negative) regions.
9. **Hard Bake** — Hardens the remaining resist.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Minimum Feature Size (Rayleigh's Criterion):** The smallest printable feature width is given by the **modulation transfer function** limit, simplified in KTU textbooks as the relation between wavelength (λ), numerical aperture (NA), and process parameter (k₁):
>
> $$ CD_{min} = k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA} $$
>
> where **CD_min** is the critical dimension, **λ** is the exposure wavelength, **NA** is the numerical aperture of the lens, and **k₁ ≈ 0.4** for production processes.

---

## 1.5 Resolution Equation Explanation
- Smaller wavelength (λ) → finer resolution. This is why the industry moved from **g-line (436 nm)** to **i-line (365 nm)** to **DUV (193 nm)** immersion lithography.
- Larger NA → better resolution. **NA ≈ 1.35** in modern immersion systems.
- The **Depth of Focus (DOF)** is given by:
$$ DOF = k_2 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA^2} $$
Higher NA reduces DOF, demanding ultra-flat wafers.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS — Etching, Bulk & Surface Micromachining

## 2.1 Wet Chemical Etching (WCE)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** *Wet etching* is a material removal process where a substrate is selectively dissolved by a liquid chemical reagent, with the photoresist (or oxide/nitride) acting as a mask.

### Two Sub-Modes of Wet Etching
| Mode | Mechanism | Etch Direction | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Isotropic** | Chemical reaction equally in all directions | Horizontal = Vertical etch rate | Limited to large features; not preferred for MEMS |
| **Anisotropic** | Etch rate depends on crystal plane orientation | Planar-dependent (e.g., (111) vs (100) in Si) | Used to create V-grooves, pits, membranes |

### Key Etchants for Silicon
| Etchant | Composition | Selectivity | Anisotropy | Mask |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **KOH** (Potassium Hydroxide) | KOH + H₂O + IPA | Si : SiO₂ ≈ 400 : 1 | High | Si₃N₄, SiO₂ |
| **TMAH** (Tetramethyl Ammonium Hydroxide) | (CH₃)₄NOH + H₂O | CMOS compatible | Moderate | SiO₂, Al |
| **EDP** (Ethylene Diamine Pyrocatechol) | EDP + water | High (100)/(111) ratio | Very high | SiO₂ |
| **HNA** (Hydrofluoric–Nitric–Acetic) | HF + HNO₃ + CH₃COOH | None (isotropic) | Isotropic | Hard masks |

### Anisotropic Etching of Silicon in KOH
Silicon has a **diamond cubic lattice** with planes (100), (110), and (111) exhibiting vastly different atomic densities. KOH etches these planes at very different rates:

$$ R_{(100)} : R_{(110)} : R_{(111)} \approx 100 : 160 : 1 $$

This anisotropy is what produces the famous **54.74° V-groove** on a (100) wafer (see SECTION 3 for derivation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 54.74° Angle:** When a (100) silicon wafer is etched through a rectangular mask opening aligned to the ⟨110⟩ direction, the (111) sidewalls emerge at exactly:
>
> $$ \theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{a/\sqrt{2}}{a}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) = 54.7356° \approx 54.74° $$

---

## 2.2 Dry Etching

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** *Dry etching* uses plasma or vapor-phase chemistry to remove material, offering better anisotropy and control than wet etching for sub-micron features.

### Three Principal Dry Etching Modes
1. **Reactive Ion Etching (RIE)** — Combines chemical reaction (radicals) with physical ion bombardment.
2. **Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE)** — The Bosch process for deep, high-aspect-ratio silicon structures.
3. **Ion Beam Etching (IBE)** — Pure physical sputtering using inert ions (Ar⁺).

### The Bosch DRIE Process (Si DRIE)
The **Bosch process** alternates between two steps cyclically:
- **Etch step** (SF₆ plasma, ~5–15 s): Isotropic chemical etching of Si → forms F radicals.
- **Passivation step** (C₄F₈ plasma, ~5–12 s): Deposits a fluoropolymer (Teflon-like) on all surfaces.

The result: nearly **vertical sidewalls** with characteristic **"scalloping"** ripples on the etched sidewalls.

> [!TIP]
> **Aspect Ratio Dependent Etching (ARDE):** In DRIE, etch rate decreases as feature aspect ratio (depth ÷ width) increases. This is critical in designing high-aspect-ratio inertial sensors.

### DRIE Performance Metrics
- **Etch rate:** 1–10 µm/min
- **Selectivity to photoresist:** 50:1 to 100:1
- **Selectivity to SiO₂:** 200:1
- **Sidewall verticality:** 90° ± 1°
- **Aspect ratios achieved:** Up to **30:1** routinely; >50:1 in research.

---

## 2.3 Surface Micromachining — Building Up

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** *Surface micromachining* builds micromechanical structures by successive deposition and selective etching of thin films *on top of* the substrate, using a **sacrificial layer** that is later removed to free the mechanical structure.

### The Sacrificial Layer Concept
1. Deposit **sacrificial layer** (e.g., SiO₂ or PSG — phosphosilicate glass).
2. Deposit **structural layer** (e.g., polysilicon, Si₃N₄, metal).
3. Pattern the structural layer using photolithography + etching.
4. **Release etch** — Dissolve the sacrificial layer in **HF** (for SiO₂) or another selective etchant, freeing the structure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it works:** The structural material is essentially *insoluble* in the release etchant, while the sacrificial material dissolves rapidly. For example:
> - **Sacrificial:** SiO₂ (or PSG) → **Release etchant:** HF
> - **Sacrificial:** Photoresist → **Release etchant:** Acetone
> - **Sacrificial:** Cu → **Release etchant:** Nitric acid

### Stiction Problem
After release etch, surface tension of the rinsing liquid pulls compliant microstructures into the substrate — a major failure mode called **stiction**. Mitigations:
- **Critical point drying (CPD)** using CO₂.
- **HF vapor-phase release** (no liquid).
- **Self-assembled monolayer (SAM) coatings** for hydrophobic surfaces.
- **Dimples** etched into the structural layer at anchor points.

---

## 2.4 The LIGA Process

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** *LIGA* is the German acronym for **Li**thographie, **G**alvanoformung, **A**bformung (X-ray lithography, electroplating, molding). It is used to manufacture **high-aspect-ratio microstructures** in metals, polymers, and ceramics.

### LIGA Process Flow
1. **Substrate preparation** — Conductive seed layer (e.g., Au, Cu) on wafer.
2. **PMMA resist coating** — Thick layer, **100 µm to 1000 µm** (or more).
3. **X-ray exposure** through a synchrotron-generated **hard X-ray mask** (Au absorber on a Be or Si membrane).
4. **Development** — Removes exposed PMMA in a chemical developer (GG developer).
5. **Electroplating** — Fills the mold with metal (Ni, Cu, Au, Fe-Ni alloy).
6. **PMMA removal** — Leaves a free-standing metal microstructure.
7. *(Optional)* **Molding step** — Use the metal structure as an insert to mass-produce polymer parts via injection molding or hot embossing.

### LIGA vs. DRIE Comparison
| Parameter | LIGA | DRIE (Bosch) |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum depth | > 1000 µm | 200–700 µm |
| Sidewall verticality | Excellent (~89.9°) | Excellent (~90°) |
| Material | Metals, polymers, ceramics | Silicon only |
| Cost | Very high (synchrotron) | Moderate |
| Throughput | Low | High |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Applications of LIGA:** Comb drives with very tall fingers, micro-gears, X-ray collimators, optical fiber alignment structures, and micro-fluidic channels for biological cells.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference Table

| Formula | Meaning | Typical Values / Units |
|---|---|---|
| $CD_{min} = k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA}$ | Minimum printable feature | k₁ ≈ 0.4, NA = 0.5–1.35 |
| $DOF = k_2 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA^2}$ | Depth of focus | k₂ ≈ 0.5 |
| $R_a = R_0 \cdot e^{-E_a/k_BT}$ | Arrhenius etch rate | Eₐ in eV, k_B = 8.617×10⁻⁵ eV/K |
| $\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$ | V-groove angle on Si(100) | **54.74°** |
| $AR = \frac{d}{w}$ | Aspect ratio | d = depth, w = width |
| $ARDE \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{AR}}$ | Etch rate drop with aspect ratio | Empirical |
| $E_{field} = \frac{V}{d}$ | Electric field in DRIE plasma | V in volts, d in m |
| $Q_{etch} = n \cdot F \cdot z$ | Etch moles (charge balance) | F = Faraday constant = 96485 C/mol |
| $\Delta V = \rho \cdot \frac{L^3}{3EI} \cdot F$ | Beam deflection (stiction) | ρ is residual stress, E Young's modulus |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations directly drive MEMS accelerometer design (DRIE depth defines proof mass), pressure sensor membranes (bulk etch thickness defines diaphragm stiffness), and optical MEMS (LIGA defines comb-drive height → drive force).

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the 54.74° V-Groove Angle in Anisotropic Etching of Si(100)

> [!NOTE]
> **Context:** This is the most frequently derived result in KTU MEMS examinations. It explains why V-grooves and pyramidal pits have a specific angle when KOH etches (100) silicon.

### Geometric Setup
- A silicon wafer has a **(100) surface plane**.
- The crystal has **four equivalent ⟨111⟩ directions** that intersect the (100) surface at some angle.
- We need to find that angle.

### Step-by-Step Derivation
Consider the cubic unit cell of silicon. The (100) plane is the top face. The (111) plane intersects it. The Miller indices define the normal vector:

- (100) plane normal: $\hat{n}_{100} = [1, 0, 0]$
- (111) plane normal: $\hat{n}_{111} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}[1, 1, 1]$

The angle θ between these two plane normals (which equals the angle between the planes) is given by:

$$
\cos\theta = \frac{\hat{n}_{100} \cdot \hat{n}_{111}}{\vert \hat{n}_{100} \vert \cdot \vert \hat{n}_{111} \vert}
$$

Compute the dot product:
$$
\hat{n}_{100} \cdot \hat{n}_{111} = (1)(1/\sqrt{3}) + (0)(1/\sqrt{3}) + (0)(1/\sqrt{3}) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

Magnitudes:
$$
\vert \hat{n}_{100} \vert = 1
$$

$$
\vert \hat{n}_{111} \vert = \sqrt{(1/\sqrt{3})^2 + (1/\sqrt{3})^2 + (1/\sqrt{3})^2} = \sqrt{1/3 + 1/3 + 1/3} = 1
$$

Substitute:
$$
\cos\theta = \frac{1/\sqrt{3}}{1 \cdot 1} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

Solve for θ:
$$
\theta = \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) = \cos^{-1}(0.5774) = 54.7356°
$$

Rounded to the KTU-marked value:
$$
\boxed{\theta = 54.74°}
$$

### Physical Interpretation
- The **etched sidewalls** are (111) planes — the slowest etching planes in KOH.
- The **bottom** of the V-groove is also (111) — etching self-terminates.
- A **square opening** aligned to ⟨110⟩ on the (100) surface produces a **pyramidal pit** bounded by four (111) planes.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Wet Etch Depth for a Square Mask Opening

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A square opening of side $L$ is etched into a (100) Si wafer using KOH. Find the etch time required to reach a membrane thickness $t$.

### Step-by-Step Solution
For a square opening aligned to the ⟨110⟩ direction, the four (111) planes meet at a point at depth $h$:

The depth at which the V-groove closes (or the etch stops) is geometrically:
$$
h = \frac{L}{2 \tan(54.74°)} = \frac{L}{2 \cdot 1.4142} = \frac{L}{2.8284}
$$

The etch time required to reach depth $h$ is:
$$
t = \frac{h}{R_{(100)}}
$$

### Numerical Example (KTU Style)
Given:
- Mask opening $L = 400 \, \mu m$
- KOH etch rate on (100) plane: $R_{(100)} = 1.0 \, \mu m/\min$

Compute h:
$$
h = \frac{400}{2.8284} = 141.42 \, \mu m
$$

Compute time:
$$
t = \frac{141.42}{1.0} = 141.42 \, \text{minutes} \approx 2 \, \text{hours} \, 21 \, \text{minutes}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Bosch DRIE Cycle Time

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A 200 µm deep silicon structure is etched using the Bosch process. The etch cycle is 8 s SF₆ and 7 s C₄F₈. The net silicon etch rate per cycle is 0.5 µm/cycle. Calculate total cycle count and time.

### Step-by-Step Solution
Number of cycles:
$$
N = \frac{200 \, \mu m}{0.5 \, \mu m/\text{cycle}} = 400 \, \text{cycles}
$$

Time per cycle:
$$
T_{cycle} = 8 + 7 = 15 \, s
$$

Total time:
$$
T_{total} = 400 \times 15 = 6000 \, s = 100 \, \text{minutes} = 1 \, \text{hour} \, 40 \, \text{minutes}
$$

### Average Effective Etch Rate
$$
R_{eff} = \frac{200 \, \mu m}{6000 \, s} = 0.0333 \, \mu m/s = 2.0 \, \mu m/\text{min}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Aspect Ratio Dependent Etching (ARDE)

> [!NOTE]
> **Background:** As feature aspect ratio increases, reactant transport to the etch front is limited, reducing etch rate. A common empirical model used in KTU problems is the **Coburn-Winters** or the simple inverse-square-root form.

### Model Equation
$$
R(h, w) = R_0 \left(1 - e^{-\alpha w/h}\right)
$$

For a deep, narrow feature, $w \ll h$, expand to first order:
$$
R(h, w) \approx R_0 \cdot \frac{\alpha w}{h}
$$

So:
$$
\boxed{R \propto \frac{1}{h/w} = \frac{1}{AR}}
$$

For a more accurate model:
$$
R = R_0 \cdot \frac{1}{(1 + \beta \cdot AR)^2}
$$

where $\beta$ is a process-dependent constant (~0.05–0.2 for DRIE).

### Numerical Example
Given:
- $R_0 = 3 \, \mu m/\min$ (open-area etch rate)
- $\beta = 0.1$
- Aspect ratio $AR = 10$

$$
R = \frac{3}{(1 + 0.1 \times 10)^2} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.75 \, \mu m/\min
$$

This explains why deep narrow trenches etch 4× slower than open areas.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Three Major Etching Techniques

| Parameter | KOH (Wet) | DRIE (Bosch) | RIE (Plasma) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Anisotropy | High (crystal-dependent) | Very high (sidewall tunable) | High (ion energy dependent) |
| Typical etch rate | 0.5–2 µm/min | 1–10 µm/min | 0.1–1 µm/min |
| Selectivity (mask/Si) | >100:1 (SiO₂ mask) | 50–150:1 (photoresist) | 1–10:1 |
| Surface roughness | Low (smooth) | Medium (scallops ~50–200 nm) | High (ion damage) |
| CMOS compatibility | Poor (K⁺ contamination) | Excellent | Excellent |
| Mask material | Si₃N₄, SiO₂ | Photoresist, SiO₂, Al | Photoresist, metal |
| Cost | Low | High | Moderate |
| Feature size limit | ~1 µm | ~0.5 µm | ~0.1 µm |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** For KTU 14-mark questions, always draw a cross-section diagram and annotate sidewall angles, mask material, and etch chemistry — this typically earns 2 marks of "presentation" credit.

# STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Bulk Micromachining Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Si Wafer 100] --> B[RCA Cleaning]
    B --> C[Thermal Oxidation SiO2 Growth]
    C --> D[Photoresist Coating Spin]
    D --> E[Soft Bake]
    E --> F[UV Exposure Through Mask]
    F --> G[Development]
    G --> H[Patterned PR on SiO2]
    H --> I[BOE Etch Opens SiO2 Window]
    I --> J[Photoresist Strip]
    J --> K[KOH Anisotropic Etch]
    K --> L{V-Groove Converged?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Free-Standing Diaphragm or Pyramid]
    L -->|No| K
    M --> N[End: MEMS Structure]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Surface Micromachining Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Si Substrate] --> B[LPCVD SiO2 Sacrificial Layer 1 to 2 micrometer]
    B --> C[LPCVD Polysilicon Structural Layer]
    C --> D[Photolithography Pattern Polysilicon]
    D --> E[RIE Etch Polysilicon]
    E --> F[Pattern Anchors by Opening Sacrificial Layer]
    F --> G[HF Vapor Release Etch]
    G --> H[Critical Point Drying]
    H --> I[Free-Standing Polysilicon Cantilever or Bridge]
    I --> J[End: Released MEMS Structure]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — LIGA Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Conductive Seed Layer on Substrate] --> B[Spin Coat Thick PMMA Resist 100 to 1000 micrometer]
    B --> C[Soft Bake at 90 C]
    C --> D[Synchrotron X-Ray Exposure Through Au Mask]
    D --> E[GG Developer Dissolves Exposed PMMA]
    E --> F[PMMA Mold Cavity Formed]
    F --> G[Electroplating Ni or Cu into Cavity]
    G --> H[PMMA Removal by Acetone or Plasma]
    H --> I[Free Standing Metal Microstructure]
    I --> J[Optional: Hot Embossing for Polymer Replication]
    J --> K[End: Final LIGA Part]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Bosch DRIE Alternating Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cycle Start] --> B[Step 1: SF6 Plasma Etch 5 to 15 s]
    B --> C[Si Isotropic Etch F Radicals Attack Si]
    C --> D[Step 2: C4F8 Plasma Deposition 5 to 12 s]
    D --> E[Fluoropolymer Passivation Layer Forms on All Surfaces]
    E --> F[Step 3: Directional Ion Bombardment at Etch Front Removes Passivation at Bottom]
    F --> G[Net: Vertical Etch Proceeds]
    G --> H{Desired Depth Reached?}
    H -->|No| B
    H -->|Yes| I[End: High AR Structure]
```

# KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

### Question 1. Define photolithography and list its main steps.
**Model Answer:** Photolithography is the process of transferring geometric patterns from a photomask onto a photosensitive photoresist layer coated on a substrate, using UV light exposure. The main steps are:
1. Wafer cleaning and dehydration bake
2. HMDS priming
3. Photoresist spin coating
4. Soft bake (pre-bake)
5. Alignment and UV exposure
6. Post-exposure bake (for chemically amplified resists)
7. Development
8. Hard bake

### Question 2. Differentiate between isotropic and anisotropic etching.
**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Isotropic Etching | Anisotropic Etching |
|---|---|---|
| Etch direction | Equal in all directions | Direction-dependent (crystal plane) |
| Sidewall profile | Curved, undercut | Vertical or angled plane |
| Process type | e.g., HNA on Si | e.g., KOH on Si(100) |
| Application | Bulk material removal | MEMS structure definition |

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

### Question 3. What is LIGA? Mention its advantages.
**Model Answer:** LIGA (Lithographie, Galvanoformung, Abformung) is a microfabrication process combining X-ray deep lithography, electroplating, and molding. **Advantages:** produces very high aspect ratio microstructures (>100:1), excellent sidewall verticality, ability to fabricate in metals/polymers/ceramics, and enables mass replication via injection molding.

### Question 4. What is the Bosch process? Why is it used?
**Model Answer:** The Bosch process is a Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE) technique that alternates between an SF₆ etch step and a C₄F₈ passivation step. It is used to etch deep, high-aspect-ratio (HAR >20:1) features into silicon with nearly vertical sidewalls.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper as per 2024 Scheme]**

### ⭐ CHOICE A: 14 Marks (Full Question)

#### Question A
**(a) Explain in detail the bulk micromachining of silicon using KOH anisotropic etching. Include the etch mechanism, etch rate anisotropy, V-groove formation with derivation of the 54.74° angle, and a process flow diagram. (7 marks)**
**(b) Describe the LIGA process with neat process flow, applications, and compare it with DRIE. (7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for (a):

**1. Definition (1 mark):** Bulk micromachining is a subtractive fabrication process where material is selectively removed from the bulk of a silicon wafer using etchants, with the crystal orientation controlling the etch profile.

**2. Etch Mechanism (1.5 marks):** In KOH + water + IPA, hydroxide ions (OH⁻) attack silicon atoms. The reaction proceeds faster on planes with higher surface energy and lower atomic density (100, 110) and is dramatically slower on the densely packed (111) plane. Reaction:
$$ Si + 2OH^- + 2H_2O \rightarrow SiO_2(OH)_2^{2-} + 2H_2 \uparrow $$

**3. Anisotropy (1.5 marks):** Etch rate ratio is approximately:
$$ R_{(100)} : R_{(110)} : R_{(111)} \approx 100 : 160 : 1 $$

**4. V-Groove Angle Derivation (2 marks):** As derived in Section 3.1, the angle between the (100) plane and the (111) plane is:
$$ \cos\theta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \implies \theta = 54.74° $$

**5. Process Flow (1 mark):**
- Wafer cleaning
- Thermal oxidation (mask)
- Photolithography
- BHF etch to open window in SiO₂
- KOH etch at 80°C
- Strip mask and rinse

##### Model Solution for (b):

**1. LIGA Definition (1 mark):** LIGA stands for Lithographie, Galvanoformung, Abformung. It is a high-aspect-ratio microfabrication technology.

**2. Process Flow (3 marks):**
- Deposit conductive seed layer (Au/Cu) on substrate
- Coat thick PMMA (100–1000 µm)
- Soft bake
- X-ray exposure using synchrotron radiation through Au absorber mask
- Develop in GG developer (removes exposed PMMA)
- Electroplate Ni/Cu into the PMMA mold cavities
- Remove PMMA with acetone or plasma ashing
- Optional: hot embossing or injection molding using the metal insert

**3. Applications (1.5 marks):**
- Micro gears and comb drives
- X-ray collimators (zone plates)
- Optical fiber alignment structures
- Micro-fluidic channels for cell sorting
- Microreactors for chemistry

**4. Comparison with DRIE (1.5 marks):** Refer to the comparison table in Section 2.4. Key points: LIGA produces higher aspect ratios and works in non-silicon materials, but DRIE is far more cost-effective for silicon-only applications.

---

### ⭐ CHOICE B: 14 Marks (Full Alternative Question)

#### Question B
**(a) Describe the surface micromachining process in detail. Explain the role of sacrificial layers, materials used, and the stiction problem with mitigation techniques. (7 marks)**
**(b) Explain the Bosch DRIE process with cycle diagram, parameters, advantages, and limitations. (7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for (a):

**1. Definition (1 mark):** Surface micromachining builds MEMS structures layer-by-layer on top of a substrate by depositing and selectively etching thin films, using a sacrificial layer that is later removed to release the structure.

**2. Sacrificial Layer Concept (2 marks):** A material (e.g., SiO₂, PSG, polymer) is deposited first, then a structural material (polysilicon, Si₃N₄, metal) is deposited and patterned on top. The sacrificial layer is dissolved in a release etch, leaving the structural layer freestanding, anchored only at designed support points.

**3. Materials (1.5 marks):**
- **Sacrificial:** SiO₂ (HF-released), PSG (HF-released, faster than thermal SiO₂), Cu, photoresist, polyimide
- **Structural:** Polysilicon (most common), LPCVD Si₃N₄, sputtered Al, Au, Ti

**4. Stiction Problem (1.5 marks):** When drying after wet release, surface tension of the rinse liquid pulls compliant microstructures into contact with the substrate, where Van der Waals forces permanently adhere them. **Mitigations:** critical point drying, HF vapor-phase release, hydrophobic SAM coatings (FDTS), dimples on structural layer, freeze-drying.

**5. Process Flow (1 mark):**
- Substrate prep
- LPCVD SiO₂ sacrificial
- LPCVD polysilicon structural
- Photolithography
- RIE etch
- Anchor definition
- HF release
- Critical point drying

##### Model Solution for (b):

**1. Bosch Process Definition (1 mark):** The Bosch DRIE process, developed by Robert Bosch GmbH in the 1990s, is a deep reactive ion etching technique that alternates between isotropic chemical etching (using SF₆) and conformal passivation deposition (using C₄F₈) to achieve high aspect ratio silicon microstructures with nearly vertical sidewalls.

**2. Cycle Description (2 marks):** Each cycle consists of:
- **Etch phase (5–15 s):** SF₆ plasma generates F radicals that isotropically attack Si.
- **Passivation phase (5–12 s):** C₄F₈ plasma deposits a Teflon-like fluoropolymer layer on all surfaces.
- **Directional ion bombardment:** At the start of the next etch phase, ions remove the passivation at horizontal (bottom) surfaces due to their directionality, while the sidewall passivation remains intact, preventing lateral etching.

**3. Parameters (1.5 marks):**
- **SF₆ flow:** 100–300 sccm
- **C₄F₈ flow:** 50–150 sccm
- **RF coil power:** 600–3000 W
- **Platen power:** 10–30 W
- **Chamber pressure:** 10–100 mTorr
- **Substrate temperature:** –20°C to +20°C (often cooled)

**4. Advantages (1.5 marks):**
- Aspect ratios up to 30:1 routinely
- Sidewall verticality 90° ± 1°
- CMOS compatible
- Fast etch rates (1–10 µm/min)
- Independent control of etch depth and lateral dimension

**5. Limitations (1 mark):**
- Sidewall scalloping (50–200 nm) — affects optical/precision mechanical surfaces
- ARDE (aspect ratio dependent etching) — narrow trenches etch slower
- Mask erosion over long etches
- Cost of DRIE equipment
- Requires precise gas switching and temperature control

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing isotropic and anisotropic etching** — students often forget to mention crystal plane dependence for anisotropy.
> 2. **Skipping the V-groove derivation** — Examiner often awards 2 marks *specifically* for deriving 54.74°; merely stating it is a partial answer.
> 3. **Forgetting stiction** in surface micromachining — it is a *must-mention* for full marks.
> 4. **Wrong DRIE chemistries** — students often write CF₄ instead of SF₆ and C₄F₈. Memorize the cycle correctly.
> 5. **LIGA confusion** — LIGA does **not** use UV; it uses **X-rays** (synchrotron source). UV is for photolithography.
> 6. **Missing units** — etch rate must be in µm/min with clear conditions (e.g., KOH 30 wt%, 80°C).
> 7. **Not drawing diagrams** — KTU values diagrams at 1–2 marks each. Skipping them is a 4–5 mark loss in a 14-mark question.

---

## 5.3 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- [x] **Micro manufacturing = micrometer-scale fabrication** for MEMS devices.
- [x] **Two pillars:** Silicon-based and non-silicon/polymer-based.
- [x] **Three core techniques:** Bulk micromachining, Surface micromachining, LIGA.
- [x] **Photolithography:** Pattern transfer using UV light + photoresist. Steps = clean → prime → coat → soft bake → expose → develop → hard bake.
- [x] **Resolution:** $CD_{min} = k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA}$.
- [x] **Wet etching:** KOH (anisotropic), HNA (isotropic), TMAH (CMOS-compatible).
- [x] **V-groove angle = 54.74°** (derived from (111)–(100) plane geometry).
- [x] **Dry etching:** RIE, DRIE, IBE.
- [x] **Bosch DRIE:** SF₆ etch + C₄F₈ passivation cycle; aspect ratio > 30:1.
- [x] **ARDE:** Higher aspect ratio → lower etch rate.
- [x] **Surface micromachining:** Sacrificial layer + structural layer + release etch.
- [x] **Stiction:** Capillary forces pull structures to substrate; mitigated by critical point drying and HF vapor release.
- [x] **LIGA:** X-ray lithography + electroplating + molding; > 1000 µm depth; non-Si materials.
- [x] **Sacrificial/structural pairs:** SiO₂/Poly-Si, PSG/Poly-Si, Photoresist/Polyimide, Cu/Ni.
- [x] **Anisotropy ratio in KOH:** (100) : (111) ≈ 100 : 1.
- [x] **Aspect ratio formula:** $AR = \frac{d}{w}$ (depth ÷ width).
- [x] **Real-world applications:** Accelerometers, gyroscopes, pressure sensors, inkjet printer heads, DNA microarrays, micro-mirrors.
- [x] **Key exam tip:** Always draw a labeled cross-section diagram and include the etch chemistry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Examiner Advice:** In KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3 (Micro Manufacturing) typically contributes **20–25%** of the OEMET836 paper. Master the **V-groove derivation**, the **DRIE cycle**, and the **surface micromachining sacrificial concept** — these three topics alone can fetch 8–10 marks in a 14-mark question. Always write answers in **structured points with subheadings**; KTU evaluators reward legibility and organization.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LIGA** is a German acronym for **LI**thographie (X-ray **L**ithography), **G**alvanoformung (**E**lectroplating), and **A**bformung (**M**olding/Plastic Molding). It is a high-aspect-ratio MEMS fabrication process that combines deep X-ray lithography, electroforming, and plastic molding to create three-dimensional microstructures with vertical sidewalls, heights of several hundred micrometers, and structural widths in the micrometer range.

The LIGA process was originally developed at the **Forschungszentrum Karlsruhe (KfK)** in Germany during the early 1980s. It is one of the most precise and versatile micromanufacturing techniques, capable of producing microstructures with aspect ratios exceeding **100:1** and structural heights up to **1000 µm** with sub-micrometer lateral precision.

| LIGA Term | German Meaning | English Translation |
|------------|----------------|---------------------|
| **L** | Lithographie | Lithography (Deep X-ray) |
| **I** | — | — |
| **G** | Galvanoformung | Electroplating / Electroforming |
| **A** | Abformung | Molding / Replica Casting |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Ice-Cube Tray Method:**
> Imagine making intricate chocolate figurines. First, you pour melted chocolate into a beautifully carved mold (made of metal), let it solidify, then peel the mold away to reveal a perfect replica. LIGA works identically: a precision "mold" is created using X-rays and metal electroplating, and then plastic or metal copies are mass-produced from this mold. The difference is that LIGA molds operate at the micrometer scale, carved by ultra-powerful X-rays from a particle accelerator.

**Conceptual Breakdown:**
- **Lithography Stage** → Creates a *template pattern* in a thick polymer resist using synchrotron X-rays.
- **Galvanof ormung Stage** → Fills the patterned voids with metal (typically nickel) to form a sturdy metallic mold insert.
- **Abformung Stage** → Uses this mold to mass-produce thousands of identical plastic or metal micro-parts through injection molding or hot embossing.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Synchrotron X-ray wavelength range:** $\lambda = 0.2$–$0.5 \text{ nm}$ (hard X-rays)
- **Typical resist thickness:** $300$–$1000 \text{ µm}$
- **Typical structural width:** $5$–$20 \text{ µm}$
- **Achievable aspect ratio:** Up to **100:1** (standard) and **500:1** (advanced)
- **Exposure dose (PMMA):** $2$–$4 \text{ kJ/cm}^3$
- **Sidewall verticality:** $\pm 0.1^\circ$ to $\pm 0.5^\circ$
- **Surface roughness:** $R_a = 30$–$50 \text{ nm}$
- **Young's modulus of PMMA resist:** $E \approx 3 \text{ GPa}$
- **Electroplating current density (nickel):** $J = 5$–$20 \text{ mA/cm}^2$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Module 3 specifically expects students to differentiate LIGA from surface micromachining, bulk micromachining, and other non-silicon micromachining processes. The defining uniqueness of LIGA is the use of **deep X-ray lithography** to penetrate thick resists, an aspect no UV-based process can replicate.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of LIGA

The LIGA process is executed in three primary stages, each critical to the final geometry.

### **Stage 1: Deep X-Ray Lithography (DXRL)**

- A **synchrotron radiation source** (electron storage ring) emits intense, collimated, monochromatic hard X-rays.
- The X-rays pass through a **mask** consisting of an X-ray absorber (typically **gold**, $10$–$20 \text{ µm}$ thick) patterned on an X-ray-transparent membrane (e.g., **silicon, beryllium, or titanium**).
- The X-rays selectively expose a thick layer of positive **polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA)** resist spin-coated or bonded onto a conductive substrate.
- Exposed PMMA undergoes chain scission (molecular weight reduction from $\sim 10^6$ to $\sim 10^4$), making it soluble in a developer (e.g., GG developer: morpholine, 2-aminoethanol, diglycolamine in water).
- Unexposed PMMA remains intact, forming the structural pattern.

### **Stage 2: Electroplating (Galvanoformung)**

- The patterned PMMA-on-substrate is immersed in an **electrolytic bath** (e.g., nickel sulfamate solution: $\text{Ni(SO}_3\text{NH}_2)_2$).
- The conductive substrate acts as the **cathode**; a nickel block acts as the **anode**.
- Under applied current density $J$, $\text{Ni}^{2+}$ ions are reduced and deposited within the PMMA cavities, gradually filling them from bottom to top.
- After complete filling, the remaining PMMA is dissolved (e.g., using acetone or NMP), leaving behind a free-standing **nickel mold insert** (also called the "primary structure" or "master").

### **Stage 3: Plastic Molding (Abformung)**

- The nickel mold insert is mounted in an **injection molding** or **hot embossing** apparatus.
- A thermoplastic (e.g., PMMA, POM, COC, PEEK) or thermoset is injected/pressed into the mold cavities at elevated temperature and pressure.
- After cooling/solidification, the part is **demolded**, producing a high-fidelity polymer replica.
- The nickel mold can be reused to produce **millions of identical parts**, making LIGA economically attractive for mass production.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Synchrotron X-rays?** Conventional UV lithography has a penetration depth of only $\sim 1 \text{ µm}$ in PMMA. To expose $500 \text{ µm}$ of resist, the photon energy must be in the **$2$–$10 \text{ keV}$** range — only synchrotron radiation provides sufficient intensity at these energies with adequate collimation.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **exam-critical formulas** for LIGA-related problems. Memorize their physical meaning, units, and typical numerical ranges.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\text{AR} = \dfrac{H}{W}$ | Aspect Ratio (Height / Width) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $D = E \cdot t$ | Exposure Dose (Energy per Volume) | $\text{kJ/cm}^3$ |
| 3 | $I(x) = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$ | Beer–Lambert X-ray Attenuation | $\text{photons/cm}^2$ |
| 4 | $\mu = \rho \cdot \left(\dfrac{Z}{A}\right) \cdot N_A \cdot \sigma$ | Linear Absorption Coefficient | $\text{cm}^{-1}$ |
| 5 | $v_{\text{dep}} = \dfrac{J \cdot M}{z \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ | Electroplating Deposition Rate | $\text{µm/h}$ |
| 6 | $t_{\text{dep}} = \dfrac{H}{v_{\text{dep}}}$ | Total Plating Time | hours |
| 7 | $\Delta t_{\text{side}} = \dfrac{H \cdot \tan(\theta)}{2}$ | Sidewall Run-out due to Divergence | $\text{µm}$ |
| 8 | $\eta_{\text{current}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100\%$ | Plating Current Efficiency | % |
| 9 | $m_{\text{theoretical}} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{z \cdot F}$ | Faraday's Law of Electrolysis | grams |
| 10 | $R_{\text{demold}} = \dfrac{\mu_{\text{f}} \cdot P \cdot A}{H^2}$ | Demolding Force per Unit Width | $\text{N/mm}$ |

**Variable Glossary:**
- $H$ = structural height; $W$ = structural width
- $I_0$ = incident X-ray intensity; $\mu$ = linear absorption coefficient
- $J$ = current density; $M$ = molar mass of Ni ($58.69 \text{ g/mol}$)
- $z$ = ion valence ($z = 2$ for $\text{Ni}^{2+}$); $F$ = Faraday constant ($96485 \text{ C/mol}$)
- $\theta$ = X-ray beam divergence half-angle (typically $0.5$–$2 \text{ mrad}$)
- $\mu_f$ = friction coefficient between polymer and metal mold

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

LIGA-fabricated components are deployed in several high-value engineering domains:

- **Microfluidics:** Lab-on-chip devices, microreactors, micropumps, inkjet printer heads.
- **Optics & Photonics:** Fiber-optic connectors, micro-lens arrays, infrared sensor grids, Fresnel zone plates for X-ray microscopy.
- **Automotive:** Acceleration sensors, pressure sensors, micro-relays, fuel injection nozzles.
- **Biomedical:** Micro-needles for transdermal drug delivery, neural probes, micro-tweezers for cell manipulation.
- **RF & Telecommunications:** Comb drives, micro-gears, inductors, and tunable capacitors for MEMS-IC integration.
- **Aerospace:** Gyroscopes, vibration dampers, and inertial measurement units (IMUs) requiring high-aspect-ratio proof masses.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: X-Ray Penetration Depth in PMMA Resist

The dose delivered to the bottom of a resist layer of thickness $H$ must exceed the developer solubility threshold. From the Beer–Lambert law:

$$I(H) = I_0 \, e^{-\mu_{\text{PMMA}} \cdot H}$$

The dose absorbed per unit volume at depth $x$ is:

$$D(x) = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x} \cdot \Delta t \cdot \rho_{\text{PMMA}}$$

For uniform exposure across the full resist depth, the bottom dose $D(H)$ must equal the threshold dose $D_{\text{th}}$. Therefore, the **maximum practical resist thickness** is:

$$H_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_{\text{PMMA}}} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{D_0}{D_{\text{th}}}\right)$$

**Numerical Evaluation (Typical PMMA at $\lambda = 0.4 \text{ nm}$):**
- $\mu_{\text{PMMA}} \approx 6.5 \text{ cm}^{-1}$
- $D_0$ (surface dose) $\approx 4 \text{ kJ/cm}^3$
- $D_{\text{th}}$ (developer threshold) $\approx 0.3 \text{ kJ/cm}^3$

$$H_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{6.5} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{4}{0.3}\right) = 0.1538 \times \ln(13.33) = 0.1538 \times 2.590$$

$$H_{\max} \approx 0.398 \text{ cm} = 3980 \text{ µm}$$

This is why LIGA can expose resist thicknesses of several **millimeters** — a feat impossible with optical lithography.

## 3.2 Derivation: Nickel Electroplating Deposition Rate

Using Faraday's law of electrolysis, the mass of nickel deposited is:

$$m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{z \cdot F}$$

For a deposit area $A$ and density $\rho_{\text{Ni}}$:

$$m = \rho_{\text{Ni}} \cdot A \cdot v_{\text{dep}} \cdot t$$

Equating both expressions and solving for deposition rate $v_{\text{dep}}$:

$$\rho_{\text{Ni}} \cdot A \cdot v_{\text{dep}} \cdot t = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{z \cdot F}$$

$$v_{\text{dep}} = \dfrac{I \cdot M}{z \cdot F \cdot \rho_{\text{Ni}} \cdot A} = \dfrac{J \cdot M}{z \cdot F \cdot \rho_{\text{Ni}}}$$

where $J = I/A$ is the current density.

**Numerical Example (Filling a 500 µm PMMA cavity with Nickel):**
- $J = 10 \text{ mA/cm}^2 = 0.01 \text{ A/cm}^2$
- $M_{\text{Ni}} = 58.69 \text{ g/mol}$
- $z = 2$
- $F = 96485 \text{ C/mol}$
- $\rho_{\text{Ni}} = 8.908 \text{ g/cm}^3$

$$v_{\text{dep}} = \dfrac{0.01 \times 58.69}{2 \times 96485 \times 8.908}$$

$$v_{\text{dep}} = \dfrac{0.5869}{1719218.6} = 3.41 \times 10^{-7} \text{ cm/s}$$

Converting to micrometers per hour:

$$v_{\text{dep}} = 3.41 \times 10^{-7} \times 10^4 \times 3600 = 12.3 \text{ µm/h}$$

**Total deposition time for a $500 \text{ µm}$ deep mold:**

$$t_{\text{dep}} = \dfrac{500 \text{ µm}}{12.3 \text{ µm/h}} \approx 40.7 \text{ hours}$$

This long plating time is a major process limitation in LIGA.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: LIGA vs. Other Micromachining Techniques

| Property | LIGA | Bulk Si Micromachining | Surface Micromachining | UV-LIGA / SU-8 |
|----------|------|------------------------|------------------------|----------------|
| **Max structural height** | 1000 µm | 100–500 µm | 2–10 µm | 200–500 µm |
| **Aspect ratio** | 100:1+ | 50:1 | 5:1 | 20:1 |
| **Lateral resolution** | < 0.5 µm | 1–5 µm | 0.5–2 µm | 1–5 µm |
| **Sidewall verticality** | Excellent | Good (etch-dependent) | Variable | Good |
| **Material options** | Plastics, metals, ceramics | Silicon only | Polysilicon, SiO₂, metals | UV-curable polymers |
| **Mask/source** | Synchrotron X-ray | UV/optical | UV/optical | UV/optical |
| **Process cost** | Very high | Moderate | Low | Low–Moderate |
| **Mass production** | Excellent (molding) | Difficult | Moderate | Moderate |
| **3D capability** | Excellent (multi-level) | Limited (2.5D) | Layered only | Limited |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked to compare LIGA with silicon-based processes, always emphasize that LIGA is a **material-flexible, mass-production-friendly** process, while silicon micromachining is **material-restricted but IC-compatible**. This contrast is a recurring question in KTU 2024 pattern.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common Variants of LIGA

| Variant | Description | Key Advantage |
|---------|-------------|---------------|
| **X-ray LIGA (Standard)** | Uses synchrotron X-ray exposure of PMMA | Highest aspect ratio, finest resolution |
| **UV-LIGA** | Replaces X-ray with UV exposure of SU-8 resist | Low cost, no synchrotron required |
| **Laser-LIGA** | Uses excimer laser ablation to pattern resist | Avoids mask costs, flexible prototyping |
| **Pseudo-LIGA / Hot Embossing** | Uses mechanical pressure + heat with a silicon or metal mold | High throughput, low cost |
| **Hierarchical LIGA** | Combines X-ray + UV exposures in a single resist for multi-level structures | Creates true 3D microstructures |
| **Sacrificial LIGA** | Adds a sacrificial layer beneath the primary structure for free-standing parts | Enables movable MEMS devices |

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define LIGA. Expand the acronym and briefly state the three primary process steps involved.**

**Model Answer:**

LIGA is a German acronym that stands for **LI**thographie (X-ray **L**ithography), **G**alvanoformung (**E**lectroplating), and **A**bformung (**M**olding). The three primary process steps are:

1. **Deep X-Ray Lithography (Lithographie):** A thick PMMA resist is selectively exposed using synchrotron X-rays through a gold absorber mask. The exposed regions become soluble and are developed away, leaving a patterned resist template.
2. **Electroforming (Galvanoformung):** The developed cavities are filled with a metal (typically nickel) via electroplating, forming a robust metallic mold insert.
3. **Plastic Molding (Abformung):** The nickel mold is used in injection molding or hot embossing to mass-produce identical polymer (or secondary metal) micro-parts at low per-unit cost.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why is synchrotron radiation used in LIGA instead of conventional UV light? Mention two specific reasons.**

**Model Answer:**

Synchrotron radiation is used because it offers two critical advantages over UV light:

1. **High Penetration Depth:** Hard X-rays from a synchrotron have photon energies of $2$–$10 \text{ keV}$, allowing them to penetrate PMMA resist layers of $500$–$1000 \text{ µm}$. UV light is limited to $\sim 1 \text{ µm}$ penetration in PMMA.
2. **High Collimation and Intensity:** Synchrotron beams have very low divergence ($\sim 0.5 \text{ mrad}$), enabling near-vertical sidewalls, and extremely high brilliance, allowing short exposure times even for thick resists.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Jul 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the complete LIGA process with a detailed step-by-step description of each stage. Discuss the role of synchrotron radiation, the X-ray mask structure, and the choice of PMMA as a resist material. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the maximum usable resist thickness in LIGA. Calculate the maximum thickness for PMMA exposed at $\lambda = 0.4 \text{ nm}$ if the surface dose is $4 \text{ kJ/cm}^3$ and the developer threshold dose is $0.4 \text{ kJ/cm}^3$. The linear absorption coefficient of PMMA at this wavelength is $6.5 \text{ cm}^{-1}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Detailed Process Explanation:**

**Step 1 — Substrate Preparation:** A silicon wafer is coated with a thin conductive metal seed layer (e.g., $\text{Ti/Cu}$, $50\text{ nm}/200\text{ nm}$) by sputtering or evaporation. This layer acts as the cathode during electroplating.

**Step 2 — Resist Application:** A thick layer of PMMA ($\sim 500 \text{ µm$) is either spin-coated in multiple passes, solvent-bonded from a pre-cast sheet, or cast directly. PMMA is preferred because it has high X-ray sensitivity, excellent contrast, low internal stress, and is removable with common solvents.

**Step 3 — Mask Fabrication:** The X-ray mask consists of a low-stress **silicon nitride ($\text{Si}_3\text{N}_4$) or silicon membrane** ($1$–$2 \text{ µm}$ thick) with a patterned **gold absorber** ($10$–$20 \text{ µm}$ thick). The gold absorbs X-rays strongly due to its high atomic number ($Z = 79$).

**Step 4 — Synchrotron Exposure:** The resist-coated substrate is placed in a vacuum chamber and exposed through the mask. Typical exposure time is $1$–$10$ hours, depending on ring current and resist thickness.

**Step 5 — Development:** The wafer is immersed in **GG developer** (a mixture of morpholine, 2-aminoethanol, and diglycolamine in water) at $35^\circ\text{C}$ for several hours. Exposed PMMA dissolves; unexposed PMMA remains as the structural pattern.

**Step 6 — Electroplating:** The patterned substrate is immersed in a nickel sulfamate bath ($\text{pH} \approx 3.5$, $T = 50^\circ\text{C}$). A current density of $5$–$20 \text{ mA/cm}^2$ is applied, depositing nickel from the seed layer upward, filling the cavities in $20$–$60$ hours.

**Step 7 — Mold Release:** The remaining PMMA is dissolved in acetone or NMP, releasing a free-standing nickel mold insert.

**Step 8 — Replica Molding:** The nickel mold is mounted in an injection-molding or hot-embossing machine. Polymers such as PMMA, COC, or POM are molded, demolded, and inspected.

**(b) Derivation and Calculation:**

Starting from the Beer–Lambert law:

$$I(H) = I_0 \, e^{-\mu H}$$

The dose at depth $H$ is $D(H) = D_0 \, e^{-\mu H}$. For successful development, $D(H) \geq D_{\text{th}}$:

$$D_0 \, e^{-\mu H_{\max}} = D_{\text{th}}$$

$$e^{-\mu H_{\max}} = \dfrac{D_{\text{th}}}{D_0}$$

$$-\mu H_{\max} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{D_{\text{th}}}{D_0}\right)$$

$$H_{\max} = -\dfrac{1}{\mu} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{D_{\text{th}}}{D_0}\right) = \dfrac{1}{\mu} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{D_0}{D_{\text{th}}}\right)$$

**Substituting numerical values:**

$$H_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{6.5 \text{ cm}^{-1}} \times \ln\!\left(\dfrac{4}{0.4}\right) = 0.1538 \times \ln(10)$$

$$H_{\max} = 0.1538 \times 2.3026 = 0.3542 \text{ cm}$$

$$\boxed{H_{\max} \approx 3542 \text{ µm} \approx 3.54 \text{ mm}}$$

This confirms that LIGA can expose resist thicknesses exceeding **3 mm** — orders of magnitude beyond UV lithography.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast LIGA with bulk silicon micromachining and surface micromachining. Discuss the relative advantages of LIGA in terms of aspect ratio, material flexibility, and mass-production capability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A LIGA nickel mold must be fabricated to a structural depth of $400 \text{ µm}$. If the electroplating is performed at a current density of $15 \text{ mA/cm}^2$ in a nickel sulfamate bath with $100\%$ current efficiency, calculate: (i) the deposition rate, and (ii) the total time required to fill the mold cavities. Given: $M_{\text{Ni}} = 58.69 \text{ g/mol}$, $z = 2$, $F = 96485 \text{ C/mol}$, $\rho_{\text{Ni}} = 8.908 \text{ g/cm}^3$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Comparative Analysis:**

| Feature | LIGA | Bulk Si | Surface Si |
|---------|------|---------|------------|
| Max height | 1000 µm | 500 µm | 10 µm |
| Aspect ratio | 100:1 | 50:1 | 5:1 |
| Material range | Plastics, Ni, Au, Cu, ceramics | Si only | Poly-Si, metals, oxides |
| Mass production | Excellent (molding) | Difficult | Moderate |
| Sidewall quality | Excellent | Etch-dependent | Layer-limited |
| Cost | Very high | Moderate | Low |

**Key Advantages of LIGA:**

1. **Ultra-high aspect ratio:** The combination of deep X-ray exposure and PMMA development enables aspect ratios up to **100:1**, ideal for high-force actuators and inertial sensors.
2. **Material versatility:** Final parts can be polymers, metals, alloys, or ceramics — not restricted to silicon, enabling applications in fluidics, optics, and biomedicine.
3. **Mass-production economy:** Once the nickel master is made, **millions of replicas** can be produced at per-unit costs of a few cents, making LIGA economically viable for automotive and consumer applications.

**Limitations of LIGA:**
- Requires a **synchrotron light source**, restricting accessibility.
- Long exposure and plating times ($20$–$60$ hours each).
- High capital cost for mask fabrication and molding equipment.

**(b) Electroplating Calculations:**

**Given:** $J = 15 \text{ mA/cm}^2 = 0.015 \text{ A/cm}^2$, $H = 400 \text{ µm}$, $M = 58.69 \text{ g/mol}$, $z = 2$, $F = 96485 \text{ C/mol}$, $\rho_{\text{Ni}} = 8.908 \text{ g/cm}^3$.

**(i) Deposition rate:**

$$v_{\text{dep}} = \dfrac{J \cdot M}{z \cdot F \cdot \rho_{\text{Ni}}}$$

$$v_{\text{dep}} = \dfrac{0.015 \times 58.69}{2 \times 96485 \times 8.908}$$

$$v_{\text{dep}} = \dfrac{0.88035}{1719218.6}$$

$$v_{\text{dep}} = 5.12 \times 10^{-7} \text{ cm/s}$$

Converting to µm/h:

$$v_{\text{dep}} = 5.12 \times 10^{-7} \times 10^4 \times 3600 = 18.4 \text{ µm/h}$$

$$\boxed{v_{\text{dep}} \approx 18.4 \text{ µm/h}}$$

**(ii) Total plating time:**

$$t_{\text{dep}} = \dfrac{H}{v_{\text{dep}}} = \dfrac{400 \text{ µm}}{18.4 \text{ µm/h}}$$

$$\boxed{t_{\text{dep}} \approx 21.7 \text{ hours}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency Trap:** Students frequently mix up $\text{cm/s}$, $\text{µm/h}$, and $\text{mm/h}$ when calculating deposition rate. Always explicitly show unit conversions.
> 2. **Forgetting Current Efficiency:** If the problem states current efficiency $\eta < 100\%$, the deposition rate must be multiplied by $\eta/100$ — a common error costing 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Confusing X-ray Attenuation Direction:** In Beer–Lambert's law, the intensity *decreases* with depth. Always write $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ — never $e^{+\mu x}$.
> 4. **Skipping Mask Description:** When describing LIGA, students often omit the **gold absorber on a silicon nitride membrane** mask structure. This is a 2-mark deduction item in KTU valuation.
> 5. **Neglecting Demolding Constraints:** LIGA's economic advantage depends on mold reusability. Examiners reward mention of demolding forces and mold lifetime ($\sim 10^6$ cycles).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — LIGA**

- ✅ **LIGA = Lithographie + Galvanoformung + Abformung** (X-ray Lithography + Electroplating + Molding).
- ✅ Developed at **Forschungszentrum Karlsruhe, Germany, early 1980s**.
- ✅ **Synchrotron X-rays** are mandatory because of their deep penetration ($>500 \text{ µm$) and high collimation.
- ✅ **PMMA** is the standard positive X-ray resist; **SU-8** is the UV-LIGA alternative.
- ✅ **X-ray masks** use **gold absorbers** ($10$–$20 \text{ µm$) on **silicon nitride or beryllium membranes**.
- ✅ **Nickel electroplating** is the standard electroforming process using nickel sulfamate electrolyte.
- ✅ **Maximum aspect ratio:** $100:1$ (standard), up to $500:1$ (advanced).
- ✅ **Maximum structural height:** $\sim 1000 \text{ µm$ ($1 \text{ mm}$).
- ✅ **Exposure dose (PMMA):** $2$–$4 \text{ kJ/cm}^3$.
- ✅ **Sidewall verticality:** $\pm 0.1^\circ$ — among the best of any micromachining process.
- ✅ **Mold lifetime:** $\sim 10^6$ injection cycles per nickel master.
- ✅ **Beer–Lambert law** governs X-ray penetration: $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$.
- ✅ **Maximum resist thickness:** $H_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{\mu} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{D_0}{D_{\text{th}}}\right)$.
- ✅ **Faraday's law** governs electroplating: $v_{\text{dep}} = \dfrac{J M}{z F \rho}$.
- ✅ **Variants of LIGA:** X-ray LIGA, UV-LIGA, Laser-LIGA, Pseudo-LIGA, Hierarchical LIGA, Sacrificial LIGA.
- ✅ **Key advantage over silicon processes:** material flexibility (plastics, metals, ceramics) and mass-production capability.
- ✅ **Key limitations:** Synchrotron access, long process times, high capital cost.
- ✅ **Applications:** microfluidics, micro-optics, automotive sensors, biomedical devices, RF MEMS.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microsystems Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET836 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Microsystems Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Microsystems Design** is the systematic, interdisciplinary engineering methodology used to conceive, model, simulate, and validate micro-scale devices that integrate mechanical, electrical, thermal, fluidic, and optical functional elements onto a single silicon (or non-silicon) substrate. It is governed fundamentally by the principle that **physical phenomena do not scale linearly with dimension**, demanding the re-evaluation of every design rule used in conventional macro-scale mechanical and electrical engineering.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, Microsystems Design is treated as a **multi-domain concurrent engineering process** that synchronizes four key pillars: (i) **system-level specification**, (ii) **device-level physical design**, (iii) **process-level fabrication compatibility**, and (iv) **package-level reliability**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Definition of Microsystem**
> A *Microsystem* is defined as a miniaturized system that performs sensing, actuation, signal processing, and communication functions, fabricated using microfabrication technologies and integrating electrical, mechanical, optical, fluidic, and chemical components on a single chip (System-on-Chip) or package (System-in-Package).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a civil engineer who has spent 20 years designing **massive concrete bridges**. Suddenly, you are asked to design a **"bridge" for an ant** that must carry tiny loads, withstand tiny winds, but operate under *different physical laws* because the surface-to-volume ratio is wildly different.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy: A Boat on the Sea vs. A Boat in a Bathtub**
> A normal cargo ship (1 meter scale) slices through ocean waves easily — *gravity and inertia* dominate. But the same ship shrunk to the size of a fingernail would behave like a *blob of jelly*: **surface tension, viscous drag, and electrostatic forces** would suddenly dominate, while *gravity becomes irrelevant*. The water surface would feel as rigid as a concrete wall.
>
> This is exactly what happens in MEMS — the design engineer must **swap** the dominant physics and re-engineer the device from scratch.

## 1.3 The Fundamental Paradigm Shift

At the macro-scale, design is intuitive because humans live in this world. At the micro-scale (typically **1 µm to 1 mm**), the following must be completely re-thought:

| Macro-Scale Dominant Force | Micro-Scale Dominant Force |
| :--- | :--- |
| Gravity (Volumetric, $\propto L^3$) | Surface Tension (Areal, $\propto L^2$) |
| Inertia (Mass-based) | Viscous Damping (Surface-based) |
| Electromagnetic (Far-field) | Electrostatic (Near-field, capacitive) |
| Bulk Mechanical Strength | Fracture Toughness & Fatigue |
| Convective Heat Transfer | Conductive Heat Transfer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle:** Microsystems Design is the *art and science of identifying the dominant physics at small length scales* and re-engineering the device so that the desired function is preserved or enhanced.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants Used in Microsystems Design

The following constants appear in nearly every MEMS design derivation and must be memorized:

- **Vacuum permittivity:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$
- **Surface tension of water:** $\gamma_{water} \approx 72 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N/m}$
- **Young's modulus of silicon:** $E_{Si} = 169 \text{ GPa}$ (single crystal, \<100\>)
- **Density of silicon:** $\rho_{Si} = 2330 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Standard gravitational acceleration:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Microsystems Design

Microsystems design is built on **three analytical layers** that must be solved simultaneously:

1. **Mechanical / Structural Design** — Beam bending, plate theory, stress-strain, fracture mechanics.
2. **Electrical / Electromagnetic Design** — Capacitance, inductance, electrostatic actuation, piezoelectric coupling.
3. **Thermal / Fluidic Design** — Heat conduction, convection, mass transport, fluid flow in microchannels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Multi-Domain"?** Unlike a pure mechanical part or a pure electrical circuit, a MEMS accelerometer contains a *mechanical proof mass* (mechanical domain), *capacitive sense plates* (electrical domain), and *self-heating under ambient conditions* (thermal domain). All three must be analyzed together — this is the essence of **concurrent design**.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Design Process (KTU-Relevant)

The KTU 2024 Scheme specifies a **top-down design flow** for microsystems:

1. **Step 1 — Define Specifications:** Identify the *physical input* (e.g., acceleration, pressure, chemical concentration) and the required *electrical output* (voltage, current, frequency shift).
2. **Step 2 — Identify Functional Domain:** Decide whether the transduction is *capacitive, piezoresistive, piezoelectric, thermal,* or *resonant*.
3. **Step 3 — Apply Scaling Laws:** Use dimensional analysis to determine which physical force will dominate and what material will perform best.
4. **Step 4 — Select Fabrication Process:** Match the device geometry to available processes (bulk micromachining, surface micromachining, LIGA, polymer-based).
5. **Step 5 — Simulate & Iterate:** Use finite-element tools (CoventorWare, COMSOL, ANSYS, IntelliSuite) to validate structural, electrical, and thermal performance.
6. **Step 6 — Design for Packaging & Test:** Add dicing streets, bond pads, alignment marks, and test structures *during* the layout phase — not as an afterthought.

## 2.3 Scaling Laws — The Heart of Microsystems Design

When a device's linear dimension is reduced by a **scaling factor $S$** (where $0 < S < 1$ for miniaturization), every physical quantity transforms predictably. Let $L$ be the original dimension; the new dimension is $L' = S \cdot L$.

| Quantity | Scaling Relation | Order in S |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Linear dimension | $L' = S \cdot L$ | $S^1$ |
| Area | $A' = S^2 \cdot A$ | $S^2$ |
| Volume (Mass) | $V' = m' = S^3 \cdot V$ | $S^3$ |
| Surface / Volume ratio | $(A/V)' = S^{-1} \cdot (A/V)$ | $S^{-1}$ |
| Cross-sectional stiffness | $k' = S \cdot k$ | $S^1$ |
| Resonant frequency | $f' = S^{-1} \cdot f$ | $S^{-1}$ |
| Heat capacity | $C' = S^3 \cdot C$ | $S^3$ |
| Heat dissipation | $Q' = S^2 \cdot Q$ | $S^2$ |
| Thermal time constant | $\tau' = S \cdot \tau$ | $S^1$ |

### 2.3.1 Force Scaling Comparison

The reason MEMS design is non-trivial is that **forces scale at different rates**:

- **Gravitational Force:** $F_g = \rho V g \;\Rightarrow\; F_g' = S^3 \cdot F_g$ (vanishes at micro-scale)
- **Electrostatic Force:** $F_e = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 d^2} \;\Rightarrow\; F_e' = S^2 \cdot F_e$ (dominant at micro-scale)
- **Capillary Force:** $F_c = \gamma L \;\Rightarrow\; F_c' = S^1 \cdot F_c$ (very important)
- **Magnetic Force:** $F_m \propto B^2 A \;\Rightarrow\; F_m' = S^2 \cdot F_m$
- **Viscous Drag:** $F_v = 6 \pi \mu R v \;\Rightarrow\; F_v' = S^1 \cdot F_v$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Because electrostatic force $\propto S^2$ while gravitational force $\propto S^3$, the *ratio* $F_e/F_g$ scales as $S^{-1}$. Therefore, **electrostatic actuation becomes the dominant transduction mechanism in MEMS**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Equation | Physical Meaning | Standard Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $F_e = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 d^2}$ | Electrostatic (capacitive) force | N (Newton) |
| 2 | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}$ | Parallel-plate capacitance | F (Farad) |
| 3 | $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x} = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d^2}$ | Capacitive sensitivity to gap | F/m |
| 4 | $k_{beam} = \dfrac{E w t^3}{4 L^3}$ | Spring constant of cantilever beam | N/m |
| 5 | $f_{res} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Natural resonant frequency | Hz |
| 6 | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{6 F L}{w t^2}$ | Max bending stress in a beam | Pa |
| 7 | $\sigma_{f} = \dfrac{K_{IC}}{Y \sqrt{\pi a}}$ | Fracture strength (Griffith) | Pa |
| 8 | $\Delta R = R_0 \cdot \pi_{l} \cdot \sigma$ | Piezoresistive change | $\Omega$ |
| 9 | $Q = \dfrac{\sqrt{k \cdot m}}{b}$ | Quality factor (with damping $b$) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $R_{th} = \dfrac{L}{k_{th} A}$ | Conductive thermal resistance | K/W |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse **piezoresistive** effect (resistance change under stress, used in silicon) with **piezoelectric** effect (charge generation under stress, used in quartz and PZT). KTU questions routinely test this distinction.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Microsystems design principles are the bedrock of:

- **Automotive airbag accelerometers** (Analog Devices ADXL — capacitive sensing)
- **Inkjet printer heads** (thermal bubble ejection, HP / Canon)
- **Smartphone gyroscopes & magnetometers** (STMicroelectronics, Bosch)
- **Medical pressure sensors** (cardiac catheters, intracranial monitors)
- **MEMS microphones** in Apple AirPods, hearing aids, and Amazon Echo
- **RF-MEMS switches** in 5G front-end modules

Every one of these products started with the *same three steps*: identify the physical input, apply scaling laws, and choose a dominant transduction mechanism.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Surface-Area-to-Volume Ratio Scaling

This is the **single most important scaling result** in MEMS design. It explains why thermal, fluidic, and surface effects dominate at the micro-scale.

**Given:** A cubic block of side length $L$. 

**Step 1 — Write the original geometric quantities:**

$$
A = 6 L^2 \quad \text{(surface area)}
$$

$$
V = L^3 \quad \text{(volume)}
$$

**Step 2 — Form the original area-to-volume ratio:**

$$
\left(\frac{A}{V}\right)_{orig} = \frac{6 L^2}{L^3} = \frac{6}{L}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the miniaturization transformation** $L \rightarrow L' = S \cdot L$:

$$
A' = 6 (S L)^2 = 6 S^2 L^2
$$

$$
V' = (S L)^3 = S^3 L^3
$$

**Step 4 — Form the new area-to-volume ratio:**

$$
\left(\frac{A}{V}\right)_{new} = \frac{6 S^2 L^2}{S^3 L^3} = \frac{6}{S L} = S^{-1} \cdot \frac{6}{L}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclude the scaling law:**

$$
\boxed{\left(\frac{A}{V}\right)' = S^{-1} \cdot \left(\frac{A}{V}\right)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** When you shrink a device by $S = 0.01$ (10 µm from 1 mm), the surface-area-to-volume ratio *increases by 100×*. This is why micro-scale devices:
> - Cool down and heat up *much faster* (more surface for heat loss, less volume to store heat).
> - Are *overwhelmed by surface tension* in liquid environments.
> - Are *extremely sensitive to surface contamination* and stiction.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Resonant Frequency Scaling of a Cantilever

This derivation shows why **MEMS devices vibrate at very high frequencies** — critical for oscillators, gyroscopes, and filters.

**Given:** A rectangular cantilever beam of length $L$, width $w$, thickness $t$, made of material with Young's modulus $E$ and density $\rho$.

**Step 1 — Spring constant of a cantilever under end-load:**

$$
k = \frac{E w t^3}{4 L^3}
$$

**Step 2 — Mass of the cantilever (≈ 1/3 of the distributed mass contributes effectively):**

$$
m_{eff} = 0.24 \, \rho \, L \, w \, t
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the miniaturization transformation** $L \rightarrow SL$, $w \rightarrow Sw$, $t \rightarrow St$:

For spring constant (note that $w$ and $L$ both appear in the same ratio $w/L$, so the $S^2$ from $w$ cancels with $L^2$ in the denominator):

$$
k' = \frac{E (S w) (S t)^3}{4 (S L)^3} = \frac{E \cdot S w \cdot S^3 t^3}{4 \cdot S^3 L^3} = S \cdot \frac{E w t^3}{4 L^3} = S \cdot k
$$

**Step 4 — For effective mass:**

$$
m_{eff}' = 0.24 \, \rho \, (S L) (S w) (S t) = S^3 \cdot (0.24 \, \rho \, L w t) = S^3 \cdot m_{eff}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the new resonant frequency:**

$$
f_{res}' = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k'}{m_{eff}'}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{S \cdot k}{S^3 \cdot m_{eff}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m_{eff}}} \cdot S^{-1}
$$

**Step 6 — Final scaling law:**

$$
\boxed{f_{res}' = S^{-1} \cdot f_{res}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** Shrinking a cantilever by $S = 0.01$ makes it vibrate **100× faster**. A 1 kHz macro-resonator becomes a 100 kHz micro-resonator. This is the principle behind silicon MEMS oscillators that replace quartz crystals in modern electronics.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Why Electrostatic Force Dominates at the Micro-Scale

This is the **classic KTU derivation** that every student must know.

**Given:** A parallel-plate capacitor with area $A$, gap $d$, and applied voltage $V$. The electrostatic force between the plates is:

$$
F_e = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 d^2}
$$

**Step 1 — Express the gravitational force on the upper plate** (mass $m = \rho A d$, where $\rho$ is the plate's density):

$$
F_g = m g = \rho A d \, g
$$

**Step 2 — Form the ratio $F_e / F_g$ (note: $A$ cancels out!):**

$$
\frac{F_e}{F_g} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 V^2}{2 d^2} \cdot \frac{1}{\rho \, d \, g} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 V^2}{2 \rho \, g \, d^3}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply scaling $d \rightarrow S d$:**

$$
\left(\frac{F_e}{F_g}\right)' = \frac{\varepsilon_0 V^2}{2 \rho \, g \, (S d)^3} = S^{-3} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_0 V^2}{2 \rho \, g \, d^3}
$$

**Step 4 — Final scaling law:**

$$
\boxed{\left(\frac{F_e}{F_g}\right)' = S^{-3} \cdot \left(\frac{F_e}{F_g}\right)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** When you shrink the gap by $S = 0.1$ (e.g., from 1 mm to 100 µm), the *electrostatic dominance over gravity* increases by a factor of $10^3 = 1000$. This is why almost all commercial MEMS actuators (accelerometers, micromirrors, RF switches) are **electrostatic**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Thermal Time Constant Scaling

The thermal time constant $\tau = R_{th} \cdot C_{th}$ governs how fast a device reaches thermal equilibrium.

**Step 1 — Thermal resistance of conduction through a slab** of length $L$, area $A$, conductivity $k_{th}$:

$$
R_{th} = \frac{L}{k_{th} A}
$$

**Step 2 — Thermal capacity** (mass × specific heat):

$$
C_{th} = \rho \, c_p \, V = \rho \, c_p \, A L
$$

**Step 3 — Form the time constant:**

$$
\tau = R_{th} \cdot C_{th} = \frac{L}{k_{th} A} \cdot \rho c_p A L = \frac{\rho c_p L^2}{k_{th}}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply scaling $L \rightarrow S L$:**

$$
\tau' = \frac{\rho c_p (S L)^2}{k_{th}} = S^2 \cdot \frac{\rho c_p L^2}{k_{th}} = S^2 \cdot \tau
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Wait — earlier we said $\tau' = S \cdot \tau$.** This discrepancy arises because in real systems, **convection** (with heat-transfer coefficient $h$) dominates over conduction at larger scales. The full result $\tau' \propto S$ uses $R_{th} \propto 1/(hA)$ which scales as $S^{-2}$, and $C_{th} \propto S^3$, giving $\tau' \propto S$. Both are valid in their respective limits.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Why Each Material Wins

| Material | Young's Modulus (GPa) | Density (kg/m³) | Dominant MEMS Use | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Single-Crystal Silicon | 169 | 2330 | Mechanical structures | High strength-to-mass, low thermal expansion |
| Polysilicon | 160 | 2300 | Surface-micromachined beams | CMOS-compatible, tunable stress |
| Silicon Dioxide (SiO₂) | 70 | 2200 | Sacrificial layer, insulation | Easy to etch with HF |
| SU-8 (epoxy) | 2 | 1200 | High-aspect-ratio structures | Photopatternable, thick (up to 1 mm) |
| PDMS (silicone elastomer) | 0.001 | 970 | Bio-MEMS, microfluidics | Biocompatible, flexible |
| PZT (piezoelectric) | 60 | 7500 | Actuators, energy harvesters | Strong piezoelectric coupling |
| Aluminum | 70 | 2700 | Electrical interconnects, mirrors | Low resistivity, easy to pattern |
| Gold | 78 | 19300 | Electrodes, bio-functionalization | Inert, bondable |

## 3.6 Summary of Key Scaling Equations (Reference Card)

For rapid revision, the **scaling exponent $n$** for each quantity under linear scaling $S$ is given below. A negative exponent means the quantity *grows* as the device shrinks.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L' &= S^1 L \\
A' &= S^2 A \\
V' &= S^3 V \\
F_g' &= S^3 F_g \\
F_e' &= S^2 F_e \\
F_c' &= S^1 F_c \\
k' &= S^1 k \\
f_{res}' &= S^{-1} f_{res} \\
\tau_{th}' &= S^1 \tau_{th} \quad \text{(convective limit)} \\
P_{fluid}' &= S^2 P_{fluid} \quad \text{(pumping power)}
\end{aligned}
$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]
**Define the term "Microsystem" and list any four functional components that a typical microsystem integrates on a single chip.**

**Model Answer:**
A *Microsystem* is a miniaturized multifunctional system fabricated using microfabrication technologies that integrates mechanical, electrical, optical, fluidic, and chemical components on a single silicon substrate.

The four typical functional components are:
1. **Micro-sensor** — converts physical input (pressure, acceleration, temperature) into an electrical signal.
2. **Micro-actuator** — converts electrical signal into mechanical motion (e.g., electrostatic comb drive).
3. **Signal processing unit** — typically a CMOS ASIC that amplifies and filters the sensor output.
4. **Micro-package** — provides hermetic sealing, electrical interconnection, and mechanical protection.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention the *signal processing* block. A microsystem is **not** just a sensor — it must have on-chip or in-package signal conditioning. Marks are deducted for this omission.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]
**State and explain the four scaling laws that are most important in microsystems design. Why does the surface-area-to-volume ratio become critically important at the micro-scale?**

**Model Answer:**
The four most important scaling laws are:
1. **Surface / Volume Ratio:** Scales as $S^{-1}$ (increases as device shrinks). This dominates thermal and chemical behavior.
2. **Resonant Frequency:** Scales as $S^{-1}$ (increases with miniaturization), enabling kHz-to-MHz micro-resonators.
3. **Electrostatic Force Dominance:** $F_e / F_g$ scales as $S^{-3}$, so electrostatic actuation wins at the micro-scale.
4. **Thermal Time Constant:** Scales as $S$ (decreases with miniaturization), so micro-devices cool down very fast.

The surface-area-to-volume ratio is critical because it controls *heat dissipation*, *chemical reactivity*, *surface friction*, and *stiction* — all of which become dominant failure mechanisms in MEMS.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 Style]

**(a) [7 Marks] Derive the scaling law for the resonant frequency of a cantilever beam when its linear dimensions are scaled by a factor $S$. Show that the resonant frequency increases as the device shrinks. (CO4, Apply)**

**(b) [7 Marks] Compare the scaling of gravitational force, electrostatic force, and capillary force with respect to a linear scaling factor $S$. Which force becomes dominant in microsystems and why? (CO4, Analyze)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1:** Write the spring constant of a rectangular cantilever beam of length $L$, width $w$, thickness $t$:

$$
k = \frac{E w t^3}{4 L^3}
$$

**Step 2:** Write the effective mass of the beam (which is $0.24 \rho L w t$ for the first mode):

$$
m_{eff} = 0.24 \, \rho \, L \, w \, t
$$

**Step 3:** Express the natural resonant frequency:

$$
f_{res} = \frac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\frac{k}{m_{eff}}} = \frac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\frac{E w t^3}{4 L^3 \cdot 0.24 \rho L w t}} = \frac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\frac{E t^2}{0.96 \rho L^4}}
$$

$$
f_{res} = \frac{t}{2 \pi L^2} \sqrt{\frac{E}{0.96 \rho}} \propto \frac{t}{L^2}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply scaling transformation $L \to S L$ and $t \to S t$:

$$
f_{res}' = \frac{S t}{2 \pi (S L)^2} \sqrt{\frac{E}{0.96 \rho}} = S^{-1} \cdot f_{res}
$$

**Step 5:** Conclude:

$$
\boxed{f_{res}' = \frac{1}{S} \cdot f_{res}}
$$

Since $S < 1$ for miniaturization, $f_{res}' > f_{res}$, proving that **resonant frequency increases as the device shrinks**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

| Force Type | Original Expression | Scaled Expression | Scaling Exponent |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Gravitational | $F_g = \rho V g$ | $F_g' = S^3 \rho V g$ | $S^3$ |
| Electrostatic | $F_e = \varepsilon_0 A V^2 / (2 d^2)$ | $F_e' = S^2 F_e$ | $S^2$ |
| Capillary | $F_c = \gamma L$ | $F_c' = S^1 F_c$ | $S^1$ |

**Analysis:**
- Gravitational force decreases *the fastest* ($S^3$) because it depends on mass.
- Capillary force decreases linearly ($S$).
- Electrostatic force decreases the slowest ($S^2$), so its *relative* importance increases as $S \to 0$.

**Conclusion:** **Electrostatic force becomes the dominant force in microsystems** because $F_e / F_g \propto S^{-3}$, which grows enormously as the device shrinks. This is the fundamental reason why all commercial MEMS actuators (comb drives, parallel-plate actuators, micromirrors) use electrostatic transduction.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Many students confuse **electrostatic** with **electromagnetic** actuation. The correct law is $F_e = \varepsilon_0 A V^2 / (2d^2)$, *not* $F = B I L$. **Electromagnetic** is rarely used in MEMS because it requires coils that are hard to fabricate at the micro-scale and produce weak fields. **Electrostatic** is preferred. Marks are deducted for confusing these.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the step-by-step top-down design process for a microsystem. Mention the role of CAD/CAE tools in this process. (CO4, Understand)**

**(b) [7 Marks] A MEMS pressure sensor uses a 200 µm × 200 µm × 5 µm silicon diaphragm that deflects 2 µm under an applied pressure. Calculate the spring constant of the diaphragm and its resonant frequency, given $E_{Si} = 169$ GPa, $\rho_{Si} = 2330$ kg/m³, and the diaphragm effective stiffness $k = 16 E w t^3 / (5(1-\nu^2) L^3)$ for a square plate with $\nu = 0.28$. (CO4, Apply)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The top-down design process for a microsystem consists of **six sequential steps**:

1. **System-Level Specification:** Define the input physical quantity (pressure, acceleration, force), the required output (voltage, frequency, digital code), and the operating environment (temperature range, media, lifetime).

2. **Functional Block Definition:** Break the system into blocks — *sensor*, *actuator*, *signal conditioning*, *interface*, *package*. Identify the *transduction principle* (capacitive, piezoresistive, piezoelectric, etc.).

3. **Scaling Law and Material Selection:** Use dimensional analysis to determine dominant forces. Select materials based on the application's needs (silicon for mechanical, gold for electrodes, PDMS for bio-MEMS).

4. **Fabrication Process Compatibility:** Decide between *bulk micromachining* (deep etching of silicon wafer), *surface micromachining* (deposited thin-film structures), *LIGA* (X-ray lithography for high-aspect-ratio metal), or *soft lithography* (polymers).

5. **CAD Layout and FEA Simulation:** Draw the 2D mask layout (using tools like **L-Edit** or **KLayout**) and run **Finite Element Analysis** (using **COMSOL Multiphysics**, **CoventorWare**, **ANSYS**, or **IntelliSuite**) to validate stress, displacement, modal frequency, capacitance, and thermal behavior.

6. **Design for Test and Packaging:** Add **dicing streets**, **alignment marks**, **bond pads**, and **test structures** (e.g., Van der Pauw crosses for resistivity, cantilever arrays for release validation) *before* tape-out.

**Role of CAD/CAE Tools:**
- **CAD** (L-Edit, Cadence Virtuoso) ensures the design obeys the foundry's **Design Rules** (minimum line width, spacing, alignment tolerance).
- **CAE** (COMSOL, ANSYS) performs *coupled-field* simulation — e.g., electro-mechanical, thermo-fluidic — which is essential because MEMS is inherently multi-physics.
- Reduces the **cost of fabrication iterations** by catching errors *before* the expensive photomask set is made.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $L = w = 200 \, \mu\text{m} = 200 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}$, $t = 5 \, \mu\text{m} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}$, $E = 169 \times 10^9 \, \text{Pa}$, $\rho = 2330 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $\nu = 0.28$.

**Step 1 — Calculate the spring constant** using the square plate formula:
