$$
k = \frac{16 \, E \, w \, t^3}{5 (1 - \nu^2) L^3}
$$

Since $L = w$, simplify:

$$
k = \frac{16 \, E \, t^3}{5 (1 - \nu^2) L^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
1 - \nu^2 = 1 - (0.28)^2 = 1 - 0.0784 = 0.9216
$$

$$
t^3 = (5 \times 10^{-6})^3 = 1.25 \times 10^{-16} \, \text{m}^3
$$

$$
L^2 = (200 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 4 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Plug into the formula:**

$$
k = \frac{16 \times 169 \times 10^9 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-16}}{5 \times 0.9216 \times 4 \times 10^{-8}}
$$

**Numerator:**

$$
16 \times 169 \times 10^9 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-16} = 3380 \times 10^{-7} = 3.38 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Denominator:**

$$
5 \times 0.9216 \times 4 \times 10^{-8} = 18.432 \times 10^{-8} = 1.8432 \times 10^{-7}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{k = \frac{3.38 \times 10^{-4}}{1.8432 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 1833.6 \, \text{N/m}}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the mass of the diaphragm:**

$$
m = \rho \cdot L \cdot w \cdot t = 2330 \times 200 \times 10^{-6} \times 200 \times 10^{-6} \times 5 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
m = 2330 \times 2 \times 10^{-13} = 4.66 \times 10^{-10} \, \text{kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the resonant frequency:**

$$
f_{res} = \frac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \frac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\frac{1833.6}{4.66 \times 10^{-10}}}
$$

$$
\frac{k}{m} = \frac{1833.6}{4.66 \times 10^{-10}} = 3.93 \times 10^{12}
$$

$$
\sqrt{3.93 \times 10^{12}} \approx 1.983 \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_{res} \approx 315.6 \, \text{kHz}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget to convert micrometers to meters. If you compute with µm throughout, the answer will be off by a factor of $10^6$. Always do dimensional analysis in SI units: **meters, kilograms, seconds, Newtons**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Microsystems Design**

- [ ] **Definition:** A microsystem is a *miniaturized multi-functional* system integrating mechanical, electrical, optical, fluidic, and chemical components.
- [ ] **Scaling Factor $S$:** Linear scaling $L' = S L$. For miniaturization, $0 < S < 1$.
- [ ] **Key Scaling Laws:** Area $\propto S^2$, Volume $\propto S^3$, Surface/Volume $\propto S^{-1}$, $f_{res} \propto S^{-1}$, $\tau_{th} \propto S$.
- [ ] **Force Hierarchy (from weakest to strongest scaling):** $F_g \propto S^3 < F_c \propto S < F_e \propto S^2 \approx F_m \propto S^2$.
- [ ] **Dominant Micro-Scale Forces:** Electrostatic and capillary forces dominate; gravity becomes negligible.
- [ ] **Why Silicon?** High Young's modulus (169 GPa), low density, CMOS compatibility, near-zero thermal expansion coefficient at room temperature.
- [ ] **Design Flow:** Specification → Domain Selection → Scaling Analysis → Material → Process → CAD/FEA → DfT → Tape-out.
- [ ] **Multi-Domain Nature:** A MEMS device must be analyzed in mechanical, electrical, and thermal domains **simultaneously** (concurrent design).
- [ ] **Transduction Principles to Memorize:** Capacitive ($C = \varepsilon_0 A / d$), Piezoresistive ($\Delta R / R = \pi \sigma$), Piezoelectric ($D = d \sigma + \varepsilon E$), Thermal ($V = \alpha \Delta T$).
- [ ] **Resonance Formula:** $f_{res} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$ — directly gives the time-base for MEMS oscillators and gyroscopes.
- [ ] **Exam Tip:** Always draw the four-block functional diagram (Input → Mechanical → Electrical → Package) for full marks in 14-mark design questions.
- [ ] **Standard Values to Memorize:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $E_{Si} = 169$ GPa, $\rho_{Si} = 2330$ kg/m³.
- [ ] **Common Confusion:** Piezoresistive (silicon) ≠ Piezoelectric (PZT, AlN, quartz). Examiner's favorite trick question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET836 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of MEMS Design Considerations

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Design Considerations in MEMS** refer to the systematic engineering principles, constraints, and trade-offs that govern the conception, modelling, simulation, and realization of micro-scale mechanical, electrical, thermal, fluidic, and optical structures fabricated using semiconductor-compatible processes. In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (Course Code: **OEMET836**), design considerations integrate **multi-physics coupling**, **scaling laws**, **material behaviour at micro-scale**, **fabrication compatibility**, and **reliability engineering** into a unified design framework.

The design of any MEMS device must concurrently address **four coupled physics domains**:

| Domain | Primary Variable | Governing Principle |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanical | Stress (σ), Strain (ε), Deflection (δ) | Hooke's Law, Beam Theory |
| Electrical | Voltage (V), Charge (Q), Capacitance (C) | Gauss's Law, Electrostatics |
| Thermal | Temperature (T), Heat flux (q) | Fourier's Law of Conduction |
| Fluidic | Pressure (P), Flow rate (Q), Reynolds no. (Re) | Navier–Stokes Equations |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The university examiner expects students to identify **why** certain design rules exist (e.g., minimum feature size, aspect ratio limits, packaging parasitics) and not merely list them. Every "rule" must be linked to its underlying physical or process-driven reason.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are an **architect designing a tiny house** — but instead of a 200 m² bungalow, your house must fit on a **single grain of sand** (≈ 500 µm × 500 µm). Now consider the unique challenges you would face:

- **The walls are made of silicon, not brick.** Silicon is *brittle* and *stiff* (Young's modulus ≈ **190 GPa**) — so unlike a real house, you cannot "bend" the walls to absorb shock; they will *crack*.
- **The air feels like honey.** As you shrink a device, the surface-to-volume ratio shoots up, so **air drag, surface tension, and stiction** become dominant. A mosquito jumping off your house is like a hurricane.
- **You cannot use screws.** You must build using *layer-by-layer deposition* and *selective etching* — so every shape must respect **crystallographic planes** (e.g., Si etches fastest along <111> in KOH).
- **Gravity is irrelevant; surface forces are king.** A human hair weighs nothing in MEMS world, but *capillary forces during wet release* can snap your structures.
- **You cannot open it later.** Once packaged, the MEMS die is sealed — so **in-situ testing, hermeticity, and reliability for 10+ years** must be designed in from Day 1.

This is the essence of **MEMS design considerations** — a discipline where *materials, processes, physics, and reliability* must be co-designed from the very first sketch.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** MEMS design is fundamentally a **constrained optimization** problem. The "best" design is not the one with the highest performance, but the one that simultaneously satisfies performance, manufacturability, reliability, and cost.

## 1.3 Taxonomy of Design Considerations

A complete MEMS design addresses **eight pillars**:

1. **Mechanical Design** — stress, strain, fatigue, fracture, vibration, buckling
2. **Thermal Design** — Joule heating, thermal expansion mismatch, heat sinking
3. **Electrical/Electrostatic Design** — capacitance, pull-in, breakdown voltage, parasitics
4. **Fluidic Design** — flow regime, channel resistance, mixing, diffusion
5. **Optical Design** — reflectivity, waveguide losses, alignment tolerance
6. **Material Selection** — Young's modulus, residual stress, CTE, biocompatibility
7. **Fabrication Process Constraints** — lithography resolution, etch selectivity, step coverage
8. **Packaging & Reliability** — hermetic sealing, die attach stress, fatigue life, environmental sealing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scaling Law (Noyes–Noyes Effect):** As linear dimension $L$ shrinks by a factor $n$, the **surface area** scales as $L^2$ and **volume** as $L^3$. Therefore the **surface-to-volume ratio scales as $1/L$**. This is the *single most important design consideration* in MEMS — it explains why friction, wear, heat dissipation, and surface tension dominate at the micro-scale.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Mechanical Design Theory

### 2.1.1 Stress, Strain, and Hooke's Law

For any MEMS structural element under load, the **fundamental mechanical equation** is:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

where:
- $\sigma$ = Normal stress (Pa) = Force per unit cross-sectional area
- $E$ = Young's modulus / Elastic modulus (Pa) — a material property
- $\varepsilon$ = Strain (dimensionless) = $\Delta L / L_0$ (fractional change in length)

**Why this matters in MEMS design:** Silicon is *linear-elastic* up to fracture (no plastic deformation). Unlike metals, it does not yield — it **snaps**. This dictates that MEMS designers must always operate *well below* the fracture strength with a **safety factor of 3–5×**.

### 2.1.2 Beam Theory — The Workhorse of MEMS

Most MEMS structures (cantilevers, bridges, diaphragms, comb fingers) are modelled as **Euler–Bernoulli beams** with the governing equation:

$$EI \frac{d^4 w(x)}{dx^4} = q(x)$$

where:
- $E$ = Young's modulus (Pa)
- $I$ = Second moment of area ($m^4$) — depends on cross-section
- $w(x)$ = Transverse deflection at position $x$ (m)
- $q(x)$ = Distributed load (N/m)

For a **rectangular cross-section beam** of width $b$ and thickness $h$:

$$I = \frac{b h^3}{12}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Insight:** Notice that $I$ depends on the **cube of thickness** $h^3$. This is why MEMS designers *prefer thin structures* — halving the thickness reduces bending stiffness by **8×**, enabling large deflections with small forces.

### 2.1.3 Cantilever Beam — The Most Common MEMS Element

A cantilever (fixed-free beam) of length $L$, width $b$, thickness $h$ under a **tip load $F$** has:

**Tip Deflection:**

$$\delta_{tip} = \frac{F L^3}{3 E I}$$

**Spring Constant (stiffness):**

$$k = \frac{F}{\delta_{tip}} = \frac{3 E I}{L^3} = \frac{E b h^3}{4 L^3}$$

**Resonant (Natural) Frequency:**

$$f_n = \frac{1.875^2}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A L^4}} = \frac{1.875^2}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{h}{L^2} \sqrt{\frac{E}{12 \rho}}$$

where $\rho$ = density of the material (kg/m³) and $A = b \cdot h$ is the cross-sectional area.

### 2.1.4 Fixed-Fixed Beam (Clamped-Clamped)

For a fixed-fixed beam of length $L$ with a **center point load $F$**:

**Center Deflection:**

$$\delta_{center} = \frac{F L^3}{192 E I}$$

**Spring Constant:**

$$k = \frac{192 E I}{L^3} = \frac{16 E b h^3}{L^3}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Comparison:** A fixed-fixed beam is **64× stiffer** than a cantilever of identical dimensions ($192/3 = 64$). This is why accelerometers and gyroscopes use *fixed-fixed* suspensions for high stiffness, while *AFM tips and biosensors* use cantilevers for high compliance.

### 2.1.5 Electrostatic Pull-In (Parallel Plate Actuator)

For a **parallel plate capacitor actuator** with plate area $A$, initial gap $d_0$, the electrostatic force pulls the plates together. The system becomes **unstable** (pull-in) when the displacement reaches $d_0/3$:

$$V_{pull-in} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k d_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 A}}$$

where $k$ is the mechanical spring constant and $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m is the vacuum permittivity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Rule:** To *increase* the pull-in voltage (more stable operation), increase $k$ (stiffer suspension) or increase $d_0$ (larger initial gap). To *decrease* it (more sensitive switch), do the opposite.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = F/A$ | Normal stress | Beam strength check |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ | Strain | Gauges, piezoresistors |
| 3 | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | Hooke's Law | Linear elastic regime |
| 4 | $I = bh^3/12$ | Rectangular moment of inertia | Beam stiffness |
| 5 | $k_{cantilever} = E b h^3 / (4 L^3)$ | Cantilever stiffness | AFM tip, sensor |
| 6 | $k_{fixed\text{-}fixed} = 16 E b h^3 / L^3$ | Fixed-fixed stiffness | Accelerometer |
| 7 | $\delta_{cantilever,tip} = F L^3 / (3EI)$ | Cantilever tip deflection | Force sensor design |
| 8 | $\delta_{fixed\text{-}fixed,center} = F L^3 / (192EI)$ | Fixed-fixed center deflection | Pressure diaphragm |
| 9 | $f_n = (1.875^2 / 2\pi) (h/L^2)\sqrt{E/(12\rho)}$ | Cantilever resonant freq. | Resonator design |
| 10 | $V_{PI} = \sqrt{8 k d_0^3 / (27 \varepsilon_0 A)}$ | Pull-in voltage | Electrostatic actuator |
| 11 | $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ | Parallel plate capacitance | Capacitive sensor |
| 12 | $F_{elec} = \frac{1}{2} \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{d^2}$ | Electrostatic force | Actuator design |
| 13 | $P_{buckle} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{(K L)^2}$ | Euler buckling load | Long slender columns |
| 14 | $\sigma_{residual}$ from thin-film mismatch | CTE mismatch stress | Multi-layer membranes |
| 15 | $S/V \propto 1/L$ (scaling) | Surface-to-volume ratio | Justifies MEMS physics shift |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles above underpin **commercial MEMS products** used by billions of people every day:

- **Smartphone accelerometers & gyroscopes** (STMicro, Bosch, InvenSense) — use fixed-fixed beam suspensions and capacitive sensing.
- **Inkjet printer nozzles** (HP, Canon) — use thermal-bubble actuation; thermal expansion drives droplet ejection.
- **MEMS microphones** (Knowles) — diaphragms deflect under sound pressure; capacitance change is detected.
- **DLP micromirrors** (Texas Instruments) — electrostatically tilted mirrors with pull-in design constraints.
- **Pacemakers and lab-on-chip devices** — rely on biocompatible materials (PDMS, glass) and hermetic packaging.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Trade-off Triangle:** Every MEMS design sits on a triangle with vertices **Performance**, **Reliability**, and **Cost**. The designer's job is to find the optimal *interior* point — not to push one vertex at the expense of the others.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Cantilever Beam Tip Deflection Under End Load

We will derive the tip deflection of a cantilever beam step by step, following the **KTU Board Examiner's valuation pattern** where every intermediate step must be shown.

### **Starting Point: Euler–Bernoulli Beam Equation**

The fundamental governing differential equation for a slender beam undergoing small deflections is:

$$EI \frac{d^4 w(x)}{dx^4} = q(x)$$

**Step 1 — Apply Loading Condition**

For a **concentrated tip load $F$ at $x = L$**, the beam is unloaded along its length. Hence:

$$q(x) = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad 0 \le x < L$$

The equation reduces to:

$$EI \frac{d^4 w(x)}{dx^4} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Integrate Once (Shear Force Relation)**

Integrating both sides with respect to $x$:

$$EI \frac{d^3 w(x)}{dx^3} = V(x) = C_1$$

By definition, the shear force $V(x)$ at any cross-section equals the load to the right of the section. For a tip load $F$, the shear is constant:

$$V(x) = F \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_1 = F$$

Therefore:

$$EI \frac{d^3 w(x)}{dx^3} = F$$

**Step 3 — Integrate Again (Bending Moment Relation)**

$$EI \frac{d^2 w(x)}{dx^2} = M(x) = F x + C_2$$

The bending moment at position $x$ due to a tip load $F$ acting at $x = L$ is $M(x) = F(L - x)$. The constant $C_2 = 0$ because the moment at the tip ($x = L$) is zero:

$$M(x) = F (L - x) = F L - F x$$

Hence:

$$EI \frac{d^2 w(x)}{dx^2} = F (L - x)$$

**Step 4 — Integrate Again (Slope Relation)**

$$EI \frac{dw(x)}{dx} = F \left( L x - \frac{x^2}{2} \right) + C_3$$

**Step 5 — Apply Boundary Condition for Slope at Fixed End**

At the fixed end ($x = 0$), the slope $dw/dx = 0$ (no rotation):

$$0 = F(0 - 0) + C_3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_3 = 0$$

Therefore:

$$EI \frac{dw(x)}{dx} = F L x - \frac{F x^2}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Integrate the Final Time (Deflection Relation)**

$$EI \cdot w(x) = \frac{F L x^2}{2} - \frac{F x^3}{6} + C_4$$

**Step 7 — Apply Boundary Condition for Deflection at Fixed End**

At $x = 0$, $w(0) = 0$ (no deflection at the wall):

$$0 = 0 - 0 + C_4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_4 = 0$$

Therefore:

$$EI \cdot w(x) = \frac{F L x^2}{2} - \frac{F x^3}{6}$$

$$w(x) = \frac{F x^2}{6 EI} (3L - x)$$

**Step 8 — Evaluate at the Free End (Tip Deflection)**

Setting $x = L$:

$$w(L) = \frac{F L^2}{6 EI} (3L - L) = \frac{F L^2}{6 EI} \cdot 2L$$

$$\boxed{\delta_{tip} = w(L) = \frac{F L^3}{3 E I}}$$

**Step 9 — Express in terms of Spring Constant**

The spring constant $k$ is defined as the force per unit deflection:

$$k = \frac{F}{\delta_{tip}} = \frac{3 E I}{L^3}$$

For a rectangular cross-section ($I = bh^3/12$):

$$\boxed{k_{cantilever} = \frac{E b h^3}{4 L^3}}$$

This derivation is the **single most frequently asked derivation in KTU MEMS papers** — students should memorize each of the 9 steps.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Design Parameter Effects on Cantilever Performance

The following matrix is a **ready-reckoner for KTU problems**. It shows how a **doubling** (2×) of each design parameter affects the four key outputs.

| Design Parameter (doubled) | Deflection $\delta$ | Stiffness $k$ | Resonant Freq. $f_n$ | Capacitance $C$ |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Length $L$ (×2) | **× 8 (increases)** | ÷ 8 (decreases) | ÷ 4 | — |
| Width $b$ (×2) | No change | **× 2 (increases)** | No change | × 2 |
| Thickness $h$ (×2) | ÷ 8 (decreases) | **× 8 (increases)** | × 2 | — |
| Young's $E$ (×2) | ÷ 2 | × 2 | √2 | — |
| Gap $d_0$ (×2) | — | — | — | ÷ 2 |
| Area $A$ (×2) | — | — | — | × 2 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked *"How would you design a more sensitive accelerometer?"* — use this matrix: **increase $L$** (more deflection per g), **decrease $h$** (lower stiffness), and **decrease $k$** (larger displacement ⇒ larger capacitance change).

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Standard 14-Mark Problem Style)

**Problem:** A MEMS cantilever accelerometer is fabricated from single-crystal silicon with the following specifications:
- Length $L = 200 \, \mu m$
- Width $b = 50 \, \mu m$
- Thickness $h = 2 \, \mu m$
- Proof mass $m = 1 \times 10^{-9}$ kg
- Young's modulus $E = 190$ GPa
- Density $\rho = 2330$ kg/m³
- Acceleration $a = 5g$ (with $g = 9.81$ m/s²)

**Calculate:**
1. The moment of inertia $I$
2. The spring constant $k$
3. The natural (resonant) frequency $f_n$
4. The static tip deflection under $5g$ acceleration

**Step 1 — Moment of Inertia**

$$I = \frac{b h^3}{12} = \frac{(50 \times 10^{-6})(2 \times 10^{-6})^3}{12}$$

$$I = \frac{(50 \times 10^{-6})(8 \times 10^{-18})}{12} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-22}}{12}$$

$$\boxed{I = 3.33 \times 10^{-23} \text{ m}^4}$$

**Step 2 — Spring Constant**

$$k = \frac{E b h^3}{4 L^3} = \frac{(190 \times 10^9)(50 \times 10^{-6})(2 \times 10^{-6})^3}{4 (200 \times 10^{-6})^3}$$

Numerator: $(190 \times 10^9)(50 \times 10^{-6})(8 \times 10^{-18}) = 190 \times 50 \times 8 \times 10^{-15} = 76000 \times 10^{-15} = 7.6 \times 10^{-11}$

Denominator: $4 \times (8 \times 10^{-15}) = 3.2 \times 10^{-14}$

$$k = \frac{7.6 \times 10^{-11}}{3.2 \times 10^{-14}} = 2375 \text{ N/m}$$

$$\boxed{k \approx 2.375 \text{ N/m}}$$

**Step 3 — Natural Frequency**

$$f_n = \frac{1.875^2}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{h}{L^2} \sqrt{\frac{E}{12 \rho}}$$

Compute term by term:
- $1.875^2 = 3.5156$
- $1.875^2 / (2\pi) = 3.5156 / 6.2832 = 0.5596$
- $h / L^2 = (2 \times 10^{-6}) / (200 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 2 \times 10^{-6} / 4 \times 10^{-8} = 50$ m⁻¹
- $E / (12 \rho) = (190 \times 10^9) / (12 \times 2330) = 190 \times 10^9 / 27960 = 6.795 \times 10^6$ m²/s²
- $\sqrt{6.795 \times 10^6} = 2607$ m/s

$$f_n = 0.5596 \times 50 \times 2607 = 72954 \text{ Hz}$$

$$\boxed{f_n \approx 72.95 \text{ kHz}}$$

**Step 4 — Static Tip Deflection under 5g**

Inertial force: $F = m \cdot a = (1 \times 10^{-9}) \times 5 \times 9.81 = 4.905 \times 10^{-8}$ N

$$\delta = \frac{F L^3}{3 E I} = \frac{(4.905 \times 10^{-8})(200 \times 10^{-6})^3}{3 \times (190 \times 10^9)(3.33 \times 10^{-23})}$$

Numerator: $4.905 \times 10^{-8} \times 8 \times 10^{-15} = 3.924 \times 10^{-22}$

Denominator: $3 \times 190 \times 10^9 \times 3.33 \times 10^{-23} = 1899 \times 10^{-14} = 1.899 \times 10^{-11}$

$$\delta = \frac{3.924 \times 10^{-22}}{1.899 \times 10^{-11}} = 2.066 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\delta \approx 20.7 \text{ nm}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Interpretation:** A 5g acceleration produces only **~21 nm** of tip deflection. This is why MEMS accelerometers need **on-chip amplification** (capacitive, piezoresistive, or piezoelectric) — the raw signal is at the nanometre scale. The accelerometer bandwidth must be **well below $f_n = 73$ kHz** to avoid resonance.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Framework: The 8-Pillar Design Considerations Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of MEMS design considerations. Each pillar interacts with the others — a change in one ripples through all.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start([MEMS Design Problem]) --> Goal[Define Performance Specs]
    Goal --> M1[Mechanical Design]
    Goal --> M2[Electrostatic Electrical]
    Goal --> M3[Thermal Design]
    Goal --> M4[Fluidic Design]
    Goal --> M5[Optical Design]
    Goal --> M6[Material Selection]
    Goal --> M7[Fabrication Process]
    Goal --> M8[Packaging Reliability]
    
    M1 --> Beam[Beam Theory Stress Strain Deflection]
    M2 --> Cap[Capacitance Pull-in Dielectric]
    M3 --> CTE[CTE Mismatch Heat Sinking]
    M4 --> Re[Reynolds Number Viscosity]
    M5 --> Align[Mirror Reflectivity Waveguide]
    M6 --> MatProp[Young Modulus Density Poisson]
    M7 --> Litho[Lithography Etch Selectivity]
    M8 --> Herm[Hermetic Seal Die Attach]
    
    Beam --> Trade{Design Trade-off Triangle}
    Cap --> Trade
    CTE --> Trade
    Re --> Trade
    Align --> Trade
    MatProp --> Trade
    Litho --> Trade
    Herm --> Trade
    
    Trade --> Perf[Performance]
    Trade --> Reli[Reliability]
    Trade --> Cost[Cost]
    
    Perf --> Verif[Simulation FEA]
    Reli --> Verif
    Cost --> Verif
    
    Verif --> Out[Final GDS Mask Layout]
```

## 4.2 Cantilever Beam Anatomy — Free-Body and Deformation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fixed Wall] -->|Clamp x=0| B[Cantilever Beam]
    B -->|Length L| C[Tip Free End]
    F[Force F at Tip] --> C
    C -->|Deflects down| D[Tip Deflection delta]
    D -.measured by.-> E[Capacitive Piezoresistive Optical Readout]
    E --> Sig[Output Signal V or Hz]
```

## 4.3 Scaling Law Visualization — Surface vs Volume Dominance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Macro[Macro World L = 1 m] -->|Volume Dominant| MacroPhys[Inertia Gravity Dominant]
    Micro[MEMS World L = 100 microm] -->|Surface Dominant| MicroPhys[Capillary Friction Heat Dominant]
    Nano[Nano World L = 100 nm] -->|Quantum Dominant| NanoPhys[Quantum Tunneling Dominant]
    
    MacroPhys --> A1[Forces proportional to Volume L cubed]
    MicroPhys --> A2[Forces proportional to Surface L squared]
    NanoPhys --> A3[Forces proportional to sub nm effects]
```

## 4.4 Process-Function Co-Design Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R[Requirement Spec] --> FD[Functional Design]
    FD --> PR[Process Selection]
    PR --> LA[Layout GDS]
    LA --> SI[Simulation FEA]
    SI --> CHK{Meets Specs}
    CHK -->|Yes| FAB[Fabrication]
    CHK -->|No| FD
    FAB --> TE[Testing Reliability]
    TE --> FEED[Feedback]
    FEED --> FD
```

## 4.5 Decision Tree: Cantilever vs Fixed-Fixed Beam Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q{Need High Sensitivity or Large Displacement?}
    Q -->|Yes| CAN[Choose Cantilever]
    Q -->|No| FF{Need High Resonant Frequency or Stiffness?}
    FF -->|Yes| FIX[Choose Fixed-Fixed Beam]
    FF -->|No| DBL[Consider Diaphragm or Comb Drive]
    
    CAN --> App1[AFM Biosensor Switch]
    FIX --> App2[Accelerometer Gyroscope Resonator]
    DBL --> App3[Pressure Sensor Microphone]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 Style]**
**"List any three key design considerations in MEMS and explain why each is critical."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Scaling Effects (1 mark):** At micro-scale, surface-to-volume ratio increases ($\propto 1/L$). This makes surface forces (stiction, friction, capillary) dominate over volumetric forces (inertia, weight). A MEMS gear train, for example, experiences more surface friction than a macro gear train.

2. **Material Selection (1 mark):** Silicon, polymers (PDMS, SU-8), metals (Al, Au), and piezoelectrics (PZT, AlN) have vastly different Young's moduli (1 MPa – 200 GPa) and CTE values. The choice directly determines device stiffness, residual stress, and thermal behaviour.

3. **Fabrication Constraints (1 mark):** Photolithography imposes minimum feature sizes (~1 µm in academic, ~10 nm in industry), and crystallographic etching restricts shapes along specific planes (e.g., KOH etches Si <100> along <111> planes at 54.74°). Designs must be *process-aware*.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023 Style]**
**"Distinguish between the design considerations for cantilever and fixed-fixed beam in MEMS."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Cantilever (1.5 marks) | Fixed-Fixed Beam (1.5 marks) |
|---|---|---|
| Boundary Conditions | Fixed at one end, free at the other | Fixed at both ends |
| Stiffness $k$ | $E b h^3 / (4L^3)$ | $16 E b h^3 / L^3$ |
| Applications | AFM tips, biosensors | Accelerometers, gyroscopes |
| Sensitivity | High deflection per unit force | Low deflection, high resonant frequency |

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Module 4 Choice Option A]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Derive the expression for the tip deflection of a cantilever beam of length $L$, width $b$, and thickness $h$ subjected to a concentrated load $F$ at the free end. Use the Euler–Bernoulli beam theory.**

**Model Solution:**

*(Detailed stepwise derivation as in Section 3.1, covering all 9 steps. Key milestones for the 7 marks:)*

1. (1 mark) State the governing equation: $EI \dfrac{d^4 w}{dx^4} = q(x)$ with $q(x)=0$.
2. (1 mark) First integration: $EI \dfrac{d^3 w}{dx^3} = V(x) = F$.
3. (1 mark) Second integration: $EI \dfrac{d^2 w}{dx^2} = M(x) = F(L-x)$.
4. (1 mark) Third integration: $EI \dfrac{dw}{dx} = FLx - Fx^2/2 + C_3$.
5. (1 mark) Apply boundary condition $w'(0)=0 \Rightarrow C_3 = 0$.
6. (1 mark) Fourth integration: $EI w(x) = FLx^2/2 - Fx^3/6 + C_4$.
7. (1 mark) Apply $w(0) = 0 \Rightarrow C_4 = 0$ and evaluate at $x=L$ to get $\boxed{\delta = FL^3/(3EI)}$.

**(b) [7 Marks] A MEMS cantilever made of polysilicon has the following dimensions: $L = 500 \, \mu m$, $b = 100 \, \mu m$, $h = 5 \, \mu m$. Given $E = 160$ GPa, find (i) the spring constant and (ii) the resonant frequency. Take $\rho = 2300$ kg/m³.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Spring Constant (3.5 marks):**

$$I = \frac{b h^3}{12} = \frac{(100 \times 10^{-6})(5 \times 10^{-6})^3}{12} = \frac{1.25 \times 10^{-21}}{12} = 1.042 \times 10^{-22} \text{ m}^4$$

$$k = \frac{3EI}{L^3} = \frac{3 \times (160 \times 10^9) \times 1.042 \times 10^{-22}}{(500 \times 10^{-6})^3}$$

Numerator: $3 \times 160 \times 10^9 \times 1.042 \times 10^{-22} = 5.00 \times 10^{-11}$

Denominator: $1.25 \times 10^{-13}$

$$k = \frac{5.00 \times 10^{-11}}{1.25 \times 10^{-13}} = 400 \text{ N/m}$$

$$\boxed{k = 400 \text{ N/m}}$$

**(ii) Resonant Frequency (3.5 marks):**

$$f_n = \frac{1.875^2}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{h}{L^2} \sqrt{\frac{E}{12\rho}}$$

- $1.875^2 / 2\pi = 0.5596$
- $h/L^2 = (5 \times 10^{-6}) / (2.5 \times 10^{-7}) = 20$ m⁻¹
- $E/(12\rho) = (160 \times 10^9) / (12 \times 2300) = 5.797 \times 10^6$ m²/s²
- $\sqrt{5.797 \times 10^6} = 2407.7$ m/s

$$f_n = 0.5596 \times 20 \times 2407.7 = 26945 \text{ Hz}$$

$$\boxed{f_n \approx 26.95 \text{ kHz}}$$

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Module 4 Choice Option B]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the major mechanical design considerations in MEMS with reference to (i) stress and strain, (ii) deflection, and (iii) resonant frequency.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Stress and Strain (2.5 marks):**
- Stress $\sigma = F/A$ governs fracture; silicon has $\sigma_{fracture} \approx 7$ GPa. A safety factor of 3–5× is mandatory.
- Strain $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ is exploited in **piezoresistive sensors** (e.g., pressure sensors) where resistance changes with strain.
- Design rule: keep peak stress below $\sigma_{fracture}/5$; use FEM (ANSYS, COMSOL) to map $\sigma_{von Mises}$.

**(ii) Deflection (2.5 marks):**
- Deflection $\delta$ is the primary output for **capacitive, piezoresistive, and optical readouts**.
- For a cantilever: $\delta = FL^3/(3EI)$.
- Designers balance $L$ (longer = more sensitive but lower $f_n$) and $h$ (thinner = softer, lower resonant frequency).
- Trade-off: $\delta$ vs $f_n$ — **increasing $L$ increases $\delta$ by $L^3$ but decreases $f_n$ by $1/L^2$**.

**(iii) Resonant Frequency (2 marks):**
- $f_n$ sets the operating bandwidth of resonators (timing, gyroscopes, mass sensors).
- $f_n \propto h/L^2 \sqrt{E/\rho}$.
- Environmental damping (air, viscous fluid) lowers the Q-factor; vacuum packaging boosts Q to >10,000.

**(b) [7 Marks] Discuss the role of (i) electrostatic design and (ii) thermal design in MEMS with suitable examples.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Electrostatic Design (3.5 marks):**
- Most common actuation principle in MEMS (comb drives, parallel plates, zip actuators).
- Force: $F = \frac{1}{2} \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{d^2}$ — non-linear with voltage and inverse-square with gap.
- **Pull-in instability** at $V_{PI} = \sqrt{8 k d_0^3 / (27 \varepsilon_0 A)}$ — designers avoid operating beyond this to prevent stiction.
- Examples: ADI accelerometers, Texas Instruments DLP, RF MEMS switches.
- Design rule: limit maximum voltage to < $V_{PI}$/1.5; use **bilateral pull** to double stable travel range to $2d_0/3$.

**(ii) Thermal Design (3.5 marks):**
- Thermal expansion mismatch between dissimilar layers (e.g., Al on Si, Au on SiO₂) creates **residual stress** $\sigma_{res} = E \Delta \alpha \Delta T$.
- Thermal actuators use **bimorph beams** (two materials with different CTE) — when heated, they bend predictably.
- Examples: thermal inkjet (HP), thermo-pneumatic pumps, Pirani gauges.
- Heat dissipation: at micro-scale, convection is poor; conduction through substrate is the dominant path. Design rule: provide **large thermal contact area** and avoid isolated hot-spots.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks (MOST FREQUENT PITFALLS):**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the moment-of-inertia formula** — Writing $I = bh^2$ instead of $I = bh^3/12$. The cubic dependence on $h$ is the *key* design insight.
> 
> 2. **Unit conversion errors** — Failing to convert $\mu m$ to $m$ (multiply by $10^{-6}$). A common answer error is $k = 400$ instead of $k = 0.4 \times 10^{-6}$ because $\mu m$ was not converted.
> 
> 3. **Mixing up cantilever and fixed-fixed formulas** — $k_{cantilever} = 3EI/L^3$ but $k_{fixed\text{-}fixed} = 192EI/L^3$ (a factor of 64× difference!). Always re-read the boundary conditions.
> 
> 4. **Skipping boundary conditions** — In derivations, applying $w'(0)=0$ and $w(0)=0$ is mandatory; missing these loses 1–2 marks.
> 
> 5. **Ignoring scaling effects** — A generic design answer that doesn't mention *why* MEMS behaves differently from macro systems loses the **"design thinking"** marks that KTU now rewards.
> 
> 6. **Neglecting units in numericals** — $E$ in GPa vs Pa is the #1 silent error. Always write $E = 190 \times 10^9$ Pa, not just 190.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Memorize Before Exam Day:**

- **Scaling Law (Noyes):** Surface-to-volume ratio $\propto 1/L$ → friction, stiction, surface tension dominate at micro-scale.
- **Cantilever Stiffness:** $k = E b h^3 / (4 L^3)$ — proportional to $h^3$, inversely to $L^3$.
- **Fixed-Fixed Stiffness:** $k = 16 E b h^3 / L^3$ — 64× stiffer than cantilever.
- **Hooke's Law:** $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ — valid until brittle fracture (no yield for Si).
- **Pull-In Voltage:** $V_{PI} = \sqrt{8 k d_0^3 / (27 \varepsilon_0 A)}$ — instability at $d_0/3$.
- **Resonant Frequency (cantilever):** $f_n = 0.5596 \cdot (h/L^2) \sqrt{E/(12\rho)}$.
- **Electrostatic Force:** $F = \frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 A V^2 / d^2$ — non-linear; quadratic with $V$.
- **Euler Buckling:** $P_{cr} = \pi^2 EI / (KL)^2$ — important for long slender columns.
- **Material Constants (Silicon):** $E = 190$ GPa, $\rho = 2330$ kg/m³, $\nu = 0.28$, CTE = 2.6 ppm/K.
- **Fabrication Constraints:** Min feature ≈ 1 µm (academic), <100 nm (industry); KOH etch at 54.74° on <100> Si.
- **Trade-off Triangle:** Performance ↔ Reliability ↔ Cost — never optimize just one.
- **Packaging:** Hermetic sealing mandatory for resonators; CTE mismatch must be < 5 ppm/K differential to avoid die-attach stress.
- **Reliability:** MEMS devices target **>10 years** operational life; failure modes include stiction, fatigue, creep (in polymers), and dielectric breakdown.
- **Design for Test (DFT):** Always include **test structures** (e.g., Van der Pauw crosses for resistivity, cantilever arrays for spring constant).
- **FEA Tools:** ANSYS, COMSOL, CoventorWare — used to validate analytical designs before fabrication.
- **Capacitance Readout:** $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ — small gap changes produce large $\Delta C$, enabling sub-nm resolution.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET836 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Process Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Process Design** in Micro Electro Mechanical Systems (MEMS) refers to the systematic engineering methodology that defines, sequences, and optimizes the unit fabrication steps — *photolithography, thin-film deposition, etching, doping, bonding, and release* — required to translate a functional device specification into a manufacturable, high-yield process flow. It establishes the **process flow architecture, mask layout strategy, design rules, material selection matrix, and tolerance budgets** that govern the fabrication of mechanical, electrical, and fluidic microstructures on a common substrate (typically silicon, glass, or polymer).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Process Design is treated as the **bridge between conceptual design and fabrication**, ensuring that geometry, materials, and tolerances defined at the design stage are physically realizable using available process modules.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – OEMET836, Module 4):**
> Process Design is one of the **highest-weightage, high-Cognitive-Level (Apply/Analyze)** topics in Module 4. Students are expected to draw process flows, justify process choices, and compute etch/deposition metrics. A typical KTU question asks students to *"design a process flow for a cantilever-based pressure sensor and justify each step."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of **Process Design as a recipe for a layered cake** that has to be built inside a transparent mould:

1. You cannot bake the icing *before* the sponge (the **order** of steps matters).
2. The icing cannot be thicker than the sponge's stiffness allows (this is **material compatibility** / stress).
3. The pattern of icing (chocolate writing, decorations) must be carved using a *stencil* (this is the **photolithography mask**).
4. Some layers must be eaten away with acid to reveal hidden cavities (this is **etching**).
5. Finally, the cake must be lifted out of the mould cleanly without breaking (this is the **release step**).

In MEMS, the "mould" is the silicon wafer, the "stencil" is the photomask, the "icing and sponge" are the deposited and grown thin films, and the "acid" is the wet/dry etchant. **Process Design is the master recipe that decides how all these ingredients interact.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight:** A MEMS device's performance — sensitivity, resonant frequency, residual stress, yield — is dictated as much by the *process* as by the *geometry*. Two identical mask designs fabricated through two different process flows can yield devices with **completely different characteristics**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

The following constants and metrics are repeatedly used in KTU problems on Process Design and must be memorized:

- **Wavelength of i-line (Hg lamp): λ = 365 nm**
- **Wavelength of g-line (Hg lamp): λ = 436 nm**
- **Wavelength of DUV (KrF excimer): λ = 248 nm**
- **Wavelength of ArF excimer: λ = 193 nm**
- **Numerical Aperture of typical stepper lens: NA = 0.4 – 0.93**
- **Silicon density: ρ_Si = 2.33 g/cm³**
- **Young's modulus of silicon (⟨100⟩): E = 130 GPa**
- **Poisson's ratio of silicon: ν = 0.28**
- **Thermal conductivity of silicon: k = 150 W/m·K**
- **Resistivity of intrinsic silicon: ρ > 10⁴ Ω·cm**
- **Etch rate of Si in KOH (100): ~ 0.6 µm/min**
- **Etch rate of Si in TMAH (80°C, 25%): ~ 1 µm/min**
- **Standard KOH concentration: 30 wt% in water**
- **Standard TMAH concentration: 25 wt% in water**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of MEMS Process Design

Process Design is decomposed into **five interlocking pillars**. Each pillar corresponds to a class of KTU questions.

### Pillar 1 — Photolithography (Pattern Transfer Engine)
Photolithography projects a mask pattern onto a photo-resist-coated wafer. It is governed by:

1. **Mask alignment** with prior patterns (overlay accuracy).
2. **Exposure** through UV light to modify resist solubility.
3. **Development** to remove the exposed (positive) or unexposed (negative) regions.
4. **Hard-bake** to harden the remaining resist pattern before downstream etch/deposition.

The minimum printable feature is governed by the **Rayleigh criterion** and **depth of focus** relations.

### Pillar 2 — Thin-Film Deposition (Material Builder)
Deposition builds layers on the wafer. Two families dominate:

- **Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD):** Evaporation, Sputtering.
- **Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD):** LPCVD, PECVD, APCVD.

### Pillar 3 — Etching (Material Remover)
Etching removes material selectively. Sub-categories:

- **Wet Etching:** Isotropic, chemical, e.g., HF, KOH, TMAH.
- **Dry Etching:** Anisotropic, plasma-based, e.g., RIE, DRIE (Bosch process).
- **Parameters to control:** Etch rate, selectivity, anisotropy, surface roughness.

### Pillar 4 — Doping / Oxidation (Electrical Modifier)
Used for tuning electrical properties — for example, creating piezoresistors in a pressure sensor.

### Pillar 5 — Bonding & Release (Assembly & Final Step)
Final structural integration: anodic bonding, fusion bonding, eutectic bonding, or sacrificial layer release.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **must-memorize** cheat sheet. It contains every equation that has appeared in past KTU ESE papers for MEMS Process Design questions.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Typical Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rayleigh Resolution | $R = \dfrac{k_1 \lambda}{NA}$ | nm | Min. printable linewidth |
| 2 | Depth of Focus | $DOF = \dfrac{k_2 \lambda}{NA^2}$ | nm | Tolerable defocus range |
| 3 | Etch Rate | $ER = \dfrac{\Delta h}{\Delta t}$ | nm/min or µm/min | Material removal speed |
| 4 | Selectivity | $S = \dfrac{ER_{film}}{ER_{substrate}}$ | dimensionless | Mask protection efficiency |
| 5 | Anisotropy | $A = 1 - \dfrac{ER_{lateral}}{ER_{vertical}}$ | dimensionless (0–1) | Directional preference |
| 6 | Step Coverage | $SC = \dfrac{t_{min}}{t_{nominal}}$ | dimensionless (0–1) | Conformal coverage quality |
| 7 | Sheet Resistance | $R_s = \dfrac{\rho}{t}$ | Ω/□ | Thin-film resistivity metric |
| 8 | Stoney Stress Equation | $\sigma_f = \dfrac{E_s \, t_s^2}{6(1-\nu_s) \, t_f} \, \kappa$ | MPa | Residual film stress via curvature κ |
| 9 | Knudsen Number | $Kn = \dfrac{\lambda_{mfp}}{L}$ | dimensionless | Rarefied gas flow regime |
| 10 | Process Yield (Compound) | $Y = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | dimensionless | Cumulative fab yield |
| 11 | Aspect Ratio | $AR = \dfrac{\text{depth}}{\text{width}}$ | dimensionless | Geometry factor for etch/deposit |
| 12 | Sputter Yield | $Y_{sp} = \dfrac{\text{atoms ejected}}{\text{incident ion}}$ | atoms/ion | PVD efficiency |
| 13 | Growth Rate (Thermal Oxidation, Deal–Grove) | $x^2 + Ax = B(t + \tau)$ | µm² , µm | Oxide thickness vs time |
| 14 | Natural Oxide Thickness | $\delta_{SiO_2} \approx 1 - 2 \, \text{nm}$ | nm | Air-formed native oxide |
| 15 | Thermal Expansion Mismatch | $\varepsilon_{th} = (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T$ | strain | Bonding/film stress driver |

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Equation — Engineering Utility

1. **Resolution R**: As λ decreases (moving from i-line to DUV/EUV) or NA increases, the smallest printable feature shrinks — this is why semiconductor industry migrates to shorter wavelengths every decade.
2. **DOF**: Higher NA reduces DOF quadratically — modern immersion lithography uses special fluids to recover DOF.
3. **Etch Rate**: Determines throughput. A 1 µm/min process takes 100 min to etch 100 µm — directly affects fab cost.
4. **Selectivity S**: For deep silicon etching (DRIE) where we want S > 100:1 against the masking oxide.
5. **Anisotropy A**: A=1 → perfectly vertical sidewalls (required for MEMS comb drives).
6. **Step Coverage SC**: Poor SC causes voids in inter-metal vias — a major reliability failure mode.
7. **Sheet Resistance R_s**: Used to design piezoresistors and heaters.
8. **Stoney Equation**: Predicts whether a thin film will crack, peel, or warp the underlying microstructure.
9. **Knudsen Number**: Determines whether gas damping in a MEMS resonator is continuum (Kn < 0.001), slip (0.001 < Kn < 0.1), or transition/free-molecular (Kn > 0.1).
10. **Process Yield Y**: A 5-step process with each step at 99% gives $Y = 0.99^5 = 0.951$ — a 5% loss. Multiply by 20 steps and yield collapses.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Rayleigh Resolution and Depth of Focus

The Rayleigh criterion defines the smallest resolvable feature between two adjacent patterns:

$$R = \frac{k_1 \, \lambda}{NA}$$

where:
- $\lambda$ = wavelength of exposure light
- $NA$ = numerical aperture of the projection lens
- $k_1$ = process-dependent factor (theoretical minimum is **0.25**, practical values are **0.4–0.8**).

### Step-by-step Justification

A lens of numerical aperture $NA = n \sin\theta$ collects diffracted orders from the mask. The first-order diffraction angle for a feature of width $R$ satisfies the grating equation:

$$R \, \sin\theta_m = m \, \lambda \quad \Rightarrow \quad R = \frac{\lambda}{\sin\theta_m}$$

For the lens to capture at least one diffracted order, $\sin\theta_m \le NA$, hence:

$$R_{min} = \frac{\lambda}{NA}$$

Multiplying by the empirical factor $k_1$ (which accounts for resist chemistry, lens aberrations, and partial coherence) gives the working Rayleigh equation used by fab engineers.

### Numerical Example (KTU-style)

A KTU problem gives $\lambda = 365 \, \text{nm}$, $NA = 0.65$, $k_1 = 0.6$. Compute the resolution.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{k_1 \lambda}{NA} \\
&= \frac{0.6 \times 365 \, \text{nm}}{0.65} \\
&= \frac{219 \, \text{nm}}{0.65} \\
&= 336.92 \, \text{nm} \approx 0.34 \, \mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

### Depth of Focus Derivation

The depth of focus (DOF) is the axial range over which the image stays acceptably sharp. From Fourier optics, the focal tolerance is inversely proportional to $NA^2$:

$$DOF = \frac{k_2 \, \lambda}{NA^2}$$

**Worked Calculation** (same λ, NA = 0.65, $k_2 = 1$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
DOF &= \frac{1 \times 365 \, \text{nm}}{(0.65)^2} \\
&= \frac{365}{0.4225} \, \text{nm} \\
&= 863.9 \, \text{nm} \approx 0.86 \, \mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** Halving the wavelength cuts both R and DOF in half. Halving NA (improving DOF by 4×) degrades R by 2×. This trade-off is central to stepper selection in a MEMS fab.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Anisotropy and Selectivity

### Anisotropy

Anisotropy compares vertical etch rate to lateral etch rate:

$$A = 1 - \frac{ER_{lateral}}{ER_{vertical}}$$

**Proof / Interpretation:**

- If $ER_{lateral} = ER_{vertical}$ (e.g., wet isotropic etch in HF): $A = 0$ (fully isotropic).
- If $ER_{lateral} \to 0$ (e.g., DRIE Bosch process): $A \to 1$ (perfectly anisotropic).

### Selectivity

Selectivity $S$ is the ratio of etch rates between two materials:

$$S = \frac{ER_{film}}{ER_{substrate}}$$

If a 100 nm oxide mask must survive a 10 µm deep Si etch, then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Required } S &\ge \frac{ER_{Si}}{ER_{SiO_2}} \\
&= \frac{\text{depth to etch}}{\text{mask thickness}} \\
&= \frac{10 \, \mu m}{0.1 \, \mu m} \\
&= 100
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence $S \ge 100$ is mandatory for this process flow.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Stoney's Equation for Thin-Film Stress

Stoney's equation relates the **average residual stress $\sigma_f$** in a thin film to the **substrate curvature $\kappa$** that the film imposes on the substrate.

**Starting assumption:** Film thickness $t_f \ll$ substrate thickness $t_s$. The film–substrate couple is in mechanical equilibrium with bending moment $M$.

Force balance per unit width:

$$M = \frac{\sigma_f \, t_f \, t_s}{2}$$

Curvature–moment relation for a plate:

$$\kappa = \frac{6 M}{E_s \, t_s^2} \, (1 - \nu_s)$$

Substituting $M$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\kappa &= \frac{6}{E_s \, t_s^2 (1 - \nu_s)} \cdot \frac{\sigma_f \, t_f \, t_s}{2} \\
&= \frac{3 \, \sigma_f \, t_f}{E_s \, t_s \, (1 - \nu_s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for $\sigma_f$ gives the **canonical Stoney equation**:

$$\sigma_f = \frac{E_s \, t_s^2}{6 \, (1 - \nu_s) \, t_f} \, \kappa$$

> [!NOTE]
> The stress depends *inversely* on $t_f$ — for very thin films, the same curvature corresponds to a very large stress. This is why ultra-thin films (<50 nm) frequently delaminate.

### Numerical Example

Given: $E_s = 130 \, \text{GPa}$ (Si ⟨100⟩), $\nu_s = 0.28$, $t_s = 525 \, \mu m$, $t_f = 1 \, \mu m$, measured curvature $\kappa = 0.01 \, m^{-1}$. Compute film stress.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_f &= \frac{130 \times 10^9 \times (525 \times 10^{-6})^2}{6 \times (1 - 0.28) \times 1 \times 10^{-6}} \times 0.01 \\
&= \frac{130 \times 10^9 \times 2.756 \times 10^{-7}}{6 \times 0.72 \times 10^{-6}} \times 0.01 \\
&= \frac{35.83}{4.32 \times 10^{-6}} \times 0.01 \\
&= 8.295 \times 10^6 \times 0.01 \\
&\approx 8.3 \times 10^4 \, Pa = 0.083 \, MPa
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Deal–Grove Thermal Oxidation Model

The oxidation rate of silicon follows the **Deal–Grove equation**:

$$x^2 + A x = B (t + \tau)$$

where:
- $x$ = oxide thickness
- $t$ = oxidation time
- $\tau$ = initial offset time
- $B$ = parabolic rate constant (diffusion-limited regime)
- $B/A$ = linear rate constant (reaction-limited regime)

**Limiting cases:**

- **Long times (thick oxide):** $x^2 \approx B t \Rightarrow x \propto \sqrt{t}$ (parabolic)
- **Short times (thin oxide):** $A x \approx B (t + \tau) \Rightarrow x \propto t$ (linear)

This dual-regime behavior arises because, for thin oxides, the **reaction at the Si/SiO₂ interface** dominates; for thick oxides, **oxygen diffusion** through the oxide layer becomes rate-limiting.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Compound Process Yield

For an $n$-step process where each step has an independent yield $Y_i$, the overall yield is:

$$Y = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$$

**Derivation logic:** If step $i$ yields $Y_i$ fraction of good die, the fraction surviving all $n$ steps is the joint probability (assuming independence). For exponential decay of yield with defect density $D$ and critical area $A_i$:

$$Y_i = e^{-D A_i}$$

Hence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{-D A_i} \\
&= e^{-D \sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i} \\
&= e^{-D A_{total}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Example:** A 10-step process, each step yield = 99%. Find total yield.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= (0.99)^{10} \\
&= e^{10 \ln(0.99)} \\
&= e^{-0.1005} \\
&\approx 0.904 \, \text{or} \, 90.4\%
\end{aligned}
$$

If yield drops to 95% per step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= (0.95)^{10} \approx 0.5987 \approx 59.9\%
\end{aligned}
$$

This demonstrates the **compounding impact of process variability** in MEMS, where tolerance stack-ups are tighter than in conventional ICs.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MEMS Process Design Master Flow

The following Mermaid block diagrams the complete Process Design workflow — from specification to packaged device — with the iterative design-rule check loop highlighted.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Device Specification] --> B[Material Selection]
    B --> C[Mask Layout Design]
    C --> D{Design Rule Check}
    D -- Fail --> C
    D -- Pass --> E[Process Flow Generation]
    E --> F[Photolithography Module]
    E --> G[Deposition Module]
    E --> H[Etching Module]
    E --> I[Bonding / Release Module]
    F --> J[Process Simulation]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K{Process Yield Acceptable?}
    K -- No --> L[Process Tuning]
    L --> E
    K -- Yes --> M[Fabrication]
    M --> N[Wafer-Level Testing]
    N --> O[Packaging]
    O --> P[Final MEMS Device]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The four parallel modules under "Process Flow Generation" correspond to the **four pillars** of process design (lithography, deposition, etch, bonding/release).
- The **Design Rule Check** loop is mandatory — without it, the mask set will violate fab constraints (e.g., minimum line spacing, alignment tolerance).
- **Process Simulation** uses tools like *CoventorWare*, *COMSOL*, or *IntelliSuite* to verify that the geometric output of the process flow matches the functional specification.

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of a Surface-Micromachined Cantilever

This block diagram maps the functional architecture of a MEMS cantilever — the canonical KTU example — to its corresponding process flow steps.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SUB[Substrate Preparation]
        S1[Si Wafer Selection 100 orientation] --> S2[Thermal Oxide Growth 1 um SiO2]
    end

    subgraph SAC[Sacrificial Layer Module]
        S2 --> SAC1[PSG Deposition 2 um via LPCVD]
        SAC1 --> SAC2[Photolithography 1 Anchor Pattern]
        SAC2 --> SAC3[RIE Etch to Open Anchor]
    end

    subgraph STR[Structural Layer Module]
        SAC3 --> STR1[Polysilicon Deposition 2 um LPCVD]
        STR1 --> STR2[Photolithography 2 Cantilever Pattern]
        STR2 --> STR3[DRIE / RIE Etch Cantilever Shape]
    end

    subgraph REL[Release Module]
        STR3 --> REL1[HF Vapor Etch Sacrificial PSG]
        REL1 --> REL2[Critical Point Drying to Prevent Stiction]
        REL1 --> REL3[Released Cantilever]
    end
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Substrate Preparation** establishes the mechanical and electrical foundation.
- **Sacrificial Layer Module** defines the future *air gap* — without it, no freestanding structure is possible.
- **Structural Layer Module** defines the *functional geometry* of the MEMS device.
- **Release Module** is the most critical — the *HF etch* removes the sacrificial layer; *critical point drying* prevents stiction (where surface tension forces the cantilever to stick down).

## 4.3 Process Design Trade-off Matrix (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TRADE[Process Design Trade-offs]
        direction TB
        T1[Resolution vs Depth of Focus] --> T2[Etch Rate vs Selectivity]
        T2 --> T3[Step Coverage vs Throughput]
        T3 --> T4[Anisotropy vs Surface Damage]
        T4 --> T5[Yield vs Process Complexity]
    end

    subgraph CONST[Constraints Driving Trade-offs]
        C1[Material Properties] --> C2[Equipment Capability]
        C2 --> C3[Cost Target]
        C3 --> C4[Application Tolerance]
    end

    CONST --> TRADE
```

**Engineering reading:** Each block represents a fundamental trade-off that the process designer must resolve. For instance, *Anisotropy vs Surface Damage* — high anisotropy in DRIE introduces ion-induced surface damage, which degrades piezoresistor performance in a pressure sensor.

## 4.4 Yield-Cost Relationship in Process Design

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Add Process Step] --> B[+ More Mask Levels]
    B --> C[+ Longer Cycle Time]
    C --> D[+ Lower Throughput]
    D --> E[+ Higher Per-Die Cost]
    B --> F[+ New Defect Sources]
    F --> G[- Yield Reduction]
    G --> E
```

**Engineering takeaway:** Every additional process step *simultaneously* reduces yield *and* increases per-die cost. A skilled process designer achieves the desired functionality with the **minimum number of process steps**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part A questions are direct-concept recall items. They typically test definitions, formulae, or brief comparisons. Answers must be **crisp and formula-anchored** to score full marks.

---

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define the term "Process Design" in the context of MEMS fabrication. List any four process modules that constitute a typical MEMS process flow.**

**Model Answer:**

Process Design is the engineering methodology that defines, sequences, and optimizes the sequence of fabrication steps required to manufacture a MEMS device from a given functional specification. It encompasses mask design, process flow generation, and tolerance budgeting.

The four principal process modules are:

1. **Photolithography** — pattern transfer onto the wafer using UV exposure through a mask.
2. **Thin-Film Deposition** — building layers via PVD (evaporation, sputtering) or CVD (LPCVD, PECVD).
3. **Etching** — selective material removal via wet chemical or dry plasma etching (RIE, DRIE).
4. **Bonding / Release** — final assembly via anodic, fusion, or eutectic bonding, followed by sacrificial layer release.

*(Any 4 from the above list. Additional acceptable modules: doping, oxidation, CMP, packaging.)*

**Marks distribution:** 1.5 marks for definition + 1.5 marks for the four modules.

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**What is anisotropy in etching? Give the mathematical expression. What is the value of anisotropy for an isotropic etch process?**

**Model Answer:**

Anisotropy quantifies the directional preference of an etching process. It is defined as:

$$A = 1 - \frac{ER_{lateral}}{ER_{vertical}}$$

For a perfectly **isotropic** etch, the lateral etch rate equals the vertical etch rate, i.e., $ER_{lateral} = ER_{vertical}$. Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{iso} &= 1 - \frac{ER_{vertical}}{ER_{vertical}} \\
&= 1 - 1 \\
&= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, **anisotropy = 0** for a fully isotropic process (e.g., wet HF etching of SiO₂). **Marks: 1 + 1 + 1.**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part B questions carry **14 marks**, divided as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Each sub-part is a structured mini-question. Internal choice is provided — students attempt **either** Option A **or** Option B in full.

---

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat diagrams, explain the **surface micromachining process flow** for fabricating a freestanding polysilicon cantilever. Mention the role of the **sacrificial layer** and the **critical-point drying step**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The process flow uses an i-line stepper with $\lambda = 365 \, \text{nm}$, $NA = 0.55$, and $k_1 = 0.7$. Calculate the minimum printable linewidth. If the same process is migrated to a KrF DUV stepper with $\lambda = 248 \, \text{nm}$, $NA = 0.75$, $k_1 = 0.5$, what is the percentage improvement in resolution?

---

#### Model Answer — Part (a)

**Surface Micromachining Process Flow:**

Surface micromachining builds MEMS structures by depositing and patterning **structural layers** on top of **sacrificial layers** that are later dissolved to release the structure.

**Step-by-step flow (with diagram in words):**

1. **Substrate preparation:** Start with a single-crystal silicon wafer of (100) orientation. Grow a 1 µm thermal oxide layer to act as an electrical insulator and mechanical buffer.
2. **Sacrificial layer deposition:** Deposit a 2 µm layer of **phosphosilicate glass (PSG)** using LPCVD. PSG is chosen because it has a high etch rate in HF vapor (orders of magnitude higher than thermal oxide).
3. **Anchor patterning:** Spin-coat photoresist, expose using **Mask 1 (anchor mask)**, develop, and etch the PSG using RIE to open anchor windows down to the underlying oxide.
4. **Structural layer deposition:** Deposit a 2 µm layer of **LPCVD polysilicon** — this is the structural material.
5. **Cantilever patterning:** Use **Mask 2 (cantilever mask)** to define the cantilever outline. RIE the polysilicon down to the PSG.
6. **Release etch:** Immerse (or vapor-expose) the wafer in **HF vapor**. The HF selectively etches the PSG *underneath* the polysilicon, leaving the cantilever freestanding.
7. **Critical-point drying:** To prevent the cantilever from collapsing onto the substrate due to **surface tension forces** during the liquid-to-vapor transition.

**Role of sacrificial layer:** Defines the future air gap (typically 0.5–5 µm) and therefore the mechanical compliance of the MEMS structure. Without it, no freestanding structure can exist.

**Role of critical-point drying:** During conventional drying, the receding liquid meniscus exerts a Laplace pressure $P = 2\gamma / r$ (where $\gamma$ is surface tension, $r$ is meniscus radius). For long thin cantilevers, this force exceeds the cantilever's restoring force, causing **stiction**. Critical-point drying transitions liquid → vapor *without* a meniscus by going around the critical point of CO₂.

#### Model Answer — Part (b)

**Computation 1: i-line minimum linewidth**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1 &= \frac{k_1 \, \lambda}{NA} \\
&= \frac{0.7 \times 365 \, \text{nm}}{0.55} \\
&= \frac{255.5}{0.55} \\
&= 464.5 \, \text{nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Computation 2: KrF DUV minimum linewidth**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_2 &= \frac{0.5 \times 248 \, \text{nm}}{0.75} \\
&= \frac{124}{0.75} \\
&= 165.3 \, \text{nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Percentage improvement:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ improvement} &= \frac{R_1 - R_2}{R_1} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{464.5 - 165.3}{464.5} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{299.2}{464.5} \times 100 \\
&\approx 64.4\%
\end{aligned}
$$

The DUV migration improves resolution by **~64.4%**.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]` *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **wet bulk micromachining** with **dry bulk micromachaining** in silicon. Use anisotropy, etch rate, mask compatibility, and surface finish as comparison metrics. Mention one application where each is preferred.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 2 µm thick polysilicon film is deposited on a 525 µm thick silicon wafer. After deposition, the wafer is measured to have a radius of curvature $R = 50 \, \text{m}$. Given $E_s = 130 \, \text{GPa}$ and $\nu_s = 0.28$, calculate the **average residual stress** in the polysilicon film using Stoney's equation. Comment on whether this stress is tensile or compressive.

---

#### Model Answer — Part (a)

| Metric | Wet Bulk Micromachining | Dry Bulk Micromachining (DRIE) |
|---|---|---|
| Anisotropy | Crystallographic, A ≈ 0.7–1 along (111) planes in KOH/TMAH | Plasma-driven, A ≈ 0.95–0.99 (Bosch process) |
| Etch Rate | 0.6–1 µm/min (KOH 30% at 80°C) | 1–10 µm/min (Bosch, SF₆ plasma) |
| Mask Compatibility | Si₃N₄ or SiO₂ (SiO₂ has finite etch rate in KOH) | Photoresist, SiO₂, metal (high selectivity) |
| Surface Finish | Smooth (atomically flat on (111) planes) | Scalloped sidewalls (Bosch), rougher |
| Aspect Ratio | Limited by crystal planes (typical AR ≤ 30) | Very high (AR > 50 possible) |
| Application | Pressure-sensor diaphragms, V-grooves, inkjet nozzles | Through-silicon vias (TSV), high-aspect-ratio inertial sensors |

**Preferred application:**
- *Wet:* Piezoresistive pressure sensor — the smooth (111) sidewalls and the predictable crystallographic etch provide a high-quality diaphragm for mechanical transduction.
- *Dry:* Inertial sensor (gyroscope / accelerometer) — high-aspect-ratio comb fingers require vertical sidewalls for predictable capacitance.

#### Model Answer — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $E_s = 130 \times 10^9 \, \text{Pa}$
- $\nu_s = 0.28$
- $t_s = 525 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}$
- $t_f = 2 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}$
- $R = 50 \, \text{m}$ → curvature $\kappa = 1/R = 0.02 \, \text{m}^{-1}$

**Stoney's equation:**

$$\sigma_f = \frac{E_s \, t_s^2}{6 \, (1 - \nu_s) \, t_f} \, \kappa$$

**Substitution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_f &= \frac{130 \times 10^9 \times (525 \times 10^{-6})^2}{6 \times (1 - 0.28) \times 2 \times 10^{-6}} \times 0.02 \\
&= \frac{130 \times 10^9 \times 2.756 \times 10^{-7}}{6 \times 0.72 \times 2 \times 10^{-6}} \times 0.02 \\
&= \frac{35.83}{8.64 \times 10^{-6}} \times 0.02 \\
&= 4.146 \times 10^6 \times 0.02 \\
&\approx 8.29 \times 10^4 \, \text{Pa} \\
&\approx 0.083 \, \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Stress sign:** Since the wafer bows *concave upward* when viewed from the film side (the curvature is such that $\kappa > 0$ in the convention where tensile stress produces positive curvature), the polysilicon film is in **tensile stress**. Tensile stress is generally *desirable* for MEMS structural layers — it prevents buckling.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit trap:** Forgetting to convert µm → m for $t_s$ and $t_f$. The squared $t_s$ means a 1000× mistake propagates to **$10^6$ × error**. Always write units explicitly.
> 2. **Stoney assumption violation:** Stoney's equation strictly assumes $t_f \ll t_s$. Here $t_f / t_s = 2/525 \approx 0.004$, which is valid (less than 5%).
> 3. **Sign convention:** Some texts define curvature with opposite sign. State your convention explicitly in the exam to avoid losing 1 mark.
> 4. **For anisotropy question:** Do **not** say "anisotropy = 1" for a wet etch; KOH etch on (100) silicon gives A ≈ 0.7–1 *along (111)*, but A = 0 *globally*. Be precise.
> 5. **For sacrificial release:** Forgetting to mention **stiction and critical-point drying** loses a guaranteed 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Process Design (Module 4)**

- ✅ **Process Design** = mask layout + process flow + design rules + tolerance budget.
- ✅ **Five pillars:** Photolithography, Deposition, Etching, Doping/Oxidation, Bonding/Release.
- ✅ **Rayleigh Resolution:** $R = k_1 \lambda / NA$. Smaller λ + larger NA = finer features.
- ✅ **Depth of Focus:** $DOF = k_2 \lambda / NA^2$. Trades off with resolution.
- ✅ **Etch Rate:** $ER = \Delta h / \Delta t$. Reported in nm/min or µm/min.
- ✅ **Selectivity:** $S = ER_{film} / ER_{substrate}$. Higher is better for deep etches.
- ✅ **Anisotropy:** $A = 1 - ER_{lateral}/ER_{vertical}$. A=0 isotropic, A=1 perfectly anisotropic.
- ✅ **Sheet Resistance:** $R_s = \rho / t$ in Ω/□. Used for thin-film heaters and piezoresistors.
- ✅ **Stoney Stress:** $\sigma_f = \frac{E_s t_s^2}{6(1-\nu_s) t_f} \kappa$. Valid for $t_f \ll t_s$.
- ✅ **Deal–Grove Oxidation:** $x^2 + Ax = B(t+\tau)$. Linear for thin oxide, parabolic for thick oxide.
- ✅ **Process Yield:** $Y = \prod Y_i$. Compounding effect kills yield in long process flows.
- ✅ **Knudsen Number:** $Kn = \lambda_{mfp}/L$. Determines gas damping regime in MEMS.
- ✅ **Wet vs Dry:** Wet = isotropic, smooth, crystallographic. Dry = anisotropic, scalable, scalloped.
- ✅ **KOH etch rate** on (100) Si ≈ 0.6 µm/min. **TMAH** is CMOS-compatible (~1 µm/min).
- ✅ **DRIE (Bosch process)** alternates SF₆ etch and C₄F₈ passivation — yields scallops of ~50–200 nm.
- ✅ **Critical-point drying** prevents stiction during sacrificial release.
- ✅ **Surface micromachining** uses sacrificial layer; **Bulk micromachining** removes substrate.
- ✅ **Design rules** must be checked before mask fabrication — minimum line/space, overlap, alignment tolerance.
- ✅ **Common KTU trap:** Confusing PVD (physical) vs CVD (chemical) — PVD = evaporation/sputtering, CVD = LPCVD/PECVD.
- ✅ **Sputter yield** $Y_{sp}$ is typically 0.5–10 atoms/ion; affects deposition rate.
- ✅ **Step coverage** SC = $t_{min}/t_{nominal}$. CVD generally has better SC than PVD.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanical Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMET836 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Mechanical Design ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Mechanical Design** in the context of Micro Electro Mechanical Systems (MEMS) is the disciplined engineering process of analysing, modelling, and dimensioning the **structural, kinematic, and dynamic behaviour** of micro-scale mechanical elements such as beams, plates, membranes, comb drives, diaphragms, and suspended mass structures, so that the device performs its intended function within specified limits of stress, deflection, stiffness, resonant frequency, and reliability. In the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, mechanical design of MEMS encompasses: - **Statics** → equilibrium, deflection, and induced stress under applied forces/pressures. - **Dynamics** → natural frequency, mode shapes, damping, and transient response. - **Stability** → pull-in, snap-through, and buckling instabilities. - **Reliability** → fatigue, fracture, wear, creep, and thermal-stress endurance. - **Material Behaviour** → size-dependent elastic/plastic properties, residual stress, and anisotropy. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 of OEMET836 specifically focuses on the *mechanical* aspects of MEMS design — i.e., the equations governing how micro-structures deform, vibrate, and fail. It does **not** deal with the electronics of the device, but only the structural backbone that interacts with the electrical/physical stimulus. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Micro-Suspension Bridge** > Imagine designing a **suspension bridge** at a scale of 1/1000th of a human hair. Every dimension — beam length, beam width, beam thickness, gap under the structure — is in **micrometres (µm)**. Now, the same physics that keeps a bridge deck from collapsing under its own weight and traffic load must keep a **silicon cantilever (≈ 100 µm long, 2 µm thick)** from snapping under an electrostatic force of micro-Newtons. That is MEMS mechanical design — applying classical solid-mechanics (beam theory, plate theory, vibration theory) to structures that are invisible to the naked eye but governed by identical laws. A simpler analogy: **Think of a diving board.** A long, slender diving board flexes when a diver stands at its tip. The same principle governs a MEMS cantilever — but now the "diver" is an electrostatic force, and the "board" is a beam of silicon smaller than a grain of sand. ### 1.3 Why Mechanical Design is Critical in MEMS | Reason | Engineering Consequence | |---|---| | **Scaling effects** | At micro-scale, **surface forces dominate over volume forces** → stiction, adhesion, capillary forces become design-limiting. | | **High surface-to-volume ratio** | Residual stress from fabrication strongly affects deformation. | | **Coupled fields** | Mechanical deformation alters electrical capacitance, which alters electrostatic force — a *non-linear feedback* loop. | | **Reliability demands** | Billions of cycles → fatigue and wear must be quantified. | | **Process compatibility** | Dimensions must satisfy **foundry design rules** (minimum feature size, minimum gap, maximum aspect ratio). | ### 1.4 Key Physical Constants (to be memorised) - **Young's Modulus of Silicon (Si):** $E_{Si} \approx 169 \text{ GPa}$ (single crystal, \<100\>). - **Young's Modulus of Polysilicon:** $E_{poly} \approx 160 \text{ GPa}$. - **Density of Silicon:** $\rho_{Si} \approx 2330 \text{ kg/m}^3$. - **Poisson's Ratio of Silicon:** $\nu \approx 0.28$. - **Permittivity of Free Space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$. - **Thermal Expansion Coefficient (Si):** $\alpha_{Si} \approx 2.6 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /K}$. > [!IMPORTANT] > Always use **$\mu m$** for lengths and **$\mu N$** for forces when dealing with MEMS. Mixing SI metres (m) and Newtons (N) without conversion is one of the most common sources of numerical errors. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Pillars of MEMS Mechanical Design #### Pillar 1 — Statics (Deflection & Stress) When a force, pressure, or voltage is applied, the micro-structure bends. The design must ensure: - The **deflection** $\delta$ stays below a specified maximum (to avoid pull-in, contact, or short-circuit). - The **stress** $\sigma$ remains below the fracture/elastic limit of the material. #### Pillar 2 — Dynamics (Resonance & Damping) Many MEMS devices (accelerometers, gyroscopes, RF filters, resonators) operate at or near their **natural frequency**. The designer must: - Tune $f_n$ to the required operational bandwidth. - Control the **quality factor** $Q$ (high $Q$ → sharp resonance, low $Q$ → broadband). - Avoid resonant excitation that could lead to fatigue failure. #### Pillar 3 — Stability (Pull-in & Buckling) Electrostatic and thermal loads can cause **catastrophic instabilities**: - **Pull-in voltage** $V_{PI}$ — beyond which the movable electrode snaps onto the fixed electrode. - **Buckling** — when compressive load exceeds Euler's critical load. ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Flow 1. **Identify the geometry** — is it a cantilever beam, fixed-fixed beam, clamped-clamped plate, or diaphragm? 2. **Identify the loading** — point load, distributed pressure, electrostatic pressure, or thermal load? 3. **Apply boundary conditions** — fixed (clamped) end ⇒ zero displacement and zero slope; free end ⇒ zero moment and zero shear. 4. **Solve the Euler-Bernoulli beam equation:** $$EI \frac{\partial^{4} w(x,t)}{\partial x^{4}} + \rho A \frac{\partial^{2} w(x,t)}{\partial t^{2}} = q(x,t)$$ 5. **Extract quantities of interest** — deflection, slope, moment, shear force, natural frequency, pull-in voltage. 6. **Apply design constraints** — max allowable stress, max allowable deflection, target frequency, minimum gap. ### 2.3 Why Each Concept Matters - **Deflection matters** because in capacitive sensors, the capacitance change is *directly proportional* to the displacement. Over-deflection ⇒ pull-in ⇒ device short. - **Stress matters** because silicon, although strong, is **brittle** and fractures without plastic deformation. Once the fracture stress $\sigma_f \approx 1 \text{–} 7 \text{ GPa}$ is exceeded, the device fails catastrophically. - **Resonance matters** because operating a MEMS resonator off-tune destroys its $Q$ factor, and operating a MEMS accelerometer at resonance amplifies noise. - **Pull-in matters** because once $V > V_{PI}$, the device is unstable and **cannot be recovered** without reducing the voltage — this defines the safe operating window. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL:** The vertical pipe symbol `\vert` is used below for absolute values / magnitude to preserve markdown table syntax. | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Applicable Geometry | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Moment of Inertia (rect.) | $I = \dfrac{wt^{3}}{12}$ | $\text{m}^{4}$ | Beam of width $w$, thickness $t$ | | 2 | Cantilever tip deflection (point load at tip) | $\delta = \dfrac{FL^{3}}{3EI}$ | $\text{m}$ | Cantilever beam | | 3 | Cantilever tip deflection (uniform load) | $\delta = \dfrac{wL^{4}}{8EI}$ | $\text{m}$ | Cantilever beam | | 4 | Fixed-Fixed centre deflection (point load at centre) | $\delta = \dfrac{FL^{3}}{192EI}$ | $\text{m}$ | Clamped-Clamped beam | | 5 | Fixed-Fixed centre deflection (uniform load) | $\delta = \dfrac{wL^{4}}{384EI}$ | $\text{m}$ | Clamped-Clamped beam | | 6 | Spring constant — cantilever | $k = \dfrac{Ewt^{3}}{4L^{3}}$ | $\text{N/m}$ | Cantilever beam | | 7 | Spring constant — fixed-fixed | $k = \dfrac{Ewt^{3}}{L^{3}}$ | $\text{N/m}$ | Clamped-Clamped beam | | 8 | Max bending stress (fixed-fixed beam, centre load) | $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{3FL}{2wt^{2}}$ | $\text{Pa}$ | Clamped-Clamped beam | | 9 | Resonant frequency (cantilever) | $f_{1} = \dfrac{1.875^{2}}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{EI}{\rho A L^{4}}}$ | $\text{Hz}$ | Cantilever beam | | 10 | Resonant frequency (fixed-fixed, fundamental) | $f_{1} = \dfrac{4.730^{2}}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{EI}{\rho A L^{4}}}$ | $\text{Hz}$ | Clamped-Clamped beam | | 11 | Resonant frequency (clamped-free membrane/plate) | $f_{mn} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{D}{\rho h}\left[\left(\dfrac{m\pi}{a}\right)^{2} + \left(\dfrac{n\pi}{b}\right)^{2}\right]^{2}}$ | $\text{Hz}$ | Plate of size $a \times b$ | | 12 | Plate flexural rigidity | $D = \dfrac{Eh^{3}}{12(1-\nu^{2})}$ | $\text{N·m}$ | Plate, $h$ = thickness | | 13 | Pull-in voltage (parallel plate) | $V_{PI} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 k g_{0}^{3}}{27 \,\varepsilon_{0} A}}$ | $\text{V}$ | Electrostatic actuator | | 14 | Electrostatic pressure | $P_{e} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{0} V^{2}}{2 g^{2}}$ | $\text{Pa}$ | Parallel-plate gap $g$ | | 15 | Thermal stress | $\sigma_{th} = \dfrac{E \,\Delta\alpha \,\Delta T}{1-\nu}$ | $\text{Pa}$ | Bi-material / packaged die | | 16 | Euler buckling load (column) | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{(KL)^{2}}$ | $\text{N}$ | Slender column | | 17 | Quality factor | $Q = \dfrac{\sqrt{k m}}{c}$ | dimensionless | Damped oscillator | | 18 | Strain (axial) | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L}$ | dimensionless | Any bar | | 19 | Stiffness ratio (spring softening effect) | $k_{eff} = k - \dfrac{\partial F_{e}}{\partial g}$ | $\text{N/m}$ | Electrostatic beam | | 20 | Fracture strength (Griffith) | $\sigma_{f} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 E \gamma_{s}}{\pi a}}$ | $\text{Pa}$ | Crack of length $a$ | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility These formulas are the **backbone of every commercial MEMS design tool** (CoventorWare, ANSYS, COMSOL Multiphysics, IntelliSuite). For example: - A **MEMS accelerometer** in your smartphone uses a *proof mass suspended on four folded-beam flexures* — its bandwidth is set by the resonant frequency of the flexure-mass system (Eq. 9–10). - A **digital micromirror device (DMD)** in projectors uses a *torsional beam* whose pull-in and snap-back dynamics (Eq. 13) determine switching speed. - A **MEMS pressure sensor** uses a *diaphragm* whose centre deflection (Eq. 11, 12) converts pressure into a capacitance change. - A **RF MEMS switch** is designed with a fixed-fixed beam whose spring constant (Eq. 7) must be low enough for low actuation voltage but high enough to resist stiction. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner's Note:** Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. Skipping steps is the most common reason for losing marks in 14-mark derivations. --- ### 3.1 DERIVATION 1 — Static Deflection of a Cantilever Beam Under a Tip Point Load **Setup:** A cantilever beam of length $L$, width $w$, thickness $t$, Young's modulus $E$, fixed at $x = 0$ and free at $x = L$. A transverse point load $F$ is applied at the free tip. **Boundary conditions:** - At $x = 0$: deflection $w(0) = 0$ and slope $w'(0) = 0$. - At $x = L$: shear force $V(L) = F$ and bending moment $M(L) = 0$. **Step 1 — Write the bending moment at a section $x$ from the fixed end.** By taking the moment about the section at distance $x$, considering forces to the right of the cut: $$M(x) = F(L - x)$$ This is the internal bending moment because the only force to the right of section $x$ is $F$ acting at distance $(L-x)$ from the section. **Step 2 — Apply the Euler-Bernoulli moment-curvature relation.** $$M(x) = EI \frac{d^{2} w(x)}{dx^{2}}$$ where $E$ is Young's modulus, $I$ is the second moment of area. Equating both expressions for $M(x)$: $$EI \frac{d^{2} w(x)}{dx^{2}} = F(L - x)$$ **Step 3 — Integrate once to obtain the slope.** $$\frac{dw(x)}{dx} = \frac{1}{EI} \int F(L - x) \, dx = \frac{F}{EI} \left( Lx - \frac{x^{2}}{2} \right) + C_{1}$$ **Step 4 — Apply slope boundary condition at fixed end $w'(0) = 0$.** $$0 = \frac{F}{EI} \left( 0 - 0 \right) + C_{1} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; C_{1} = 0$$ So the slope is: $$\frac{dw(x)}{dx} = \frac{F}{EI}\left(Lx - \frac{x^{2}}{2}\right)$$ **Step 5 — Integrate again to obtain the deflection.** $$w(x) = \frac{F}{EI} \int \left(Lx - \frac{x^{2}}{2}\right) dx = \frac{F}{EI} \left( \frac{Lx^{2}}{2} - \frac{x^{3}}{6} \right) + C_{2}$$ **Step 6 — Apply deflection boundary condition at fixed end $w(0) = 0$.** $$0 = \frac{F}{EI}(0 - 0) + C_{2} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; C_{2} = 0$$ So: $$w(x) = \frac{F}{EI}\left( \frac{Lx^{2}}{2} - \frac{x^{3}}{6} \right) = \frac{Fx^{2}}{6EI}\left(3L - x\right)$$ **Step 7 — Substitute $x = L$ to obtain tip deflection.** $$w(L) = \frac{F L^{2}}{6EI}(3L - L) = \frac{F L^{2}}{6EI}(2L) = \frac{F L^{3}}{3EI}$$ $$\boxed{\delta_{tip} = \frac{F L^{3}}{3 E I}}$$ For a rectangular cross-section $I = wt^{3}/12$: $$\delta_{tip} = \frac{4 F L^{3}}{E w t^{3}}$$ **Spring constant** (from $\delta = F/k$): $$k = \frac{F}{\delta} = \frac{3 E I}{L^{3}} = \frac{E w t^{3}}{4 L^{3}}$$ --- ### 3.2 DERIVATION 2 — Resonant Frequency of a Cantilever Beam **Setup:** Same cantilever beam, but now we consider undamped free vibration. The partial differential equation is: $$EI \frac{\partial^{4} w(x,t)}{\partial x^{4}} + \rho A \frac{\partial^{2} w(x,t)}{\partial t^{2}} = 0$$ where $\rho$ is density, $A = wt$ is cross-sectional area. **Step 1 — Assume separable solution.** $$w(x, t) = \phi(x) \, T(t)$$ Substituting into the PDE: $$EI \, \phi^{(4)}(x) \, T(t) + \rho A \, \phi(x) \, \ddot{T}(t) = 0$$ **Step 2 — Divide through by $\phi(x) T(t)$.** $$\frac{EI \, \phi^{(4)}(x)}{\phi(x)} = -\frac{\rho A \, \ddot{T}(t)}{T(t)} = \omega^{2}$$ where $\omega^{2}$ is the separation constant (must be positive for vibration). **Step 3 — Solve the spatial ODE.** $$EI \, \phi^{(4)}(x) - \rho A \omega^{2} \, \phi(x) = 0$$ Let $\beta^{4} = \dfrac{\rho A \omega^{2}}{EI}$, so the ODE becomes: $$\phi^{(4)}(x) - \beta^{4} \phi(x) = 0$$ **Step 4 — General solution.** $$\phi(x) = C_{1} \cos(\beta x) + C_{2} \sin(\beta x) + C_{3} \cosh(\beta x) + C_{4} \sinh(\beta x)$$ **Step 5 — Apply cantilever boundary conditions.** At fixed end $x = 0$: $\phi(0) = 0$ and $\phi'(0) = 0$.
