The resultant bond is **hermetic** and **strong** (>10 MPa). ### 2.2 Fusion / Direct (Si-Si) Bonding In **fusion bonding**, two ultra-flat, ultra-clean wafers (Si-to-Si or Si-to-SiO₂) are brought into contact at room temperature (**pre-bonding**), then annealed at high temperature (**800 °C – 1100 °C**) to strengthen the bond. - **Pre-bond:** Driven by **van der Waals forces** and **hydrogen bonding** between surface –OH groups. - **Anneal:** Surface siloxane (Si–O–Si) bonds form, releasing H₂O and H₂. ### 2.3 Eutectic Bonding Eutectic bonding uses a **low-melting-point alloy** (typically **Au-Si eutectic at 363 °C**) sandwiched between two wafers. Upon heating past the eutectic temperature, the alloy melts, wets both surfaces, and forms a metallurgical bond upon cooling. | Parameter | Au-Si Eutectic | |---|---| | Eutectic Temperature | **363 °C** | | Composition | **Au: 97.15 %, Si: 2.85 %** (by weight, approx.) | | Bond Strength | **50 – 100 MPa** | | Hermeticity | Excellent (with proper seal rings) | ### 2.4 Adhesive Bonding A **polymer intermediate layer** (SU-8, BCB, polyimide, or even dry-film adhesives) is used to bond wafers at low temperatures (**< 200 °C**). Curing may be thermal or UV-induced. - **Advantages:** Low temperature, large wafer tolerance, electrical isolation. - **Disadvantages:** Lower hermeticity, outgassing risk, lower thermal stability. ## 2.5 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations — they appear in KTU ESE and module tests every year.** | # | Formula | Description | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{F_b}{A_b}$ | Bond strength = Bonding force / Bond area | **MPa** | | 2 | $F_{elec} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r \, A \, V^2}{2 d^2}$ | Electrostatic force in anodic bonding (parallel-plate model) | **N** | | 3 | $P_{elec} = \dfrac{F_{elec}}{A} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r \, V^2}{2 d^2}$ | Equivalent electrostatic pressure at the bond interface | **Pa** | | 4 | $\sigma_{th} = E \, \Delta\alpha \, \Delta T$ | Thermal mismatch stress between two bonded materials | **Pa** | | 5 | $\delta_{bow} = \dfrac{3 \, \sigma_{th} \, r^2}{2 \, E \, d^2}$ | Wafer bow (deflection) due to thermal mismatch; $r$ = wafer radius, $d$ = wafer thickness | **m** | | 6 | $\alpha_{eff} = \dfrac{\alpha_1 \, d_1 + \alpha_2 \, d_2}{d_1 + d_2}$ | Effective coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) of a bilayer | **1/°C** | | 7 | $T_{eut} = f(\text{composition})$ | Eutectic temperature — material dependent (e.g., 363 °C for Au-Si) | **°C** | | 8 | $\Delta G_{bond} = \gamma_1 + \gamma_2 - \gamma_{12}$ | Work of adhesion / bond energy (surface energy balance) | **J/m²** | | 9 | $R_q^{eff} = \sqrt{R_{q1}^2 + R_{q2}^2}$ | Effective RMS roughness for two mating surfaces | **nm** | | 10 | $t_{diff} = \dfrac{x^2}{2D}$ | Approximate diffusion time for mobile ions (Na⁺) in glass; $x$ = depletion depth, $D$ = diffusivity | **s** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Constants to memorize for KTU exams:** > - Permittivity of free space: $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \, \text{F/m}$ > - CTE of Silicon: $\alpha_{Si} = 2.6 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{°C}^{-1}$ > - CTE of Pyrex 7740: $\alpha_{glass} = 3.2 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{°C}^{-1}$ (closely matched to Si) > - Young's Modulus of Si: $E_{Si} \approx 170 \, \text{GPa}$ ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Pressure Sensors & Accelerometers:** Anodic bonding of Si to Pyrex glass provides a hermetic vacuum cavity — used in **Bosch BMP280, ST LIS2DH, Analog Devices ADXL345**. - **Wafer-Level Packaging (WLP):** Eutectic Au-Si bonding is the **industry standard** for MEMS capping. - **Bio-MEMS & Microfluidics:** Adhesive bonding of PDMS, glass, and silicon is essential for lab-on-chip devices. - **Optoelectronics & MOEMS:** Fusion bonding enables 3D-stacked image sensors (e.g., **Sony's stacked CMOS**). --- # 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Electrostatic Pressure in Anodic Bonding Consider the simplified model of an anodic bonding interface as a **parallel-plate capacitor** with: - Plate area: $A$ (m²) - Plate separation: $d$ (m) — initial glass depletion depth - Applied voltage: $V$ (V) - Relative permittivity of glass: $\varepsilon_r$ **Step 1 — Capacitance of the parallel plate model** The capacitance is given by the standard formula: $$ C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r \, A}{d} $$ **Step 2 — Energy stored in the capacitor** The electrostatic energy stored in this capacitor is: $$ U = \frac{1}{2} \, C \, V^2 $$ Substituting $C$: $$ U = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r \, A}{d} \cdot V^2 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r \, A \, V^2}{2d} $$ **Step 3 — Force on the plates** The attractive force between the plates is obtained by differentiating the energy with respect to plate separation $d$ (keeping $V$ constant, since it is a voltage source): $$ F_{elec} = -\frac{\partial U}{\partial d} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial d}\left( \frac{\varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r \, A \, V^2}{2d} \right) $$ Differentiating with respect to $d$ (treating $V$, $A$, $\varepsilon_0$, $\varepsilon_r$ as constants): $$ F_{elec} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r \, A \, V^2}{2 d^2} $$ **Step 4 — Convert force to equivalent pressure** Dividing force by the bond area $A$: $$ P_{elec} = \frac{F_{elec}}{A} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r \, V^2}{2 d^2} $$ **Step 5 — Numerical Sanity Check (KTU-style)** Given: $V = 800 \, \text{V}$, $d = 1 \, \mu\text{m} = 1 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}$, $\varepsilon_r = 4.6$ (Pyrex). $$ P_{elec} = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot (4.6) \cdot (800)^2}{2 \cdot (1 \times 10^{-6})^2} $$ Evaluating the numerator: $$ (8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot (4.6) = 4.073 \times 10^{-11} $$ $$ 4.073 \times 10^{-11} \cdot 6.4 \times 10^{5} = 2.607 \times 10^{-5} $$ Evaluating the denominator: $$ 2 \cdot (1 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 2 \times 10^{-12} $$ Final value: $$ P_{elec} = \frac{2.607 \times 10^{-5}}{2 \times 10^{-12}} = 1.303 \times 10^{7} \, \text{Pa} \approx 13 \, \text{MPa} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** With just **800 V** across a **1 µm depletion layer**, the anodic bonding process generates an equivalent pressure of **~13 MPa** — more than enough to deform surface asperities and create intimate contact. This explains why anodic bonds are exceptionally strong and hermetic. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Thermal Mismatch Stress and Wafer Bow When two materials with different **Coefficients of Thermal Expansion (CTE)** are bonded at temperature $T_b$ and then cooled to room temperature $T_{RT}$, residual stress develops. **Step 1 — Free strain due to differential contraction** If the two materials are strain-matched at $T_b$, then upon cooling by $\Delta T = T_b - T_{RT}$, the differential free strain is: $$ \varepsilon_{th} = (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \cdot \Delta T = \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T $$ **Step 2 — Stress in the bonded bilayer (biaxial, equal-biaxial approximation)** Assuming both layers are thin and constrained together, the in-plane biaxial stress is: $$ \sigma_{th} = \frac{E}{1 - \nu} \cdot \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T $$ For a simplified **uniaxial / plane-stress** estimate (used in KTU problems), the Poisson-corrected term is often dropped: $$ \sigma_{th} \approx E \cdot \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T $$ **Step 3 — Resulting wafer bow (Stoney equation, simplified)** The Stoney formula for the bow $\delta_{bow}$ of a thin film (or bonded layer) of thickness $d_f$ on a thick substrate of thickness $d_s$ is: $$ \delta_{bow} = \frac{3 \, \sigma_{th} \, d_s^2}{2 \, E_s \, d_f} \cdot r^2 $$ For a symmetric bilayer of equal thickness $d$, this simplifies to: $$ \delta_{bow} = \frac{3 \, \sigma_{th} \, r^2}{2 \, E \, d^2} $$ **Step 4 — Numerical Example** Bonding **Si (E = 170 GPa, α = 2.6 ppm/°C)** to **Pyrex (E = 63 GPa, α = 3.2 ppm/°C)** at $T_b = 400$ °C, cooling to $T_{RT} = 25$ °C. $$ \Delta T = 400 - 25 = 375 \, \text{°C} $$ $$ \Delta\alpha = 3.2 - 2.6 = 0.6 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{°C}^{-1} $$ $$ \sigma_{th} = (170 \times 10^9) \cdot (0.6 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 375 = 3.825 \times 10^{7} \, \text{Pa} \approx 38.25 \, \text{MPa} $$ For a 100 mm wafer ($r = 0.05$ m) of 500 µm thickness ($d = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m): $$ \delta_{bow} = \frac{3 \cdot (3.825 \times 10^{7}) \cdot (0.05)^2}{2 \cdot (170 \times 10^9) \cdot (5 \times 10^{-4})^2} $$ Computing the numerator: $3 \cdot 3.825 \times 10^{7} \cdot 2.5 \times 10^{-3} = 2.869 \times 10^{5}$ Computing the denominator: $2 \cdot 1.7 \times 10^{11} \cdot 2.5 \times 10^{-7} = 8.5 \times 10^{4}$ Final bow: $$ \delta_{bow} = \frac{2.869 \times 10^{5}}{8.5 \times 10^{4}} \approx 3.37 \, \mu\text{m} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Practical Insight:** A 3.37 µm bow over 100 mm is acceptable for most MEMS processes. However, **larger wafers (200 mm, 300 mm)** amplify this effect and demand tighter CTE matching — which is why **borosilicate glass (Pyrex 7740)** with its near-matched CTE to silicon is the **gold standard** for anodic bonding. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Choosing the Right Bonding Technique | Parameter | Anodic | Fusion | Eutectic (Au-Si) | Adhesive (SU-8) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Bond Temp (°C)** | 300 – 450 | 800 – 1100 | 363 | 150 – 250 | | **Pressure (MPa)** | 0.1 – 0.5 | None / light | 0.5 – 5 | 0.1 – 1 | | **Voltage (V)** | 200 – 1000 | 0 | 0 | 0 | | **Hermeticity** | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Poor–Moderate | | **Bond Strength (MPa)** | 10 – 30 | 10 – 20 | 50 – 100 | 5 – 20 | | **CTE Sensitivity** | Moderate (use Pyrex) | Low (same material) | Low (metal film) | Low | | **Surface Roughness Tolerance (nm)** | < 50 | < 1 | < 200 | < 500 | | **Throughput** | Medium | Low (high-T anneal) | High | High | | **Cost** | Medium | High | High | Low | | **Typical Application** | Pressure sensors | SOI, 3D stacking | Wafer-level packaging | Microfluidics, Bio-MEMS | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Quick Tip:** When asked "which bonding technique is best for a low-temperature process?", the answer is **Adhesive Bonding**. For "highest hermeticity with low thermal budget?", answer is **Au-Si Eutectic at 363 °C**. --- # 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of MEMS Bonding Techniques ```mermaid graph TD A[Wafer-to-Wafer Bonding] --> B1[Direct Fusion] A --> B2[Intermediate Layer] A --> B3[Field Assisted] B1 --> C1[Si-Si at 1000 C] B1 --> C2[Si-SiO2 at 800 C] B2 --> D1[Eutectic Au-Si 363 C] B2 --> D2[Eutectic Au-Sn 280 C] B2 --> D3[Glass Frit 400 C] B2 --> D4[Adhesive SU-8 BCB 200 C] B2 --> D5[Thermocompression 350 C] B3 --> E1[Anodic Si-Glass 400 C] B3 --> E2[Plasma Activated RT] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Start from the root "Wafer-to-Wafer Bonding" and traverse the branches to identify the four primary classes — Direct, Intermediate-Layer, and Field-Assisted bonding — and the sub-types within each. ## 4.2 Anodic Bonding Process Flow (Block Architecture) ```mermaid graph LR S1[Step 1: Wafer Cleaning RCA Process] --> S2[Step 2: Align Si and Pyrex Wafers] S2 --> S3[Step 3: Load into Bonding Chuck] S3 --> S4[Step 4: Heat to 400 C] S4 --> S5[Step 5: Apply 800 V DC Cathode on Glass] S5 --> S6[Step 6: Na Plus Ions Drift to Cathode] S6 --> S7[Step 7: Electrostatic Attraction Forms Bond] S7 --> S8[Step 8: SiO2 Layer Grows at Interface] S8 --> S9[Step 9: Cool to Room Temperature] S9 --> S10[Step 10: Hermetic Bond Complete] ``` ## 4.3 Au-Si Eutectic Bonding — Phase Concept Flow ```mermaid graph TD P1[Deposit Au Layer on Si Wafer] --> P2[Align with Si Cap Wafer] P2 --> P3[Heat to 363 C Eutectic Point] P3 --> P4[Au and Si Melt Forming Liquidus Phase] P4 --> P5[Wetting of Both Surfaces] P5 --> P6[Cool to Room Temperature] P6 --> P7[Solid Au-Si Intermetallic Bond] P7 --> P8[Hermetic Metallurgical Seal Formed] ``` ## 4.4 Comparative Selection Logic Diagram ```mermaid graph TD Q1{Is the application
hermetic and
vacuum-critical?} Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{High thermal
budget allowed?} Q1 -- No --> R1[Consider Adhesive Bonding] Q2 -- Yes --> Q3[Use Fusion Si-Si Bonding] Q2 -- No --> Q4{Is Si-Glass
geometry
available?} Q4 -- Yes --> Q5[Use Anodic Bonding] Q4 -- No --> Q6[Use Au-Si Eutectic Bonding] R1 --> R2[Use SU-8 or BCB Adhesive] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **How to use this diagram in an exam:** When the question states a constraint (e.g., "low temperature, hermetic, electrical feedthrough needed"), traverse the decision tree from the root. This mirrors the **design methodology** KTU examiners expect students to articulate. --- # 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Define anodic bonding. Mention the role of Na⁺ ions in the bonding process.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Anodic bonding is a process of joining a glass wafer (typically Pyrex 7740) to a silicon wafer at elevated temperature (300–450 °C) under a high DC voltage (200–1000 V) **(1 mark)**. When voltage is applied, the cathode is placed on the glass side, which causes the mobile **Na⁺ ions in the borosilicate glass to drift toward the cathode**, leaving behind a thin negatively charged depletion layer near the bond interface **(1 mark)**. The resulting electrostatic attraction between this depletion layer and the positively biased silicon pulls the wafers into intimate contact, enabling a permanent chemical bond via Si–O–Si formation **(1 mark)**. ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **List any four bonding techniques used in MEMS packaging and state one key advantage of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Anodic bonding** — Advantage: hermetic and low residual stress when CTE-matched glass is used **(0.75 marks)**. 2. **Fusion bonding** — Advantage: high bond strength and excellent hermeticity without intermediate layers **(0.75 marks)**. 3. **Eutectic bonding (Au-Si)** — Advantage: low bonding temperature (363 °C) with strong metallurgical bond **(0.75 marks)**. 4. **Adhesive bonding (SU-8)** — Advantage: very low process temperature (< 200 °C) and tolerance to surface roughness **(0.75 marks)**. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before You Write:** > 1. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers leads to 1–2 mark deductions. Always state units (°C, MPa, V, m, etc.). > 2. **Skipping the bond interface chemistry** (e.g., Na⁺ drift, SiO₂ formation) in anodic bonding answers costs at least 2 marks. KTU examiners explicitly look for this mechanism. > 3. **Confusing "fusion bonding" with "anodic bonding"** is a common error. Remember: fusion = thermal only, anodic = thermal + electrostatic. > 4. **Not stating the bonding temperature range** for each technique is a recurring mark-loss area. Always include T_b. ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023] **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **anodic bonding process** for joining a borosilicate glass wafer to a silicon wafer. Discuss the role of temperature, voltage, and the migration of Na⁺ ions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An anodic bonding setup uses a voltage of **V = 600 V** across a glass depletion depth of **d = 1.5 µm** with a relative permittivity of **ε_r = 4.6**. Calculate the equivalent electrostatic pressure generated at the bond interface. State whether this pressure is sufficient for effective bonding. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a): - **Step 1 (2 marks):** State the basic setup — Si wafer and Pyrex 7740 glass wafer placed in contact, heated to 300–450 °C (typical 400 °C), and a DC voltage of 200–1000 V is applied with **cathode on glass, anode on silicon**. - **Step 2 (2 marks):** Explain the **ion migration mechanism** — at elevated T, the **Na⁺ ions become mobile** and drift toward the cathode (placed on glass), leaving a **negatively charged depletion zone** near the Si-glass interface. - **Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Describe the **electrostatic attraction** — the depletion zone (negative) and Si (positive) form a microscopic capacitor, producing a strong attractive force (10–30 MPa) that pulls the wafers into intimate contact. - **Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Describe the **chemical bond formation** — oxygen released at the Si anode reacts with Si to form a thin **SiO₂ layer** at the interface, creating a permanent hermetic Si–O–Si bond. A block diagram showing the layered structure (Si | SiO₂ | depleted glass | bulk glass | cathode) should be sketched. #### Model Solution — Part (b): **Given:** $V = 600 \, \text{V}$, $d = 1.5 \, \mu\text{m} = 1.5 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}$, $\varepsilon_r = 4.6$, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \, \text{F/m}$. **Formula (1 mark):** $$ P_{elec} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r \, V^2}{2 d^2} $$ **Substitution and calculation (4 marks):** $$ P_{elec} = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot (4.6) \cdot (600)^2}{2 \cdot (1.5 \times 10^{-6})^2} $$ Numerator evaluation: $$ (8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot (4.6) = 4.073 \times 10^{-11} $$ $$ 4.073 \times 10^{-11} \cdot (3.6 \times 10^{5}) = 1.466 \times 10^{-5} $$ Denominator evaluation: $$ 2 \cdot (1.5 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 2 \cdot 2.25 \times 10^{-12} = 4.5 \times 10^{-12} $$ Final result: $$ P_{elec} = \frac{1.466 \times 10^{-5}}{4.5 \times 10^{-12}} = 3.258 \times 10^{6} \, \text{Pa} \approx 3.26 \, \text{MPa} $$ **Conclusion (2 marks):** The generated pressure of **3.26 MPa** is well within the typical range (5–30 MPa) required to overcome surface roughness and asperity deformation during anodic bonding. **Yes, this pressure is sufficient for effective bonding** when combined with the elevated temperature (which promotes Na⁺ mobility and SiO₂ formation). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] **(a)** Compare **anodic bonding, fusion bonding, and eutectic bonding** with respect to bonding temperature, hermeticity, bond strength, and typical application areas. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A silicon wafer (E = 170 GPa, α = 2.6 × 10⁻⁶ /°C, thickness 500 µm) is bonded to a glass wafer (E = 63 GPa, α = 3.2 × 10⁻⁶ /°C, thickness 500 µm) at **400 °C** and cooled to **25 °C**. Using the biaxial Stoney approximation, calculate the **thermal mismatch stress** and the resulting **wafer bow** for a **100 mm diameter wafer**. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a): | Parameter | Anodic Bonding | Fusion Bonding | Eutectic Bonding (Au-Si) | |---|---|---|---| | **Bond Temp (°C)** | 300 – 450 **(0.5)** | 800 – 1100 **(0.5)** | 363 **(0.5)** | | **Hermeticity** | Excellent **(0.5)** | Excellent **(0.5)** | Excellent **(0.5)** | | **Bond Strength (MPa)** | 10 – 30 **(0.5)** | 10 – 20 **(0.5)** | 50 – 100 **(0.5)** | | **Typical Application** | Pressure sensors, accelerometers (glass cap) **(0.5)** | SOI wafers, 3D integration **(0.5)** | Wafer-level MEMS packaging **(0.5)** | | **Surface Roughness Tolerance** | Moderate (< 50 nm) **(0.5)** | Very strict (< 1 nm) **(0.5)** | Tolerant (< 200 nm) **(0.5)** | | **Cost & Throughput** | Medium **(0.5)** | High cost, low throughput **(0.5)** | High cost, high throughput **(0.5)** | **(Total: 7 marks for tabular comparison with 6 rows + 1 mark for any additional insight / summary statement.)** #### Model Solution — Part (b): **Given:** $E_{Si} = 170 \, \text{GPa}$, $\alpha_{Si} = 2.6 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{°C}^{-1}$, $E_{glass} = 63 \, \text{GPa}$, $\alpha_{glass} = 3.2 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{°C}^{-1}$, $T_b = 400 \, \text{°C}$, $T_{RT} = 25 \, \text{°C}$, $d = 500 \, \mu\text{m} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}$, wafer radius $r = 50 \, \text{mm} = 0.05 \, \text{m}$. **Step 1 — Calculate ΔT (1 mark):** $$ \Delta T = T_b - T_{RT} = 400 - 25 = 375 \, \text{°C} $$ **Step 2 — Calculate Δα (1 mark):** $$ \Delta\alpha = \alpha_{glass} - \alpha_{Si} = (3.2 - 2.6) \times 10^{-6} = 0.6 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{°C}^{-1} $$ **Step 3 — Calculate thermal stress using dominant Si modulus (1 mark):** $$ \sigma_{th} = E_{Si} \cdot \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T $$ $$ \sigma_{th} = (170 \times 10^9) \cdot (0.6 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (375) = 3.825 \times 10^{7} \, \text{Pa} = 38.25 \, \text{MPa} $$ **Step 4 — State the Stoney bow formula (1 mark):** $$ \delta_{bow} = \frac{3 \, \sigma_{th} \, r^2}{2 \, E \, d^2} $$ **Step 5 — Substitute and calculate (2 marks):** $$ \delta_{bow} = \frac{3 \cdot (3.825 \times 10^{7}) \cdot (0.05)^2}{2 \cdot (170 \times 10^9) \cdot (5 \times 10^{-4})^2} $$ Numerator: $$ 3 \cdot 3.825 \times 10^{7} \cdot 2.5 \times 10^{-3} = 2.869 \times 10^{5} $$ Denominator: $$ 2 \cdot 1.7 \times 10^{11} \cdot 2.5 \times 10^{-7} = 8.5 \times 10^{4} $$ Final result: $$ \delta_{bow} = \frac{2.869 \times 10^{5}}{8.5 \times 10^{4}} = 3.37 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m} = 3.37 \, \mu\text{m} $$ **Step 6 — Conclusion (1 mark):** The thermal mismatch stress is **38.25 MPa** and the resulting wafer bow is **3.37 µm** for a 100 mm wafer. This is acceptable for most MEMS processes, demonstrating why **borosilicate glass (with closely matched CTE to Si)** is the preferred anodic bonding partner. --- ## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:** - ✅ **Bonding** = permanent joining of MEMS substrates; classified as **Direct (Fusion)**, **Intermediate-Layer (Eutectic, Adhesive, Glass-Frit)**, and **Field-Assisted (Anodic, Plasma)**. - ✅ **Anodic bonding** uses **Pyrex glass + Si** at **300–450 °C** and **200–1000 V DC**; mechanism relies on **Na⁺ ion drift** and **electrostatic attraction** leading to **SiO₂ formation** at the interface. - ✅ **Fusion bonding** requires **ultra-clean, ultra-flat surfaces** (< 1 nm roughness) and high anneal temperatures (**800–1100 °C**); provides best hermeticity. - ✅ **Au-Si eutectic bonding** occurs at the famously low **363 °C** and produces bond strengths of **50–100 MPa** — ideal for wafer-level packaging. - ✅ **Adhesive bonding** uses polymers like **SU-8, BCB, polyimide**; lowest temperature (< 200 °C) but **poor hermeticity** and outgassing risk. - ✅ **Bonding parameters to always state:** Temperature $T_b$, Pressure $P_b$, Voltage $V$, Time $t_b$, Bond strength $\sigma_b$. - ✅ **Electrostatic pressure** in anodic bonding: $P_{elec} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r V^2}{2 d^2}$ — typically generates **5–30 MPa**. - ✅ **Thermal mismatch stress:** $\sigma_{th} = E \cdot \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T$ — minimized by choosing **CTE-matched** materials (Si ↔ Pyrex). - ✅ **Wafer bow (Stoney):** $\delta_{bow} = \dfrac{3 \sigma_{th} r^2}{2 E d^2}$ — scales with the square of wafer radius, so larger wafers suffer more bow. - ✅ **Pyrex 7740 CTE** = **3.2 ppm/°C** (vs. Si 2.6 ppm/°C) — close enough for low-stress anodic bonding. - ✅ **Industry standard for capping:** Au-Si eutectic for hermeticity + electrical feedthroughs simultaneously. - ✅ **For KTU answers — always include:** (1) Bond temperature, (2) Bond mechanism (electrostatic / chemical / metallurgical), (3) Bond strength, (4) Application, (5) One limitation. > [!NOTE] > **Final Exam Mantra:** *Bonding in MEMS is the bridge between a working microstructure and a reliable product. KTU expects you to choose the right bonding technique by balancing temperature, hermeticity, strength, and cost — and to defend your choice with quantitative analysis.* ---

topics.title: Interfaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMET836 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Interfaces # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of MEMS Interfaces > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Interfaces in MEMS)** > Interfaces in MEMS refer to the physical, electrical, thermal, optical, and chemical boundaries that allow a micro-scale device to communicate, exchange energy, or transfer signals with the macro-scale world. They act as the **bridge** between the microscopic sensing/actuation domain and the macroscopic signal-processing, packaging, and user domain. ## 1.1 What Exactly Is an "Interface" in MEMS? In a Micro Electro Mechanical System, the silicon die itself is only one part of a much larger functional chain. The **interface** is the engineered boundary that performs the following jobs: 1. **Mechanical coupling** – physically mounting the MEMS die to a package or substrate. 2. **Electrical coupling** – routing low-level signals (often microvolts to millivolts) from the MEMS element to readout ASICs or PCBs. 3. **Thermal coupling** – managing the heat generated by the device (especially in high-power or optical MEMS). 4. **Optical coupling** – channeling light into or out of MOEMS (Micro-Optical-Electro-Mechanical Systems). 5. **Chemical / Biological coupling** – selectively exposing the active surface to analytes while protecting the rest of the system. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight – Why this topic is high-weightage** > A perfect MEMS die is *useless* without proper interfacing. In industry, **>60% of MEMS product failures** are attributed to packaging and interface issues, not to the core sensor/actuator. KTU examiners love this module because it connects theory to **real-world product design** – expect 14-mark analysis questions on signal conditioning, thermal mismatch, and packaging. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Loudspeaker" View of a MEMS System Imagine a MEMS accelerometer sitting inside your smartphone. Inside the chip, a tiny proof mass moves a few **nanometers**. This nanometer motion must be: | Macro World | MEMS World | The Interface That Connects Them | |---|---|---| | Phone's display (cm) | Proof mass (µm) | Mechanical suspension beams | | Battery voltage (V) | Capacitance change (aF) | Capacitance-to-Voltage converter | | PCB (rigid, thick) | Silicon die (brittle, thin) | Die attach / underfill | | Ambient air (25 °C) | Hot junction (often >100 °C) | Thermal management layer | Think of a **stage performer (MEMS die)** speaking a faint whisper. The **microphone (interface electronics)** amplifies the whisper so the **audience (processor)** can hear it. Without the microphone, the whisper is lost in the noise. > [!TIP] > **Key Metric to Remember:** Interface circuits in MEMS typically deal with signal levels in the range **µV to mV**, frequencies from **DC to MHz**, and capacitances from **aF to pF** (atto-femto Farads). These extreme values are what make MEMS interfacing non-trivial. ## 1.3 Classification of MEMS Interfaces (KTU Classification) Based on the energy domain, interfaces are broadly classified into: - **Mechanical Interfaces** – mounting, alignment, stress isolation - **Electrical Interfaces** – wire bonding, flip-chip, signal conditioning - **Thermal Interfaces** – heat sinks, thermal vias, TIMs - **Optical Interfaces** – waveguides, fiber alignment, anti-reflection coatings - **Chemical / Fluidic Interfaces** – microchannels, membranes, selective coatings # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mechanical Interfaces – The Physics of Mounting When a MEMS die (typically silicon, **CTE ≈ 2.6 ppm/°C**) is mounted on a package (ceramic, plastic, or PCB with CTE ranging from **3 to 17 ppm/°C**), a temperature change causes differential expansion. This produces **thermal stress** that can crack the die or deform the suspended structure. ### 2.1.1 Why the Mismatch Matters A temperature swing of just **ΔT = 50 °C** can cause: - A 10 mm × 10 mm silicon die mounted on FR4 (CTE ≈ 14 ppm/°C) to experience a shear strain of: $$\varepsilon = (\alpha_{pkg} - \alpha_{Si}) \cdot \Delta T$$ For the numerical example: $$\varepsilon = (14 - 2.6) \times 10^{-6} \times 50 = 5.7 \times 10^{-3}$$ This strain, multiplied by Young's modulus of silicon (**E ≈ 169 GPa**), yields stress: $$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon = 169 \times 10^{9} \times 5.7 \times 10^{-3} \approx 963 \text{ MPa}$$ > [!WARNING] > The fracture strength of silicon is roughly **1–7 GPa** depending on surface finish. The calculated 963 MPa is dangerously close to this limit – this is exactly why *stress-isolation* mounting and *soft die-attach* materials are mandatory in production MEMS. ### 2.1.2 Hermetic vs. Non-Hermetic Packaging - **Hermetic packages** (metal, ceramic) prevent moisture ingress, essential for resonant devices (gyroscopes, oscillators) and optical MEMS. - **Non-hermetic packages** (plastic overmold, epoxy) are cheaper but permit moisture, which can cause stiction and corrosion. Used for low-cost pressure sensors and accelerometers. ## 2.2 Electrical Interfaces – The Signal Conditioning Chain A raw MEMS sensor output is rarely usable directly. The interface electronics chain typically follows the pattern: > **MEMS Element → Charge/Capacitance → Charge Amplifier → Buffer → Filter → ADC → Digital Processor** ### 2.2.1 Capacitive Readout (Very Common in KTU Problems) A parallel-plate capacitive sensor with gap $g$, overlap area $A$, and permittivity $\varepsilon$ has: $$C = \frac{\varepsilon A}{g}$$ A displacement $\Delta g$ produces a capacitance change: $$\Delta C = -\frac{\varepsilon A}{g^{2}} \Delta g$$ The sensitivity is: $$S_{C} = \frac{\partial C}{\partial g} = -\frac{\varepsilon A}{g^{2}} \quad [\text{F/m}]$$ ### 2.2.2 The Wheatstone Bridge for Piezoresistive Sensors Piezoresistive pressure sensors and accelerometers are typically arranged in a Wheatstone bridge: $$V_{out} = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} - \frac{R_3}{R_3+R_4} \right)$$ Under applied stress, each resistor changes by $\Delta R$, and: $$V_{out} \approx V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$ ### 2.2.3 The Charge Amplifier (For Piezoelectric MEMS) A piezoelectric element produces charge $Q = d \cdot F$ (where $d$ is the piezoelectric coefficient). The charge amplifier converts this to voltage: $$V_{out} = -\frac{Q}{C_f} = -\frac{d \cdot F}{C_f}$$ The feedback capacitor $C_f$ (typically **pF range**) sets the gain. ### 2.2.4 Noise Considerations The **input-referred voltage noise** of the front-end amplifier must be lower than the sensor's own noise floor. Key noise types: - **Thermal (Johnson) noise:** $v_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$ - **Shot noise:** $i_n = \sqrt{2 q I \Delta f}$ - **1/f (flicker) noise:** dominant below ~1 kHz in MOSFETs ## 2.3 Thermal Interfaces Heat generated in a MEMS device (e.g., a heated micro-bolometer, or a power-consuming RF MEMS switch) must flow to a heat sink. The **thermal resistance** is: $$R_{th} = \frac{L}{kA} \quad [\text{K/W}]$$ For a multi-layer stack, thermal resistances add in series: $$R_{th,total} = \sum_{i} \frac{L_i}{k_i A_i}$$ The **junction-to-ambient** thermal resistance is critical for reliability; exceeding the maximum junction temperature (typically **125 °C to 150 °C** for commercial parts) accelerates failure. ## 2.4 Optical Interfaces (MOEMS) In MOEMS (Micro-Optical-EMS) such as DLP (Digital Light Processing) chips or optical switches, the interface includes: - **Anti-reflection (AR) coatings:** $n_f = \sqrt{n_1 n_2}$ for quarter-wave matching - **Fiber-to-chip coupling:** alignment tolerance typically **<1 µm** - **Diffractive optical elements (DOEs):** rely on precise surface profiles The quarter-wave AR coating thickness is: $$t_{AR} = \frac{\lambda}{4 n_f}$$ ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Domain | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Mechanical | $\sigma = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermal stress from CTE mismatch | Pa | | 2 | Mechanical | $\varepsilon = (\alpha_2 - \alpha_1)\Delta T$ | Differential strain | dimensionless | | 3 | Capacitive | $C = \varepsilon A / g$ | Parallel-plate capacitance | F | | 4 | Capacitive | $S_C = \varepsilon A / g^2$ | Capacitive sensitivity | F/m | | 5 | Piezoresistive | $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R / R$ | Wheatstone bridge output | V | | 6 | Piezoelectric | $V_{out} = -Q / C_f$ | Charge amplifier output | V | | 7 | Thermal Noise | $v_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$ | Johnson noise voltage | V/√Hz | | 8 | Shot Noise | $i_n = \sqrt{2 q I \Delta f}$ | Shot noise current | A/√Hz | | 9 | Thermal | $R_{th} = L / (kA)$ | Conduction thermal resistance | K/W | | 10 | Optical | $t_{AR} = \lambda / (4 n_f)$ | AR coating thickness | m | | 11 | Optical | $n_f = \sqrt{n_1 n_2}$ | Index for zero reflectance | dimensionless | | 12 | Mechanical | $k = E w t^3 / L^3$ | Spring constant of cantilever | N/m | | 13 | SNR | $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(V_s / V_n)$ | Signal-to-noise ratio | dB | | 14 | Resonant | $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$ | Natural frequency of resonator | Hz | Where the standard constants used in KTU problems are: - $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K - $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C - $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Interface Type | Industrial Application | Failure Mode if Poorly Designed | |---|---|---| | Mechanical (CTE) | Smartphone IMU | Die cracking, bias drift | | Electrical (Cap. readout) | MEMS microphone | Reduced SNR, distortion | | Thermal | IR bolometer, RF MEMS | Overheating, drift, failure | | Optical | DLP projector, fiber switch | Loss, misalignment | | Chemical | Glucose sensor, gas sensor | Fouling, false positives | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 – Thermal Stress on a MEMS Die **Problem statement:** A silicon die (length $L = 8$ mm, width $w = 6$ mm, $E = 169$ GPa, $\alpha_{Si} = 2.6$ ppm/°C) is mounted on an FR-4 PCB ($\alpha_{PCB} = 14$ ppm/°C). It is cured at 150 °C and operates at 25 °C. Calculate the thermal stress at the die-attach interface. **Step 1 – Identify the temperature change.** $$\Delta T = T_{cure} - T_{op} = 150 - 25 = 125 \text{ °C}$$ **Step 2 – Compute differential strain.** $$\varepsilon = (\alpha_{PCB} - \alpha_{Si}) \cdot \Delta T$$ Substituting the values: $$\varepsilon = (14 - 2.6) \times 10^{-6} \times 125$$ $$\varepsilon = 11.4 \times 10^{-6} \times 125 = 1425 \times 10^{-6} = 1.425 \times 10^{-3}$$ **Step 3 – Apply Hooke's Law for thermal stress.** $$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon = 169 \times 10^{9} \cdot 1.425 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$\sigma = 240.825 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} \approx 240.8 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 4 – Compare to silicon fracture strength.** $$\sigma_{fracture} \approx 1 \text{ GPa (with surface flaws)}$$ $$\text{Safety factor} = \frac{1000}{240.8} \approx 4.15$$ > The design is safe, but only because the temperature swing is moderate. For an under-hood automotive sensor (ΔT = 200 °C), the stress would exceed 1 GPa, requiring stress-isolated packaging. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 – Capacitive Sensor Sensitivity & Signal Conditioning **Problem statement:** A MEMS accelerometer has a differential capacitor with $A = 500 \times 500$ µm², nominal gap $g = 2$ µm, and proof mass displacement under acceleration $a$ given by $x = m a / k$, with $m = 5 \times 10^{-10}$ kg and $k = 1$ N/m. A square-wave excitation of $V_{ex} = 5$ V at 100 kHz is applied. Calculate the output voltage for $a = 1g$ (9.81 m/s²). **Step 1 – Proof mass displacement.** $$x = \frac{m a}{k} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-10} \times 9.81}{1} = 4.905 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m} \approx 4.9 \text{ nm}$$ **Step 2 – Capacitance sensitivity.** $$S_C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{g^2}$$ $$S_C = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times (500 \times 10^{-6})^2}{(2 \times 10^{-6})^2}$$ $$\varepsilon_0 A = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-7} = 2.2135 \times 10^{-18} \text{ F·m}$$ $$g^2 = 4 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^2$$ $$S_C = \frac{2.2135 \times 10^{-18}}{4 \times 10^{-12}} = 5.534 \times 10^{-7} \text{ F/m}$$ **Step 3 – Capacitance change for 4.9 nm displacement.** $$\Delta C = S_C \cdot x = 5.534 \times 10^{-7} \times 4.905 \times 10^{-9}$$ $$\Delta C \approx 2.714 \times 10^{-15} \text{ F} = 2.71 \text{ fF}$$ **Step 4 – Output voltage using differential readout.** For a half-bridge configuration: $$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta C}{C_0}$$ First compute $C_0$: $$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{g} = \frac{2.2135 \times 10^{-18}}{2 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.107 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F} = 1.107 \text{ pF}$$ Then: $$V_{out} = 5 \cdot \frac{2.71 \times 10^{-15}}{1.107 \times 10^{-12}} = 5 \cdot 2.45 \times 10^{-3} \approx 12.2 \text{ mV}$$ > **Conclusion:** A 1g acceleration produces only ~12 mV output, illustrating why MEMS readouts require very low-noise charge amplifiers and why **interface design is the limiting factor** in MEMS performance. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 – Johnson Noise of the Front-End **Problem statement:** The piezoresistive sensor has an equivalent resistance $R = 1$ kΩ at room temperature ($T = 300$ K). The signal bandwidth is $\Delta f = 10$ kHz. Calculate the RMS noise voltage at the input. **Step 1 – Apply the Johnson noise formula.** $$v_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$$ **Step 2 – Substitute numerical values.** $$v_n = \sqrt{4 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300 \times 1000 \times 10000}$$ $$v_n = \sqrt{4 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}$$ $$v_n = \sqrt{16.56 \times 10^{-15}} = \sqrt{1.656 \times 10^{-14}}$$ $$v_n \approx 1.287 \times 10^{-7} \text{ V} \approx 0.129 \text{ µV}$$ > **Interpretation:** The 12.2 mV signal from the previous derivation has an SNR of: $$SNR = \frac{12.2 \times 10^{-3}}{0.129 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 94,500 \;\;\; (99.5 \text{ dB})$$ This is excellent, but remember that **1/f noise** at low frequencies and **electromagnetic interference** (from the 100 kHz excitation) will degrade the practical SNR considerably. ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix – Choosing the Right Interface | Design Requirement | Recommended Interface | Reason | |---|---|---| | Lowest cost, low precision | Wire bond + plastic package | Simple, mature | | High reliability, harsh environment | Flip-chip + hermetic ceramic | Robust, no bond-wire sag | | High-frequency signals (RF MEMS) | Coplanar waveguide + flip-chip | Shortest electrical path | | Optical I/O | Fiber pigtail + lens stack | Mode matching | | Bio/chemical sensing | Microfluidic channel + selective membrane | Selective transport | | Harsh thermal environment (ΔT > 200 °C) | Compliant die attach + stress-isolated lead frame | CTE mismatch compensation | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a MEMS System (End-to-End Interface) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Physical Quantity
Pressure, Acceleration, Light] --> B[MEMS Transducer
Proof Mass, Membrane, Resonator] B --> C[Transduction
Capacitive, Piezoresistive, Piezoelectric, Optical] C --> D[Analog Front End
Charge Amplifier, Bridge, LIA] D --> E[Signal Conditioning
Filter, Gain, Linearization] E --> F[ADC and DSP
Digital Conversion, Calibration] F --> G[Digital Interface
I2C, SPI, CAN, USB] G --> H[Application Processor
ECU, Smartphone, Medical Device] subgraph Feedback[Feedback and Compensation] H -->|Compensation Data| F end subgraph Pkg[Mechanical and Thermal Interface] B -. Die Attach .-> Pkg1[Package Substrate] Pkg1 -. Heat Path .-> Pkg2[Heat Sink or PCB] end ``` ## 4.2 Capacitive Readout Interface – Detailed Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TB Vex[Square Wave Vex
100 kHz, 5V] --> C1[Top Plate C1] Vex --> C2[Bottom Plate C2] C1 --> N1[Node A] C2 --> N2[Node B] N1 --> A1[Charge Amplifier 1] N2 --> A2[Charge Amplifier 2] A1 --> SUB[Differential Subtractor] A2 --> SUB SUB --> LPF[Low Pass Filter
fc = 10 kHz] LPF --> ADC[16-bit ADC] ADC --> DSP[Digital Calibration] DSP --> OUT[Digital Output] ``` ## 4.3 Decision Flow for Selecting a Packaging Interface ```mermaid flowchart TD START([Begin: New MEMS Product]) --> Q1{Application Area?} Q1 -->|Automotive| AUTO[High Temp, High Reliability] Q1 -->|Consumer| CONS[Low Cost, Small Size] Q1 -->|Medical| MED[Biocompatibility, Sterilization] Q1 -->|Industrial| IND[Long Life, Vibration] AUTO --> H1{Hermetic Required?} H1 -->|Yes| HPKG[Ceramic or Metal Package] H1 -->|No| SAPKG[Overmolded Plastic with Conformal Coat] CONS --> C1{Budget Constraint?} C1 -->|Tight| WB[Wire Bond + Epoxy Mold] C1 -->|Moderate| FC[Flip-Chip on Substrate] MED --> M1{Contact with Body Fluids?} M1 -->|Yes| BIOPKG[Titanium or PEEK Hermetic] M1 -->|No| MEDPKG[Standard Medical Grade Epoxy] IND --> I1{Vibration Environment?} I1 -->|Severe| DAMP[Damper Material + Wire Bond] I1 -->|Mild| STDPKG[Standard Industrial Package] ``` ## 4.4 Thermal Interface Stack (Cross-Section View) ```mermaid flowchart TB J[Junction
MEMS Active Area] --> SOL[Die Attach Solder or Epoxy
k = 50 W/mK, t = 50 um] SOL --> LDS[Lead Frame or Substrate
k = 200 W/mK Cu, t = 200 um] LDS --> TIM[Thermal Interface Material
k = 5 W/mK, t = 25 um] TIM --> HS[Heat Sink
k = 200 W/mK Al, t = 5 mm] HS --> AMB[Ambient Air
h = 10 W/m2K] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Explain the term "MEMS interface" with a suitable example. Why is it considered a critical part of the overall system?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A MEMS interface is the engineered boundary (electrical, mechanical, thermal, optical, or chemical) that allows the micro-scale sensing or actuation element to interact with the macro-scale world. Example: The charge amplifier that converts the femto-Farad capacitance change of a MEMS accelerometer into a usable millivolt signal. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for example, 1 mark for criticality reason) It is critical because **a MEMS die without a proper interface is non-functional** – the raw signals are too small, too noisy, or environmentally incompatible. Over 60% of MEMS product failures originate at the interface, not the transducer. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **What is CTE mismatch? How does it affect MEMS packaging?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** CTE (Coefficient of Thermal Expansion) mismatch is the difference in thermal expansion rate between two bonded materials, e.g., silicon die (2.6 ppm/°C) and FR-4 PCB (14 ppm/°C). (1 mark) When temperature changes, the two materials expand by different amounts, generating **thermal stress** $\sigma = E \cdot (\alpha_2 - \alpha_1) \cdot \Delta T$ at the bond interface. (1 mark) This stress can cause **die cracking, delamination, or bias drift** in the MEMS structure. Mitigation: use low-CTE ceramic packages, compliant die-attach materials, or stress-isolated lead frames. (1 mark) --- ## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall – Read This Carefully!** > - **Never** skip units in your final answer. Marks are deducted for "12.2 mV" written as just "12.2". > - **Always** state assumptions (e.g., "assuming room temperature T = 300 K", "ignoring fringing fields"). > - In derivation questions, **intermediate steps are worth 2–3 marks each**. Skipping them costs you marks even if the final answer is correct. > - For interface circuit questions, **draw the circuit diagram** – examiners specifically allocate 2–3 marks for the diagram. ### Question A (14 Marks) – Choice Option 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]** **A (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the various types of interfaces in a typical MEMS system. Discuss the role of signal conditioning in detail. **(7 marks)** **A (b)** A piezoresistive pressure sensor is designed as a Wheatstone bridge with four identical resistors of $R = 1$ kΩ. When pressure is applied, two opposite resistors increase by 1% and the other two decrease by 1%. The bridge is excited by $V_{ex} = 5$ V. **(7 marks)** (i) Derive the output voltage expression. (ii) Calculate the output voltage. (iii) Compute the Johnson noise voltage at the output for T = 300 K and bandwidth 10 kHz. --- ### Model Solution to A (a) – 7 Marks **Block diagram (2 marks):** *See Section 4.1 above for the canonical flow.* A typical MEMS system contains the following interfaces: 1. **Mechanical Interface (1 mark):** Mounts the die, isolates stress, aligns moving parts. Includes die-attach, package, and stress-relief structures. 2. **Electrical Interface (1 mark):** Routes signals via wire bonds or flip-chip bumps. Includes ESD protection, series resistors. 3. **Signal Conditioning Interface (2 marks):** This is the *most important* electronic block. It performs: - **Amplification** (charge amp, instrumentation amp) - **Filtering** (low-pass, band-pass to remove 1/f and high-freq noise) - **Linearization** (corrects nonlinear C vs. x) - **Offset removal** (chopper stabilization, auto-zero) 4. **Thermal and Optical/Chemical Interfaces (1 mark):** Manage heat, light, or analyte transport. --- ### Model Solution to A (b) – 7 Marks **(i) Derivation (3 marks):** The bridge is excited by $V_{ex}$ between top node and ground. Top node: $R + \Delta R$ and $R - \Delta R$ in series (left side), so the left midpoint voltage is: $$V_L = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R - \Delta R}{(R + \Delta R) + (R - \Delta R)} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R - \Delta R}{2R} = \frac{V_{ex}}{2} \left(1 - \frac{\Delta R}{R}\right)$$ Right side, similarly: $$V_R = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R} = \frac{V_{ex}}{2} \left(1 + \frac{\Delta R}{R}\right)$$ Differential output: $$V_{out} = V_R - V_L = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$ **(ii) Numerical calculation (2 marks):** $$\Delta R / R = 0.01$$ $$V_{out} = 5 \times 0.01 = 0.05 \text{ V} = 50 \text{ mV}$$ **(iii) Johnson noise (2 marks):** $$v_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$$ $$v_n = \sqrt{4 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300 \times 1000 \times 10{,}000}$$ $$v_n = \sqrt{1.656 \times 10^{-14}} = 1.29 \times 10^{-7} \text{ V} \approx 0.129 \text{ µV}$$ **SNR at output:** $$SNR = \frac{50 \text{ mV}}{0.129 \text{ µV}} = 3.88 \times 10^{5} \;\;\; (\approx 111.8 \text{ dB})$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) – Choice Option 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** **B (a)** Discuss in detail the mechanical and thermal interfaces in MEMS packaging. Explain the concept of CTE mismatch and its consequences. **(7 marks)** **B (b)** A capacitive MEMS accelerometer has a nominal capacitance $C_0 = 1$ pF, sensitivity $S_C = 0.5$ pF/µm, and a proof mass displacement of 5 nm per g. The readout uses a charge amplifier with feedback capacitor $C_f = 10$ pF and excitation $V_{ex} = 3.3$ V. **(7 marks)** (i) Calculate the capacitance change per g. (ii) Compute the charge generated per g. (iii) Determine the output voltage of the charge amplifier per g. --- ### Model Solution to B (a) – 7 Marks **Mechanical Interface (3 marks):** - **Die Attach:** Connects the MEMS die to the package substrate. Methods include eutectic bonding (Au-Sn), solder, epoxy. Must provide mechanical support without transmitting excessive stress. - **Stress Isolation:** Uses compliant structures (e.g., flex leads, gel coating) to absorb CTE mismatch strain. - **Hermetic Sealing:** Required for resonant and optical MEMS. Uses metal-to-glass or metal-to-metal seals. **Thermal Interface (2 marks):** - **Heat Path:** Junction → Die Attach → Lead Frame/Substrate → TIM → Heat Sink → Ambient. - **Thermal Resistance:** $R_{th} = \sum L_i / (k_i A_i)$. Lower is better. - **Maximum junction temperature** must not be exceeded (typically 125–150 °C for commercial grade). **CTE Mismatch and Consequences (2 marks):** The CTE difference between silicon (~2.6 ppm/°C) and packaging materials (3–17 ppm/°C) causes **differential thermal strain**: $$\varepsilon = (\alpha_{pkg} - \alpha_{Si}) \cdot \Delta T$$ This strain creates **thermal stress** $\sigma = E \varepsilon$ that can crack the die, warp suspended MEMS structures (causing sensor offset and drift), or delaminate the die attach. Mitigation includes choosing matched-CTE substrates (e.g., AlN, Kovar) and using stress-isolated lead frames. --- ### Model Solution to B (b) – 7 Marks **(i) Capacitance change per g (2 marks):** Displacement per g: $x = 5$ nm = $5 \times 10^{-9}$ m $$\Delta C = S_C \cdot x = 0.5 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/µm} \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ µm}$$ $$\Delta C = 2.5 \times 10^{-15} \text{ F} = 2.5 \text{ fF per g}$$ **(ii) Charge generated per g (2 marks):** $$Q = C_0 \cdot V_{ex} = 1 \times 10^{-12} \times 3.3 = 3.3 \times 10^{-12} \text{ C (full charge)}$$ For differential readout, the *change* in charge is: $$\Delta Q = \Delta C \cdot V_{ex} = 2.5 \times 10^{-15} \times 3.3 = 8.25 \times 10^{-15} \text{ C} = 8.25 \text{ fC per g}$$ **(iii) Charge amplifier output (3 marks):** $$V_{out} = -\frac{\Delta Q}{C_f} = -\frac{8.25 \times 10^{-15}}{10 \times 10^{-12}} = -8.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V}$$ $$\boxed{V_{out} \approx -0.825 \text{ mV per g}}$$ The negative sign indicates phase inversion (inverting amplifier topology). The magnitude (0.825 mV/g) is the **scale factor** of the accelerometer – a critical specification in real inertial measurement units. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist – Print This for Exam Day** - ✅ **Interface = bridge** between MEMS micro-domain and macro-world (electrical, mechanical, thermal, optical, chemical). - ✅ **CTE mismatch formula:** $\sigma = E(\alpha_2 - \alpha_1)\Delta T$. Use this whenever temperature-related packaging questions appear. - ✅ **Capacitive sensor:** $C = \varepsilon A / g$, sensitivity $S_C = \varepsilon A / g^2$ (negative for gap change, positive for area change). - ✅ **Wheatstone bridge output:** $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot (\Delta R / R)$ for full-bridge. - ✅ **Charge amplifier output:** $V_{out} = -Q / C_f = -C_{sensor} V_{ex} / C_f$. - ✅ **Johnson noise:** $v_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$. Always state the bandwidth $\Delta f$ in your answer. - ✅ **Thermal resistance:** $R_{th} = L / (kA)$. Layers in series add; layers in parallel give $1/R_{th,total} = \sum 1/R_{th,i}$. - ✅ **AR coating thickness:** $t = \lambda / (4 n_f)$ with $n_f = \sqrt{n_1 n_2}$. - ✅ **Resonant frequency:** $f_0 = (1/2\pi)\sqrt{k/m}$ – key for gyroscopes and resonators. - ✅ **Hermetic packaging** is mandatory for **resonant, optical, and medical** MEMS. - ✅ **Non-hermetic plastic packaging** is sufficient for **low-cost consumer** sensors. - ✅ **In KTU problems:** always include a circuit/block diagram (2–3 marks), state assumptions, and show intermediate steps with units. - ✅ **Common silicon properties to memorize:** $E = 169$ GPa, $\rho = 2330$ kg/m³, $\alpha = 2.6$ ppm/°C, fracture strength ~1–7 GPa. - ✅ **Standard atmospheric pressure:** 101.325 kPa – useful for pressure-sensor problems. - ✅ **Signal levels:** MEMS signals are typically **µV–mV**; therefore, low-noise front-end design is the *bottleneck*, not the transducer itself. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Examiner's Mantra:** "A MEMS device is only as good as its interface." Show the examiners that you understand this by emphasizing **real-world reliability and signal-chain design** in every long-answer question on this module.

topics.title: Assembly
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET836 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Assembly

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**MEMS Assembly** is the critical post-fabrication sequence in which individually fabricated MEMS dies (chips), integrated circuits (ICs), and passive components are physically mounted, mechanically secured, and electrically interconnected to form a fully functional micro-system package. It encompasses **die attachment**, **wire bonding**, **flip-chip bonding**, **wafer-level packaging (WLP)**, and **3D heterogeneous integration**.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (OEMET836 Module 4) framework, assembly is positioned as the **bridge between wafer-level fabrication and system-level deployment**, transforming a free-standing microstructure into a hermetically sealed, electrically addressable, and environmentally robust module.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In the KTU 2024 OEMET836 syllabus, "Assembly" forms the capstone of Module 4 (Packaging & Assembly). It carries an expected 2-mark to 14-mark distribution and directly maps to **CO4: Design and analyse MEMS packaging and assembly processes**.

## 1.2 Key Terminology Glossary

| Term | KTU-Standard Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Die** | An individual MEMS/IC chip diced from a processed wafer |
| **Substrate** | The carrier (lead-frame, ceramic, or PCB) onto which the die is mounted |
| **Die Attach** | The bonding process that fixes the die to the package substrate |
| **Wire Bond** | A fine metallic filament (Au, Al, Cu) forming electrical interconnections |
| **Flip-Chip** | A face-down bonding technique using solder or conductive bumps |
| **Underfill** | A polymer encapsulant dispensed between flip-chip bumps and substrate |
| **Self-Assembly** | Autonomous organization of micro-components driven by surface forces |
| **Pick-and-Place** | Robotic micro-positioning of dies onto substrates with micron precision |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Watchmaker's Bench" Intuition

Imagine a **Swiss watchmaker** assembling a mechanical timepiece:

- The **silicon die** is like the *watch movement* (the functional heart).
- The **package substrate** is the *watch case* that holds and protects it.
- **Wire bonds** correspond to the *tiny gears and springs* transmitting electrical signals.
- **Die attach** is the *screws* locking the movement into the case.
- **Encapsulation/lid sealing** is the *sapphire crystal and rubber gasket* shielding it from dust and moisture.

Just as a watchmaker needs a clean room, vibration-free tools, and magnification, MEMS assembly demands **Class 100–1000 cleanrooms**, **sub-micron alignment stages**, and **bonding tools** capable of gram-scale force control.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world significance:** Over **40% of MEMS product failures** are attributed to packaging and assembly defects (per industry reliability studies). This makes assembly a *yield-defining* and *cost-defining* step in MEMS commercialization.

## 1.4 Why Assembly is a "Make-or-Break" Step

MEMS devices contain *freely moving mechanical structures* (beams, membranes, cantilevers, gears). Unlike pure ICs, MEMS cannot survive:
- High shear stress during bonding
- Particulate contamination (which can jam moving parts)
- Moisture ingress (which causes stiction)
- Excessive thermal cycling (which deforms delicate structures)

Hence, the assembly process must balance **electrical performance**, **mechanical protection**, **thermal management**, and **hermeticity** — a four-way trade-off unique to MEMS.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Assembly Process Flow — Structured Logic

The MEMS assembly sequence is canonically executed in the following stages:

1. **Wafer Dicing / Singulation**
   - The processed wafer is mounted on a dicing tape (UV-curable or thermal).
   - A diamond-impregnated dicing saw separates individual dies at scribe lines.
   - **Blade rotation:** 20,000 – 40,000 RPM.
   - **Coolant:** Deionized water (to prevent thermal damage to MEMS structures).

2. **Die Inspection & Sorting**
   - Optical / electrical probing identifies Known-Good-Dies (KGD).
   - Defective dies are ink-marked and excluded from assembly.

3. **Die Attachment**
   - The KGD is picked and placed onto a substrate (lead-frame, ceramic, or PCB).
   - Bonding media: **eutectic solder, epoxy, silver-glass, or solder paste**.

4. **Wire Bonding (or Flip-Chip)**
   - Electrical pads on the die are connected to package leads.
   - Methods: **thermosonic Au ball bonding**, **wedge bonding**, **flip-chip with solder bumps**.

5. **Encapsulation / Sealing**
   - Plastic molding, ceramic lid sealing, or wafer-level cap bonding.
   - Hermetic sealing typically uses Au-Sn, Au-Si, or glass frit.

6. **Marking, Testing & Singulation**
   - Laser marking, final electrical test, and lead trimming.

## 2.2 Detailed Classification of Assembly Techniques

### A. Die Attachment Methods

| Method | Bonding Mechanism | Typical Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Eutectic Die Attach** | Au-Si (363°C) or Au-Sn (280°C) eutectic formation | High-reliability hermetic packages |
| **Epoxy Die Attach** | Thermoset polymer curing at 150–200°C | Low-cost plastic packages |
| **Silver-Glass Attach** | Ag-glass paste sintering at 350–450°C | High-temp, high-power MEMS |
| **Solder Die Attach** | Sn-Pb or SAC alloys reflow at 220–260°C | Lead-frame based packages |
| **BCB / Polyimide Bonding** | Adhesive curing at 250–300°C | Low-stress MEMS attach |

### B. Wire Bonding Variants

| Technique | Wire Material | Bond Type | KTU Exam Note |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Ball Bonding** | Gold (Au), 25 µm diameter | Thermosonic | Most common for MEMS |
| **Wedge Bonding** | Aluminium (Al), 25–125 µm | Ultrasonic | Used for fine-pitch bonding |
| **Ribbon Bonding** | Au flat ribbon | Thermosonic | High-current power devices |

### C. Flip-Chip Bonding

- **Solder Bump Flip-Chip:** Uses evaporated or electroplated solder (typically Sn-Pb or SAC).
- **Stud Bump Flip-Chip:** Au stud bumps formed via modified ball bonding.
- **Adhesive Flip-Chip:** Anisotropic Conductive Film (ACF) or Non-Conductive Paste (NCP).
- **Copper Pillar Bump:** Emerging for fine-pitch (< 50 µm pitch) 3D integration.

## 2.3 The "Why & How" Reasoning Layer

> [!NOTE]
> **Why wire bonding for MEMS?** Wire bonds add minimal mechanical load to the die, are reworkable, and tolerate the CTE (Coefficient of Thermal Expansion) mismatch between Si (2.6 ppm/°C) and the package (3–7 ppm/°C for ceramics, 15–17 ppm/°C for FR-4).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why flip-chip for advanced MEMS?** Flip-chip offers shorter electrical path → lower parasitic inductance, higher I/O density, smaller form factor, and better thermal dissipation through the bump array.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\tau = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Shear strength of die attach adhesive | $\text{Pa} \text{ or } \text{N/m}^2$ |
| 2 | $\sigma_{\text{thermal}} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermal stress due to CTE mismatch | Pa |
| 3 | $\varepsilon_{\text{mismatch}} = \left(\alpha_{\text{sub}} - \alpha_{\text{Si}}\right) \cdot \Delta T$ | Strain at the die-substrate interface | dimensionless |
| 4 | $F_{\text{bond}} = k \cdot x_{\text{deform}}$ | Wire bond contact force (Hooke's law) | N |
| 5 | $L_{\text{wire}} = 2 \cdot \sqrt{h^2 + \left(\dfrac{p}{2}\right)^2}$ | Wire bond loop length (h = height, p = pad pitch) | m |
| 6 | $R_{\text{wire}} = \rho \cdot \dfrac{L}{A_w}$ | Electrical resistance of bond wire | $\Omega$ |
| 7 | $L_{\text{wire,ind}} = \dfrac{\mu_0 \cdot l}{2\pi}\left[\ln\left(\dfrac{2l}{r}\right) - 1\right]$ | Self-inductance of straight wire bond | H |
| 8 | $Y_{\text{system}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | Cumulative yield over $n$ assembly steps | fraction |
| 9 | $R_{\text{thermal,die}} = \dfrac{t}{k \cdot A}$ | Thermal resistance of die attach layer | K/W |
| 10 | $T_{\text{junction}} = P \cdot R_{\theta JA} + T_{\text{ambient}}$ | Junction temperature (thermal budget) | K or °C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Reminder for Tables:** All absolute values and pipe-separated notation are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity. For instance, the wire inductance uses `\ln\left(\dfrac{2l}{r}\right)` rather than `|2l/r|`.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility & Industrial Applications

- **Automotive MEMS sensors (TPMS, accelerometers):** Assembly-grade reliability is mandated by **AEC-Q100** standards.
- **Consumer electronics (smartphones):** Wafer-level assembly of gyroscopes and microphones (e.g., Bosch, STMicroelectronics).
- **Medical implants (pressure sensors):** Hermetic bio-compatible packaging is *paramount*.
- **Aerospace & Defence:** Hybrid micro-assembly of inertial measurement units (IMUs) demands < 0.1° angular alignment tolerance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Thermal Stress in a Die Attach Layer

A MEMS die (Silicon, $\alpha_{\text{Si}} = 2.6 \times 10^{-6} \, /^\circ\text{C}$) is bonded onto an Alumina substrate ($\alpha_{\text{sub}} = 7.0 \times 10^{-6} \, /^\circ\text{C}$). When the package cools from the bonding temperature $T_{\text{bond}} = 350^\circ\text{C}$ to room temperature $T_{\text{room}} = 25^\circ\text{C}$, a thermal strain develops at the interface.

**Step 1: Temperature difference**
$$
\Delta T = T_{\text{bond}} - T_{\text{room}} = 350 - 25 = 325^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Step 2: Free thermal strain mismatch**
$$
\varepsilon_{\text{mismatch}} = \left(\alpha_{\text{sub}} - \alpha_{\text{Si}}\right) \cdot \Delta T
$$

Substitute the values:
$$
\varepsilon_{\text{mismatch}} = (7.0 - 2.6) \times 10^{-6} \times 325
$$

**Step 3: Numerical evaluation**
$$
\varepsilon_{\text{mismatch}} = 4.4 \times 10^{-6} \times 325 = 1.43 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 4: Convert to percentage**
$$
\varepsilon_{\text{mismatch}} = 0.143\,\%
$$

**Step 5: Compute thermal stress** (assuming the die-attach epoxy layer has $E_{\text{epoxy}} = 3.0 \, \text{GPa}$)
$$
\sigma_{\text{thermal}} = E_{\text{epoxy}} \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{mismatch}}
$$

$$
\sigma_{\text{thermal}} = 3.0 \times 10^{9} \times 1.43 \times 10^{-3} = 4.29 \times 10^{6} \, \text{Pa}
$$

**Step 6: Final answer in MPa**
$$
\sigma_{\text{thermal}} = 4.29 \, \text{MPa}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 4.29 MPa shear stress at the interface is *typically below the yield strength* of most die-attach epoxies (15–30 MPa), meaning the joint survives. However, repeated thermal cycling (e.g., 1000 cycles) can cause fatigue cracking — a common KTU viva question topic.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Wire Bond Loop Length

A MEMS die has a bond pad pitch $p = 100 \, \mu\text{m}$, and the wire loop height above the pad is $h = 75 \, \mu\text{m}$.

**Step 1: Visualize the geometry** — the wire forms a triangular loop between two adjacent pads.

**Step 2: Half-pitch horizontal distance**
$$
\frac{p}{2} = \frac{100 \, \mu\text{m}}{2} = 50 \, \mu\text{m}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the Pythagorean loop length formula**
$$
L_{\text{wire}} = 2 \cdot \sqrt{h^2 + \left(\frac{p}{2}\right)^2}
$$

**Step 4: Square the inner terms**
$$
h^2 = (75)^2 = 5625 \, \mu\text{m}^2
$$
$$
\left(\frac{p}{2}\right)^2 = (50)^2 = 2500 \, \mu\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 5: Sum**
$$
h^2 + \left(\frac{p}{2}\right)^2 = 5625 + 2500 = 8125 \, \mu\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 6: Square root**
$$
\sqrt{8125} \approx 90.14 \, \mu\text{m}
$$

**Step 7: Multiply by 2**
$$
L_{\text{wire}} = 2 \times 90.14 = 180.28 \, \mu\text{m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** Wire loop length affects both **parasitic inductance** and **mechanical robustness**. A longer loop = higher inductance and greater sag risk. Always maintain $L_{\text{wire}} \leq 5 \times p$ for reliable thermosonic bonding.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Cumulative Assembly Yield

A MEMS product undergoes 5 sequential assembly steps with individual yields $Y_1 = 0.99$, $Y_2 = 0.98$, $Y_3 = 0.97$, $Y_4 = 0.99$, $Y_5 = 0.96$.

**Step 1: State the multiplicative yield model**
$$
Y_{\text{system}} = \prod_{i=1}^{5} Y_i
$$

**Step 2: Substitute**
$$
Y_{\text{system}} = 0.99 \times 0.98 \times 0.97 \times 0.99 \times 0.96
$$

**Step 3: Multiply sequentially**
$$
0.99 \times 0.98 = 0.9702
$$
$$
0.9702 \times 0.97 = 0.9411
$$
$$
0.9411 \times 0.99 = 0.9317
$$
$$
0.9317 \times 0.96 = 0.8944
$$

**Step 4: Final result**
$$
Y_{\text{system}} \approx 0.8944 = 89.44\,\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget that **yield compounds multiplicatively** in serial assembly lines. Even if every step has 96–99% yield, the cumulative yield drops to ~90% — this is a frequently asked KTU question.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Die Attach Thermal Resistance

A die-attach layer has thickness $t = 25 \, \mu\text{m}$, thermal conductivity $k = 1.5 \, \text{W/(m·K)}$, and area $A = 4 \times 4 = 16 \, \text{mm}^2 = 16 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^2$.

**Step 1: Convert all to SI units**
$$
t = 25 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}, \quad A = 16 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Apply thermal resistance formula**
$$
R_{\theta} = \frac{t}{k \cdot A}
$$

**Step 3: Compute denominator**
$$
k \cdot A = 1.5 \times 16 \times 10^{-6} = 24 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{W/K}
$$

**Step 4: Divide**
$$
R_{\theta} = \frac{25 \times 10^{-6}}{24 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.042 \, \text{K/W}
$$

**Step 5: Interpretation**
A die dissipating $P = 0.5 \, \text{W}$ would heat the junction by:
$$
\Delta T = P \cdot R_{\theta} = 0.5 \times 1.042 = 0.521^\circ\text{C}
$$

This is *negligible*, indicating the die-attach is not a thermal bottleneck.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Assembly Methods

| Parameter | Wire Bonding | Flip-Chip (Solder) | Flip-Chip (ACF) | Self-Assembly |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Min. Pitch ($\mu\text{m}$) | 40–60 | 100–200 | 20–50 | 5–20 |
| Inductance (nH) | 1–5 | < 0.5 | < 0.5 | N/A |
| Thermal Path | Poor | Excellent | Good | Variable |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High | Low (mass-scale) |
| Reworkability | Easy | Difficult | Difficult | Impossible |
| MEMS-Suitability | High | Medium | High | Emerging |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — MEMS Assembly Process Flow

## 4.3 Mermaid Decision Diagram — Selecting an Assembly Method

```mermaid

    C0[Start Selection] --> C1{Need Rework?}
    C1 -->|Yes| C2[Use Wire Bonding]
    C1 -->|No| C3{Pitch below 80 Micron?}
    C3 -->|Yes| C4[Use Flip Chip ACF or Cu Pillar]
    C3 -->|No| C5{High Frequency Signal?}
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Layered Hierarchy — Assembly Stack-Up

```mermaid

    D0[Top Bond Pads and Sealing Lid]
    D1[Wire Bond Plane]
    D2[Active MEMS Layer]
    D3[Buried Cavity for Movable Structure]

    D6[Package Substrate Ceramic or Organic]

    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
```
