```mermaid
    D6 --> D7
    D7 --> D8
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide:** Each block represents a *layer* in the physical stack-up. The buried cavity (D3) is a hallmark of MEMS — it allows mechanical freedom for the suspended structure (e.g., a gyroscope proof mass). KTU examiners often ask students to identify such cavity layers in cross-section diagrams.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define MEMS assembly. List any four die-attachment techniques.

`[KTU University Exam — May 2024 OEMET836]`

**Model Answer:**

**MEMS Assembly** is the post-fabrication process of mounting, interconnecting, and packaging MEMS dies into a functional, environmentally protected module that can be interfaced with external electronics.

**Four die-attachment techniques:**
1. Eutectic die attach (Au-Si, Au-Sn)
2. Epoxy die attach (thermoset polymer)
3. Silver-glass die attach
4. Solder die attach (Sn-Pb, SAC alloys)

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between wire bonding and flip-chip bonding in two key aspects.

`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 OEMET836]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Wire Bonding | Flip-Chip Bonding |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Interconnection Geometry** | Wires loop above the die surface | Bumps directly under the die pads |
| **Electrical Performance** | Higher parasitic inductance and resistance | Lower parasitics; better for RF and high-speed signals |
| **Reworkability** | Easy to remove and re-bond | Difficult; typically non-reworkable |
| **I/O Density** | Limited by peripheral pad pitch | Area-array capable; high density |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 OEMET836, Module 4 Carry-Over]`

**Question:** *(a)* Explain the **wire bonding process** in MEMS packaging with a neat sketch. Discuss the different types of wire bonding and the materials used. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A MEMS die of size $4 \, \text{mm} \times 4 \, \text{mm}$ is die-attached to an alumina substrate using an epoxy layer of thickness $25 \, \mu\text{m}$ and shear modulus $G = 1.0 \, \text{GPa}$. If the package is subjected to a thermal load $\Delta T = 200^\circ\text{C}$ with CTE mismatch of $5 \times 10^{-6} \, /^\circ\text{C}$, determine the **thermal shear stress** at the die attach interface. Assume the epoxy behaves elastically. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Explanation (7 marks):**

Wire bonding is the most widely used technique to establish electrical interconnections between a MEMS/IC die and the package lead-frame or substrate. The process flow is:

1. **Die is mounted** on the substrate via die-attach.
2. The wire bonder aligns a **capillary tool** carrying a fine metallic wire (typically 25 µm Au or Al).
3. The first bond (ball bond) is formed by melting the wire tail into a ball using an **electric spark (EFO — Electronic Flame Off)** and pressing it onto the die pad with controlled force, ultrasonic energy, and temperature (**thermosonic bonding**).
4. The tool lifts and forms a **trajectory-controlled loop** to the package lead.
5. The second bond (stitch / wedge bond) is formed by ultrasonic scrubbing.
6. The wire is clamped and torn, leaving a fresh tail for the next cycle.

**Types of Wire Bonding:**
- **Ball Bonding (Thermosonic):** Au wire, spherical first bond, used for high-density MEMS.
- **Wedge Bonding (Ultrasonic):** Al wire, wedge-shaped bonds, used for fine-pitch and high-temperature applications.
- **Ribbon Bonding:** Flat Au ribbon, used for power devices.

**Materials:** Gold (Au) for inertness, Aluminium (Al) for cost-effectiveness and compatibility with IC pads, Copper (Cu) for emerging high-conductivity needs.

*(Sketch can include a die, capillary, wire loop, and package substrate.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Identify given values**
$$
\Delta T = 200^\circ\text{C}, \quad \Delta\alpha = 5 \times 10^{-6} \, /^\circ\text{C}, \quad G = 1.0 \, \text{GPa} = 1.0 \times 10^9 \, \text{Pa}
$$

**Step 2: Compute thermal strain**
$$
\varepsilon_{\text{thermal}} = \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T = 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 200 = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 3: Compute thermal shear stress (using shear modulus)**
$$
\tau_{\text{thermal}} = G \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{thermal}} = 1.0 \times 10^9 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 4: Final result**
$$
\tau_{\text{thermal}} = 1.0 \times 10^6 \, \text{Pa} = 1.0 \, \text{MPa}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Common mistakes students make on this question: (1) Using Young's modulus $E$ instead of shear modulus $G$ when shear stress is asked — *always check the requested stress type*. (2) Forgetting to convert GPa to Pa. (3) Missing units in the final answer. Marks are deducted 1 per error.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 OEMET836]`

**Question:** *(a)* Describe the **flip-chip bonding process** with a labelled diagram. Compare its advantages and disadvantages over wire bonding. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A MEMS production line has 6 sequential assembly steps with the following individual yields: $0.98, 0.97, 0.99, 0.95, 0.98, 0.96$. If the line produces 50,000 units, calculate the **number of good units** at the end. Comment on the impact of a single low-yield step. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Explanation (7 marks):**

**Flip-Chip Bonding Process:**

1. **Bump Formation:** Solder (or Au, Cu) bumps are deposited on the die pads via evaporation, electroplating, or screen printing.
2. **Flux Application:** Flux is dispensed on the substrate to clean and aid wetting.
3. **Die Placement:** The die is flipped face-down and aligned with the substrate pads using vision systems.
4. **Reflow:** The assembly is heated above the solder melting point (e.g., 250°C for SAC), causing the bumps to coalesce and form metallurgical bonds.
5. **Flux Cleaning & Underfill:** Residual flux is cleaned; underfill is dispensed and cured to relieve CTE-induced stress.

**Advantages over wire bonding:**
- Shorter electrical path → lower inductance
- Higher I/O density (area array)
- Smaller package footprint
- Better thermal dissipation
- Higher mechanical robustness

**Disadvantages:**
- Higher cost (bumping infrastructure)
- Non-reworkable joints
- Stricter planarity and coplanarity requirements
- Inspection difficulty (hidden joints)

*(Diagram should show the die flipped over the substrate with bumps forming the connection.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Compute cumulative yield**
$$
Y_{\text{system}} = 0.98 \times 0.97 \times 0.99 \times 0.95 \times 0.98 \times 0.96
$$

**Step 2: Multiply sequentially**
$$
0.98 \times 0.97 = 0.9506
$$
$$
0.9506 \times 0.99 = 0.9411
$$
$$
0.9411 \times 0.95 = 0.8940
$$
$$
0.8940 \times 0.98 = 0.8761
$$
$$
0.8761 \times 0.96 = 0.8411
$$

**Step 3: System yield**
$$
Y_{\text{system}} \approx 0.8411 = 84.11\,\%
$$

**Step 4: Number of good units**
$$
N_{\text{good}} = 50{,}000 \times 0.8411 = 42{,}055 \, \text{units}
$$

**Step 5: Comment on low-yield step**

The step with $Y_4 = 0.95$ (95%) is the *yield bottleneck*. Even if all other steps were 99%, this single step drags the cumulative yield down by 3–5%. The "weakest link" principle in serial manufacturing: improving the lowest-yield step gives the **largest marginal gain** in overall throughput.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently (1) Add yields instead of multiplying them, (2) forget to multiply by the total unit count, and (3) miss the qualitative comment part. The 2-mark comment is *often the difference between full and partial credit*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Assembly (Module 4, OEMET836)**

- **Definition:** Assembly = die attach + interconnect (wire/flip-chip) + encapsulation + sealing.
- **Die Attach Methods:** Eutectic, epoxy, silver-glass, solder, BCB.
- **Wire Bonding:** Au ball (thermosonic), Al wedge (ultrasonic), Au ribbon.
- **Flip-Chip:** Solder bump, stud bump, ACF, Cu pillar.
- **CTE of Si = 2.6 ppm/°C;** of FR-4 ≈ 15 ppm/°C — *large mismatch → thermal stress*.
- **Thermal stress formula:** $\sigma = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ (or $G$ for shear).
- **Wire loop length:** $L = 2\sqrt{h^2 + (p/2)^2}$.
- **Yield compounds:** $Y_{\text{system}} = \prod Y_i$ — *never add yields*.
- **Self-Assembly:** Driven by surface tension, electrostatic, magnetic, or capillary forces; emerging for sub-50 µm pitch MEMS.
- **Wafer-Level Packaging (WLP):** Cap wafer bonded via glass frit, Au-Sn, or polymer; enables batch-scale hermetic sealing.
- **Hermetic vs Non-Hermetic:** Hermetic packages use metal/ceramic seals (Au-Sn, Au-Si); non-hermetic use plastic molding.
- **Class 100 Cleanroom** is mandatory for MEMS die handling.
- **Industry standards:** AEC-Q100 (automotive), MIL-STD-883 (military), IPC-A-610 (assembly).
- **Typical wire diameter:** 25 µm Au, 25–125 µm Al.
- **Typical die-attach thickness:** 25–50 µm.
- **Cumulative yield of 5 steps at 98% each** ≈ **90.4%** — always less than the weakest step.
- **Flip-chip preferred for:** RF MEMS, high-density interconnects, miniature packages.
- **Wire bond preferred for:** cost-sensitive, low-I/O-count, reworkable MEMS.

**Key Takeaway:** Assembly is the *yield-defining, reliability-defining, and cost-defining* step in MEMS commercialization. Master the trade-offs between **wire bonding, flip-chip, and self-assembly** to excel in KTU 2024 ESE questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection of Packaging Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMET836 - MICRO ELECTRO MECHANICAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Selection of Packaging Materials # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Packaging Material Selection ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU 2024 OEMET836 Module 4):** > *Selection of packaging materials* in Micro Electro Mechanical Systems (MEMS) is the systematic engineering process of identifying and qualifying substrate, housing, capping, sealing, and interconnection materials that simultaneously satisfy **mechanical integrity**, **thermal management**, **chemical/environmental hermeticity**, **electrical performance**, and **biocompatibility** requirements, while remaining compatible with wafer-level and post-wafer fabrication processes of the MEMS device. Unlike integrated circuit (IC) packaging — which primarily protects the die electrically — MEMS packaging must protect **moving mechanical structures** (beams, membranes, cantilevers, proof masses, fluidic channels) from particulate contamination, moisture, mechanical shock, and process-induced damage. The package is therefore a **functional part of the MEMS device**, not merely an enclosure. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** > Packaging cost in MEMS can constitute **30 % – 95 %** of the total device cost, depending on the application domain. Hence, *material selection* is a primary cost-and-performance driver and is treated as a co-design problem alongside MEMS structure design. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition) Think of a MEMS device as a **pocket watch**: - The **gears and springs** (the MEMS microstructure) are extremely tiny, fragile, and must move freely. - The **watch case** (the package) must be: - **Dust-proof** → hermeticity against particulates. - **Water-resistant** → hermeticity against moisture. - **Strong enough** not to crack if dropped → mechanical robustness. - **Not too tight** that it squeezes the gears → matched thermal expansion. - **Comfortable on the wrist** → low weight and biocompatible (for implants). If you make the case from a material that expands/contracts much more than the gears (mismatched **CTE**), the case will warp with temperature and either crush the gears or let dust in. The "art" of packaging material selection is choosing a case material that **behaves like the gears thermally, protects them chemically, conducts their heat away, and is cheap enough to mass-produce.** --- ## 1.3 Primary Functional Requirements of a MEMS Package > [!TIP] > Every MEMS package must satisfy the **"6-H" rule** — a useful mnemonic popularized in KTU MEMS modules: | Symbol | Requirement | Engineering Meaning | |:------:|:-----------|:--------------------| | **H₁** | Hermeticity | Leak rate < 10⁻⁸ atm·cc/s of He | | **H₂** | Heat dissipation | Adequate thermal conductivity (k) | | **H₃** | Hardness / Strength | Survives drop, vibration, handling | | **H₄** | Hermetic sealing | Reliable bonds (glass frit, Au-Sn, anodic) | | **H₅** | Housing match | CTE matched to Si (2.6 ppm/°C) | | **H₆** | Hostility resistance | Chemical, biological, radiological inertness | --- ## 1.4 Categories of Packaging Materials (Overview) ```mermaid graph TD A[Packaging Materials]:::root --> B[Ceramics] A --> C[Metals & Alloys] A --> D[Polymers] A --> E[Glasses] A --> F[Semiconductors] B --> B1[Alumina Al2O3] B --> B2[Aluminum Nitride AlN] B --> B3[LTCC] B --> B4[HTCC] C --> C1[Kovar Fe-Ni-Co] C --> C2[Invar Fe-Ni] C --> C3[Stainless Steel] C --> C4[Cu-Mo / Cu-W] C --> C5[Titanium] D --> D1[Epoxy] D --> D2[Silicone] D --> D3[Polyimide] D --> D4[Parylene C] D --> D5[BCB] E --> E1[Pyrex 7740] E --> E2[Quartz] E --> E3[Schott Glass] F --> F1[Silicon Si] F --> F2[Germanium] F --> F3[GaAs] classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0b1d33,stroke-width:2px; ``` **Figure 1.1** — Hierarchical taxonomy of MEMS packaging materials. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Material Selection Decision Framework Selecting a MEMS packaging material is a **multi-objective constrained optimization problem**. The KTU 2024 OEMET836 module frames the decision as a sequential filter: 1. **CTE (Coefficient of Thermal Expansion) matching** to the die (typically Si, α_Si = **2.6 ppm/°C**). 2. **Process compatibility** — can the material survive dicing, bonding, soldering temperatures (often 300 °C – 400 °C)? 3. **Hermeticity requirement** — application critical (vacuum, medical, military) or commercial? 4. **Thermal conductivity** — high-power or low-power device? 5. **Mechanical rigidity** — harsh environment (automotive, aerospace)? 6. **Electrical isolation** — does the package need to be insulating? 7. **Cost & manufacturability** — high-volume or low-volume? 8. **Regulatory / biocompatibility** — ISO 10993 for implants. --- ## 2.2 Property-by-Property Analysis ### 2.2.1 Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE) CTE mismatch (Δα) between die and package generates **thermally induced stress**: $$\sigma_{th} = \frac{E_p \cdot (\alpha_p - \alpha_{die}) \cdot \Delta T}{1 - \nu_p}$$ where: - $E_p$ = Young's modulus of the package material - $\alpha_p, \alpha_{die}$ = CTE of package and die - $\Delta T$ = temperature excursion (often –55 °C to +150 °C for MIL-STD) - $\nu_p$ = Poisson's ratio of the package > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Design Rule of Thumb:** $|\alpha_p - \alpha_{Si}| \le 1.0 \text{ ppm/°C}$ is desirable for **direct die-attached** packages without a stress-buffer layer. Kovar (α ≈ 5.1 ppm/°C) is widely used precisely because it can be **compositional-tuned** (varying Ni:Co ratio) to land in the 4.5–6.0 ppm/°C band. ### 2.2.2 Thermal Conductivity (k) For high-power MEMS (e.g., RF MEMS switches, micro-bolometers, inkjet printheads), heat must be conducted from the die to the package and then to the ambient. Thermal resistance: $$R_{th} = \frac{L}{k \cdot A}$$ Lower $R_{th}$ is better → high $k$ materials preferred. **AlN** (k ≈ 180 W/m·K) and **BeO** (k ≈ 250 W/m·K) dominate; Al₂O₃ (k ≈ 25 W/m·K) is acceptable for low-power. ### 2.2.3 Hermeticity Hermeticity is measured as a **leak rate**, standardized per **MIL-STD-883, Method 1014**: $$L_{He} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot V}{\Delta t} \quad \text{(atm·cc/s of He)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Industry thresholds (KTU syllabus data):** > - **Class A (Vacuum):** $L_{He} < 10^{-9}$ atm·cc/s > - **Class B (Hermetic):** $L_{He} < 10^{-8}$ atm·cc/s > - **Class C (Non-hermetic):** $L_{He} > 10^{-7}$ atm·cc/s (polymer-encapsulated) ### 2.2.4 Mechanical Properties Key indices: - **Young's modulus $E$** → stiffness; affects resonance and stress coupling. - **Fracture toughness $K_{IC}$** → resistance to crack propagation. - **Vickers hardness HV** → scratch and dicing resistance. ### 2.2.5 Chemical / Biological Inertness For **biomedical MEMS** (pressure sensors, neural probes, drug-delivery pumps), the package must be **biocompatible** per **ISO 10993-5** (cytotoxicity) and **USP Class VI**. **Titanium (Ti)** and **Parylene C** dominate. --- ## 2.3 Detailed Material Property Comparison | Material | CTE α (ppm/°C) | Thermal Cond. k (W/m·K) | Density ρ (g/cm³) | Electrical | Hermetic? | Typical Use Case | Cost Index | |:---------|:--------------:|:-----------------------:|:-----------------:|:----------:|:---------:|:-----------------|:----------:| | **Silicon (Si)** | 2.6 | 150 | 2.33 | Semi | Yes (wafer) | Die + cap wafer | Low | | **Pyrex 7740 glass** | 3.3 | 1.1 | 2.23 | Insulator | Yes | Anodic bond cap | Low | | **Alumina (96 % Al₂O₃)** | 6.5 | 25 | 3.75 | Insulator | Yes | Multi-chip module | Medium | | **AlN (Aluminum Nitride)** | 4.5 | 180 | 3.26 | Insulator | Yes | High-power RF MEMS | High | | **LTCC** | 3.0–7.0 | 2–5 | 2.5–3.0 | Insulator | Yes | Integrated passives | Medium | | **HTCC** | 5.0–7.0 | 15–25 | 3.6 | Insulator | Yes | Military/aerospace | High | | **Kovar (Fe-29Ni-17Co)** | 5.1 (tunable) | 17 | 8.35 | Conductor | Yes | Metal-can packages | Medium | | **Invar (Fe-36Ni)** | 1.2 | 11 | 8.13 | Conductor | Yes | Cryo / matched-CTE | Medium | | **Stainless Steel 304** | 17.3 | 16 | 8.00 | Conductor | Yes | Harsh env. housing | Low | | **Titanium (Ti Grade 2)** | 8.6 | 17 | 4.51 | Conductor | Yes | Biomedical implants | High | | **Cu-Mo (85/15)** | 6.0 | 165 | 10.0 | Conductor | Yes | Power modules | High | | **Epoxy (e.g., Epoxy 7110)** | 60 | 0.2 | 1.2 | Insulator | No | Encapsulation | Very low | | **Silicone (PDMS)** | 310 | 0.15 | 0.97 | Insulator | No | Flexible microfluidics | Low | | **Polyimide (PI-2611)** | 3.0 | 0.2 | 1.42 | Insulator | No | Stress buffer | Medium | | **Parylene C** | 35 | 0.08 | 1.29 | Insulator | Semi | Conformal coating | Medium | | **BCB (Cyclotene 4026)** | 42 | 0.29 | 1.05 | Insulator | No | Wafer-level pack | Medium | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Parylene** as fully hermetic. While its **moisture permeability is very low**, it is *not* classified as hermetic by MIL-STD-883. Use it as a **moisture barrier layer**, not a hermetic seal. --- ## 2.4 Sealing Technologies & Their Material Requirements | Sealing Method | Typical Materials | Temp. (°C) | Hermeticity Class | CTE Tolerance | |:---------------|:-----------------|:----------:|:-----------------:|:-------------:| | **Anodic bonding** | Pyrex 7740 – Si | 300–450 | Class A | Excellent | | **Glass-frit bonding** | Low-T glass paste | 380–450 | Class A/B | Good | | **Eutectic Au-Sn (80/20)** | Au-Sn solder | 280–320 | Class A | Good | | **Eutectic Au-Si** | Au film on Si | 363 | Class A | Excellent | | **Fusion bonding (Si-Si)** | Native SiO₂ | 800–1100 | Class A | Perfect | | **Thermocompression** | Au-Au | 300–400 | Class A | Good | | **Adhesive (epoxy)** | Epoxy | 150–200 | Class C | Excellent | | **Solder (Sn-Pb, SAC)** | Pb-free solders | 220–260 | Class B | Medium | --- ## 2.5 The KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet | Equation | Formula | Units | Engineering Use | |:---------|:--------|:------|:----------------| | **Thermal stress (bimorph)** | $\sigma = E_p \cdot \Delta \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Pa | Die-attach stress | | **Thermal resistance** | $R_{th} = L/(kA)$ | K/W | Heat-sink design | | **Leak rate (He)** | $L = \Delta P \cdot V / \Delta t$ | atm·cc/s | Hermeticity test | | **Bimaterial curvature (Stoney)** | $\kappa = \frac{6 \cdot \sigma_f \cdot h_f}{M_s \cdot h_s^2}$ | 1/m | Wafer bow | | **Young's modulus rule** | $E_{eff} = \sum V_i E_i$ | Pa | Composite estimate | | **CTE rule of mixtures** | $\alpha_{eff} = \sum V_i \alpha_i$ | ppm/°C | Composite estimate | | **Dew point (vacuum pack)** | $T_{dp} = f(P_{H_2O})$ | °C | Moisture control | | **Q-factor of sealed cavity** | $Q = \omega \cdot V / (\delta P)$ | – | Pressure damping | > **Notation in figure:** $\sigma_f$ = film stress, $h_f,h_s$ = thicknesses, $M_s$ = biaxial modulus of substrate, $V_i$ = volume fraction. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Thermal Stress at a Die-Attach Interface > **Problem statement:** A silicon die ($E_{Si}=130$ GPa, $\alpha_{Si}=2.6$ ppm/°C, $\nu_{Si}=0.28$) is bonded at 320 °C to a Kovar package ($\alpha_{K}=5.1$ ppm/°C). Calculate the residual thermal stress at 25 °C, taking $E_K=140$ GPa, $\nu_K=0.30$. **Step 1 — Identify the temperature excursion.** The bonding (stress-free) temperature is $T_{bond}=320$ °C, and the operating temperature is $T_{op}=25$ °C. $$ \Delta T = T_{op} - T_{bond} = 25 - 320 = -295 \text{ °C} $$ (The package is now *cooler* than its stress-free state, so the higher-CTE Kovar wants to contract *more* than the Si, putting the Si die under **tensile** stress — but the package is constrained, putting the die under **compressive** stress in practice; the magnitude is what matters.) **Step 2 — Write the general bi-material stress equation.** $$ \sigma = \frac{E_p \cdot (\alpha_p - \alpha_{die}) \cdot \Delta T}{1 - \nu_p} $$ **Step 3 — Plug in numerical values.** $$ \sigma = \frac{(140 \times 10^{9}) \cdot (5.1 - 2.6) \times 10^{-6} \cdot (-295)}{1 - 0.30} $$ **Step 4 — Simplify step by step.** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta \alpha &= (5.1 - 2.6) \times 10^{-6} = 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ °C}^{-1} \\ E_p \cdot \Delta \alpha &= 140 \times 10^{9} \cdot 2.5 \times 10^{-6} = 350 \times 10^{3} \text{ Pa/°C} \\ E_p \cdot \Delta \alpha \cdot \Delta T &= 350 \times 10^{3} \cdot (-295) = -103.25 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} \\ \sigma &= \frac{-103.25 \times 10^{6}}{0.70} = -147.5 \text{ MPa} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Physical interpretation.** The negative sign indicates the die is in **compressive** stress (Kovar wants to shrink more than Si, so it pulls the Si into compression). Magnitude **147.5 MPa** exceeds the typical fracture strength of bulk Si (~ 7 GPa in tension but only ~ 200 MPa at the die-attach interface due to edge defects and the high stress gradient). The KTU examiner will expect students to **flag this as a risk** and propose either: - Using a **compliant die-attach (epoxy/solder)** with low modulus to absorb strain, or - Choosing **Invar (α = 1.2)** or **Mo (α = 5.0)** for better CTE matching. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hermeticity (Helium Leak-Rate Calculation) > **Problem statement:** A MEMS pressure-sensor cavity of internal volume $V=5$ mm³ is filled with **10 % He + 90 % N₂** at 1.2 atm and sealed. After 24 hours the He partial pressure drops to 9.4 % in an external He-detector. Compute the leak rate. **Step 1 — Use the ideal-gas conversion.** Internal He partial pressure initially: $$ P_{He,i} = 0.10 \times 1.2 = 0.12 \text{ atm} $$ Final partial pressure after leak: $$ P_{He,f} = 0.094 \times P_{final} $$ **Step 2 — Assume external He detector at vacuum; pressure drop dominates.** $$ \Delta P_{He} = 0.10 - 0.094 = 0.006 \text{ atm (approx., for small leak)} $$ A more accurate way uses the **partial pressure drop approach**: $$ \Delta P_{He} = P_{He,i} - P_{He,f} = 0.120 - 0.1128 = 0.0072 \text{ atm} $$ **Step 3 — Apply the leak-rate formula.** $$ L_{He} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot V}{\Delta t} $$ where $V = 5 \text{ mm}^3 = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm}^3$ and $\Delta t = 24 \text{ h} = 86400 \text{ s}$. $$ \begin{aligned} L_{He} &= \frac{0.0072 \text{ atm} \cdot 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm}^3}{86400 \text{ s}} \\ L_{He} &= \frac{3.6 \times 10^{-5}}{86400} \\ L_{He} &= 4.17 \times 10^{-10} \text{ atm·cc/s} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Classification per MIL-STD-883 Method 1014.** $$ L_{He} = 4.17 \times 10^{-10} \text{ atm·cc/s} < 10^{-9} \text{ → Class A (vacuum-grade hermetic).} $$ The KTU 2024 rubric awards full marks only if the student (a) clearly states the standard, (b) computes the *sign* correctly, and (c) classifies per the standard. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — CTE Selection via Stoney's Equation (Thin-Film Stress on a Wafer) > **Problem statement:** A 1 μm parylene-C film is deposited on a 500 μm Si wafer. After deposition at 25 °C, the wafer curvature changes by $\Delta \kappa = 0.005 \text{ m}^{-1}$. Compute the film stress. Given: $M_s = 180.5$ GPa (biaxial modulus of (100) Si), $\nu_s = 0.28$, $E_s = 130$ GPa. **Step 1 — Recall Stoney's equation.** $$ \sigma_f = \frac{M_s \cdot h_s^2 \cdot \Delta \kappa}{6 \cdot h_f} $$ **Step 2 — Insert values.** $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_f &= \frac{(180.5 \times 10^9) \cdot (500 \times 10^{-6})^2 \cdot 0.005}{6 \cdot (1 \times 10^{-6})} \\ &= \frac{180.5 \times 10^9 \cdot 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 0.005}{6 \times 10^{-6}} \\ &= \frac{180.5 \times 10^9 \cdot 1.25 \times 10^{-9}}{6 \times 10^{-6}} \\ &= \frac{225.6}{6 \times 10^{-6}} \\ &= 37.6 \text{ MPa} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Interpretation.** Positive $\sigma_f$ indicates a **tensile** film — parylene is known to have a low intrinsic tensile stress (typically 5–60 MPa), confirming the calculation is realistic. Parylene's low stress is a *key reason* it is the preferred conformal coating for MEMS. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection Flowchart Logic The KTU 2024 module assesses this decision tree explicitly. Below is the analytical decision matrix students should be able to reproduce: ``` START → Is the application vacuum / military / medical? ├─ YES → CTE match to Si required │ → CTE tolerance ≤ 1 ppm/°C ? │ ├─ YES → Invar / Mo / direct Si-Si fusion bond │ └─ NO → Kovar with compliant die-attach │ OR AlN / LTCC ceramic └─ NO → Is biocompatibility required? ├─ YES → Ti / Parylene C / medical-grade epoxy └─ NO → Is thermal dissipation critical? ├─ YES → AlN (insulator) or Cu-Mo (conductor) └─ NO → Cost-driven? ├─ YES → Alumina (Al₂O₃) / Kovar └─ NO → LTCC for 3-D integration ``` **End of analytical matrix.** --- ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — KTU-Style 14-Mark Style > **Problem (KTU 2018/2019/2020 repeated pattern):** > A MEMS accelerometer die (Si, $5 \text{ mm} \times 5 \text{ mm} \times 0.5 \text{ mm}$) is to be packaged in a metal-can Kovar housing with Au-Sn eutectic sealing. The die is bonded at 310 °C. Estimate: > (a) The thermal stress on the die at –40 °C operating temperature. > (b) Justify whether Kovar is the correct material choice, or suggest an alternative. **Solution:** **(a)** $\Delta T = -40 - 310 = -350$ °C. $$ \sigma = \frac{140 \times 10^9 \cdot (5.1 - 2.6) \times 10^{-6} \cdot (-350)}{0.70} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} &= \frac{140 \times 10^9 \cdot 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \cdot (-350)}{0.70} \\ &= \frac{350 \times 10^3 \cdot (-350)}{0.70} \\ &= \frac{-122.5 \times 10^6}{0.70} \\ &= -175 \text{ MPa (compressive)} \end{aligned} $$ **(b)** Since 175 MPa is approaching Si's safe compressive limit (~ 200 MPa edge, ~ 1.5 GPa bulk), **Kovar is acceptable for this size of die**, but for larger dies (e.g., $10 \times 10$ mm²) the same stress can cause **die cracking**. Recommendation: - Use **Au-Sn eutectic die-attach** (low modulus) to absorb strain. - For larger dies, switch to **Invar (α = 1.2)** or use a **stress-buffer polyimide layer** (α ≈ 3 ppm/°C, low modulus E ≈ 3 GPa). - Verify hermeticity with **He leak test** at $L < 10^{-8}$ atm·cc/s. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a MEMS Package > **Diagram Note:** The block diagram below is rendered in Mermaid and shows the **functional decomposition** of a typical MEMS package and which material class serves which function. Green = suitable for direct die-attach; Yellow = require a compliant buffer; Red = unsuitable without a stress-decoupling scheme. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **"List any four key material-property criteria for selecting MEMS packaging materials."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The four most important criteria are: 1. **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE) match** to the silicon die (target: |Δα| ≤ 1 ppm/°C for direct die-attach). 2. **Hermeticity**, quantified by He leak rate (Class A < 10⁻⁹ atm·cc/s, MIL-STD-883). 3. **Thermal conductivity (k)** for adequate heat dissipation from the die. 4. **Mechanical robustness** (Young's modulus, fracture toughness) to survive dicing, handling, and shock. *(Auxiliary criteria worth ½ mark each: chemical inertness, biocompatibility for medical, cost, and process compatibility with bonding temperature.)* --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **"Differentiate between hermetic and non-hermetic packaging. Give one example material for each."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Hermetic | Non-Hermetic | |:-------|:---------|:-------------| | Definition | Impermeable to moisture & gases over device lifetime | Permits slow moisture ingress; relies on passivation | | Leak rate | < 10⁻⁸ atm·cc/s He | > 10⁻⁷ atm·cc/s He | | Example material | **Kovar** with **Au-Sn eutectic** seal | **Epoxy molding compound** | | Typical use | Vacuum, military, medical | Consumer, low-cost IoT | | Lifetime | > 20 years | 5–10 years | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > The following two questions are designed to mirror **KTU 2024 ESE OEMET836 Module 4** style. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- ### 🔵 Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Jul 2024 Model]` **(a)** Explain the **"6-H rule"** of MEMS packaging material requirements. Discuss in detail how CTE, thermal conductivity, and hermeticity are evaluated for a chosen packaging material. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A silicon pressure-sensor die ($E_{Si} = 130$ GPa, $\alpha_{Si} = 2.6$ ppm/°C) is to be packaged in **Kovar** ($E_K = 140$ GPa, $\alpha_K = 5.1$ ppm/°C, $\nu_K = 0.30$). Bonding temperature = 300 °C; operating temperature = –40 °C. Calculate the residual thermal stress and comment on whether Kovar is a suitable choice. Suggest an alternative if not. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A **Part (a) — The 6-H Rule (7 marks):** > The **6-H rule** is a mnemonic capturing the six primary functional requirements a MEMS packaging material must satisfy: 1. **Hermeticity (H₁):** The package must prevent ingress of moisture and contaminants. Hermeticity is quantified by He leak rate per **MIL-STD-883 Method 1014**: Class A: L < 10⁻⁹ atm·cc/s; Class B: L < 10⁻⁸ atm·cc/s; Class C: non-hermetic (L > 10⁻⁷). 2. **Heat dissipation (H₂):** The material's thermal conductivity (k) governs the thermal resistance $R_{th} = L/(kA)$ between die and ambient. For high-power MEMS (e.g., RF switches, micro-bolometers), AlN (k ≈ 180 W/m·K) is preferred; Al₂O₃ (k ≈ 25 W/m·K) is acceptable for low-power. 3. **Hardness / Strength (H₃):** The package must survive mechanical handling, dicing, drop, and vibration. Critical parameters: Young's modulus E, fracture toughness $K_{IC}$, Vickers hardness HV. 4. **Hermetic sealing (H₄):** The chosen material must be compatible with a reliable sealing process — glass-frit, Au-Sn eutectic, anodic bonding, or thermocompression Au-Au. 5. **Housing match (H₅):** CTE of the package must be matched to the Si die. The stress generated is $\sigma = E_p \cdot \Delta \alpha \cdot \Delta T / (1-\nu_p)$. KTU tolerance: |Δα| ≤ 1 ppm/°C for high-reliability. 6. **Hostility resistance (H₆):** Chemical, biological, and radiological inertness is required for medical, military, and space applications. **Ti** and **Parylene C** are the gold standard for **biocompatibility** (ISO 10993). > **Conclusion:** A single material rarely satisfies all 6 H's; therefore MEMS packages are typically **multi-material composites** (e.g., Kovar housing + Pyrex feedthroughs + Au-Sn seal + Parylene coating). **Part (b) — Thermal Stress Calculation (7 marks):** > **Step 1 — Identify temperature excursion.** $$ \Delta T = T_{op} - T_{bond} = -40 - 300 = -340 \text{ °C} $$ > **Step 2 — Identify CTE mismatch.** $$ \Delta \alpha = \alpha_K - \alpha_{Si} = (5.1 - 2.6) \times 10^{-6} = 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ °C}^{-1} $$ > **Step 3 — Apply the thermal-stress equation.** $$ \sigma = \frac{E_K \cdot \Delta \alpha \cdot \Delta T}{1 - \nu_K} $$ > **Step 4 — Plug numerical values.** $$ \sigma = \frac{(140 \times 10^{9}) \cdot (2.5 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (-340)}{1 - 0.30} $$ > **Step 5 — Simplify.** $$ \begin{aligned} E_K \cdot \Delta \alpha &= 140 \times 10^{9} \cdot 2.5 \times 10^{-6} = 3.5 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa/°C} \\ E_K \cdot \Delta \alpha \cdot \Delta T &= 3.5 \times 10^{5} \cdot (-340) = -1.19 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} \\ \sigma &= \frac{-1.19 \times 10^{8}}{0.70} = -1.70 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} = -170 \text{ MPa (compressive)} \end{aligned} $$ > **Step 6 — Interpretation and recommendation.** A compressive stress of **170 MPa** is below the bulk fracture limit of Si (~1.5 GPa) but is **at the edge of the safe operating envelope for small die** because surface/edge defects and stress concentrations can locally exceed 1 GPa. **For this die, Kovar is acceptable provided:** - A **compliant die-attach** (epoxy or Au-Sn) absorbs shear. - Hermeticity is verified by He leak test (Class A: L < 10⁻⁹ atm·cc/s). **Alternative materials** (if tighter reliability is required): - **Invar (α = 1.2 ppm/°C):** $\Delta \alpha = 1.4 \times 10^{-6}$ → σ = ~95 MPa, **safer**. - **Molybdenum (α = 5.0 ppm/°C):** Even better matched; used in high-power modules. - **Direct Si-Si fusion bond:** eliminates the die-attach layer entirely → σ → 0. --- ### 🟢 Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]` **(a)** Compare **ceramic, metal, and polymer** packaging materials for MEMS devices. Tabulate at least three distinguishing properties for each class. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A MEMS gyroscope is sealed under vacuum (10 mTorr) inside a **ceramic (Alumina, Al₂O₃) package** using **glass-frit bonding** at 420 °C. Discuss the suitability of this packaging scheme. What failure modes should be tested for, and how would you verify hermeticity? **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B **Part (a) — Ceramic vs Metal vs Polymer (7 marks):** | Property | **Ceramics** (Al₂O₃, AlN, LTCC) | **Metals** (Kovar, Invar, Ti) | **Polymers** (Epoxy, Parylene, Polyimide) | |:---------|:--------------------------------|:------------------------------|:------------------------------------------| | **CTE (ppm/°C)** | 3.0 – 7.0 (closely matched to Si) | 1.2 – 17.3 (wide range; tunable) | 3.0 – 310 (very high; mismatched) | | **Thermal Conductivity k (W/m·K)** | 25 (Al₂O₃) – 180 (AlN) | 11 – 400 (Cu) | 0.08 – 0.3 (very low) | | **Hermeticity** | Excellent (Class A) | Excellent (Class A) | Poor (Class C) except Parylene (semi) | | **Electrical** | Insulator | Conductor | Insulator | | **Cost** | Medium–High | Medium | Very Low | | **Process Temp.** | High (400–1000 °C) | Medium (300–450 °C) | Low (150–250 °C) | | **Biocompatibility** | Good (Al₂O₃) | Excellent (Ti) | Variable (Parylene = good) | | **Typical Use** | High-reliability, multi-chip modules | Hermetic metal-can, feedthroughs | Consumer, encapsulation, conformal coat | > **Synthesis:** **Ceramics** dominate for **high-reliability hermetic** applications (military, aerospace, medical). **Metals** dominate for **high-power** and **high-pin-count feedthroughs**. **Polymers** dominate for **cost-sensitive consumer MEMS** where 5–10 year lifetime is acceptable. **Part (b) — Alumina + Glass-Frit for Vacuum Gyroscope (7 marks):** > **Step 1 — Suitability analysis.** - **Alumina (Al₂O₃, 96 %)** has α ≈ 6.5 ppm/°C, k ≈ 25 W/m·K, excellent insulation, and well-established metallization (thick-film Ag, Au) → **suitable for gyroscope housing**. - **Glass-frit bonding** at 420 °C with low-melting-point glass paste (e.g., Schott GM31107) is a mature, **wafer-level-capable** sealing process → **acceptable** for batch fabrication of gyroscopes. - **Vacuum retention (10 mTorr)** requires Class A hermeticity → glass-frit seals routinely achieve L < 10⁻⁹ atm·cc/s. > **Step 2 — Failure modes to be tested.** 1. **Hermeticity loss** — pinholes in glass-frit, delamination at the ceramic-glass interface. 2. **Vacuum degradation** — outgassing from internal surfaces, permeation through the ceramic. 3. **CTE-induced cracking** — Δα ≈ 3.9 ppm/°C (Al₂O₃ vs Si) can cause interfacial shear stress on large die. 4. **Mechanical shock / vibration** — glass-frit is brittle; needs underfill or mechanical reinforcement. 5. **Q-factor drift** — pressure rise inside the cavity increases viscous damping, degrading the gyroscope's bias stability. > **Step 3 — Hermeticity verification methods.** - **He leak test (MIL-STD-883 Method 1014):** Bomb at 3 atm He for 2 h, then measure He egress with mass spectrometer. Pass criterion: L < 10⁻⁹ atm·cc/s. - **Gross leak test (Method 1014 C, fluorocarbon bubble):** For larger leaks. - **Residual Gas Analysis (RGA):** Mass-spec the sealed cavity via a built-in port; confirm H₂O < 1000 ppm. - **Accelerated aging (85 °C / 85 % RH):** Long-term hermeticity check. - **Q-factor measurement over time:** Indirect functional test — degradation of gyroscope Q reveals pressure rise. > **Conclusion:** **Al₂O₃ + glass-frit is a suitable, industry-standard packaging scheme for vacuum-sealed MEMS gyroscopes**, with a well-defined test protocol. For ultra-high Q-factor applications (e.g., navigation-grade gyros), upgrade to **Si-Si fusion bonding** or **Au-Sn eutectic in a Kovar package** for superior hermeticity. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Traps (Module 4 OEMET836):** > 1. **Confusing hermetic with non-hermetic:** Polymers are *not* hermetic. Parylene is *not* hermetic by MIL-STD. (−1 mark) > 2. **Wrong sign in thermal stress:** The stress is **compressive** when ΔT < 0 and α_p > α_Si, *not* tensile. (−1 mark) > 3. **Unit errors in leak rate:** Always state **atm·cc/s of He**, not "sccm" or "% per hour". (−1 mark) > 4. **Confusing thermal conductivity of Al₂O₃ (25) with AlN (180):** A frequent trap in design justifications. (−1 mark) > 5. **Omitting the standard reference:** "Hermetic" without citing MIL-STD-883 Method 1014 is incomplete. (−½ mark) > 6. **Writing CTE in %:** Use **ppm/°C** consistently. (−½ mark) > 7. **Forgetting Poisson's ratio (1 − ν) factor:** The simplified form σ = E·Δα·ΔT omits it. (−1 mark) --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!TIP] > **KTU 2024 OEMET836 Module 4 — Packaging Material Selection: One-Page Recap** - ☐ MEMS packaging is a **functional co-design** problem, not a post-design afterthought. - ☐ The **6-H rule** (Hermeticity, Heat, Hardness, Hermetic-seal, Housing-match, Hostility) is the master mnemonic. - ☐ **CTE matching** to Si (α = 2.6 ppm/°C) is the single most important selection criterion. - ☐ **Thermal stress equation:** $\sigma = E_p \cdot (\alpha_p - \alpha_{die}) \cdot \Delta T / (1 - \nu_p)$ — always show all four terms. - ☐ **Hermeticity metric:** He leak rate L in **atm·cc/s**; Class A < 10⁻⁹; Class B < 10⁻⁸; Class C > 10⁻⁷. - ☐ **Material–application map:** - Vacuum / Military → Kovar + Au-Sn, or AlN + glass-frit - Biomedical → Ti + Parylene C (ISO 10993) - High-power RF → AlN or Cu-Mo - Consumer IoT → Plastic (LCP) + epoxy molding - ☐ **Sealing technologies:** Anodic, glass-frit, eutectic (Au-Sn, Au-Si), thermocompression, adhesive. - ☐ **Stoney's equation** governs thin-film (Parylene, polyimide) stress on wafers. - ☐ **Parylene C** is the gold-standard **conformal coating**; NOT hermetic but a great moisture barrier. - ☐ **Always cite the standard** (MIL-STD-883, ISO 10993, AEC-Q100) in exam answers. - ☐ KTU-typical numerics: 14-mark problems combine a 7-mark *qualitative discussion* (criteria, application mapping) with a 7-mark *quantitative derivation* (stress, leak rate, or Stoney's). > **End of OEMET836 Module 4 — Selection of Packaging Materials | KTU 2024 Scheme**
